EDCS-00300195.text_edition: \n Ferrandus Felix Liberi fidelis in pace vixit annis LXX\n
EDCS-00300195.translation: Ferrandus Felix, loyal to Liber, lived 70 years in peace.
EDCS-00300195.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of Ferrandus Felix, dating likely to the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-00381014.text_edition: \n InIn C(ai)? Clodi equitis legionis et legionis III Augustae decurionis emeriti et in honorem Cai Clodii equitis Romani filii et heredis eius IIviri II flaminis perpetui decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-00381014.translation: For Gaius Clodius, a cavalryman of the legion and a retired decurion of the III Augusta legion, and in honor of his son and heir Gaius Clodius, Roman knight, who held the office of IIvir and perpetual flamen, by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-00381014.summary: A dedication honoring Gaius Clodius, likely a funerary monument or honorary inscription, from the Roman period.

EDCS-00381015.text_edition: \n Orfito et Priscino consulibus ex ratione Callisti numero DX Certi\n
EDCS-00381015.translation: In the consulship of Orfitus and Priscinus, from the account of Callistus, number 50 certain ones.
EDCS-00381015.summary: A record of accounts or provisions, dated by the consulship of Orfitus and Priscinus, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-00381019.text_edition: \n Ex ratione officinae Agenti Calisti liberti Augusti numero X\n
EDCS-00381019.translation: From the account of the workshop of Agentius Callistus, freedman of Augustus, number 10.
EDCS-00381019.summary: An accounting entry for an imperial freedman's workshop, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-00381022.text_edition: \n Ex ratione Pluriani Augusti liberti Iun et Serviano III numero\n
EDCS-00381022.translation: From the account of Plurianus, freedman of Augustus, numbers Iun and Servianus III.
EDCS-00381022.summary: An accounting record related to Plurianus, an imperial freedman, possibly indicating quantities or individuals, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-00381023.text_edition: \n Hadriani Augusti numero Iun et Serviano III\n
EDCS-00381023.translation: Of Hadrianus Augustus, number Iun and Servianus III.
EDCS-00381023.summary: An accounting entry mentioning Hadrianus Augustus, likely related to imperial property or accounts, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-00381024.text_edition: \n Hadriani Augusti CV et Serviano III\n
EDCS-00381024.translation: Of Hadrianus Augustus, 105 and Servianus III.
EDCS-00381024.summary: An accounting entry concerning Hadrianus Augustus, possibly listing quantities or individuals, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-00381027.text_edition: \n Numero XII officinae Anullino II et Frontone consulibus caesura Athenodori procuratoris\n
EDCS-00381027.translation: Number 12 from the workshop of Anullinus and Fronto, consuls, under the supervision of Athenodorus, procurator.
EDCS-00381027.summary: A record of goods or supplies from a workshop, dated by the consulship of Anullinus and Fronto and mentioning a procurator, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-00381028.text_edition: \n Numero CCXXX officina Caelestis Luciano et Fabiano consulibus caesura Athenodori procuratoris\n
EDCS-00381028.translation: Number 230 from the workshop of Caelestis, in the consulship of Lucianus and Fabianus, under the supervision of Athenodorus, procurator.
EDCS-00381028.summary: A record of goods or supplies from Caelestis's workshop, dated by the consulship of Lucianus and Fabianus and mentioning a procurator, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-00381033.text_edition: \n Hadriani Augusti de loco Camerino et Nigro consulibus sub cura Galli\n
EDCS-00381033.translation: Of Hadrianus Augustus, from the area of Camerinus and Niger, consuls, under the care of Gallus.
EDCS-00381033.summary: An accounting record mentioning Hadrianus Augustus and dated by the consulship of Camerinus and Niger, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-00381034.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Antonini Augusti Pii numero officina Silvano et Augurino consulibus V\n
EDCS-00381034.translation: Under the consulship of Silvanus and Augurinus, during the reign of Emperor Antoninus Pius Augustus.
EDCS-00381034.summary: This is a dating inscription, likely for an official document or monument, referencing the consuls Silvanus and Augurinus during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-00381035.text_edition: \n Hadriani Augusti Aelio Caesare II consule sub cura Agathae liberti\n
EDCS-00381035.translation: Under the consulship of Aelius Caesar, during the reign of Hadrian Augustus, under the care of Agatha, freedwoman.
EDCS-00381035.summary: This is a dating inscription, likely for an official document or monument, referencing the consulship of Aelius Caesar and the reign of Hadrian Augustus, overseen by a freedwoman named Agatha.

EDCS-00381036.text_edition: \n Hadriani Augusti de loco Camerino et Nigro consulibus sub cura Agathae liberti\n
EDCS-00381036.translation: Under the consulship of Camarinus and Niger, from the estate of Camerinus and Niger, during the reign of Hadrian Augustus, under the care of Agatha, freedwoman.
EDCS-00381036.summary: This is a dating inscription, likely for an official document or monument, referencing the consuls Camarinus and Niger and the reign of Hadrian Augustus, overseen by a freedwoman named Agatha.

EDCS-00381040.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Antonini Augusti Pii domini numero officina Commodo et Laterano consulibus BAB\n
EDCS-00381040.translation: Under the consulship of Commodus and Lateranus, during the reign of Emperor Antoninus Pius Augustus.
EDCS-00381040.summary: This is a dating inscription, likely for an official document or monument, referencing the consuls Commodus and Lateranus during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-00381041.text_edition: \n Pisone et Bolano consulibus ex ratione Felicis Augusti servi de numero XXX officina nova Caelestis\n
EDCS-00381041.translation: Under the consulship of Piso and Bolanus, according to the accounts of Felix, freedman of the Emperor, from the thirty-man workshop of Caelestis.
EDCS-00381041.summary: This inscription records administrative details from a workshop under the consulship of Piso and Bolanus, managed by the freedman Felix.

EDCS-00381051.text_edition: \n Pro salute et reditu Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini voto suscepto Pientius Augusti libertus procurator marmorum Numidicorum porticum cum cella sacerdae et cisterna perfecit eaque populo Simittuensi praebenda dedicavit\n
EDCS-00381051.translation: For the health and return of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, Pientius, freedman of the Emperor, procurator of Numidian marbles, having undertaken a vow, constructed the portico with the priest's chamber and cistern, and dedicated them for the use of the people of Simitthus.
EDCS-00381051.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a portico, chamber, and cistern by Pientius, a procurator, for the people of Simitthus, in fulfillment of a vow for the Emperor's well-being.

EDCS-00800004.translation: To Marcus Aelius Aurelius Verus Caesar, consul for the second time, son of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius Augustus; the pagus and civitas of Thugga, by decree of the decurions, at public expense. Also [dedicated to] the deified Vespasian.
EDCS-00800004.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the community of Thugga to Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar, son of Antoninus Pius, and also to the deified Vespasian, funded by public money.

EDCS-00800007.text_edition: \n Corneliae Saloninae Augustae coniugi Imperatoris domini nostri Gallieni Pii Felicis Augusti res publica coloniae Liciniae Septimiae Aureliae Alexandrianae Thuggensis devota numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-00800007.translation: To Cornelia Salonina Augusta, wife of the Emperor our lord Gallienus Pius Felix Augustus, the state of the colony Licinia Septimia Aurelia Alexandriana Thuggensis, devoted to her divine majesty.
EDCS-00800007.summary: This inscription records the devotion of the community of Thugga to Cornelia Salonina, wife of Emperor Gallienus.

EDCS-00800008.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-00800008.text_edition: \n Imperatori Tiberio Caesari divi Augusti filio Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXXVIII consuli V Lucius Manilius Luci filius Arnensi Bucco IIvir dedicavit Lucius Postumius Cai filius Arnensi Chius patronus pagi nomine suo et Firmi et Rufi filiorum forum et aream ante templum Caesaris stravit aram Augusti aedem Saturni arcum de sua pecunia faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-00800008.translation: Lucius Manilius, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe Bucco, duovir, dedicated this. Lucius Postumius, son of Caius, of the Arnensian tribe Chius, patron of the pagus, on his own behalf and that of his sons Firmius and Rufus, paved the forum and the area in front of the temple of Caesar, and saw to the construction of the altar of Augustus, the temple of Saturn, and the arch, at his own expense, to Emperor Tiberius Caesar Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 38th time, consul for the 5th time.
EDCS-00800008.summary: This inscription records extensive public works funded by Lucius Postumius and Lucius Manilius for Tiberius Caesar, including paving, an altar, a temple, and an arch.

EDCS-00800009.text_edition: \n Veneri Concordiae sacrum Licinia Marci liberta Prisca Licini Tyranni uxor flaminica perpetua templum de sua pecunia faciendum curavit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-00800009.translation: Sacred to Venus Concordia. Licinia Prisca, freedwoman of Marcus and wife of Licinius Tyrannus, perpetual priestess, undertook the construction of the temple at her own expense and dedicated it.
EDCS-00800009.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a temple to Venus Concordia by Licinia Prisca, a priestess and wife of Licinius Tyrannus.

EDCS-00800010.text_edition: \n Lucio Terentio Romano patri carissimo cui cum populus Thuggensis ob aquae curam pro meritis eius ex aere conlato tunc statuam ponendam obtulisset Caius Terentius Papiria Iulianus Sabinianus flamen perpetuus vir egregius de suo posuit loco a re publica dato\n
EDCS-00800010.translation: To Lucius Terentius Romanus, dearest father, for whom the people of Thugga, when they had offered a statue at the public expense in recognition of his merits in water management, Caius Terentius Julianus Sabinianus, perpetual priest, an outstanding man, erected at his own expense in a place granted by the state.
EDCS-00800010.summary: This inscription records a statue erected by Caius Terentius Julianus Sabinianus to his father, Lucius Terentius Romanus, for his distinguished service in managing water resources for the city of Thugga.

EDCS-00800014.text_edition: \n Iovi Augusto sacrum Lucius Terentius Adiutoris filius Rufinianus signum Iovis Victoris Conservatoris et candelabra cum lucernis ob honorem patronatus civitatis suae Thuggensis in se decreto ordinis conlato donum dedit et ob dedicationem decurionibus denarios ternos dedit\n
EDCS-00800014.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Augustus. Lucius Terentius Rufinianus, son of Adiutoris, gave as a gift a statue of Jupiter Victor Conservator and candelabra with lamps on the occasion of the honor of patronage of his city Thugga, bestowed upon him by decree of the council, and for the dedication he gave the decurions three denarii each.
EDCS-00800014.summary: Lucius Terentius Rufinianus dedicated a statue of Jupiter and other items to the god Jupiter Augustus to honor his patronage of Thugga, also distributing money to the decurions upon its dedication.

EDCS-00800015.text_edition: \n XVviro sacris faciundis legato pro praetore Imperatoris Nervae Traiani Caesaris Augusti Germanici Dacici provinciae Aquitanicae defensori immunitatis perticae Carthaginiensium Quintus Marius Quinti filius Arnensi Faustinus legavit ob eam causam ex decreto decurionum sua \n
EDCS-00800015.translation: To Quintus Marius Faustinus, son of Quintus, from Arnes, as a member of the college of fifteen priests for public rites, as governor of the province of Aquitania under Emperor Nerva Trajan Caesar Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, and as defender of the liberties of the Carthaginians, bequeathed from his own property, by decree of the decurions, for this reason.
EDCS-00800015.summary: This inscription records a bequest by Quintus Marius Faustinus, a high-ranking official and governor, to the Carthaginians, made according to a decree of their decurions.

EDCS-00800019.text_edition: \n Lucio Mummio Faustiano clarissimo viro consuli ordinario XVviro sacris faciundis quaestori kandidato curatori viae Appiae et alimentorum legato Hipponensium Tarrunteniae Paulinae clarissimae feminae coniugi eius Lucio Mummio Faustiano Tarruntenio Paterno clarissimo viro quaestori kandidato Mummiae Tarrunteniae Cornelianae clarissimae feminae Lucio Mummio Faustiano Iuniori clarissimo iuveni quaestori kandidato filiis eorum res publica coloniae Thuggae publice posuit\n
EDCS-00800019.translation: To Lucius Mummius Faustianus, most illustrious man, ordinary consul, member of the college of fifteen priests for public rites, quaestor-candidate, curator of the Appian Way, and to the Hipponian ambassador, and to Tarruntenia Paulina, most illustrious woman, his wife, and to Lucius Mummius Faustianus Paterculus, most illustrious man, quaestor-candidate, and to Mummia Tarruntenia Cornelia, most illustrious woman, and to Lucius Mummius Faustianus the Younger, most illustrious youth, quaestor-candidate, their sons, the state of the colony of Thugga publicly erected.
EDCS-00800019.summary: The state of the colony of Thugga dedicated this monument to Lucius Mummius Faustianus, his wife Tarruntenia Paulina, and their sons, all of whom held significant public offices, likely in recognition of their contributions.

EDCS-00800021.text_edition: \n Calpurnio Faustino Faustini filio flamini Augusti perpetuo civitas Thugga ob eximiam eius in cives suos liberalitatem qua siquando necessitas rei frumentariae postulavit frumentum populo quantumcumque habuit multo minore pretio quam tunc erat n\n
EDCS-00800021.translation: To Calpurnius Faustinus, son of Faustinus, perpetual priest of Augustus. The city of Thugga, on account of his exceptional generosity to its citizens, for whenever the necessity of grain arose, provided grain to the people at whatever price he had, much less than it was then.
EDCS-00800021.summary: The city of Thugga honored Calpurnius Faustinus, its perpetual priest, for his great generosity in providing grain to the populace at significantly reduced prices during times of need.

EDCS-00800024.text_edition: \n Aulo Gabinio Dato filio flamini divi Titi Augusti patrono pagi et civitatis pagus et civitas Thuggensis\n
EDCS-00800024.translation: To Aulus Gabinius Datus, son of Datus, priest of the deified Titus Augustus, patron of the village and city. The village and city of Thugga.
EDCS-00800024.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the village and city of Thugga to Aulus Gabinius Datus, recognizing him as their patron and priest of the deified Titus Augustus.

EDCS-00800025.text_edition: \n Caio Sedio Africano ob insignem munificentiam eius et amorem in patriam multis ac magnis documentis declaratum res publica municipii Septimi Aureli Liberi Thuggensis decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-00800025.translation: To Caius Sedius Africanus, for his outstanding munificence and love for his homeland, declared through many great proofs, the state of the municipality of Septimius Aurelius Liberi Thuggensis, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-00800025.summary: The municipality of Septimius Aurelius Liberi Thuggensis erected a monument at public expense to Caius Sedius Africanus in recognition of his great generosity and patriotism.

EDCS-00800026.text_edition: \n Caio Sedio Honorato Africano ob egregiam indolem et summum obsequium in cives patriamque respondens exemplo familiae ac maiorum suorum res publica municipii Septimi Aureli Liberi Thuggensis decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-00800026.translation: To Caius Sedius Honoratus Africanus, for his excellent character and utmost obedience to citizens and homeland, responding to the example of his family and ancestors, the state of the municipality of Septimius Aurelius Liberi Thuggensis, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-00800026.summary: The municipality of Septimius Aurelius Liberi Thuggensis erected a monument at public expense to Caius Sedius Honoratus Africanus for his good character and devotion to his community and country, following his family's example.

EDCS-00800032.text_edition: \n Telite Marci Licini Rufi serva pia vixit annos XXVII\n
EDCS-00800032.translation: To Telis, servant of Marcus Licinius Rufus, pious. Lived 27 years.
EDCS-00800032.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Telis, a pious servant of Marcus Licinius Rufus, recording her name, owner, and age at death.

EDCS-02000005.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Hadriani Augusti Silvano deo sacrum Aebutii Saturninus et Victor Titi Aebuti filii aram sua pecunia fecerunt idemque dedicarunt\n
EDCS-02000005.translation: For the health of Emperor Hadrian Augustus. Aebutius Saturninus and Victor, sons of Titus Aebutius, made this altar to the god Silvanus with their own money and dedicated it.
EDCS-02000005.summary: This inscription records an altar dedicated to the god Silvanus by Aebutius Saturninus and Victor for the health of Emperor Hadrian, made and dedicated at their own expense.

EDCS-02000008.text_edition: \n Prote Marci Licini Rufi pia vixit nnos XXVII\n
EDCS-02000008.translation: Prote, daughter of Marcus Licinius Rufus, lived 27 years.
EDCS-02000008.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Prote, daughter of Marcus Licinius Rufus.

EDCS-03000838.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name and parentage of Flavia Amothmic.

EDCS-03000839.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name of Gaius Flavius Proculus.

EDCS-03000841.text_edition: \n Cai Flavi Procuil sic Bydbalis filii\n
EDCS-03000841.translation: Of Gaius Flavius Proculus, son of Bydbalis.
EDCS-03000841.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the name of Gaius Flavius Proculus and his father Bydbalis.

EDCS-03000844.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name of Marcus Flavius Justus.

EDCS-03000845.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name of Flaminia Gaetula.

EDCS-03000846.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Filipus sic Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicie sic potestate pater patriae et Marcus Iulius Philippus nobilissimus Caesar filius Augusti nostri milia passuum numero LVI\n
EDCS-03000846.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, and to Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, our Augustus' son, [for] 56 miles.
EDCS-03000846.summary: This inscription records an imperial dedication by Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, likely related to a public work covering 56 miles, possibly from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-03000847.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Claudio Victori Augusto tribunio sic et potestate patriaet sic patrriae sic milia LVII\n
EDCS-03000847.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Victor Augustus, holder of tribunician power and father of the country, [for] 57 miles.
EDCS-03000847.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Claudius Gothicus, possibly related to a public work covering 57 miles, from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-03000851.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Messius Luci filius Galeria Fructus Myrtili miles cohortis I urbanae stipendiorum XV vixit annis XXXIIII hic situs est sit tibi terra levis Gampudius Tullenianus frater et heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-03000851.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Messius, son of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, a soldier of the I Urban Cohort, with 15 years of service, lived 34 years. He is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you. Gampudius Tullenianus, brother and heir, had this made.
EDCS-03000851.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Messius, a soldier, erected by his brother and heir, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-03000857.text_edition: \n AVGGI mentius curator rei publicae V CIICIII dedicavit\n
EDCS-03000857.translation: Aeggius Mentius, curator of the republic, dedicated [this] in the year 103 [of the provincial era].
EDCS-03000857.summary: This inscription records the dedication by Aeggius Mentius, a local official, in the year 103 of an unspecified provincial era, likely of Roman provincial origin.

EDCS-03000858.text_edition: \n orum ma potestate VI consuli III patri patriae proconsuli municipium Thagoritanum Maius devotum numini maiestatique eius decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-03000858.translation: To him, who in his power as consul for the third time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, the municipium Thagorensium Maius is devoted in spirit and majesty by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-03000858.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the town of Thagora to an unnamed emperor, highlighting his imperial powers and titles, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-03000859.text_edition: \n Frugifero Augusto sacrum pro salute Imperatorum Marci Aureli Antonini et Luci Aeli Aureli Veri Augustorumrviocc moran\n
EDCS-03000859.translation: Sacred to Frugifer Augustus, for the safety of our Lords Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augusti.
EDCS-03000859.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus, likely for their well-being, and dates to their joint reign (161-169 CE).

EDCS-03000863.text_edition: \n IVSA ius extricatus Karthagine I Kalendas Maias Aproniano II et Paulo II consulibus\n
EDCS-03000863.translation: Justly freed from all legal bonds at Carthage, on the Kalends of May, in the consulship of Apronianus (for the second time) and Paulus (for the second time).
EDCS-03000863.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a legal act of freedom or release on a specific date in Rome, during the consulship of Apronianus and Paulus.

EDCS-03000877.text_edition: \n Dexia Celido pia vixit annis XX ossa tua bene quiescant terra tibi levis sit\n
EDCS-03000877.translation: Dexia Celido, pious, lived 20 years. May your bones rest well. May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-03000877.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Dexia Celido, a young woman who lived 20 years, expressing wishes for her peaceful afterlife.

EDCS-03300884.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto sacerdoti amplissimo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate V consuli IIII patri patriae et Marco Aurelio Alexandro nobilissimo Caesari domini nostri Antonini Augusti filio Augusto Flavius Sossianus vir egregius vice praesidis Numidiae per vexillationem legionis III Augustae piae vindicis Antoninianae devotissimi numini eorum Portam vetustate conlabsam sic lapidi quadrato arco curvato restituit omnes praeteriti cuius labore vitabant rigido vigore iuvenum tertia Augustani fecerunt creto consilio hortante Parato magistro iuncta virtus militum paucorum velocitas ingens usui compendio lapides de longe adtractos chamulco sub arcata militum virtus funibus cannabinis strictis iam nunc contendunt fieri cito milites omnes arta virtute sua opera aeternale fecerunt subsequentes stipendiis antecessorum onestia bona sumebant urguente tempore hiemis necumquam cessaverunt celerius excelsae turres quater divisae cum voce militum a terra venerunt torrens virtus legionis III Augustae piae vindicis haec ut fierent milites omnes sibi zelum tradebant animadvertentes quod priores sibi vestigia fecissent nunc et ipsi titulis suis virtutis devotionis ornaverunt contendentes si amplius esset athuc opera fecissent ex numero militum quidam amplius voluntatem suam dederit necumquam operam suam erupi exhibuerunt tantus fuit eis zelus magna vir laetis temporibus honoribus effecta opera gaudet aeterna militum virtus gemma ut auro cluditur sic castram porta decorat Florida Tertia Augusta legio cum magna virtute curavit faciendum devotionis suae honorem muneri quoque dictatores strenue cesserunt animosos duo contemnentes residuum turpis oppresserat capita versorum relegens adgnosce curantem Porcius Iasucthan centurio legionis fecit curante macistro sic\n
EDCS-03300884.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, and to Marcus Aurelius Alexander, most noble Caesar, son of our lord Antoninus Augustus, Flavius Sossianus, an outstanding man and vice-governor of Numidia, through a detachment of the Third Augustan Legion, pious and victorious, most devoted to their divine power, restored the gate, which had collapsed from age, with squared stone and a curved arch; all avoided the labor of the past; with rigorous strength of youths, the Third Augustan legion made it with Cretan stone, with a wise council urging, with the combined strength of soldiers, great speed; the soldiers brought the stones from afar with a crane, with strong ropes of hemp. Now they strive to finish it quickly. All the soldiers made it with their strength in eternal work, following the honors of their predecessors, taking good rewards, and did not cease during the urging of the winter time, faster than the high towers, divided four times, they came from the earth with the voice of the soldiers. The torrent of the strength of the Third Augustan Legion, pious and victorious, the soldiers gave zeal to each other to make these things, observing that the former had made their footsteps; now they too have adorned themselves with their titles of strength and devotion, striving to have done more if there were more. Some of the soldiers gave their will even more, and never shirked their work. Such was their zeal. The work, made in happy times with great honor, rejoices in eternal military strength. As a gem is enclosed with gold, so the gate adorns the camp. The Third Augustan Legion, with great strength, took care to have this work done as a tribute of their devotion. The dictators also strenuously yielded, despising the two, the remaining base had crushed them. Reading again the heads of the verses, recognize the one who cares. Porcius Iasucthan, centurion of the legion, made it, under the care of the master.
EDCS-03300884.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a gate by Flavius Sossianus, vice-governor of Numidia, and soldiers of the Third Augustan Legion, dedicated to Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Marcus Aurelius Alexander, likely dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-03300886.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto devota Segermes numini maiestatique eius decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-03300886.translation: To Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantine, pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, Segermes is devoted in spirit and majesty by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-03300886.summary: This inscription records a civic dedication by Segermes to Emperor Constantine the Great, emphasizing his titles and divine favor, likely dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-03300889.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII consul III pontem fecit per legionem III Augustam Sexto Iulio Maiore legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-03300889.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, built the bridge through the Third Augustan Legion, under Sextus Julius Maior, legate of the emperor and propraetor.
EDCS-03300889.summary: This inscription records the construction of a bridge by Emperor Hadrian, built by the Third Augustan Legion under the legate Sextus Julius Maior, dating to Hadrian's reign (117-138 CE).

EDCS-03300890.text_edition: \n Calpurniae Ceiae Aemilianae clarissimae feminae coniugi Quinti Aradi Rufini clarissimi viri civitas Thibicaaensis decreto decurionum patronae\n
EDCS-03300890.translation: To Calpurnia Caeia Aemiliana, most distinguished lady, wife of Quintus Aradius Rufinus, most distinguished man, patroness of the city of Thibicaa by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-03300890.summary: This is a dedication to Calpurnia Caeia Aemiliana, honoring her as patroness of the city of Thibicaa.

EDCS-03300902.text_edition: \n Lucius Litussius Verianius veteranus vixit annos L semissem III dies Sextia Bona coniugi suo piissimo de suo fecit\n
EDCS-03300902.translation: Lucius Litussius Verianius, a veteran, lived 50 years and 3.5 years, 3 days. Sextia Bona made this for her most devoted husband at her own expense.
EDCS-03300902.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Sextia Bona for her veteran husband, Lucius Litussius Verianius, detailing his age and her devotion.

EDCS-03300903.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caecilius Felix vixit annos LXXV menses III dies V horas III semissem Zabullus gener eius adiutor a c commentariis\n
EDCS-03300903.translation: Sacred to the Departed Shades. Caecilius Felix lived 75 years, 3 months, 5 days, 3.5 hours. Zabullus, his son-in-law, assistant from the commentarii, [set this up].
EDCS-03300903.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caecilius Felix, detailing his age and listing his son-in-law, Zabullus, as the dedicator, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300904.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Aufidius Muntanus vixit annos XII hic situs est Viator Auggustorum nnostrorum servus pater infelicissimus filio dulcissimo fecit\n
EDCS-03300904.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Aufidius Muntanus lived for 12 years; he is buried here. Viator, a freedman of our Emperors, his most unhappy father, made this for his dearest son.
EDCS-03300904.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aufidius Muntanus, who died at age 12, dedicated by his father Viator.

EDCS-03300905.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Annius Ingenus vixit anno mensibus X Lucius Annnius Melicerta piissimo fecit hic situs\n
EDCS-03300905.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Annius Ingenus lived for 1 year and 10 months. Lucius Annnius Melicerta made this for the most devoted [Marcus Annius Ingenus]; he is buried here.
EDCS-03300905.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Annius Ingenus, who died at age 1 year and 10 months, dedicated by Lucius Annnius Melicerta.

EDCS-03300906.text_edition: \n Antistia Stercila Luci Antisti filia vixit annis XIII hic sita\n
EDCS-03300906.translation: Antistia Stercila, daughter of Lucius Antistius, lived for 13 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-03300906.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antistia Stercila, who died at age 13, recording her father's name.

EDCS-03300907.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aurelia Catta vixit annos XXII hic sita est Aurelius Speratus uxori piae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-03300907.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Catta lived for 22 years; she is buried here. Aurelius Speratus arranged for this to be made for his devoted wife.
EDCS-03300907.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Catta, who died at age 22, commissioned by her husband Aurelius Speratus.

EDCS-03300908.text_edition: \n Clodia Clodi filia Salsa pia vixit annis XXV hic sita est\n
EDCS-03300908.translation: Clodia Salsa, daughter of Clodius, devoted, lived for 25 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-03300908.summary: A funerary epitaph for Clodia Salsa, who died at age 25, mentioning her father.

EDCS-03300911.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely a funerary epitaph, recording the name Crippia Quarta.

EDCS-03300916.text_edition: \n Festus Luci Ortesi filius vixit nnos IX\n
EDCS-03300916.translation: Festus, son of Lucius Ortesius, lived for 9 years.
EDCS-03300916.summary: A funerary epitaph for Festus, son of Lucius Ortesius, who died at age 9.

EDCS-03300917.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Flavia Urbana pia vixit annos III menses V dies VII hic sita est Lucius Flavius Donatus filiae piissimae memoriam fecit\n
EDCS-03300917.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Urbana, devoted, lived for 3 years, 5 months, and 7 days; she is buried here. Lucius Flavius Donatus made this memorial for his most devoted daughter.
EDCS-03300917.summary: A funerary epitaph for Flavia Urbana, who died at age 3 years, 5 months, and 7 days, dedicated by her father Lucius Flavius Donatus.

EDCS-03300918.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Iulio Luci filio Papiria Vno Extricato iuveni egregio sancto bono V\n
EDCS-03300918.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Iulius Extricatus, son of Lucius Iulius, of the Papiria tribe, an outstanding, holy, good young man. 5 [years old].
EDCS-03300918.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Iulius Extricatus, a young man described with positive qualities, who died at age 5.

EDCS-03300919.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulia Donatilla vixit annos XII hic sita est \n
EDCS-03300919.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Donatilla lived for 12 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-03300919.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Donatilla, who died at age 12.

EDCS-03300920.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Larius Maximus Saturninus vixit annis XXXC Larius Saturninus fratri carissimo fecit hic situs est\n
EDCS-03300920.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Larius Maximus Saturninus lived for 30 years. Larius Saturninus made this for his dearest brother. He is buried here.
EDCS-03300920.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial place of Larius Maximus Saturninus, with the monument erected by his brother.

EDCS-03300921.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Leoncius vixit annos LXXXI\n
EDCS-03300921.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Leoncius lived for 81 years.
EDCS-03300921.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Leoncius.

EDCS-03300922.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Longeius Vindex vixit annos XXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-03300922.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Longeius Vindex lived for 35 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-03300922.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial place of Marcus Longeius Vindex.

EDCS-03300923.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Me Catta vixit annos XXXII\n
EDCS-03300923.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Me Catta lived for 32 years.
EDCS-03300923.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Me Catta.

EDCS-03300924.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucio Pinario Luci filio Papiria Rufino Theveste vixit annos XXXV fratres piissimi fecerunt hic situs est\n
EDCS-03300924.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucio Pinarius, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, Rufinus Theveste lived for 35 years. His most devoted brothers made this. He is buried here.
EDCS-03300924.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucio Pinarius Rufinus Theveste, who lived 35 years, erected by his devoted brothers.

EDCS-03300925.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Pompeius Martialis vixit annos XXX Pompeius Telesinus fratri\n
EDCS-03300925.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Pompeius Martialis lived for 30 years. Pompeius Telesinus made this for his brother.
EDCS-03300925.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Quintus Pompeius Martialis, with the monument erected by his brother, Pompeius Telesinus.

EDCS-03300926.text_edition: \n D(is) M(anibus) s(acrum) Pompeia vixit annos LX meritae matri filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-03300926.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. [Pompeia] lived for 60 years. Her sons made this for their deserving mother.
EDCS-03300926.summary: A funerary epitaph for Pompeia, who lived 60 years, erected by her sons.

EDCS-03300927.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Postumius Saturninus vixit annos LIIII hic situs\n
EDCS-03300927.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Postumius Saturninus lived for 54 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-03300927.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial place of Postumius Saturninus.

EDCS-03300928.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Titinia Sapiens vixit annos XXXV dies XV Pompeius Telesinus maritus fecit\n
EDCS-03300928.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Titinia Sapiens lived for 35 years and 15 days. Her husband, Pompeius Telesinus, made this.
EDCS-03300928.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial of Titinia Sapiens, erected by her husband, Pompeius Telesinus.

EDCS-03300929.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Turranius Felix filius aram posuit exitum de suo fecit Turranio patri piissimo vixit annis LXXXI\n
EDCS-03300929.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Turranius Felix, the son, set up this altar and provided for his exit (burial). Lived for 81 years for his most devoted father Turranius.
EDCS-03300929.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Turranius Felix, who lived 81 years, and also mentions his father Turranius, with Felix setting up an altar and arranging his own burial.

EDCS-03300930.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Verria Primitiva pia vixit annos LXXXX Annia Ianuaria filia matri piae fecit hic sita est\n
EDCS-03300930.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Verria Primitiva lived piously for 90 years. Annia Ianuaria, her daughter, made this for her pious mother. She is buried here.
EDCS-03300930.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Verria Primitiva, who lived 90 years, and was erected by her daughter Annia Ianuaria.

EDCS-03300931.text_edition: \n ------] Vettia Cai filia Severina vixit annos II- hic sita est\n
EDCS-03300931.translation: Vettia Severina, daughter of Caius, lived for 2 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-03300931.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Vettia Severina, who lived for 2 years.

EDCS-03300932.text_edition: \n Umb<i=L>lia Cai filia Victoria pia vixit annis XXIII hic sita est Firmus vir fecit \n
EDCS-03300932.translation: Umbilia Victoria, daughter of Caius, lived piously for 23 years. Her husband Firmus made this. She is buried here.
EDCS-03300932.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Umbilia Victoria, who lived for 23 years, and was erected by her husband Firmus.

EDCS-03300933.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Volumnius Quinti filius Papiria Sollemnis vixit annos LXV hic situs est Quintus Volumnius Amabilis heres patrono piissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-03300933.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Volumnius Sollemnis, son of Quintus, of the Papirian tribe, lived for 65 years. He is buried here. Quintus Volumnius Amabilis, his heir, arranged for this monument to be made for his most devoted patron.
EDCS-03300933.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Quintus Volumnius Sollemnis, who lived 65 years, and was erected by his heir Quintus Volumnius Amabilis.

EDCS-03300935.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Baebiae Primulae vixit Cael[ius ------\n
EDCS-03300935.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Baebia Primula lived for... Cael[ius ...
EDCS-03300935.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription records the death of Baebia Primula and likely mentions Cael[ius] as a dedicant or relative.

EDCS-03300936.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a person who lived for 30 years.

EDCS-03300937.text_edition: \n Euticius annis LVIIII menses VII dies XXII\n
EDCS-03300937.translation: Euticius lived for 59 years, 7 months, and 22 days.
EDCS-03300937.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Euticius as 59 years, 7 months, and 22 days.

EDCS-03300938.text_edition: \n tula vixit annis LX filii fecerunt hic sita est\n
EDCS-03300938.translation: ...lived for 60 years. Her sons made this. She is buried here.
EDCS-03300938.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a woman who lived for 60 years and was erected by her sons.

EDCS-03300939.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aurelius Iovinus miles legioni(s) porimae Minerviae militavit aquilifer vixit annos XXX ficit sic Verila marito rarissimo titulum\n
EDCS-03300939.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Iovinus, a soldier of the First Minervia legion, served as an aquilifer, and lived for 30 years. Verila made this monument for her most rare husband.
EDCS-03300939.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aurelius Iovinus, a soldier and aquilifer, who lived 30 years, and was erected by his wife Verila.

EDCS-03300940.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Castricius Crassipes vixit annos VC hic situs est Lucius Castricius Maior patrono fecit\n
EDCS-03300940.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Castricius Crassipes lived for 95 years. He is buried here. Lucius Castricius Maior made this for his patron.
EDCS-03300940.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Lucius Castricius Crassipes, who lived 95 years, and was erected by his patron Lucius Castricius Maior.

EDCS-03300941.text_edition: \n D(is) M(anibus) Iuli Luci filii Natalis vixit annos LVIII menses III hic situs est Helvia Procula uxor marito karissimo fecit Iulii patri fecerunt\n
EDCS-03300941.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Lucius Natalis, son of Julius, lived 58 years and 3 months; he is buried here. Helvia Procula, his wife, made this for her dearest husband. His fathers, the Julii, made it.
EDCS-03300941.summary: A funerary monument for Lucius Natalis, dedicated by his wife Helvia Procula and possibly his fathers, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300942.text_edition: \n Iuliae Lycoridi Quintus Tedius Firmus uxori heres fecit\n
EDCS-03300942.translation: Quintus Tedius Firmus made this for his wife, Iulia Lycoris, his heir.
EDCS-03300942.summary: A funerary monument for Iulia Lycoris, erected by her husband and heir, Quintus Tedius Firmus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300943.text_edition: \n Marcus Servilius Severus pius vixit annos V menses II dies XX hic est situs\n
EDCS-03300943.translation: Marcus Servilius Severus, dutiful, lived 5 years, 2 months, and 20 days; he is buried here.
EDCS-03300943.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Servilius Severus, recording his age and burial, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300944.text_edition: \n Titus Cassius Maior miles legionis III Augustae centuria Valeri Terti vixit annos XXIV militavit nnos V hic situs est Caius Mevius optio bene merenti\n
EDCS-03300944.translation: Titus Cassius Maior, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, of Valerius Tertius' century, lived 24 years and served 5 years; he is buried here. Caius Mevius made this for the deserving optio.
EDCS-03300944.summary: A funerary inscription for Titus Cassius Maior, a soldier, erected by Caius Mevius, likely a fellow soldier or superior, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300950.text_edition: \n Lucius Aurelius Urbanus vixit nnos XXV militavit nnos VIIII hic situs est Maria Commoda uxor faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-03300950.translation: Lucius Aurelius Urbanus lived 25 years and served 9 years; he is buried here. Maria Commoda, his wife, saw to its construction.
EDCS-03300950.summary: A funerary monument for Lucius Aurelius Urbanus, a soldier, erected by his wife Maria Commoda, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300953.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Publius Munatius Gallus miles legionis III Augustae probatus annos XVI et menses III militavit annos X vixit annos XXVI menses IIII Munatius Victor pater filio piissimo fecit\n
EDCS-03300953.translation: Sacred to the gods of the underworld. Publius Munatius Gallus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, approved (for service?), served 10 years, lived 26 years and 4 months. His father, Munatius Victor, made this for his most dutiful son.
EDCS-03300953.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Munatius Gallus, a soldier, detailing his service and age, dedicated by his father Munatius Victor, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300954.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Publius Munatius Victor vixit annis LXXXV Flavia Avonia uxor piissima fecit\n
EDCS-03300954.translation: Sacred to the gods of the underworld. Publius Munatius Victor lived 85 years. Flavia Avonia, his most dutiful wife, made this.
EDCS-03300954.summary: A tombstone for Publius Munatius Victor, erected by his wife Flavia Avonia, recording his great age, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300955.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Castus Vaga vixit annos XXX menses VII dies XII hic situs est Cammarenses amico optimo fecerunt\n
EDCS-03300955.translation: Sacred to the gods of the underworld. Castus Vaga lived 30 years, 7 months, and 12 days; he is buried here. The people of Cameria made this for their excellent friend.
EDCS-03300955.summary: A funerary monument for Castus Vaga, erected by the citizens of Cameria, recording his life and burial, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300956.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulia Victoria vixit annos XXIII menses V dies VI hic sita est Iulius Hilarinus coniugi karissimae posuit\n
EDCS-03300956.translation: Sacred to the gods of the underworld. Iulia Victoria lived 23 years, 5 months, and 6 days; she is buried here. Iulius Hilarinus placed this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-03300956.summary: A funerary inscription for Iulia Victoria, erected by her husband Iulius Hilarinus, recording her age and burial, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300957.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Titus Flavius Speratus vixit annos XXXV Furia Pompeia marito piissimo fecit hic situs est\n
EDCS-03300957.translation: Sacred to the gods of the underworld. Titus Flavius Speratus lived 35 years. Furia Pompeia made this for her most dutiful husband; he is buried here.
EDCS-03300957.summary: A tombstone for Titus Flavius Speratus, erected by his wife Furia Pompeia, recording his life and burial, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300958.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Annius Marci libertus Verecundus pius vixit annos XXX Marcus Annius Martialis |(centurio) legionis III Augustae liberto optimo\n
EDCS-03300958.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Annius Verecundus, freedman of Marcus, devoted and pious, lived 30 years. Marcus Annius Martialis, centurion of the Legio III Augusta, [erected this] to his most excellent freedman.
EDCS-03300958.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Annius Verecundus, erected by his patron, Marcus Annius Martialis, a centurion, likely from the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-03300959.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Annius Venerius vixit annos XLV menses IIII dies X hic situs est Annius Floridus fratri karissimo et amantissimo fecit\n
EDCS-03300959.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Annius Venerius lived 45 years, 4 months, and 10 days; he is buried here. Annius Floridus made this for his dearest and most beloved brother.
EDCS-03300959.summary: A funerary epitaph for Annius Venerius, erected by his brother Annius Floridus, likely from the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-03300960.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulius Hilarus se vivo sibi et Iuliae Monnicae uxori karissimae posuit Cirilio vixit annis LV mensibus VII diebus VIII\n
EDCS-03300960.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Iulius Hilarus placed this for himself during his lifetime and for his most beloved wife Iulia Monnica. Cirilio lived 55 years, 7 months, and 8 days.
EDCS-03300960.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulius Hilarus and his wife Iulia Monnica, erected by Iulius Hilarus himself and recording the lifespan of Cirilio, likely from the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-03700799.text_edition: \n Feliciter Faustus votum libens animo solvit\n
EDCS-03700799.translation: Successfully, Faustus gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-03700799.summary: A short votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Faustus, likely from the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-04000360.translation: To the eternal and most noble Caesar, Gaius Valerius Constantius the Invincible, the state of the colony of Thurbursicum Numidarum.
EDCS-04000360.summary: A dedication to Emperor Constantius I by the city council of Thurbursicum in Numidia, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-04000431.text_edition: \n Heracli Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Vetidius Maternus Vetidianus eques Romanus Quinti Vetidi Iuvenalis quinquennalici filius utraque lingua eruditus pius vixit annos XVIII menses IIII dies XXVIII permissu praesidis a Karthagine de studio relatis reliquiis hic situs est\n
EDCS-04000431.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Vetidius Maternus Vetidianus, a Roman knight and son of Quintus Vetidius Iuvenalis, quinquennalis, educated in both languages, devoted and pious, lived 18 years, 4 months, and 28 days. With permission of the governor from Carthage, from the study with the brought-back relics, he is buried here.
EDCS-04000431.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Vetidius Maternus Vetidianus, a Roman knight, detailing his age and education, and noting his burial with relics and permission from a governor, likely from Roman North Africa.

EDCS-04000829.translation: Under Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus [and] [M. XXX?] Augustus. Sacred. [Lucius Gra]nius Honoratus, son of [Lucius], [from the tribe] Quirina, [XXX]anus, twice duumvir, [XXX]em [XXX]MVT had it made.
EDCS-04000829.summary: A sacred dedication under Emperors Nerva and a second Augustus, recording the name of Lucius Granius Honoratus who served as a duumvir, likely from the late 1st century CE.

EDCS-04000907.text_edition: \n Marco Cornelio Frontoni Quirina Gabiniano equiti Romano ex inquisitione allecto flamini perpetuo IIvirali honestae memoriae viro Marci Corneli Victorini flaminis perpetui bis IIviralis filio splendissimus ordo et populus coloniae Madaurensium ob insignem in se amorem et frumenti copiam tempore inopiae sibi largiter praestitam honorem bigae et statuae decreverunt pecunia publica quam Corneliae Romanilla Postumiana et Victorina Claudiana et Eulogia Romanilla filiae et heredes eius sua pecunia posuerunt sportulis decurionibus et curialibus datis\n
EDCS-04000907.translation: To Marcus Cornelius Frontonus Quirinus Gabinianus, Roman knight, promoted by inquiry, perpetual flamen, twice duumvir, a man of honorable memory, son of Marcus Cornelius Victorinus, perpetual flamen and twice duumvir. The most distinguished order and people of the colony of Madaura decreed the honor of a chariot and a statue to him, because of his exceptional love for them and for generously providing grain during a time of scarcity. His daughters Cornelia Romanilla Postumiana and Victorina Claudiana, and Eulogia Romanilla, and their heirs, erected it with public funds, giving handouts to the decurions and curial councillors.
EDCS-04000907.summary: A dedication by the city of Madaura to Marcus Cornelius Frontonus, a distinguished local official, honoring him with a chariot and statue for his public service and generosity, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001431.translation: If you ask about the circumstances of [his] life, Flavius Victorianus Na[XXX] who, innocent, in his youth, while dutifully following his studies, [XXX] with a rivalous love, alas, piety did not grant [XXX] who, with religious affection [XXX] to place forever [XXX] the earth will cover his body S[XXX] now resting in peace, [his] parents.
EDCS-04001431.summary: A fragmentary epitaph lamenting the early death of Flavius Victorianus, suggesting a life of promise cut short by fate, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001516.text_edition: \n Dibus sic Mauris Iulius Donatianus sacerdos deae Caelestis Augustae ex viso suo cum suis omnibus votum solvit\n
EDCS-04001516.translation: To the gods, thus to the Moors. Iulius Donatianus, priest of the August goddess Caelestis, fulfilled his vow according to his vision, with all his household.
EDCS-04001516.summary: A votive inscription by Iulius Donatianus, a priest of the goddess Caelestis, fulfilling a vow after a vision, likely from Roman North Africa.

EDCS-04001574.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Babullius Felix, a veteran, lived 80 years and is buried here. Soetonia Procula, his wife, and Babullius Thevestinus, his son, made this for their dutiful father.
EDCS-04001574.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Babullius Felix, erected by his wife and son, dating likely to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001576.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Flavius Flavinus, optio principalis, the name SAT[...]
EDCS-04001576.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription for Flavius Flavinus, an optio principalis, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001577.text_edition: \n Titus Sergius Satur miles legionis III Augustae centuria Valeri Marcelli militavit annis XIX vixit annis XXXX hic situs est Lucius Flavius Ferox milex sic legionis III Augustae centuria Augustalis exs sic testamento arbitratu Fulviae Saturninae factum est\n
EDCS-04001577.translation: Titus Sergius Satur served as a soldier in the III Augusta legion, in the centuria of Valerius Marcellus, for 19 years, and lived 40 years. He is buried here. Lucius Flavius Ferox, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, in the centuria Augustalis, was buried here by will and at the arbitration of Fulvia Saturnina.
EDCS-04001577.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Sergius Satur, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, and Lucius Flavius Ferox, also of the same legion, indicating military service and burial arrangements.

EDCS-04001584.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Aelius Marcianus the Younger lived 2 years, 5 months, and 7 days and is buried here. Victorinus made this for his son.
EDCS-04001584.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius Marcianus the Younger, a very young child, erected by his father Victorinus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001587.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Baebius Balbus lived 3 years and is buried here. Quintus Baebius Bassinus, his father, saw to it that this was made.
EDCS-04001587.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Baebius Balbus, a young child, erected by his father Quintus Baebius Bassinus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001588.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Bo[---] Ser[---] lived 5 years, 4 months, and 10 days. Sextus B[---]us Felix made this for his most dutiful son. He is buried here.
EDCS-04001588.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unidentified child, erected by his father Sextus [---]us Felix, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001589.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Caecilius Felicianus lived 2 years. Felicior made this for his dutiful son.
EDCS-04001589.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caecilius Felicianus, a young child, erected by Felicior, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001590.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Caecilia Saturnina lived 30 years. Felicior made this for his dutiful wife.
EDCS-04001590.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caecilia Saturnina, erected by her husband Felicior, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001596.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Titus Decimius Speratus lived 55 years [...]. SORO[---] [---]RAI[---]. He is buried here.
EDCS-04001596.summary: An incomplete funerary epitaph for Titus Decimius Speratus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001598.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For his daughter Euodia, who lived 5 years and 4 months.
EDCS-04001598.summary: A funerary epitaph for Euodia, erected by her father, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04001600.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Flavia Ingenua lived 45 years. Insteius Monitus, her husband, erected this tomb for his most sacred and most dutiful wife.
EDCS-04001600.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Ingenua, erected by her husband, Insteius Monitus.

EDCS-04001601.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Folius Urbanus lived [unknown age]. Martialis erected this for his dutiful son.
EDCS-04001601.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Martialis's son, Publius Folius Urbanus.

EDCS-04001603.translation: [Name missing] lived 51 years. Curtia(?) erected this for her most dear husband.
EDCS-04001603.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a husband, erected by his wife Curtia(?).

EDCS-04001608.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Caius Memmius Victor lived 50 years. His sons erected this.
EDCS-04001608.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Memmius Victor, erected by his sons.

EDCS-04001609.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Milesia Silvana lived 45 years. Her husband erected this for his dutiful wife.
EDCS-04001609.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Milesia Silvana, erected by her husband.

EDCS-04001611.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Petronius Ianuarius lived 40 years. His wife, Flavina, erected this for him who deserved well. He is buried here.
EDCS-04001611.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Petronius Ianuarius, erected by his wife Flavina.

EDCS-04001615.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Valeria Celsa, dutiful daughter of Caius, lived 24 years, 4 months, and 27 days. She is buried here.
EDCS-04001615.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valeria Celsa, likely erected by her family.

EDCS-04001633.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Cornelius Donatus [ inscription incomplete ]
EDCS-04001633.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelius Donatus, with the inscription incomplete.

EDCS-04001634.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulius Saturninus lived 31 years.
EDCS-04001634.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulius Saturninus, recording his age at death.

EDCS-04001639.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Valerius, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, [ inscription incomplete ]
EDCS-04001639.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius, son of Quintus, with the inscription incomplete.

EDCS-04001643.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aufidius Messor lived 63 years; he is buried here. Aufidius Messor, his son, made this for his most beloved father.
EDCS-04001643.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aufidius Messor, erected by his son of the same name, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-04001644.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Amabilis lived 35 years. Her devoted sisters made this for her.
EDCS-04001644.summary: A funerary epitaph for Amabilis, erected by her sisters, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-04001646.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. The sons made this for their most beloved mother, Sabinia(?) Felicula, who lived about 76 years, and for their father, Marcus Julius Cupitus, dutiful, who lived 70 years.
EDCS-04001646.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sabinia(?) Felicula and Marcus Julius Cupitus, erected by their sons, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-04001647.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Laelia(?) Tertulla lived sixty years.
EDCS-04001647.summary: A funerary epitaph for Laelia(?) Tertulla, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-04001758.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Constantinus Pius Felix Augustus, our lord Magnvs Decentivs, most noble Caesar, twice. To our lords Flavius Valentinianus and Valens Augusti, twice. To our lord Flavius Gratianus, ever Augustus.
EDCS-04001758.summary: A dedication to multiple Roman emperors and a Caesar, likely from the 4th century CE, mentioning Constantinus (Constantine I or II), Magnvs Decentivs, Valentinianus, Valens, and Gratianus.

EDCS-04901012.text_edition: \n Marcus Aedius Marci filius Teretina Allifis vixit annis LVI militavit annis XXVII centuria Cosconi ossa de Risucade vecta hic situs est\n
EDCS-04901012.translation: Marcus Aedius, son of Marcus, of the Teretina tribe, from Allifae, lived 56 years, served in the army 27 years in the century of Cosconius. His bones were brought from Risucade. He is buried here.
EDCS-04901012.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aedius, a soldier from Allifae, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-04901013.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto sacrum Publius Caelius Victor sacerdos et Furnia Donatula uxor votum solverunt libentes animo faciendos curaverunt gradus numero IIII\n
EDCS-04901013.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus. Publius Caelius Victor, priest, and Furnia Donatula, his wife, fulfilled their vow, and gladly took care to have four steps made.
EDCS-04901013.summary: A dedication to Mars Augustus by a priest and his wife, likely from the Imperial period, recording the construction of four steps.

EDCS-04901014.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto sacrum pro salute Impperatorum Caessarum Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Getae Caesaris et Iuliae Augustae matris castrorum Caius Rusticelius Proculus sacerdos pro salute mea et coniugis meae Faoniae Doniatulae et filiorum meorum votum solvi libens animo\n
EDCS-04901014.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Geta Caesar and Julia Augusta, mother of the camps. Caius Rusticelius Proculus, priest, paid his vow gladly for the safety of myself and my wife Faonia Doniatula and my sons.
EDCS-04901014.summary: A dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus, his son Geta, and Julia Domna, by a priest for himself, his family, and the emperors, dating to the Severan period (early 3rd century CE).

EDCS-04901015.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Septimi Severi Pertinacis Augusti eiusque domus divinae Augustae Iani custodibus domino Saturno Augusto sacrum Asidonius Pilatus Saracinus sacerdos pro salute mea et coniugis meae Cassiae Vinniolae et filiorum meorum in anniversali meo votum solvi libens animo\n
EDCS-04901015.translation: Sacred to Saturn, guardian of Janus, for the safety of the Emperor Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus and his divine household Augustan. Asidonius Pilatus Saracinus, priest, paid his vow gladly for the safety of myself and my wife Cassia Vinniola and my sons on my anniversary.
EDCS-04901015.summary: A dedication to Saturn by a priest for himself and his family, likely during the reign of Septimius Severus (late 2nd/early 3rd century CE), mentioning a vow made on his anniversary.

EDCS-04901020.text_edition: \n Votum sacro Saturno Rufinus cum suis solvit\n
EDCS-04901020.translation: Rufinus and his household paid their vow to sacred Saturn.
EDCS-04901020.summary: A votive offering to Saturn by Rufinus and his family, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-04901021.text_edition: \n Ennius Felix Marianus solum templo Telluris cum arboribus duabus copressi sic sacratis concessit\n
EDCS-04901021.translation: Ennius Felix Marianus granted the temple of Tellus with its two cypress trees, so consecrated, in its entirety.
EDCS-04901021.summary: This inscription records a private grant of land, including a temple and trees, to the goddess Tellus by Ennius Felix Marianus.

EDCS-04901036.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum itemque Quintus Sentius Saturninus equestri dignitate exornatus hoc opus instituit et post vitam eius perpetuo pe\n
EDCS-04901036.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Quintus Sentius Saturninus, honored with equestrian rank, established this work and after his life, perpetually...
EDCS-04901036.summary: This funerary inscription records the establishment of a monument by Quintus Sentius Saturninus, a man of equestrian rank, dedicated to the Divine Shades.

EDCS-04901037.text_edition: \n Marco Aelio Aurelio Caesari consuli II designato III Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate XXIII imperatoris II consulis IIII patris patriae filio Lucius Sempronius Paetus Nivis ob honorem flamonii perpetui ci filii sui HS III milia legitimae summae item \n
EDCS-04901037.translation: To Marcus Aelius Aurelius Caesar, twice consul and designated consul, son of Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, in his 23rd year of tribunician power, Imperator II, consul IIII, Father of the Fatherland. Lucius Sempronius Paetus Nivis, for the honor of perpetual flaminate, [donated] 3,000 legitimate sesterces and...
EDCS-04901037.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcus Aelius Aurelius Caesar (later Marcus Aurelius) by Lucius Sempronius Paetus Nivis upon receiving the honor of perpetual flaminate, likely dating to the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-04901038.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filii divi Commodi fratris divi Pii nepotis divi Hadriani pronepotis divi Traiani Parthici abnepotis divi Nervae adnepotis Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi et Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Publi Septimi Getae nobilissimi Caesaris et Iuliae Domnae Augustae matris castrorum Selicius Saturninus Selici Felicis filius ob honorem XIprimatus quem ordo civitatis suae in loco patris eius suffragio detulit ex HS III milibus nummum legitimae summae honorariae adiectis a se HS |(mille) nummum item adiectis a se HS |(mille) D qui fiunt partis suae dimidium de HS III milibus XIprimatus summa honoraria Felicis patris sui defuncti insuper hoc amplius adiecta a se tertia eius summae id est HS D nummum statuae Martis dei quae est ultima pars taxationis HS VI |(milium) nummum sua pecunia fecit idemque dedicavit d(ecreto)? d(ecurionum)\n
EDCS-04901038.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus. For the safety of Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and the most noble Caesar Publius Septimius Geta and Julia Domna Augusta, Mother of the Camps. Selicius Saturninus, son of Selicius Felix, for the honor of XIprimatus, which the town council bestowed upon him in his father's place by vote, [paid] 3,000 legitimate sesterces of the honorarium sum, with an additional 1,000 sesterces added by himself, and an additional 1,500 sesterces added by himself, which makes half of the 3,000 sesterces for the honorarium XIprimatus of his deceased father Felix. Furthermore, he additionally contributed a third of this sum, that is, 500 sesterces, for the statue of the god Mars, which is the final part of the valuation of 6,000 sesterces, and he made it with his own money and dedicated it, [by decree] of the decurions.
EDCS-04901038.summary: This inscription records a substantial dedication by Selicius Saturninus to the god Mars and for public honors, made on behalf of the imperial family and for his own magistracy, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-04901039.translation: To Gnaeus Apertio Gaetulicus, son of Lucius, twice duovir, cereal official of the most splendid colony of Carthage, of singular goodness and innocence, most humane to individuals and the fatherland, who, in addition to what he contributed to the public aqueduct project, further joined another discovered water source by constructing his own work with his own money, and also built a bridge over the river with finely cut stone, a very costly project, to carry the public water; and in addition to all these things, he also made a reservoir for the use of both water supplies and for the adornment of the city from his own funds, and decorated it with marble and mosaic work. For all these generous gifts and munificence, the council decreed that a statue be made from their own contributions to repay his merits, which he himself chose the location for in the forum and erected it with its base, the site having been granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-04901039.summary: This inscription honors Gnaeus Apertio Gaetulicus for his extensive public works, including an aqueduct, bridge, and reservoir, in the colony of Carthage, for which the town council erected a statue in his honor.

EDCS-04901042.text_edition: \n Lucio Aelio Aurelio Commodo consuli Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii patris patriae filio Lucius Manilius Felix ob magistratum ampliata legitima sufetatus summa ex HS milibus nummum posuit decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-04901042.translation: To Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus, consul of Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Father of the Fatherland. Lucius Manilius Felix, for the magistracy, having expanded the legitimate suphate sum, erected [this monument] with 10,000 sesterces, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-04901042.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Lucius Manilius Felix to Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus (later Commodus) upon completing a magistracy, likely dating to the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-04901044.text_edition: \n Saturno Augusto sacrum civitas Chul sufetatus Caius Lepthini et Mani Senti anni LXXXXI\n
EDCS-04901044.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. The community of Chul, under the suphates Caius Lepthini and Mani Senti, [in the year] 91.
EDCS-04901044.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Saturn Augustus by the community of Chul, mentioning two suphates and a regnal year, likely from North Africa.

EDCS-04901045.text_edition: \n Saturno Augusto sacrum Civitas Chul sufetatus Titi Trebelli Saturnini et Marci Corneli Honorati Iuliani anni LXXXXVIIII\n
EDCS-04901045.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. The community of Chul, under the suphates Titi Trebelli Saturnini and Marci Corneli Honorati Iuliani, [in the year] 99.
EDCS-04901045.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Saturn Augustus by the community of Chul, mentioning two suphates and a regnal year, likely from North Africa and similar to EDCS-04901044.

EDCS-04901049.text_edition: \n Diti et Saturno Augustis sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris divi Vespasiani filii Domitiani Augusti Germanici pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate XIII imperatoris XXII consulis XVI censoris perpetui patris patriae civitas Thignicensis templum et basilicam et porticus et cisternam et custodiam sua pecunia facienda curavit\n
EDCS-04901049.translation: Sacred to Dis and Saturn Augustus. For the safety of Imperator Caesar Domitian Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Vespasian, Pontifex Maximus, in his 13th year of tribunician power, Imperator XXII, Consul XVI, Censor for life, Father of the Fatherland. The community of Thignica took care of the construction of the temple, basilica, porticoes, cistern, and guardhouse with their own funds.
EDCS-04901049.summary: This inscription records the construction of several public buildings by the community of Thignica for the worship of Dis and Saturn, and for the safety of Emperor Domitian, likely dating to the late 1st century CE.

EDCS-04901050.text_edition: \n Publio Septimio Getae Caesari Lucius Clodius Quirina Rufinus statuam quam dum adviveret ex HS IImilibus promiserat adiectis ex testamento eius HS IImilibus nummum Lucius Caecilius Quietus Rufinianus Quintus Caecilius Victor Quintianus Blandius flamines perpetui municipii heredes ampliata pecunia posuerunt idemque dedicaverunt\n
EDCS-04901050.translation: To Publius Septimius Geta Caesar. Lucius Clodius Quirinus Rufinus, who had promised this statue in his lifetime for 2,000 sesterces, with an additional 2,000 sesterces from his will. Lucius Caecilius Quietus Rufinianus, Quintus Caecilius Victor Quintianus Blandius, perpetual flamines of the municipality, as heirs, with the increased funds, erected it and also dedicated it.
EDCS-04901050.summary: This inscription records the erection and dedication of a statue to Publius Septimius Geta Caesar by his heirs, who fulfilled a promised donation that was augmented by his will.

EDCS-04901051.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano Augusto Genio civitatis pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici fili divi Pii nepotis divi Hadriani pronepotis divi Traiani Parthici abnepotis divi Nervae adnepotis Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici patris patriae pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate III imperatoris VII consulis II procons(ulis) Castricius Rogatianus Silvani SCI filius ornandae patriae causa fecit et in testamento suo HS II(milia) DV suis legavit ut ob dedicationem universis decurionibus sportulas praestarent\n
EDCS-04901051.translation: To the god Silvanus Augustus, and the Genius of the state, for the health of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus, grandson of the deified Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, father of the country, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, Imperator for the 7th time, Consul for the 2nd time, Proconsul. Castricius Rogatianus, son of Silvanus, made this for the adornment of his fatherland and bequeathed 2,500 sesterces in his will to his freedmen, so that for the dedication they might receive gifts. The date is inferable from Severus' titulature.
EDCS-04901051.summary: This inscription records a public works dedication to the god Silvanus and the Genius of the state, funded by Castricius Rogatianus, and includes a bequest for future celebrations, likely dating to the reign of Septimius Severus.

EDCS-04901052.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Venidius Repostus miles legionis III Augustae centuria Antoni Clementis vixit annis XXXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-04901052.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Venidius Repostus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, in the century of Antonius Clemens, lived 45 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-04901052.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Venidius Repostus, a soldier in the III Augusta legion, who died at age 45.

EDCS-04901053.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Cassius Iscoesius Valens miles cohortis II Flaviae equitatae pius vixit nnos XXXII Anicius Celer Andronicus commilito et contibernalis sic libens fecit hic situs est\n
EDCS-04901053.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Cassius Iscoesius Valens, a soldier of the II Flavia equitata cohort, a dutiful man, lived 32 years. Anicius Celer Andronicus, a fellow soldier and tent-mate, willingly made this. He is buried here.
EDCS-04901053.summary: This tombstone records the death of Cassius Iscoesius Valens, a soldier in the II Flavia equitata cohort, who died at age 32, and was erected by his comrade and tent-mate.

EDCS-04901057.text_edition: \n Lucius Obulcius miles legionis III Augustae centuria Sallusti vixit nnos \n
EDCS-04901057.translation: Lucius Obulcius, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, in the century of Sallustius, lived [lacuna] years.
EDCS-04901057.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone for Lucius Obulcius, a soldier in the III Augusta legion, recording his name and unit but not his lifespan.

EDCS-04901059.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Volaseina Honoratus miles legionis III Augustae centuria Cressi pius vixit annos XXI hic situs est\n
EDCS-04901059.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Volaseina Honoratus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, in the century of Cressus, a dutiful man, lived 21 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-04901059.summary: This tombstone records the death of Lucius Volaseina Honoratus, a soldier in the III Augusta legion, who died at the age of 21.

EDCS-05000782.text_edition: \n Vanammoni Augusto sacrum Aurelius Varixen ordinarius qui ex fortia et suffragio vexillationi profecit ex voto a solo extruxit libens animo\n
EDCS-05000782.translation: Sacred to Vanammon Augustus. Aurelius Varixen, an ordinary official, who advanced with strength and by selection to the detachment, erected this from the ground according to his vow, with a willing heart.
EDCS-05000782.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Aurelius Varixen, an official, to the god Vanammon Augustus, likely related to his military service or promotion.

EDCS-05000815.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Delius Felicissimus avunculus dulcissimus vixit annis LVI mense I diebus XVI\n
EDCS-05000815.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Delius Felicissimus, a most beloved uncle, lived 56 years, 1 month, and 16 days.
EDCS-05000815.summary: This tombstone records the death of Delius Felicissimus, an uncle, who died at the age of 56 years, 1 month, and 16 days.

EDCS-05000823.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Alexandria pia vixit plus minus annos XXVIII quod fuit scriptum debitum communem reddidit cui Galata Augusti nostri verna dispensator marmorum Numidicorum alumnae merenti fecit\n
EDCS-05000823.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Alexandria the dutiful lived approximately 28 years. She paid the common debt owed. Galata, a slave of our Emperor and a freedman steward of Numidian marble, made this for his deserving foster-child.
EDCS-05000823.summary: This tombstone commemorates Alexandria, who died at about 28 years old, and was erected by her foster-father, a steward in the imperial administration.

EDCS-06000019.text_edition: \n Divae Faustinae Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii\n
EDCS-06000019.translation: To the deified Faustina, wife of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius.
EDCS-06000019.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription dedicated to the deified Empress Faustina, wife of Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-06000024.text_edition: \n Marco Annio Vero Augusti filio Lucio Aurelio Commodo Augusti filio Lucillae Augustae\n
EDCS-06000024.translation: To Marcus Annius Verus, son of Augustus, to Lucius Aurelius Commodus, son of Augustus, [and] to Lucilla Augusta.
EDCS-06000024.summary: This inscription records dedications to Marcus Aurelius (as Caesar), Commodus (as Caesar), and Lucilla, likely from the mid-to-late 2nd century CE.

EDCS-06000027.text_edition: \n Fadillae Augusti filiae Cornificiae Augusti filiae Faustinae Augusti filiae\n
EDCS-06000027.translation: To Faustina, daughter of Augustus, daughter of Cornificia, daughter of Augustus, daughter of Faustina, daughter of Augustus.
EDCS-06000027.summary: This inscription is a dedication likely to an imperial woman, possibly Faustina the Younger, given the triple filiation.

EDCS-06000051.text_edition: \n Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari Pio Felici Augusto principi iuventutis domino nostro Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Iuli Philippi Pii Felicis Augusti domini nostri filio Sabrathenses publice\n
EDCS-06000051.translation: To Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, prince of youth, our lord, son of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, our lord. The people of Sabratha publicly [dedicated this].
