EDCS-06000459.text_edition: \n Cum basilica vetus ex maxima parte ruina esset deformata conlabsu sic ac spatio sui breviasset aream forensem divino icta conflagrarat incendio adque is locus saeculo fortunatissimo meliora deposceret tantae stragis labe sublata tripertita porticus magnitudine sui ac Troadensium columnarum adornata operis provincialium ac sumptu publico disponente Laenatio Romulo viro perfectissimo rectore provinciae intra anni spatium perfecta ac dedicata est adque ad sempiternam memoriam statuam marmoream suo numine radiantem domino nostro Constantino maximo victori semper Augusto idem vir perfectissimus dicata mente constituit curante Claudio Aurelio Generoso viro egregio curatore rei publicae et splendidissimo ordine coloniae Lepcimagnensium\n
EDCS-06000459.translation: Since the old basilica was in ruins for the most part and had become deformed, and its space had been diminished by the fire that had struck it with divine force and the forum area, and since that place demanded better things in the most fortunate age, with such a ruin removed, a portico with three aisles, adorned with its size and the workmanship of Phrygian columns and provincial work, was completed and dedicated within the space of a year at public expense, under the administration of Laenatius Romanus, most perfect man, governor of the province; and for eternal memory, the same most perfect man erected a marble statue radiant with his divine power to our lord Constantine the Great Victorious Always Augustus, under the supervision of Claudius Aurelius Generosus, most excellent man, curator of the republic, and the most splendid order of the colonus of Lepcimagnesium.
EDCS-06000459.summary: This long inscription details the reconstruction of a basilica and the dedication of a statue to Constantine the Great by governor Laenatius Romanus and others in Lepcimagnesium, completed within a year, likely in the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-06000485.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription mentioning Emperor Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-06000486.summary: This inscription likely records the name of Emperor Antoninus, possibly in a dedicatory or honorific context, dating to the Antonine period (2nd century AD).

EDCS-06000490.summary: This inscription fragment mentions the deified Emperor Marcus Aurelius, suggesting a monument or dedication related to him, likely from the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-06000494.summary: This inscription fragment likely refers to the deified Emperor Nerva, possibly as part of a dedication or title, dating from the period after his reign (from 98 AD onwards).

EDCS-06000499.translation: The people of Lepcis, most devoted to his divine will...
EDCS-06000499.summary: This inscription records a public dedication by the people of Lepcis to the divine will of an emperor, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-06000502.summary: This inscription fragment mentions 'Augustus', referring to an emperor, possibly in a context of 'our emperor', dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-06000503.summary: This inscription fragment includes the epithet 'Pius Felix' (Devout and Fortunate), commonly associated with Roman emperors, likely referring to one from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-06000504.summary: This inscription fragment contains the beginning of the imperial epithet 'Pius Felix', suggesting a reference to an emperor, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-06000505.summary: This inscription fragment mentions someone as the 'son of Augustus', possibly referring to a deified emperor or a descendant, dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-06000506.summary: This inscription fragment refers to 'our lord Pius Felix', a common imperial titulature, likely dating to the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-06000507.text_edition: \n VS Liberatori orbis ac RLepcitani devoti numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-06000507.translation: ...Liberator of the world and the people of Lepcis, devoted to his divine will and majesty...
EDCS-06000507.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the people of Lepcis to an emperor, hailed as the 'Liberator of the world', emphasizing their devotion to his divine authority, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-06000509.text_edition: \n Marcus Annius Messalla adlectus in senatum a divo a divo Vespasiano legatus Africae\n
EDCS-06000509.translation: Marcus Annius Messalla, appointed to the Senate by the deified Vespasian, governor of Africa.
EDCS-06000509.summary: This inscription records the career of Marcus Annius Messalla, who served as governor of Africa after being appointed to the Senate by Emperor Vespasian.

EDCS-06000510.text_edition: \n Marcus Annius Messalla adlectus in senatum a divo a divo Vespasiano legatus Africae\n
EDCS-06000510.translation: Marcus Annius Messalla, appointed to the Senate by the deified Vespasian, governor of Africa.
EDCS-06000510.summary: This inscription records the career of Marcus Annius Messalla, who served as governor of Africa after being appointed to the Senate by Emperor Vespasian.

EDCS-06000511.text_edition: \n Marco Atilio Metilio Braduae Caucidio Tertullo Claudio Attico Vibullio Pollioni Gavidio Latiari Atrio Basso proconsuli Decimus Iunius Crescens Decimus Iunius Galba Quintus Calpurnius Capito Lucius Plautius Octavianus patrono\n
EDCS-06000511.translation: To Marcus Atilius Metilius Bradua Cauccidius Tertullus Claudius Atticus Vibullius Pollio Gavidius Latiaris Atrius Bassus, proconsul, by Decimus Iunius Crescens, Decimus Iunius Galba, Quintus Calpurnius Capito, Lucius Plautius Octavianus, his patron.
EDCS-06000511.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Marcus Atilius Metilius Bradua Cauccidius Tertullus, a proconsul, likely honoring his service, by several individuals including his patron.

EDCS-06000513.text_edition: \n Elpidii Omnium virtutum praedicabili viro et supra documenta bonitatis insigni adque magnifico Caecilio Severo viro clarissimo agenti vices praeffectorum praetorio ob multiformem iudiciorum eius in se moderationem ex decreto et sufragio M Lepcitani publice\n
EDCS-06000513.translation: To Caecilius Severus, a man of the highest distinction, acting on behalf of the prefects of the Praetorian Guard, for his multifaceted moderation in his judgments concerning him, publicly by the decree and vote of Lepcis, a man of all virtues, praiseworthy, and outstanding and magnificent beyond measure for his goodness.
EDCS-06000513.summary: This inscription honors Caecilius Severus, who acted in place of the Praetorian prefect, for his judicial moderation, with a public dedication from Lepcis.

EDCS-06000514.text_edition: \n Cnaeus Calpurnius Cnaei filius Piso consul pontufex sic proconsul\n
EDCS-06000514.translation: Gnaeus Calpurnius Piso, son of Gnaeus, consul, pontiff, proconsul.
EDCS-06000514.summary: This inscription lists the high offices held by Gnaeus Calpurnius Piso, including consul, pontiff, and proconsul.

EDCS-06000515.text_edition: \n Lucius Caninius Luci filius Gallus XVvir sacris faciundis consul proconsul patronus dedicavit\n
EDCS-06000515.translation: Lucius Caninius Gallus, son of Lucius, appointed to the XVviri sacris faciundis, consul, proconsul, patron, dedicated this.
EDCS-06000515.summary: This inscription records Lucius Caninius Gallus, who held multiple prestigious religious and political offices, as the dedicator of a monument.

EDCS-06000516.text_edition: \n Claudio A proconsuli provinciae Africae omnium virtutum viro innocentis integritatis vicoratae lenitatis sublimis moderationis laudavilis sic iustitiae Lepcimagnenses ex decreto ordinis patrono perpetuo\n
EDCS-06000516.translation: To Claudius, proconsul of the province of Africa, a man of all virtues, of innocent integrity, victorious gentleness, sublime moderation, praiseworthy justice, by Lepcimagnenses, by decree of the council, his perpetual patron.
EDCS-06000516.summary: This inscription honors Claudius, proconsul of Africa, for his virtues and justice, with a dedication from the people of Lepcimagnenses who acclaimed him their perpetual patron.

EDCS-06000518.text_edition: \n Caio Fulvio Plautiano praefecto praet[orio] Marcus Cornelius Bassus Servianus egregiae memoriae vir fieri iussit Cornelia Servianilla clarissima femina filia et heres posuit\n
EDCS-06000518.translation: To Gaius Fulvius Plautianus, prefect of the Praetorian Guard, Marcus Cornelius Bassus Servianus, a man of exceptional memory, ordered it to be made; Cornelia Servianilla, a woman of the highest distinction, his daughter and heir, placed it.
EDCS-06000518.summary: This monument was ordered by Marcus Cornelius Bassus Servianus and erected by his daughter and heir, Cornelia Servianilla, in honor of Gaius Fulvius Plautianus, the Praetorian prefect.

EDCS-06000524.text_edition: \n Dimittendus in splendidissimam coloniam Leptim Magnam iussu Fulvi Plautiani clarissimi viri praefecti praetorio ac necessari dominorum nostrorum\n
EDCS-06000524.translation: He is to be dismissed into the most splendid colony of Leptis Magna by order of Fulvius Plautianus, a man of the highest distinction, prefect of the Praetorian Guard and confidant of our lords.
EDCS-06000524.summary: This inscription records an order by Fulvius Plautianus, a Praetorian prefect, regarding the dismissal of someone to the colony of Leptis Magna.

EDCS-06000526.text_edition: \n Gaio Gavio Macro legato pro praetore III Lepcitani publice\n
EDCS-06000526.translation: To Gaius Gavius Macro, legate with the power of a proconsul, publicly by the Lepcitani.
EDCS-06000526.summary: This inscription is a public dedication by the people of Lepcis to Gaius Gavius Macro, who served as a legate with proconsular authority.

EDCS-06000529.text_edition: \n Proscaenium columnis et marmoribus ex HS CCducentibus milibus a Marcio Vitale itemque ex HS CCCtrecentibus milibus a Iunio Galba in eam rem datis item tetrastylis lacunarum pecunia publica exornatum dedicatum est Lucio Hedio Rufo Lolliano Avito proconsule Caio Vibio Gallione Claudio Severo legato\n
EDCS-06000529.translation: The stage building, adorned with columns and marble and a money grant of 200,000 sesterces from Marcius Vitalis, and likewise with 300,000 sesterces from Junius Galba, with its coffered ceilings decorated by the state treasury, was dedicated under the proconsul Lucius Hedius Rufus Lollianus Avitus and the legate Gaius Vibius Gallio Claudius Severus.
EDCS-06000529.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a theater's stage building, funded by private donations and public money, under the proconsul and legate.

EDCS-06000532.text_edition: \n Proculus legatus pro praetore dedicavit Caius Maecius Cnaei filius Falerna Flaccus sua pecunia fecit\n
EDCS-06000532.translation: Proculus, legate with praetorian power, dedicated [this monument], which Gaius Maecius Flaccus, son of Cnaeus, from the Falernian tribe, made at his own expense.
EDCS-06000532.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument built at private expense by Gaius Maecius Flaccus, commissioned by Proculus, a legate with praetorian power.

EDCS-06000534.text_edition: \n Caius Rubellius Blandus proconsul Marcus Etrilius Lupercus legatus pro praetore \n
EDCS-06000534.translation: Gaius Rubellius Blandus, proconsul, and Marcus Etrilius Lupercus, legate with praetorian power.
EDCS-06000534.summary: This inscription simply lists the names and titles of two officials, likely a proconsul and a legate.

EDCS-06000537.text_edition: \n Curni Lucio Volusio Basso Caereali clarissimo viro et consulari curatori rei publicae suae quae sic inter cetera opera quae industria et labore maximo renovavit Ulpiam basilicam cum ORVS in meliorem faciem publico sumptu restituit gratiam afferentes Lepcitani publice \xce\x9b\xe1\xbd\xb3\xcf\x80\xce\xba\xce\xb9\xcf\x82 \xe1\xbc\xa1 \xce\x9c\xce\xb5\xce\xb3\xe1\xbd\xb1\xce\xbb\xce\xb7 \xcf\x86\xce\xb9\xce\xbb(\xce\xb5)\xe1\xbf\x96 \xcf\x83\xce\xb5 \xe1\xbf\xac\xe1\xbd\xbd\xce\xbc\xce\xb7 Genio coloniae Lepcis Magnae Crescentina fecit\n
EDCS-06000537.translation: The people of Lepcis Magna, bringing gratitude, dedicated to Lucius Volusius Bassus Cerealis, a most distinguished man and consular, the curator of their republic, who, among other works, renewed the Ulpian Basilica with great industry and effort, restoring it to a better appearance at public expense, [and to the] Genius of the colony of Lepcis Magna, Crescentina made [this].
EDCS-06000537.summary: This inscription records the restoration of the Ulpian Basilica and a dedication to Lucius Volusius Bassus Cerealis and the Genius of Lepcis Magna, executed by Crescentina at public expense.

EDCS-06000538.text_edition: \n Lucio Volusio Basso Cereali clarissimo viro legato totius innocentiae et aequitatis et consimilis moderationis viro Lepcimagnenses ex decreto ordinis municipi et patrono perpetuo\n
EDCS-06000538.translation: To Lucius Volusius Bassus Cerealis, a most distinguished man, legate, a man of all innocence, equity, and similar moderation, the people of Lepcis Magna, by decree of the town council and as perpetual patron.
EDCS-06000538.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Lucius Volusius Bassus Cerealis, honoring him as a legate and patron of Lepcis Magna for his moderation and justice.

EDCS-06000552.translation: To the most distinguished man, acting in place of the praetorian prefects for the African provinces, the council and people of Lepcis Magna decreed that a marble statue be erected to him, in addition to the guest-token, so that the memory of his incomparable benefits might be passed down to posterity, and because of his preservation of justice and moderation through many trials.
EDCS-06000552.summary: This inscription records the decision by the council and people of Lepcis Magna to erect a marble statue to an official acting as governor for the African provinces, honoring his benefits and just rule.

EDCS-06000553.text_edition: \n Dulciti Aurelio Sempronio Sereno equiti Romano principali Alexandriae ob insigne et admirabile studiorum ingenium praeter deccurionatuum honorem et statuam apud se conlocatam etiam apud Lepcimag[nenses ------ clarissimo viro agenti vices praefectorum praetorio per Africanas provincias Lepcimagnensis ordo et populus ut incomparabilium beneficiorum eius memoria etiam ad posteros mitteretur praeter <h=K>ospitalem tesseram etiam statuam marmoream constituendam esse duxerunt ut servati moderatione iudiciorum eius ac multis tempestatibus to \n
EDCS-06000553.translation: To Dulcitius Aurelius Sempronius Serenus, a Roman knight, a principal official in Alexandria, for his remarkable and admirable intellectual talent, in addition to the honor of a decurionate and a statue erected there, also to [the most distinguished man] acting in place of the praetorian prefects for the African provinces, the council and people of Lepcis Magna decreed that a marble statue be erected to him, in addition to the guest-token, so that the memory of his incomparable benefits might be passed down to posterity, and because of his preservation of justice and moderation through many trials.
EDCS-06000553.summary: This inscription records the erection of a marble statue to Dulcitius Aurelius Sempronius Serenus for his talents and to an official acting as governor of the African provinces for his benefits and justice.

EDCS-06000555.text_edition: \n Consentii Lucio Domitio Iusto Aemiliano equiti Romano viro perfectissimo curatori rei publicae ob eximium amorem in patriam et cives ex decreto ordinis et suffragiis populi civi karissimo Lepcitani publice Quintii flaminis perpetui sacerdotalis Ob meritum laboris et legationis perfectae Lucio Aemilio Quinto flamini perpetuo sacerdotali ordo splendidissimus civitatis Lepcimagnensium dedit\n
EDCS-06000555.translation: To Lucius Domitius Iustus Aemilianus, a Roman knight, a most accomplished man, curator of the republic, the people of Lepcis, publicly, as their dearest citizen, for his exceptional love for his homeland and citizens, by decree of the council and by vote of the people. To Quintus, perpetual flamen and priest, for his merit of labor and perfect legation, the most distinguished council of the city of Lepcis Magna gave [this].
EDCS-06000555.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Domitius Iustus Aemilianus, a curator, and Quintus, a perpetual flamen and priest, for their service and love for the city of Lepcis.

EDCS-06000558.text_edition: \n Heraclii Benignissimo viro principali prudentissimo et integ<e=I>rrimo Tito Flavio Frontino Heraclio viro perfectissimo auguri sacerdoti Laurentium Labinatium sic IIviro ob diversarum voluptatum exhibitiones adque admirabilem ludorum editionem amoremque incomparabilem in patriam et cives suos sufragio quietissimi populi et decreto splendidissimi ordinis \n
EDCS-06000558.translation: To Heraclius, a most benignant man, a principal official, most prudent and most upright, and to Titus Flavius Frontinus Heraclius, a most accomplished man, augur, priest of the Laurentes Labinati, and IIvir of Laurentum, because of his provision of various pleasures and his admirable staging of games and incomparable love for his homeland and citizens, by vote of the most peaceful people and decree of the most distinguished council.
EDCS-06000558.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the people and council to Heraclius and Titus Flavius Frontinus Heraclius, honoring them for public entertainment, games, and their love for the city.

EDCS-06000559.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli honorarii, tituli operum
EDCS-06000559.text_edition: \n Nepotianii Flavio Nepotiano viro perfectissimo comiti et praesidi provinciae Tripolitanae iustitia et integritati sic praecipuo moderatione ac benignitate praestantissimo abstinentiae et honestatis officiorumque omnium cultori rei etiam militaris peritissimo armis consiliisque inconparabili sic quod idem ordines universosque provinciales iuridicendo fide benivolentia multis beneficiis cumulaverit quod barbarorum insolentiam exercitio scientiae militaris adtriberit quod limitis defensionem tuitionemque perpetuam futuris etiam temporibus munitam securamque ab omni hostile incursione praestiterit quod civitatum moenia operum instauratione vel novitate decoraverit ordo civitatis Lepcimagnensis cum populo statuam marmoream ob haec merita decretis et sufragiis concin<n>entibus conlocavit\n
EDCS-06000559.translation: The council of the city of Lepcis Magna, with the people, erected a marble statue to Flavius Nepotianus, a most accomplished man, count and governor of the province of Tripolitana, outstanding in justice and integrity, distinguished in moderation and kindness, a cultivator of abstinence and all duties, very skilled also in military matters, incomparable in arms and counsel, who has overwhelmed the orders and all provincials with his juridical skill, benevolence, and many benefits; who has attributed the insolence of barbarians to the exercise of his military knowledge; who has provided for the defense and perpetual protection of the border, secured and safe from any hostile incursion for future times; who has adorned the city walls with the restoration or novelty of works, because of these merits, by unanimous decrees and votes.
EDCS-06000559.summary: This inscription records the erection of a marble statue by the council and people of Lepcis Magna to Flavius Nepotianus, governor of Tripolitana, honoring his justice, military skill, and contributions to city defenses and infrastructure.

EDCS-06000560.text_edition: \n Petasii Eximiae integritatis et inconparabilis prudentiae ac totius bonitatis viro Flavio Petasio viro perfectissimo ex praesidibus provinciae Tripolitanae ordo splendidissimae civitatis Lepcimagnensis decreto suo et populi sufragiis memor meritorum in se ab eius praestantia conlatorum statuam in foro novo Severiano ad perpetuitatis memoriam patrono dignissimo posuit\n
EDCS-06000560.translation: The council and people of the most illustrious city of Lepcimagna erected this statue in the new Severian Forum to Flavis Petasius, a man of exceptional integrity, incomparable prudence, and all goodness, a most accomplished man and former governor of the province of Tripolitana, as a memorial to his merits bestowed upon them by his excellence, to their most worthy patron.
EDCS-06000560.summary: This is a civic honorific statue erected by the city of Lepcimagna to a former governor, Flavis Petasius, for his merits.

EDCS-06000561.text_edition: \n Heraclii Uno eodemque anno duumviro Lepcimagnensium et sacerdoti provinciae Tripolitanae innocentissimo viro principali integerrimo amatori patriae ac civium suorum Tito Flavio Vibiano viro perfectissimo flamini perpetuo et pontifici curatori rei publicae Lepcimagnensium sacerdoti Laurentium Labinatium sic et sacerdoti Matris Deum praefecto omnium sacrorum ob diversarum voluptatum exhibitionem et Libycarum ferarum X ex populi sufragio et ordin(is) decreto\n
EDCS-06000561.translation: Titus Flavius Vibianus, a most innocent man, most upright principal, lover of his country and citizens, a most accomplished man, perpetual flamen and pontiff, curator of the public affairs of Lepcimagna, priest of the Laurentes Labinates, and priest of the Mother of the Gods, prefect of all sacred rites, who in one and the same year served as chief magistrate of Lepcimagna and priest of the province of Tripolitana, [this monument is erected] by popular vote and decree of the council in recognition of his exhibition of various pleasures and ten beasts from Libya.
EDCS-06000561.summary: This inscription honors Titus Flavius Vibianus, a prominent local official and priest, for his service and entertainment provided to the community, likely with a statue or similar monument.

EDCS-06000565.text_edition: \n [[]] [[]] [[]] [[]] [[]] praefecto vehiculorum procuratori XX hereditatium fratri pio Fulvia Nepotilla soror cum Quinto Fulvio Didabibuliano marito et Quinto Fulvio Didabibuliano et Quinto Fulvio Severo Iuniore sacerdotio Caeninense ornato liberis posuit\n
EDCS-06000565.translation: Fulvia Nepotilla, his sister, with her husband Quintus Fulvius Didabibulianus, and her sons Quintus Fulvius Didabibulianus and Quintus Fulvius Severus the Younger, adorned with the priesthood of Caenina, erected [this monument] to [---], prefect of transport, procurator of twenty estates, her pious brother.
EDCS-06000565.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a woman and her sons for her brother, a prefect of transport and procurator, honoring his public roles and family relationship.

EDCS-06000567.text_edition: \n Romulii ob tantam moderationem iudiciorum provisionesque saluberrimas adque felicitatem adventus eius Laenatio Romulo viro perfectissimo praesidi provinciae Tripolitanae sufragio quietissimi populi et decreto splendidissimi ordinis sumptibus suis posuit\n
EDCS-06000567.translation: Laenatius Romulus, a most accomplished man and governor of the province of Tripolitana, erected [this monument] at his own expense, by the vote of the most peaceful people and the decree of the most illustrious council, because of his great moderation in judgments, his most beneficial foresight, and the felicity of his arrival.
EDCS-06000567.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the citizens of an unnamed city to Laenatius Romulus, their governor, in recognition of his good governance and beneficial presence.

EDCS-06000568.text_edition: \n Flavianii Omnium virtutum experientissimo viro ingenitae mansuetudinis et benignitate patientiae pollenti iudiciorum aequissimo moderatori antistiti innocentiae Magnio Aspero Flaviano viro perfectissimo praesidi provinciae Tripolitanae Lepcimagnenses ob eius amorem in se conlatum et integritatem perpensae sic sibi cognitam patrono dignissimo famae et gloriae perpetuum testimonium posuerunt\n
EDCS-06000568.translation: The people of Lepcimagna erected this everlasting testimony of fame and glory to their most worthy patron, Magnius Asper Flavianus, a most accomplished man and governor of the province of Tripolitana, most experienced in all virtues, abounding in innate gentleness and kindness of patience, fairest moderator of judgments, prelate of innocence, for his bestowed love and integrity recognized by them.
EDCS-06000568.summary: The citizens of Lepcimagna erected a monument to Magnius Asper Flavianus, their governor, to honor his virtues and his love and integrity towards the city.

EDCS-06000569.text_edition: \n Inachii Omnium virtutum viro Publicio Valeriano viro perfectissimo ex praesidibus singularis integritatis et moderationis Lepcimagnenses patrono praestantissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-06000569.translation: The people of Lepcimagna erected [this monument] to their most outstanding patron, Publicius Valerianus, a man of all virtues, a most accomplished man and former governor of singular integrity and moderation.
EDCS-06000569.summary: The people of Lepcimagna erected a monument to their patron, Publicius Valerianus, a former governor esteemed for his virtues, integrity, and moderation.

EDCS-06000570.text_edition: \n Obsequii Caio Valerio Vibiano viro perfectissimo praesidi provinciae Tripolitanae singularis aequitatis et beniboli sic vicoris sic M omnium virtutum viro ob tunc incipientem deinceps iugem erga se eius dignationem ordo splendidissimus Lepcimagnensium et populus patrono ex decreto splendidissimi ordinis posuerunt\n
EDCS-06000570.translation: The most illustrious council and people of Lepcimagna erected [this monument] to their patron, Gaius Valerius Vibianus, a most accomplished man and governor of the province of Tripolitana, a man of singular fairness and kindness, a victor in contests, a man of all virtues, by decree of the most illustrious council, because of his then-beginning and thereafter continuous favor towards them.
EDCS-06000570.summary: The city of Lepcimagna and its council erected a monument to their patron, Governor Gaius Valerius Vibianus, in gratitude for his ongoing favor and esteemed virtues.

EDCS-06000571.text_edition: \n Amelii Multiplici laborum merito varioque voluptatum genere stimulantibus paternis avitiis etiam documentis ab ineunte aetate patriam civesque suos promerenti Marco Vibio Anniano Gemino viro perfectissimo flamini perpetuo pontifici sacerdotali provinciae Tripolitanae bis IIviro ex sufragio quietissimi populi et decreto splendidissimi ordinis\n
EDCS-06000571.translation: Marcus Vibius Annianus Geminus, a most accomplished man, perpetual flamen, pontiff, priest of the province of Tripolitana, twice chief magistrate, [this monument is erected] by the vote of the most peaceful people and the decree of the most illustrious council, for his many merits in labors and his diverse kind of pleasures, urged on by ancestral examples and for his service to his country and citizens from his early age.
EDCS-06000571.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the people and council to Marcus Vibius Annianus Geminus, a prominent local official and priest, honoring his public service, honors, and dedication from a young age.

EDCS-06000572.text_edition: \n Aemiliae Lychnidi Clementillae Aemili Arriani Caeciliani duoviralici pontificis filiae Lucius Volusius Gallus eques Romanus pontifex duoviralis flamen perpetuus ob insignem adfectionis castitatem matri Volusiorum Appianillae Bassi et Cerealis filliorum suorum permissu splendidissimi ordinis posuit\n
EDCS-06000572.translation: Lucius Volusius Gallus, a Roman knight, pontiff, chief magistrate, perpetual flamen, erected [this monument] with the permission of the most illustrious council to his mother, Aemilia Lychnis Clementilla, daughter of Aemilius Arrianus Caecilianus, a duovir and pontiff, and to his mother Volusia Appianilla Bassus and Cerealis, his sons, for her outstanding affection and chastity.
EDCS-06000572.summary: Lucius Volusius Gallus erected a monument to his mother, Aemilia Lychnis Clementilla, honoring her affection and chastity, and also including his sons in the dedication.

EDCS-06000580.text_edition: \n Caio Aelio Rufino flamini Aquilia Blaesilla mater filio piissimo\n
EDCS-06000580.translation: Aquilia Blaesilla, mother, to her most dutiful son, Gaius Aelius Rufus, perpetual flamen.
EDCS-06000580.summary: A mother dedicates a monument to her dutiful son, Gaius Aelius Rufus, who held the religious office of perpetual flamen.

EDCS-06000581.translation: To Lucius Aemilius Quintus, perpetual priest and flamen, for his merit in labor and perfect legation, the most distinguished council of the citizens of Lepcimagna decreed this.
EDCS-06000581.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the council of Lepcimagna to Lucius Aemilius Quintus for his service as a priest and legation.

EDCS-06000583.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Amicus Marcus Heliodorius Apollonides aediles mensas pecunia sua dono dederunt\n
EDCS-06000583.translation: Tiberius Claudius Amicus and Marcus Heliodorius Apollonides, as aediles, presented tables at their own expense.
EDCS-06000583.summary: This inscription records a donation of tables by two aediles, Tiberius Claudius Amicus and Marcus Heliodorius Apollonides.

EDCS-06000602.text_edition: \n Amatori patriae et civium suorum quod ex indulgentia sacra civibus suis feras dentatas quattuor vivas donavit ex decreto splendidissimi ordinis bigam decreverunt Porfyri Porfyri\n
EDCS-06000602.translation: To a lover of his country and his citizens, because by his generosity he donated four live wild beasts with teeth to his citizens, the most distinguished council decreed a two-horse chariot to Porphyrius.
EDCS-06000602.summary: This inscription records a decree by the council honoring Porphyrius for donating four live wild beasts to the citizens.

EDCS-06000625.text_edition: \n ------ C]nae-- Calpurni ex pecunia publica O\n
EDCS-06000625.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely recording a public expense associated with someone named Calpurnius.

EDCS-06000678.summary: This is a short inscription likely recording a dedication or memorial to Calliepius by Publius Cornelius Saecularis.

EDCS-06000683.text_edition: \n [] P[] thi Presbyti Geronti Euphrasi Patricii viri clarissimi Virtute praestanti aequitate miravili sic temperantia moderato sic defensori iustitiae innocentium vindici Flavio Macedonio Patricio viro clarissimo comiti et duci provinciae Tripolitanae ordo splendidus et populus universus Lepcis Magnae civitatis digno patrono decrevit adque constituit\n
EDCS-06000683.translation: To Flavius Macedonius, a most distinguished man, count and duke of the province of Tripolitana, defender of justice and protector of the innocent, for his outstanding virtue, wonderful equity, and moderate temperance, the distinguished council and the entire populace of the city of Lepcis Magna decreed and established him as a worthy patron.
EDCS-06000683.summary: This inscription records the honoring of Flavius Macedonius as a patron of Lepcis Magna by its council and people for his virtues and service as a provincial duke.

EDCS-06000694.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fulviae Crescentillae uxoris sanctissimae Quintus Marcius Candidus Rusonianus maritus fecit\n
EDCS-06000694.translation: To the divine shades of Fulvia Crescentilla, his most devoted wife, Quintus Marcius Candidus Rusonianus, her husband, made this.
EDCS-06000694.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Quintus Marcius Candidus Rusonianus for his wife, Fulvia Crescentilla.

EDCS-06000695.text_edition: \n Quinto Furio Cereali socero Carmini Saturnini qui statuas XVI HS testa[mento ------\n
EDCS-06000695.translation: To Quintus Furius Cerealis, father-in-law of Carminus Saturninus, who by his will [bequeathed] sixteen statues for [---].
EDCS-06000695.summary: This inscription records a bequest of sixteen statues by Quintus Furius Cerealis, father-in-law of Carminus Saturninus.

EDCS-06000798.text_edition: \n Celso II et Marcello II consulibus\n
EDCS-06000798.translation: In the consulship of Celsus and Marcellus (second time).
EDCS-06000798.summary: This is a dating formula indicating the year based on the consulship of Celsus and Marcellus.

EDCS-06000810.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription that likely mentions a person named Calist.

EDCS-06000821.text_edition: \n EPONI flamin perpetu--) cura rum se s vir- perfectissim--\n
EDCS-06000821.translation: EPONI, perpetual priest, through his perfect achievement...
EDCS-06000821.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription likely recording the name and title of a priest named Eponi, possibly related to a public work or honor.

EDCS-06000822.text_edition: \n FRV fecerunt de c MK VALCI ETAT coll\n
EDCS-06000822.translation: FRV made from the bronze of VALCI ETAT and coll[egues]...
EDCS-06000822.summary: This inscription indicates that someone named FRV created something from bronze contributed by VALCI ETAT and others.

EDCS-06000825.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, only containing a few letters that offer little contextual information.

EDCS-06000826.text_edition: \n ano anos umi L uti M\n
EDCS-06000826.translation: ano anos umi L uti M...
EDCS-06000826.summary: This is a highly fragmented inscription with no clear recoverable meaning.

EDCS-06000864.text_edition: \n orum Geta et Roae et Cea Domitius C et Casana quae et gyddus es filis suis simis\n
EDCS-06000864.translation: ...orum Geta and Roae and Cea, Domitius C and Casana, who is also called Gyddus, to their most similar sons...
EDCS-06000864.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication made by parents, possibly including Domitius C and Casana (also known as Gyddus), to their sons, including Geta, Roae, and Cea.

EDCS-06000865.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris divi Vespasiani filii Domitiani Augusti Germanici pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate VI imperatoris XIIII consulis XIII censoris perpetui patris patriae iussu Suelli Flacci legati Augusti pro praetore terminus positi sic inter nationem sic Muduciuviorum et Zamuciorum ex conventione utrarumque nationum\n
EDCS-06000865.translation: By the authority of the Emperor, son of the divine Vespasian, Domitian Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, in his sixth year of tribunician power, hailed as Emperor for the 14th time, consul for the 13th time, perpetual censor, father of the country, by order of Suellius Flaccus, imperial legate with praetorian powers, this boundary was set, thus between the nation of the Muduciuvii and the Zamucii, according to the agreement of both nations.
EDCS-06000865.summary: This inscription, dated to the reign of Domitian (late 1st century AD), records the establishment of a boundary marker between two tribal groups, the Muduciuvii and the Zamucii, under the authority of the emperor and his legate.

EDCS-06000870.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flabi sic Mysun vixit annos XL\n
EDCS-06000870.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Flabia Mysun lived 40 years.
EDCS-06000870.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name and age of a deceased woman named Flabia Mysun.

EDCS-06000896.summary: This entry indicates that the inscription was not transcribed and refers the user to an image for details.

EDCS-06000899.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute et victoria dominorum nostrorum Imperatorum Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Augusti nostri filii et [Publi] [Septimi Getae] Augusti Augusti nostri filii Augusti nostri fratris et Iuliae Augustae matris castrorum Marcus Caninius Adiutor Faustinianus praefectus cohortis II Hamiorum praepositus vexillatione legionis III Augustae piae vindicis aram posuit et dedicavit\n
EDCS-06000899.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the safety and victory of our lords the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, our Emperor's son, and [Publius] [Septimius Geta] Augustus, our Emperor's brother, and Julia Augusta, mother of the camp, Marcus Caninius Adiutor Faustinianus, prefect of the II Cohort of Hamians, in command of a detachment of the III Augustan Pia Vindex legion, erected and dedicated this altar.
EDCS-06000899.summary: This inscription, dating to the early 3rd century AD, records the dedication of an altar to Jupiter Dolichenus by Marcus Caninius Adiutor Faustinianus for the well-being of the emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, Geta, and their mother Julia Domna.

EDCS-06000904.summary: This entry indicates that the inscription was not transcribed and refers the user to images for details.

EDCS-06000907.text_edition: \n Flavii Sebentius centen(arius) et Stiddin eius coniunx hunc locum didicarunt sic O bonum initium natisqui filiis concilium salvis libiris sic cum filicitati sic triumfanti Binaitir possissu sic\n
EDCS-06000907.translation: Flavius Sebentius, a centenarian, and his wife Stiddin dedicated this place. May it be a good beginning for their children and their sons, with their children and their family in happiness and triumph.
EDCS-06000907.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a place by a centenarian and his wife, likely for the benefit of their descendants, probably from the Roman period.

EDCS-06000910.summary: This entry indicates that the inscription text was not transcribed and is only available in image form.

EDCS-06000912.translation: Bodschichun Chalia, a priest of the gods, son of Avus, in good health.
EDCS-06000912.summary: This is a short dedication in an unknown language with Latin elements, possibly a personal memorial or dedication, from an ancient period.

EDCS-06000913.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Invictus Augustus et Marcus Iulius Philippus Caesar noster regionem limitis Tentheitani partitam et eius viam incursibus barbarorum constituto novo centenario -AS praecluserunt Cominio Cassiano legato Augustorum pro praetore Gallicano viro egregio praeposito limitis cura Numisii Maximi domo sia tibuni\n
EDCS-06000913.translation: Emperors Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Augustus Invictus and Marcus Julius Philippus Caesar, our Caesar, closed off the region of the Tentheitan frontier and its road from the incursions of barbarians by establishing a new fort, under the command of Cominius Cassianus, legate of the Augusti, proconsul of Gaul, an excellent man, and overseer of the frontier, with the care of Numisius Maximus.
EDCS-06000913.summary: This inscription records the fortification of a frontier region in Gallia by Emperors Philip the Elder and Philip the Younger, dated to their joint reign (c. 244-249 AD).

EDCS-06000915.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aelia Donata vixit annis tribus\n
EDCS-06000915.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Aelia Donata lived for three years.
EDCS-06000915.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young child named Aelia Donata, who died at the age of three.

EDCS-06000917.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Barbagnarus militiae praenuntiu insolito casu GIINTE MAT OI VI ICROEAT karus pater us vixit annos\n
EDCS-06000917.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Barbagnarus, a military scout, by an unusual accident [text illegible]. His dear father lived for [number] years.
EDCS-06000917.summary: This funerary inscription honors Barbagnarus, a military scout who died in an accident, and also records the lifespan of his father.

EDCS-06000919.translation: Julius Masthalul, by his father Chyrdid, son of Sir..., son of Blam, son of Saba, [rest illegible].
EDCS-06000919.summary: This inscription appears to be a patronymic, listing the ancestry of Julius Masthalul, likely a local notable, in an unknown language with some Latin elements.

EDCS-06000921.translation: Macama, by his father Isachu, tribune.
EDCS-06000921.summary: This inscription identifies Macama as a tribune, and notes his father Isachu, likely from a Romanized local population.

EDCS-06000922.text_edition: \n Iulius Ibituathif Misirkar ben Amrr ben Chyrdidry bymysiyrir aban bal sm sabare masadyth sic le Misaieruchan dni msatyrth vy mysorthim sic ys sy barrecilim tim useb sylo Machrus ben Rogate\n
EDCS-06000922.translation: Julius Ibituathif, son of Misirkar, son of Amrr, son of Chyrdidry, by his wife Irir, father of Balasm, son of Sabare, and his wife Masadyth, and their children Misaieruchan,dni, Msatyrth, and their son Ys, and his wife Barrecilim, and their son Usleb, son of Machrus, son of Rogate.
EDCS-06000922.summary: This is a complex genealogical inscription and dedication, listing multiple generations of a family, possibly related to local leadership or land ownership.

EDCS-06000923.translation: Julius Nasif, tribune, son of Siraban, son of Iyσihan, son of Rirachan, [rest illegible]. He made this for himself, the son of Rasm, son of Mycne, son of Isaufath, son of Aabsmun, son of Eos.
EDCS-06000923.summary: This inscription records Julius Nasif, a tribune, and lists his paternal lineage, possibly indicating his status and local importance.

EDCS-06000924.translation: Ainmus, son of Nimira, from Iarnvhan.
EDCS-06000924.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary marker for Ainmus, son of Nimira, with his tribal affiliation, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-06000926.translation: Flabius Isiguarus, a military tribune from Iarnvhan.
EDCS-06000926.summary: This inscription records Flabius Isiguarus, identified as a military tribune with his tribal affiliation, likely dating to the Roman military presence.

EDCS-06000931.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Naborsurnia avo sanu numero LXXX SATH MILIME DVO S NY FO\n
EDCS-06000931.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Naborsurnia, aged eighty years.
EDCS-06000931.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the departed for Naborsurnia, who lived to be eighty years old, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06000940.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate X imperatori XI consuli III patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate III consuli et Lucio Septimio GetaeCaesari Augusto per vexillationem legionis III Augustae\n
EDCS-06000940.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 10th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland; and for the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul; and for Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus, by the detachment of the Legio III Augusta.
EDCS-06000940.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, mentioning the Legio III Augusta and likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-06000941.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto tribunicia potestate IX imperatori XI consuli II patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate [IIII et Lucio] Septimio Getae Caesari Augusto Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augustorum consulari legionis III Augustae piae vindicis\n
EDCS-06000941.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, with tribunician power for the 9th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland; and for the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 4th time; and for Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus, by Quintus Annius Faustus, consular legate of the emperors and the Legio III Augusta, the pious and victorious.
EDCS-06000941.summary: This dedicatory inscription honors emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, naming the legate Quintus Annius Faustus and the Legio III Augusta, dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-06000943.text_edition: \n Quaesii multum quot sic memoriae tradere agens prae cunctos in hac castra milites votum communem sic proque reditu exercitus inter priores et futuros reddere dum quaero mecum digna divom nomina inveni tandem nomen et numen deae votis perennem sic quem sic dicare in hoc loco salutis igitur quandium sic cultores sient qua potui sanxi nomen et cunctis dedi veras salutis lymphas tantis ignibus in istis semper harenacis sic collibus nutantis austeri solis flammas fervidas tranquille ut nando delenirent corpora ita tu qui sentis magnam facti gratiam aestuantis animae fucilari sic spiritum noli pigere laudem voce reddere veram qui voluit esse te sanum tibi set sic protestare vel salutis gratia\n
EDCS-06000943.translation: Quest for much, to hand down to memory as it is, acting before all the soldiers in this camp, a common vow, to be repaid for the return of the army, both past and future, as I searched within myself for worthy names of the gods, I finally found the name and divine power of the goddess, an everlasting vow to be dedicated in this place of salvation. Therefore, as long as there are worshippers, by whatever means I could, I sanctioned the name and gave to all the true waters of salvation, so that those who sent flames in such fires on these sandy hills, gently assuaged their bodies in the burning sun, thus you who feel great gratitude for the deed, do not hesitate to render true praise with your voice, for who wanted you to be healthy, but to testify for salvation.
EDCS-06000943.summary: This is a lengthy votive inscription dedicated to a goddess for salvation and the safe return of the army, likely erected at a sanctuary or military site.

EDCS-06000945.text_edition: \n Iovi Hammoni reduci Augusti sacrum Tullius Romulus centurio ex maioriario praepositus vexillationi\n
EDCS-06000945.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Hammon the Returned, by Tullius Romulus, centurion from the larger unit, in command of a detachment.
EDCS-06000945.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Hammon the Returned by Tullius Romulus, a centurion in charge of a military detachment, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06000947.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Euasius qui et Calvana vixit annum I menses IIII hic situs\n
EDCS-06000947.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Euasius, also called Calvana, lived 1 year and 4 months. Buried here.
EDCS-06000947.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Euasius (Calvana), who died in infancy, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-06000949.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus tribuniciae potestatis tertium imperator V pontifex maximus et Caius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus pontes vetustate delapsos et iter longa iniuria corruptum restituerunt sua infaticabili sic providentia pervium commeantibus reddiderunt I\n
EDCS-06000949.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the third time, imperator for the fifth time, pontifex maximus, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, restored the bridges that had collapsed from age and the road corrupted by long neglect, and made it passable for travelers again with their tireless foresight.
EDCS-06000949.summary: This inscription records the restoration of bridges and a road by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximinus, dating to their joint rule in the 230s AD.

EDCS-06000951.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatae sic II consul milia V\n
EDCS-06000951.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, consul.
EDCS-06000951.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Tacitus, recording his titles and consular status, likely dating to his brief reign (275-276 AD).

EDCS-06000955.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Tiberi Caesaris Augusti iussu Lucius Aelius Lamia proconsul ab oppido in mediterraneum direxsit sic milia passuum XLIV\n
EDCS-06000955.translation: By order of Emperor Tiberius Caesar Augustus, the proconsul Lucius Aelius Lamia directed this road from the town inland for 44 miles.
EDCS-06000955.summary: This is a milestone inscription recording a road construction project initiated by Emperor Tiberius and carried out by the proconsul Lucius Aelius Lamia, dating to Tiberius' reign (14-37 AD).

EDCS-06000965.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio GENA Galenio sic Pio invicto PIP Augusto Pio ANMOX Parthico maximo IVGRIE ONSAI milia passuum LIII\n
EDCS-06000965.translation: To Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gena Gallienus Pius, unconquered, Augustus, Pius, of the Parthians, the greatest, etc., 53 miles.
EDCS-06000965.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Gallienus, listing his titles and indicating a distance of 53 miles, likely dating to his reign (253-268 AD).

EDCS-06000966.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis his pater patriae consul milia passuum numero LVII\n
EDCS-06000966.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, 57 miles.
EDCS-06000966.summary: This milestone inscription records Emperor Gordian III, listing his titles and indicating a distance of 57 miles, dating to his reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-06000968.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Britanici maximi filio divi Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani ab nepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurellio sic Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Britanico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XVIIII imperatori III consuli IIII patri patriae pro consuli miliarium numero LXXXII\n
EDCS-06000968.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurellius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 19th time, emperor for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, 82 miles, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, grand-nephew of the deified Hadrian, great-great-nephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva.
EDCS-06000968.summary: This extensive milestone inscription honors Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Caracalla), listing his numerous titles and achievements, and marking a distance of 82 miles, dating to his reign (198-217 AD).

EDCS-06000969.text_edition: \n Imperator Caisar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus miliarium numero LXXXII\n
EDCS-06000969.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, 82 miles.
EDCS-06000969.summary: This milestone inscription records Emperor Gordian III, listing his titles and indicating a distance of 82 miles, dating to his reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-06000970.text_edition: \n Perpetuo mperatori invicto mperatori fortissimo imperatori Lucio Domitio Aurelliano sic Pio Felici Augusto Germanico maximo Carpico maximo consuli ter patri patriae proconsule sic miliarium numero LXXXII\n
EDCS-06000970.translation: To the perpetual, unconquered, most powerful Emperor Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus Maximus, Carpicus Maximus, consul for the third time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, 82 miles.
EDCS-06000970.summary: This milestone inscription honors Emperor Aurelian, listing his titles and indicating a distance of 82 miles, dating to his reign (270-275 AD).

EDCS-06000980.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus invictus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II miliarium C \n
EDCS-06000980.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, unconquered, Pius, Felix Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, 100 miles.
EDCS-06000980.summary: This milestone inscription records Emperor Aurelian, listing his titles and indicating a distance of 100 miles, dating to his reign (270-275 AD).

EDCS-06000983.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Egnatio Gallienio sic invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo Parthico maximo Germanico tribunicia potestate X consuli V patri patriae proconsuli miliarium\n
EDCS-06000983.translation: To Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus, unconquered, Pius, Felix Augustus, chief priest, Parthicus Maximus, Germanicus, holding tribunician power for the 10th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-06000983.summary: This milestone inscription is dedicated to Emperor Gallienus, listing his titles and achievements, and likely marks a distance, dating to his reign (253-268 AD).

EDCS-06100521.text_edition: \n II io sacerdoti Antestus sic Burucus libertus et heres ec sic testamento eius ex decreto ordinis posuit\n
EDCS-06100521.translation: To the priest Antestus Burucus, his freedman and heir, erected this by decree of the council.
EDCS-06100521.summary: This is a funerary or honorary inscription for a priest named Antestus Burucus, set up by his freedman and heir, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06100522.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely a tombstone or dedication, recording a person named Crescentis, possibly indicating his parentage or a dedication to himself.

EDCS-06100523.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Spirits by Caecilius, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07000893.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Septimi Severi Adiabenici Parthici maximi Brittannici maximi filio divi Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Brittanico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis XVIII imperatori III consuli IIII I\n
EDCS-07000893.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Septimius Severus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, descendant of the deified Hadrian, later descendant of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 18th year, Imperator for the 3rd time, Consul for the 4th time. [---]
EDCS-07000893.summary: This is a long imperial titulature for Caracalla, listing his extensive honors and titles, probably from a public monument erected during his reign (circa 212-217 AD).

EDCS-07000898.text_edition: \n Publio Fabio Luci filio Q(uirina) Firmano legato consuli patrono civitatis senatus decreto pecunia publica\n
EDCS-07000898.translation: To Publius Fabio Firmano, son of Lucius, of the voting tribe Quirina, former consul, patron of the city, erected by public funds by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-07000898.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument or public work in honor of Publius Fabio Firmano, a former consul and patron of the city, funded by the state and decreed by the Senate, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07400705.summary: This short inscription indicates that Filoserapis is the author or creator of the object it is attached to, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400706.text_edition: \n Aulo Caecina Severo proconsule legio III Augusta IIII\n
EDCS-07400706.translation: To Aulus Caecina Severus, proconsul. Legio III Augusta [erected this].
EDCS-07400706.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Aulus Caecina Severus, a proconsul, by the III Augusta legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07400712.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus.
EDCS-07400712.summary: This is an inscription honoring Emperor Constantine I, addressing him as 'Dominus Noster' (Our Lord), dating to his reign (306-337 AD).

EDCS-07400715.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Iulio Constantio nobillissimo sic Caesari\n
EDCS-07400715.translation: To our lord Flavius Julius Constantius, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-07400715.summary: This inscription honors the Caesar Flavius Julius Constantius (likely Constantius II), addressing him as 'Dominus Noster' (Our Lord), dating to the Constantinian dynasty (mid-4th century AD).

EDCS-07400716.text_edition: \n Germanic tribunicia potestate Vconsul- II proconsul-) contstitui XXXXV\n
EDCS-07400716.translation: [erected by] Germanus, with tribunician power for the 5th year, consul for the 2nd, proconsul. [---] XXXXV
EDCS-07400716.summary: This inscription likely records a public work or dedication made by someone named Germanus, who held significant consular and tribunician titles, possibly dating to the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-07400718.text_edition: \n Ddominis nnostris Flavio Constantio nobilissimo Caesari\n
EDCS-07400718.translation: To our lords Flavius Constantinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-07400718.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantine I or one of his sons (likely Constantine II or Constantius II in their role as Caesar), addressing him as 'Dominus Noster' (Our Lord), dating to the Constantinian dynasty (early to mid-4th century AD).

EDCS-07400724.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Connstante sic Pio Felici Augusto miliarum LX\n
EDCS-07400724.translation: To our lord Flavius Constans, pious, happy Augustus. Sixty miles.
EDCS-07400724.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constans, referring to a distance of sixty miles, likely marking a road or milestone, dating to his reign (337-350 AD).

EDCS-07400725.text_edition: \n ------ tribunicia] potestate pater patriae consul proconsul a civitate Sucubucensis XIIII\n
EDCS-07400725.translation: holding the tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, from the town of Sucubucensis, the fourteenth [year].
EDCS-07400725.summary: This inscription lists imperial titles and a year, likely for an emperor, and mentions the town of Sucubucensis.

EDCS-07600465.text_edition: \n Geminia Lucana Geminaes sic filia Tertullaes sic vixit annos LXXXVI hic sita est ossa tua bene quiescant tibi terra levis sit\n
EDCS-07600465.translation: Gaius Julius Impetratus, dutiful. He lived 81 years.
EDCS-07600465.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Julius Impetratus, recording his name, a character attribute, and his age at death.

EDCS-07600466.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Impetratus pius vixit annis LXXXI\n
EDCS-07600466.translation: Gaius Julius Impetratus, dutiful, lived 81 years.
EDCS-07600466.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name, piety, and age at death of Gaius Julius Impetratus, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07600467.text_edition: \n Sextus Tullius Fuscus pius vixit annis LXX ex eius ffiliis duobus\n
EDCS-07600467.translation: Sextus Tullius Fuscus, dutiful. He lived 70 years; from his two sons.
EDCS-07600467.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sextus Tullius Fuscus, recording his name, a character attribute, his age at death, and his surviving sons.

EDCS-08000806.text_edition: \n [Lucillae Augustae] Imperatoris Antonini Augusti patris patriae filiae Imperatoris Luci Aureli Veri coniugi pagus Uchitanorum Maiorum decreto decurionum pecunia publica fecit Caio Mario Faustino Lucio Propertio Rogato magistris pagi\n
EDCS-08000806.translation: For Lucilla Augusta, daughter of Emperor Antoninus Augustus, father of the fatherland, wife of Emperor Lucius Aurelius Verus. The community of the Uchitani Maiorum, by decree of the decurions and at public expense, erected this monument, with Gaius Marius Faustinus and Lucius Properius Rogatus as magistrates of the community.
EDCS-08000806.summary: This is a dedication for Lucilla Augusta, wife of Marcus Aurelius Verus and daughter of Antoninus Pius, erected by the pagus Uchitanorum Maiorum.

EDCS-08000809.summary: This inscription likely records the name of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, possibly as a dedication or ownership marker.

EDCS-08201701.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatorum dddominorum nnnostrorum Auggustorum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini Britannici Parthici Germanici et Iuliae Augustae matris castrorum vexillatio legionis III Augustae piae vindicis quae Vezerei praetendit et Titus Genius Ianuarius optio VE immunis s inus us principalis Car Publilius centuria Erri Saturnini Iulius Iulius P Porcius Silvanus Granius Felix Aufidius Victorianus Varius Saturninus centuria Auli Suri Iusti Mundius Co centuria Valeri Proculi nnus Silvanus Silicius Urbanus Corn Rogatus Marius Marcellus Iulius Victor Sedius Felix Iulius Faustus Helvius Silvanus Valerius Rogatus Aurelius Severianus Iulius Primus principalis Aelius Saturn principalis Aelius Saturn sequtor sic Mussius Fuscinus Annius Quintus Flavius Victor Lurius Beatus Pomponius Silvanus Marcus Acilius Fortunatus Papirius Sallustianus Aelius Crescens Flavius Vibianus Aelius Vitalis Pontius Donatus Antistius Felix Safidius Ammon Cattius Ingenuus Iulius Primus sequtor sic Papius Fortun Pinarius Martialis Iulius Donatus Quintus Sulpicius Saturninus Scribonius Priscus Florius Rogatus centuria Flavi Victoris Pompeius Liberalis Euped sic Catullinus Flavius Victor centuria Maximi Annius Saturus Caius Aufidius Niger centuria Quinti Gelli Iuvenalis Aelius Iucundus attius Fortunatus cohortis III centuria Iuli Donatus Iulius Aemilius Victor Vitellius Ianuarius Flavius Proculus centuria Domiti Maximi Lucilius Iulianus Quintus Flavius Ianuarius Eppius Donatus Iulius Donatus Veturius Proculus Hostilius Salutaris Sulpicius Successus Clodius Antoninus Publius Clementi--- C Stertinius Iuvenalis Flavius Verecundus Agrius Felix Arrunt Maximus Caius Pompeius Donatus Silicius Quartus Cornelius Saturninus Geminius Rufus Flavius Saturninus Iulius Honoratus Fannius Catullinius Pomponius Novatianus Iunius Verissimus Gemini Fortunatus Petronius Felix Marcus Aemilius Felix Caius Pompeius Felix centuria Aureli Aucti Domitius Amo Dentilius Adiutor nnus Felix Aemilius Sa Geminius C Aurelius Iulius Iulius Iulius Iulius Statilius D [[]] [[]] DEM \n
EDCS-08201701.translation: For the safety of our lords Emperors Augustus, Lucius Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Britannicus Parthicus Germanicus, and Julia Augusta, mother of the camp. The detachment of the Third Augustan Legion, the dutiful, the victorious, which is stationed at Vezere and advances against the enemy, and Titus Genius Ianuarius, optio, immunis, principalis, from the century of Carus Publilius, centurion of Errius Saturninus, Julius Julius Porcius Silvanus, Granius Felix, Aufidius Victorianus, Varius Saturninus, from the century of Aulus Surus Iustus, Mundius Co, from the century of Valerius Proculus, nnus Silvanus, Silicius Urbanus, Corn Rogatus, Marius Marcellus, Julius Victor, Sedius Felix, Julius Faustus, Helvius Silvanus, Valerius Rogatus, Aurelius Severianus, Julius Primus, principalis, Aelius Saturn, principalis, Aelius Saturn, sequtor, from Mussius Fuscinus, Annius Quintus, Flavius Victor, Lurius Beatus, Pomponius Silvanus, Marcus Acilius Fortunatus, Papirius Sallustianus, Aelius Crescens, Flavius Vibianus, Aelius Vitalis, Pontius Donatus, Antistius Felix, Safidius Ammon, Cattius Ingenuus, Julius Primus, sequtor, from Papius Fortun, Pinarius Martialis, Julius Donatus, Quintus Sulpicius Saturninus, Scribonius Priscus, Florius Rogatus, from the century of Flavius Victor, Pompeius Liberalis, Euped, from Catullinus, Flavius Victor, from the century of Maximus, Annius Saturus, Gaius Aufidius Niger, from the century of Quintus Gellius Iuvenalis, Aelius Iucundus, attius Fortunatus, from the cohort III, from the century of Julius Donatus, Julius Aemilius Victor, Vitellius Ianuarius, Flavius Proculus, from the century of Domitius Maximus, Lucilius Iulianus, Quintus Flavius Ianuarius, Eppius Donatus, Julius Donatus, Veturius Proculus, Hostilius Salutaris, Sulpicius Successus, Clodius Antoninus, Publius Clementi[---], C. Stertinius Iuvenalis, Flavius Verecundus, Agrius Felix, Arrunt Maximus, Gaius Pompeius Donatus, Silicius Quartus, Cornelius Saturninus, Geminius Rufus, Flavius Saturninus, Julius Honoratus, Fannius Catullinius, Pomponius Novatianus, Junius Verissimus, Gemini Fortunatus, Petronius Felix, Marcus Aemilius Felix, Gaius Pompeius Felix, from the century of Aurelius Auctus, Domitius Amo, Dentilius Adiutor, nnus Felix, Aemilius Sa, Geminius C, Aurelius Julius, Julius Julius, Julius Statilius D [[]] [[]] DEM.
EDCS-08201701.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a detachment of the Third Augustan Legion and various soldiers for the well-being of the Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta (implied), and Julia Domna, dating to their co-reign.

EDCS-08201772.translation: Eusebia, brothers, a most rare and chaste wife, was here. She deserved to live a married life with me, as the times show. She lived sixteen years, eight months, and twenty days. God himself approved of her life, as I confess. An innocent wife, an example of the rarest sex. I, Successus, the archivist and her husband, implore you, brothers, to remember her always with your prayers.
EDCS-08201772.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Eusebia, written by her husband Successus, praising her virtues, recording her age at death, and asking for remembrance and prayers.

EDCS-08201775.translation: Arisus, in peace. Born at the sixth hour. Lived 9 scruples.
EDCS-08201775.summary: This is a very brief funerary epitaph for Arisus, indicating his name, status ('in peace'), time of birth, and a very short lifespan measured in scruples.

EDCS-08201776.translation: Diogas made this for his sweetest wives and sisters. The 5th day before the Kalends of February. Lord, Besse(?), Diogas.
EDCS-08201776.summary: This is a dedication by Diogas to his wives and sisters, with a date (January 28th) and a possible invocation or mention of a deity.

EDCS-0820182.translation: Fastidius lived 15 years, 7 months, and 15 days in peace.
EDCS-0820182.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of Fastidius, who died at the age of 15 years, 7 months, and 15 days.

EDCS-08201842.translation: Hermes to his dearest wife and son.
EDCS-08201842.summary: An epitaph for Hermes, dedicated to his wife and son.

EDCS-08201939.text_edition: \n Neptuno Augusto sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Antonini Augusti Pii patris patriae liberorumque eius Marcus Barigbalius Ghuddis filius Pheraditanus maius statuam cum ostiis ex HS V milibus nummum sua pecunia fecit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-08201939.translation: Marcus Barigbalius Ghuddis, son of Pheraditanus the elder, dedicated at his own expense a statue with doors to Neptune Augustus for the health of the Emperor Caesar Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, and his children, and dedicated it.
EDCS-08201939.summary: A dedication of a statue to Neptune Augustus by Marcus Barigbalius Ghuddis for the health of Emperor Antoninus Pius and his family, likely dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-08201940.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the Xth time, consul for the Xth time, proconsul.
EDCS-08201940.summary: A dedication to Emperor Constantius, listing his imperial titles and dignities, likely dating to the 4th century AD.

EDCS-08201944.text_edition: \n Didi Preiecti flamen perpetuus probatissimo adque sic integerrimo viro cuius multa praeclara venefactorum sic praemia retinentur quem adornat integritas quem fides vera conmendat sic a cuius cunabulis titulis obsequemtem sic probabimus sic liberalitatem et ita sumptu proprio indulgentem ut et fastigia moenibus dederit et colomina sic repararit quibus rebus Didio Preiecto flamini perpetuo amplissimo proceri nostrae curiae quem et laus familiae et eloqui conmendat sic instructio ordo splendissimae coloniaeDAM PRO DE\n
EDCS-08201944.translation: To Didius Praeiectus, perpetual priest, the most approved and most upright man, whose many outstanding rewards are retained by sorcerers [or: whose many outstanding deeds are remembered by wise men], who is adorned by integrity, who is commended by true faith, from whose cradle we shall test his obedience, his generosity, and his indulgence with his own expense to such an extent that he has both granted battlements to the walls and repaired the columns, by which deeds to Didius Praeiectus, perpetual priest, the most eminent, whom the praise of his family and eloquence adorn, the most splendid order of the colony recommends [him] from the family of DAM PRO DE.
EDCS-08201944.summary: A lengthy honorary inscription praising Didius Praeiectus, a perpetual priest, for his integrity, generosity, and public works, likely from a Roman colony in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-08201972.translation: Iulia Equitia Iuliana, an honorable woman, lived 84 years.
EDCS-08201972.summary: An epitaph for Iulia Equitia Iuliana, recording her age at death.

EDCS-08202032.translation: Iatuvatus willingly paid his vow to Pluto Augustus.
EDCS-08202032.summary: A votive offering to Pluto Augustus by Iatuvatus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08300554.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius ninus Achul Vuzale filius Masthan nepos vixit annis XXIX monimentum fecerunt athobbab et Imensis inpensis Thaneci filiae Sanmilc Lepcitanae item matris eorum |(denariis) CCL (ducentis miliis quinquaginta)\n
EDCS-08300554.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Flavius Ninus Achul, son of Vuzale, grandson of Masthan, lived 29 years. A monument was made by Athobbab and Imensis at the expense of Thanec, daughter of Sanmilc Lepcitana, and their mother, for 250 denarii.
EDCS-08300554.summary: A tombstone for Titus Flavius Ninus Achul, who died at 29, with details of the monument's cost and builders, likely from the Roman provinces in the Imperial period.

EDCS-08300556.text_edition: \n Iohannes fidelis in pace vixit annos LXXX depositus XIII Kalendas Octobres\n
EDCS-08300556.translation: Johannes the faithful lived 80 years in peace, buried on the 13th day before the Kalends of October.
EDCS-08300556.summary: An epitaph for Johannes, recording his lifespan and burial date, likely from a Christian context in the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-08300568.text_edition: \n Epifanius sic fidelis in pace vixit annis LX depositus IIII Nonas Sebtenbres sic\n
EDCS-08300568.translation: Epifanius the faithful lived 60 years in peace, buried on the 4th day before the Nones of September.
EDCS-08300568.summary: An epitaph for Epifanius, recording his lifespan and burial date, likely from a Christian context in the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-08300569.text_edition: \n Maria fidelis in pacae! fidelis in pacae sic vixit annis XXX \n
EDCS-08300569.translation: Maria, faithful in peace! She lived faithfully in peace for 30 years.
EDCS-08300569.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Maria, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-08300668.inscription_type: reges, termini, tituli honorarii, tituli sacri
EDCS-08300668.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pater patriae et Publius Septimius Geta [] castellanis Thudedensibus fines et immunitatem a rege Iuba per confirmatione divi Augusti concessos post multis maximisque saeculis felicissimis temporibus suis divino iudicio restituerunt Thudedenses determinatione saeculi terminos posuerunt et dedicaverunt feliciter\n
EDCS-08300668.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus pater patriae and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pater patriae and Publius Septimius Geta restored for the Thudedensian castellan community the boundaries and immunities granted by King Juba through the confirmation of the divine Augustus, after many and greatest centuries, during their most happy times by divine judgment; the Thudedenses erected boundary stones and dedicated them successfully.
EDCS-08300668.summary: An official inscription from the reign of Septimius Severus and Caracalla, confirming land rights and boundaries for the Thudedenses, a community of castellan farmers, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08400553.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Sextilius Donatus miles cohortis I urbanae militavit annos VI vixit annos XXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-08400553.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Sextilius Donatus, a soldier of the I Urbana cohort, served for 6 years, lived for 25 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-08400553.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Sextilius Donatus, a soldier who died at the age of 25 after 6 years of service.

EDCS-08400554.text_edition: \n TI perp IIII publica Africae Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Sextilius Donatus pius vixit annis LXXXX hic situs est\n
EDCS-08400554.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Sextilius Donatus, dutiful, lived for 90 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-08400554.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Sextilius Donatus, who lived to the age of 90.

EDCS-08400556.text_edition: \n Sexto Furnio Fausto Sulpiciano clarissimo puero\n
EDCS-08400556.translation: To Sextus Furnius Faustus Sulpicianus, a most distinguished boy.
EDCS-08400556.summary: A funerary dedication to a young boy named Sextus Furnius Faustus Sulpicianus.

EDCS-08400557.translation: Of Victor, freedman of the emperor.
EDCS-08400557.summary: A partial inscription likely referring to a freedman of the emperor named Victor.

EDCS-08400558.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Calpurnia Quartilla vixit annis LXXXVI mensibus IIII hic sita est Dis Manibus sacrum Selnius Optatus vixit annis LXXXVII mensibus VII hic situs est\n
EDCS-08400558.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Calpurnia Quartilla lived for 86 years and 4 months; she is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Selnius Optatus lived for 87 years and 7 months; he is buried here.
EDCS-08400558.summary: A double funerary inscription recording the deaths of Calpurnia Quartilla and Selnius Optatus.

EDCS-08400562.text_edition: \n Honeratus sic et Lucia qum sic filis suis votum quot sic fecerunt libentes animo solverunt\n
EDCS-08400562.translation: Honeratus and Lucia, together with their children, made this vow as they had vowed, and freely paid it with willing hearts.
EDCS-08400562.summary: A votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Honeratus and Lucia for themselves and their children.

EDCS-08500625.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti Taphrurenses templum Saturno impensa publica fecerunt curantibus \n
EDCS-08500625.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, the people of Taphrura built this temple to Saturn at public expense, under the care of...
EDCS-08500625.summary: A public dedication of a temple to Saturn by the people of Taphrura, likely during the reign of Hadrian.

EDCS-08500628.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aufidia dia pia mater vixit annis LX\n
EDCS-08500628.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aufidia, pious mother, lived for 60 years.
EDCS-08500628.summary: A funerary inscription for Aufidia, a mother who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-08500629.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed.
EDCS-08500629.summary: This is a short funerary dedication to the Manes.

EDCS-08500631.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Lappius Saturninus pius vixit annis XXXVII Lappia Felicitas vixit annis XVI\n
EDCS-08500631.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Lappius Saturninus, dutiful, lived for 37 years. Lappia Felicitas lived for 16 years.
EDCS-08500631.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lives of Quintus Lappius Saturninus and Lappia Felicitas, likely a family memorial.

EDCS-08500632.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Cezzonia sic Lucaniane havete Zasgia sic Victoria Cezzonia sic bene merens femina pia vixit nnos XXXV menses VIII dies XVI Lucius Turius Fabius Lucanianus filius eius pius vixit nnos IIII menses IIII dies XVIIII hic siti sunt titulo usi bene quiescant\n
EDCS-08500632.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Cezzonia, called Lucaniane, greetings! Zasgia, greetings! Victoria Cezzonia, greetings! A well-deserving, dutiful woman, lived 35 years, 8 months, and 16 days. Lucius Turius Fabius Lucanianus, her son, dutiful, lived 4 years, 4 months, and 19 days. They are buried here, having used this tombstone; may they rest well.
EDCS-08500632.summary: A detailed funerary inscription for Cezzonia and her son Lucius Turius Fabius Lucanianus, providing their ages and emphasizing their virtues.

EDCS-08500633.text_edition: \n Calpurnia Quinti filia Gemina pia vixit annos LX Lucius Calpurnius S\n
EDCS-08500633.translation: Calpurnia Gemina, daughter of Quintus, dutiful, lived 60 years. Lucius Calpurnius S...
EDCS-08500633.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription recording the life of Calpurnia Gemina, daughter of Quintus.

EDCS-08500634.text_edition: \n Sextus Terentius Saturninus Donnicianus et Bommia Saturnina Terentilla titulos sibi fecerunt et dedicaverunt\n
EDCS-08500634.translation: Sextus Terentius Saturninus Donnicianus and Bommia Saturnina Terentilla made and dedicated this monument for themselves.
EDCS-08500634.summary: A self-funded funerary inscription indicating that Sextus Terentius Saturninus Donnicianus and Bommia Saturnina Terentilla prepared their own tomb.

EDCS-08500635.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Sentius Namphamonis pius vixit annis LXI Fortunata filia pia vixit annis XV Dis Manibus sacrum Sentius Namfamo sic Tires pius vixsit sic annis XXII\n
EDCS-08500635.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sentius Namphamonis, dutiful, lived 61 years. Fortunata, daughter, dutiful, lived 15 years. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sentius Namfamo Tires, dutiful, lived 22 years.
EDCS-08500635.summary: A funerary inscription honoring three individuals: Sentius Namphamonis, his daughter Fortunata, and Sentius Namfamo Tires, likely part of the same family.

EDCS-08500637.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Saturnina M Zandulis filia vixit nnos LXXV hic sita est Dis Manibus sacrum Rufus Senlisi filius pius vixit nnos LXXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-08500637.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Saturnina, daughter of M. Zandulis, lived 75 years; she is buried here. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Rufus, son of Senlis, dutiful, lived 85 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-08500637.summary: A funerary inscription recording the burial and ages of Saturnina and Rufus, possibly related individuals.

EDCS-08500638.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Nasidia Concessa pia vixit annis LXI hic sita est\n
EDCS-08500638.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Nasidia Concessa, dutiful, lived 61 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-08500638.summary: A funerary inscription stating that Nasidia Concessa, a dutiful woman, lived for 61 years and is buried at this location.
