EDCS-08500639.translation: Caius Sempronius Ianuarius and Petronia Proba.
EDCS-08500639.summary: This is a very brief inscription likely indicating the burial or memorial of Caius Sempronius Ianuarius and Petronia Proba.

EDCS-08500641.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Bommia Luci filia Saturnina pia vixit annos hic sita est\n
EDCS-08500641.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Bommia Saturnina, daughter of Lucius, dutiful, lived... is buried here.
EDCS-08500641.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription for Bommia Saturnina, daughter of Lucius.

EDCS-08500642.text_edition: \n Titus Volusenus Potitus Rufiani filius pius vixit annos LXVII hic situs est Geminia Secunda inpensis suis fecit\n
EDCS-08500642.translation: Titus Volusenus Potitus, son of Rufianus, lived 67 years. He is buried here. Geminia Secunda had this made at her own expense.
EDCS-08500642.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span of Titus Volusenus Potitus and noting his burial by Geminia Secunda.

EDCS-08500643.text_edition: \n Perellia Fortunata Luci Perelli Luperci contubernalis Lucius Cornelius Saturninus A \n
EDCS-08500643.translation: Perellia Fortunata, consort of Lucius Perellius Lupercus, [and] Lucius Cornelius Saturninus.
EDCS-08500643.summary: An incomplete inscription that appears to be a funerary epitaph or a dedication by Perellia Fortunata and Lucius Cornelius Saturninus.

EDCS-08500644.text_edition: \n Apollini Patrio Augusto Sextus Iulius Possessor praefectus cohortis Gallorum curator numeri Syrorum sagittariorum item alae primae Hispanorum tribunus militum legionis XII fulminatae adlectus in decurias ab optimis maximisque Impperatoribus Antonino et Vero Auggustis adiutor praefecti annonae ad horrea Ostiensia et Portuensia procurator Augusti ad ripam Baetis procurator Augusti Ostis ad annonam procurator Augusti Alexandriae ad Mercurium statuam aheneam transmare advectam dono dedit\n
EDCS-08500644.translation: To Apollo the Father, Augustus, Sextus Julius Possessor, prefect of the Gallic cohort, curator of the unit of Syrian archers, also tribune of soldiers in the first Spanish ala and the twelfth Fulminata legion, appointed to the decuries by the best and greatest Emperors Antoninus and Verus Augusti, assistant prefect of the grain supply at the Ostian and Portian granaries, procurator Augusti on the bank of the Baetis, procurator Augusti at Ostia for the grain supply, procurator Augusti in Alexandria, dedicated a bronze statue of Mercury brought across the sea.
EDCS-08500644.summary: A dedication by Sextus Julius Possessor, a high-ranking official with extensive military and administrative experience, to Apollo and Mercury, detailing his career and the dedication of a statue.

EDCS-08600646.text_edition: \n Imperatori Tito Aelio Antonino Caesari Augusto templum Minervae Augustae sacravit Apisa minus ex arca publlica fecerunt sufetes Macer et Baliato\n
EDCS-08600646.translation: To Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus Caesar Augustus, they dedicated a temple to Minerva Augusta. Apisa minus had this made from the public treasury; the sufetes Macer and Baliato.
EDCS-08600646.summary: A dedication of a temple to Minerva Augusta, funded by Apisa minus and executed by the sufetes Macer and Baliato, to Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-08600647.text_edition: \n Plutoni Augusto sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Commodus! Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Sarmatici Germanici maximi patris patriae civitas AbbiritanaLICVIERIII AFCTA ampliavit VSILERIIA per Natalem llisii et Namphamonem Gudis sufetes pecunia publica fecerunt\n
EDCS-08600647.translation: Sacred to Pluto Augustus. For the health of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Sarmaticus Germanicus Maximus, father of the fatherland, the Abbiritan civic community enlarged [the temple/building] for the sixth time. The sufetes Natalis and Namphamon, sons of Gudis, made this with public money.
EDCS-08600647.summary: A dedication to Pluto Augustus by the Abbiritan community for the health of Marcus Aurelius Commodus, noting public funding and the sufetes Natalis and Namphamon.

EDCS-08600648.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Paris Igilgili vixit annos XXXV hic est situs\n
EDCS-08600648.translation: Caius Iulius Paris Igilgili lived 35 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-08600648.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span and burial of Caius Iulius Paris Igilgili.

EDCS-08600649.text_edition: \n Lucius Iulius Mustus vixit annos LXXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-08600649.translation: Lucius Iulius Mustus lived 85 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-08600649.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span and burial of Lucius Iulius Mustus.

EDCS-08600650.text_edition: \n Publius Iulius Ianuarius vixit annis XXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-08600650.translation: Publius Iulius Ianuarius lived 35 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-08600650.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span and burial of Publius Iulius Ianuarius.

EDCS-08600652.text_edition: \n Numini Augusti et Veneri Augustae sacrum Augusti libertus procurator telonei maritumi de sua pecunia fecit\n
EDCS-08600652.translation: Sacred to the Divine Augustus and Augusta Venus. An imperial freedman, procurator of maritime tolls, made this from his own money.
EDCS-08600652.summary: A religious dedication by an imperial freedman serving as a procurator of maritime tolls, made at his own expense.

EDCS-08600653.text_edition: \n Saturno Domitius Saturninus soliarius palmis argenteis ex libris IIII votum solvit\n
EDCS-08600653.translation: To Saturn, Domitius Saturninus the sandal-maker fulfilled his vow with silver palms weighing 4 pounds.
EDCS-08600653.summary: A votive offering to Saturn by Domitius Saturninus, a sandal-maker, for silver palms weighing 4 pounds.

EDCS-08600655.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Fabius Antistianus pius vixit annis XVIIII ossa tibi bene quiescant\n
EDCS-08600655.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Quintus Fabius Antistianus, dutiful, lived 19 years. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-08600655.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Quintus Fabius Antistianus, who lived 19 years.

EDCS-08600656.text_edition: \n IV sacrum Marcus Salluvius Hamillus de sua pecunia\n
EDCS-08600656.translation: Sacred. Marcus Salluvius Hamillus, from his own funds.
EDCS-08600656.summary: A dedication made by Marcus Salluvius Hamillus at his own expense.

EDCS-08600663.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus Pius Felix Augustus fecerunt coloni loci Legum Maiorum et dono dederunt\n
EDCS-08600663.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, made and dedicated this with the colonists of the place called Leges Maiorum.
EDCS-08600663.summary: An inscription recording a dedication by Emperor Tacitus and the colonists of Leges Maiorum.

EDCS-08600666.text_edition: \n Marco Asellio Rogato patri piissimo Marcus Asellius Mummius\n
EDCS-08600666.translation: To Marcus Asellius Rogatus, most dutiful father, Marcus Asellius Mummius [dedicated this].
EDCS-08600666.summary: A dedication by Marcus Asellius Mummius to his most dutiful father, Marcus Asellius Rogatus.

EDCS-08600668.text_edition: \n Quinto Calpurnio Honorato Quintus Calpurnius Ianuarius filio piissimo\n
EDCS-08600668.translation: To Quintus Calpurnius Honoratus, [dedicated by] Quintus Calpurnius Ianuarius, his most dutiful son.
EDCS-08600668.summary: A dedication by Quintus Calpurnius Ianuarius to his most dutiful son, Quintus Calpurnius Honoratus.

EDCS-08600671.text_edition: \n Pinariae Publi filiae Extricatae Sextus Valerius Fuscinus augur Thevestini filius matri piissimae item Publio Valerio Fuscino filio\n
EDCS-08600671.translation: To Pinaria Extricata, daughter of Publius, from Sextus Valerius Fuscinus, augur of Theveste, son, to his most dutiful mother; also to Publius Valerius Fuscinus, son.
EDCS-08600671.summary: A dedication by Sextus Valerius Fuscinus to his mother Pinaria Extricata and his brother Publius Valerius Fuscinus.

EDCS-08600672.text_edition: \n Lucio Marcio Luci filio Quirina Feroci flamini Augggustorum marito Iuliana eius\n
EDCS-08600672.translation: To Lucius Marcius Ferox, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, flamen of the Augusti, and to her husband, [this was dedicated by] Iuliana.
EDCS-08600672.summary: A dedication by Iuliana to her husband Lucius Marcius Ferox, flamen of the Augusti.

EDCS-08600674.translation: He made [this] to Felicis, dearest son.
EDCS-08600674.summary: A dedication made to a beloved son named Felicis.

EDCS-08600675.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aponi A Mai Helvia \n
EDCS-08600675.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. From Helvia [dedicated to] Aponius.
EDCS-08600675.summary: A funerary dedication made by Helvia to Aponius.

EDCS-08600676.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Exupius bonus vir vixit annos XXVII menses VII dies \n
EDCS-08600676.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Tiberius Claudius Exupius, a good man, lived 27 years, 7 months, [and X] days.
EDCS-08600676.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Tiberius Claudius Exupius, who lived 27 years, 7 months, and an incomplete number of days.

EDCS-08600678.text_edition: \n Lucius Munatius Pastor vixit annos XXXV Talincia uxor marito fecit\n
EDCS-08600678.translation: Lucius Munatius Pastor lived 35 years. His wife Talincia made this for her husband.
EDCS-08600678.summary: A tombstone recording the life of Lucius Munatius Pastor, erected by his wife.

EDCS-08600679.text_edition: \n Alto labsa sic loco casu defuncta nefando primis rapta annis istic Fidentia dormit cui pater hunc tumulum sibi haec solacia fecit Fidentius filiae\n
EDCS-08600679.translation: Fidentia, snatched away in her earliest years by a terrible accident in this place, sleeps here. Fidentius the father made this tomb for his daughter as a solace.
EDCS-08600679.summary: A tombstone recording the premature death of Fidentia, dedicated by her father Fidentius.

EDCS-08600806.text_edition: \n Iulius Burdigala ritus miles legionis III Augustae quui sic vixit annos militavit annos centuria Marcelli\n
EDCS-08600806.translation: Iulius Burdigala, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, lived 32 years and served 14 years in the century of Marcellus.
EDCS-08600806.summary: A tombstone for Iulius Burdigala, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, indicating his age and service length.

EDCS-08600810.translation: Quintus Fabius Victor, a veteran of the Third Augustan Legion, lived 71 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-08600810.summary: A tombstone for Quintus Fabius Victor, a veteran of the Third Augustan Legion, stating his age and burial place.

EDCS-08600811.text_edition: \n Quinto Faianio Clementi militi legionis III Augustae centuria Atrocis vixit annis XLIIII militavit annis XXIIII Marcus Cornelius centuria Sextili heres fecit hic situs est\n
EDCS-08600811.translation: Quinto Faianio Clementi, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion in the century of Atrocius, lived 44 years and served 24 years. Marcus Cornelius, of the century of Sextilius, made this as heir. He is buried here.
EDCS-08600811.summary: A tombstone for Quinto Faianio Clementi, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, erected by his heir Marcus Cornelius.

EDCS-08600812.text_edition: \n Gallico militi legionis III Augustae centuria Volusi militavit nnis XXVIIII vixit annis LVIIII hic situs est Marcus Cornelius Martialis commilito et amicus centuria Cluenti de sua pecunia fecit pro meritis Gallici amici\n
EDCS-08600812.translation: Gallicus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion in the century of Volusius, served 29 years and lived 59 years. He is buried here. Marcus Cornelius Martialis, a fellow soldier and friend from the century of Cluentius, made this from his own money for the merits of his friend Gallicus.
EDCS-08600812.summary: A tombstone for Gallicus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, erected by a fellow soldier and friend who funded it out of appreciation.

EDCS-08600813.text_edition: \n Quintus Geminius Quinti filius Polianus Thunusida eques centuria Catonis vixit annis XXXVI militavit annis IIX hic situs est\n
EDCS-08600813.translation: Quintus Geminius Polianus, son of Quintus, of the Thunusid tribe, an equestrian, in the century of Caton, lived 36 years and served 8 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-08600813.summary: A tombstone for Quintus Geminius Polianus, an equestrian, detailing his age, service length, and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-08600814.text_edition: \n Marcus Sempronius Marci filius Galeria Lugudunensi miles legionis III Augustae centuria Ex vixit annos XXV militavit annos V hic situs est Titus Baronius centuria Sallusti heres posuit\n
EDCS-08600814.translation: Marcus Sempronius Galerus, son of Marcus, of the tribe Galeria, from Lugudunum, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion in the century of Ex, lived 25 years and served 5 years. He is buried here. Titus Baronius, of the century of Sallustius, placed this as heir.
EDCS-08600814.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Sempronius Galerus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, erected by his heir Titus Baronius.

EDCS-08600815.text_edition: \n Lucius Tadius Cnaei filius Arnensi domo Carthagine miles legionis III Augustae vixit nnos XLVI militavit nnos XXV hic situs est curam egit Cassius Carus amicus post mortem\n
EDCS-08600815.translation: Lucius Tadius, son of Cnaeus, from Arna, originally from Carthage, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, lived 46 years and served 25 years. He is buried here. His friend Cassius Carus took care of this after his death.
EDCS-08600815.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Tadius, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion from Carthage, erected by his friend Cassius Carus after his death.

EDCS-08600919.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Vespasiani Caesaris Augusti patris patriae fines provinciae novae et veteris derecti qua fossa regia fuit per Rutilium Gallicum consulem pontificem et Sentium Caecilianum praetorem legatos Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-08600919.translation: By the authority of Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus, father of the country, the boundaries of the new and old provinces were demarcated, where the royal ditch was, under Rutilius Gallicus, consul and pontifex, and Sentius Caecilianus, praetor, legates of the emperor.
EDCS-08600919.summary: An official inscription recording the demarcation of provincial boundaries under Emperors Vespasian, Rutilius Gallicus, and Sentius Caecilianus, likely from the Flavian period.

EDCS-08600960.text_edition: \n Publio Attio Anniano Iuliano Publi filio Arnensi flamini divi Titi coloniae Iuliae Karthaginis sacerdoti Aesculapi bis equo publico adlecto ab Impperatoribus Caessaribus Marco Aurelio Antonino et Marco Aurelio Commodo Antonino Auggustis Sarmaticis Germanicis ob pietatis flamin--- mque N locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-08600960.translation: To Publius Attius Annianus Iulianus, son of Publius, of the Arnensian tribe, priest of the deified Titus of the colony Julia Karthago, priest of Aesculapius, twice enrolled by the equites by the Emperors Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augusti Sarmaticus Germanicus for his piety and priesthood, a place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08600960.summary: This inscription records the granting of a public space to Publius Attius Annianus Iulianus, a prominent local official in Karthago, likely dating to the co-reign of Marcus Aurelius and Commodus.

EDCS-08601077.text_edition: \n Publius Valerius Alexa Publi libertus pius vixit nnos LXX sacerdos Cererum hic situs est nni CV Luci filia Valeria Secunda pia fuit in suis vixit nnos LXX hic sita est\n
EDCS-08601077.translation: Publius Valerius Alexa, freedman of Publius, pious, lived 70 years, priest of Ceres. Here lies Lucius, son of Anna, Valeria Secunda, pious, was his. She lived 70 years, here she lies.
EDCS-08601077.summary: This funerary inscription records the lives and burial of Publius Valerius Alexa, a priest of Ceres, and Valeria Secunda, with their ages and familial connections, in the Roman colony of Karthago.

EDCS-08601123.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Caeletharithus Faustinianus lived 18 and a half years. He lies here.
EDCS-08601123.summary: This is a short funerary epitaph for Caius Caeletharithus Faustinianus, who died at the age of 18, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08601303.text_edition: \n Papiae Quinti filiae Novellae Cai Iuli Commodi Orfitiani legati Augusti pro praetore patroni coloniae decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-08601303.translation: To Papias, daughter of Quintus Novella, of Caius Iulius Commodus Orfitianus, legate of the emperor, governor of the colony, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-08601303.summary: This inscription honors Papias Novella, daughter of Quintus, for whom public funds were used to erect a monument, likely a tomb, in recognition of her association with the governor Caius Iulius Commodus Orfitianus in the colony of Karthago.

EDCS-08601312.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marco Praecilio Secundo Cai filio vixit annis LXX Praecili Rogatus et Saturninus patri karissimo fecerunt\n
EDCS-08601312.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Praecilius Secundus, son of Caius, lived 70 years. Praecilius Rogatus and Saturninus made this for their dearest father.
EDCS-08601312.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marcus Praecilius Secundus at age 70 and is dedicated by his sons, Rogatus and Saturninus, in the colony of Karthago.

EDCS-08601345.text_edition: \n Aulo Gabinio Auli filio Arnensi Dato patrono pagi et civitatis Thuggensium flamini divi Titi aedili auguri coloniae Concordiae Iuliae Karthaginis equo publico in quinque decuriis ab Imperatore Caesare Traiano Hadriano Augusto adlecto\n
EDCS-08601345.translation: To Aulus Gabinius, son of Aulus, of the Arnensian tribe, Dato, patron of the district and of the citizenship of the Thuggenses, priest of the deified Titus, aedile and augur of the colony Concordia Iulia Karthago, enrolled by the emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus among the equites in the group of the five decuries.
EDCS-08601345.summary: This inscription honors Aulus Gabinius Dato, a significant local official and priest in Karthago who was also recognized by Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to the Hadrianic period.

EDCS-08601517.translation: Publia Ogulnia made this eternal home for Gaius Attius Felix Postumus, her husband; for they lived long together, equal in fortune, flourishing together in many years.
EDCS-08601517.summary: This funerary inscription records Publia Ogulnia dedicating an eternal home for her husband, Gaius Attius Felix Postumus, emphasizing their long and harmonious marriage.

EDCS-08900650.text_edition: \n Caio Selio Rufo Quinto Ostorio Scapula consulibus Marcus Papius Rogatus \n
EDCS-08900650.translation: In the consulship of Gaius Silius Rufus and Quintus Ostorius Scapula, Marcus Papius Rogatus [erected this].
EDCS-08900650.summary: This is a dating reference within an inscription, noting the year by the consuls Gaius Silius Rufus and Quintus Ostorius Scapula, and mentioning Marcus Papius Rogatus.

EDCS-08900651.translation: In the consulship of Volasenna [erected this].
EDCS-08900651.summary: This inscription is a dating fragment, referencing the year by the consul Volasenna.

EDCS-08900656.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Felix Nargudianus pius vixit annis LXX hic situs est ossa tibi bene quiescant terra tibi levis sit\n
EDCS-08900656.translation: Gaius Iulius Felix Nargudianus, pious, lived 70 years. He lies here. May your bones rest well, may the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-08900656.summary: This is a standard funerary epitaph for Gaius Iulius Felix Nargudianus, who lived 70 years, including common wishes for the deceased's peace.

EDCS-08900659.translation: The owner of the estate established the temple of Caelestis.
EDCS-08900659.summary: A landowner erected a temple dedicated to the goddess Caelestis.

EDCS-08900661.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Peculiaris servus actor \n
EDCS-08900661.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Peculiaris, the steward.
EDCS-08900661.summary: A funerary inscription for Peculiaris, a freedman serving as a steward.

EDCS-08901175.text_edition: \n salutem a Geminio Crescente unum asinum cuis sic nobis attulisti Barlas decimum Calendas emit asinum valias sic opto te bene valere\n
EDCS-08901175.translation: Greetings from Geminius Crescens. You brought us one donkey, for which Barlas paid ten, bought the donkey. I wish you well.
EDCS-08901175.summary: A personal letter, likely on a wax tablet, concerning the purchase of a donkey and general well-being.

EDCS-08901176.text_edition: \n VIIII Kalendas Ianuarias numerus LVII in his librarius I optio I proculcator I equites VIII quintanari XXII de speculis I ad portam I ad praepositum I structor I aegri III Sulpicius Donatus Titus Buzuris Aurelius Rufus ad virgas I religui sic repungent XVII furnus XV balneus II\n
EDCS-08901176.translation: On the ninth day before the Kalends of January, a contingent of 57 men, including one scribe, one optio, one proculcator, eight equites, twenty-two quintanarii, one from the watch posts, one at the gate, one engineer, three sick, Sulpicius Donatus, Titus Buzuris, Aurelius Rufus, reporting for duty; the rest, 17, are to be compensated. Seventeen for the bakery, fifteen for the baths.
EDCS-08901176.summary: A military or administrative document detailing personnel numbers and duties, possibly related to rations or pay, from an unspecified date.

EDCS-08901177.text_edition: \n Octavio Festho sic decurioni praeposito meo Aemilius Aemilianus miles salutem transmisi at sic te domine per Macargum siddipia tridici dua quae fiunt viginti quattuor consulibus futuris post Thusco et Basso consulibus accepta XII Kalendas Februarias IT I\n
EDCS-08901177.translation: Aemilius Aemilianus, a soldier, sends greetings to my commander, Octavius Festhus. But, master, through Macargus, I send twenty-four siddipia (a type of garment or cloth) which is twenty-four. Consuls elect, after Tuscus and Bassus were consuls. Received on the twelfth day before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-08901177.summary: A soldier writes to his superior, likely regarding the delivery of goods, with a specific date reference.

EDCS-08901178.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Canapphari Augusto pro salute et incolumitate domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris divi Septimi Severi nepotis divi Magni Antonini filii Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Invicti Pii Felicis Augusti pontificis maximi tribuniciae potestatis IIII consulis patris patriae et Iuliae Mammeae Augustae matris Augusti nostri et castrorum totiusque domus divinae per vexillationem legionis III Augustae piae vindicis Severianae curante Tito Flavio Aproniano |(centurione) legionis eiusdem praeposito vexillationis\n
EDCS-08901178.translation: To the god Mars Canappharius Augustus, for the safety and well-being of our lord the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, grandson of the deified Septimius Severus, son of the deified Macrinus Antoninus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the fourth time, father of the country, and of Julia Mammea Augusta, mother of our Augustus and of the entire camp and of the divine household, by the detachment of the III Augustan Pious Vindicating Severian Legion, under the supervision of Titus Flavius Apronianus, centurion of the same legion and commander of the detachment.
EDCS-08901178.summary: A dedication to the god Mars by a military unit under the command of Titus Flavius Apronianus, honoring Emperor Severus Alexander and his mother Julia Mammea, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-08901189.text_edition: \n Publio Ennio Titi filio Quirina Saturnino Karo clarissimo viro aedili curuli designato ab actis senatus quaestori urbano flamini perpetuo alumno civi patrono municipii decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-08901189.translation: By public money, according to a decree of the decurions, for Publius Ennius Saturninus Karo, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe, a most distinguished man, designated curule aedile, quaestor urbanus, perpetual flamen, alumnus, citizen, and patron of the municipality.
EDCS-08901189.summary: A public monument or honor erected from municipal funds to commemorate Publius Ennius Saturninus Karo, who held various important civic and religious offices.

EDCS-08901190.text_edition: \n Ex indulgentia Imperatoris Caesaris Hadriani Augusti patris patriae terminus leugae coloniae Canopitanae III milia passuum\n
EDCS-08901190.translation: By the generosity of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus, father of the country, the boundary marker of the colony of Canopus, three Roman miles.
EDCS-08901190.summary: A milestone indicating the distance from the Roman colony of Canopus, erected through the emperor's beneficence.

EDCS-09001575.text_edition: \n Saturno Augusto sacrum Caius Calvissius Bullus votum solvit\n
EDCS-09001575.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Gaius Calvissius Bullus fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09001575.summary: A votive inscription to the god Saturn by Gaius Calvissius Bullus.

EDCS-09001616.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Claudio Augusto consuli III\n
EDCS-09001616.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Augustus, in his third consulship.
EDCS-09001616.summary: An inscription honoring Emperor Claudius Gothicus, likely marking a building or public work.

EDCS-09001617.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Valerio Constantio nobilissimo Caesari \n
EDCS-09001617.translation: To our lord, Flavius Valerius Constantius, the most noble Caesar.
EDCS-09001617.summary: This inscription honors Flavius Valerius Constantius, addressed as Caesar, likely dating to the Tetrarchy or early Constantinian period.

EDCS-09001619.text_edition: \n Imperatori domino nostro Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari milia passuum\n
EDCS-09001619.translation: To our lord Imperator Marcus Julius Philippus, the most noble Caesar, miles.
EDCS-09001619.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Julius Philippus, addressed as Caesar and likely indicating a milestone marking distance.

EDCS-09001620.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus Caessaribus Publio Licinio Valeriano et Licinio Gallieno Invictis Auggustis IIII\n
EDCS-09001620.translation: To the Emperors and Caesars, Publius Licinius Valerianus and Licinius Gallienus, the unconquered Augusti, for the fourth time.
EDCS-09001620.summary: This inscription honors the co-emperors Valerian and Gallienus, indicating a consulship or other significant civic honor during their reign in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09200547.text_edition: \n [Aureliae Galeriae] [Lucillae Augustae] Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Aureli Veri Augusti Armeniaci Parthici Maximi Medici decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-09200547.translation: To Aurelia Galeria Lucilla Augusta, by decree of the decurions and at public expense.
EDCS-09200547.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Aurelia Galeria Lucilla Augusta, wife of Lucius Aurelius Verus, likely erected by the local town council.

EDCS-09200548.text_edition: \n Aureliae Sabinae Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aurelii Antonini Augusti Armeniaci Parthici Medici filiae decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-09200548.translation: To Aurelia Sabina, daughter of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armenicus Parthicus Maximus Medicus, by decree of the decurions and at public expense.
EDCS-09200548.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Aurelia Sabina, daughter of Marcus Aurelius, likely erected by the local town council.

EDCS-09200550.text_edition: \n Sabiniae Tranquillinae Augustae coniugi domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Antoni Gordi[ani Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti ------\n
EDCS-09200550.translation: To Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, wife of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, [died...].
EDCS-09200550.summary: This inscription honors Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, wife of Gordian III, likely erected to commemorate her status or death.

EDCS-09200552.text_edition: \n iuridico in! Piceno et Apuliam sic legato legionis XXII Primigeniae Decianae legato legionis XIII geminae Decianae praefecto aerarii Saturni consuli\n
EDCS-09200552.translation: As legal official in Picenum and Apulia, so as legate of the 22nd Legion Primigenia Deciana, as legate of the 13th Legion Gemina Deciana, as prefect of the Saturnian treasury, as consul.
EDCS-09200552.summary: This inscription lists the various high-ranking offices held by an individual, including military commands and political appointments, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09200555.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lollius Munnas civis Belalitanus pius vixit annis LXXXX hic situs est\n
EDCS-09200555.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Lollius Munnas, a citizen of Belalita, pious, lived 90 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-09200555.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lollius Munnas, a citizen of Belalita, recording his piety and age at death.

EDCS-09200556.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to be a funerary dedication or a record of parentage, but is too incomplete to fully decipher.

EDCS-09200558.text_edition: \n Marco Lurio Marci filio Arnensi Fausto Caeciliano equo publico adlecto praefecto cohortis II Bracaraugustanorum \n
EDCS-09200558.translation: To Marcus Lurius Faustus, son of Marcus, of the Arnensian tribe, equo publico adlecto, prefect of the Second Cohort of Bracaraugustani.
EDCS-09200558.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Lurius Faustus, detailing his Roman citizenship status, military command, and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-09200559.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio Felicis Arnensis fili sui\n
EDCS-09200559.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of Felix Arnensis.
EDCS-09200559.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-09200560.text_edition: \n Publio Thermestino Crescenti Caeciliano decurioni coloniae Concordiae Iuliae Karthaginis flamini pe[rpetuo ------\n
EDCS-09200560.translation: To Publius Thermestinus Crescens Caecilianus, decurion of the colony of Concordia Julia Karthago, perpetual flamen...
EDCS-09200560.summary: This is a dedication to Publius Thermestinus Crescens Caecilianus, a local official in Roman Africa, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09200561.text_edition: \n ius Lucius Sc Ianuarius ob honorem magistratus \n
EDCS-09200561.translation: ...Lucius Sc Ianuarius for the honor of the magistracy.
EDCS-09200561.summary: This fragment records Lucius Sc Ianuarius being honored for holding a magistracy, likely in Roman Africa during the Imperial period.

EDCS-09200569.text_edition: \n Dio sic Mercurio Thermesi Augusto sacrum\n
EDCS-09200569.translation: Sacred to Mercury Thermesis Augustus by Dio.
EDCS-09200569.summary: This is a religious dedication to Mercury by Dio, likely from Roman Africa during the Imperial period.

EDCS-09200570.text_edition: \n Ex iusso sic Iovis Zabazi ara posuit Libero Patri cura Marci Oppi Vitali\n
EDCS-09200570.translation: By order, this altar to Jupiter Zabazus was set up by Libero Patri, under the care of Marcus Oppius Vitalis.
EDCS-09200570.summary: This inscription records the erection of an altar to Jupiter Zabazus, supervised by Marcus Oppius Vitalis, possibly from Roman Africa.

EDCS-09300556.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, with tribunician power for the 9th time, hailed as emperor, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, and to Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus, by Quintus Anicius Faustus, consular legate of the Augusti, [legate of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion].
EDCS-09300556.summary: This is a dedication to the emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, erected by Quintus Anicius Faustus, a high-ranking official, likely in North Africa during the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09300558.text_edition: \n Numini Praesenti Iulius Vitalis decurio votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-09300558.translation: Julius Vitalis, decurion, willingly fulfilled his vow to the Present Divinity.
EDCS-09300558.summary: This inscription records Julius Vitalis, a local official, fulfilling a vow to a divine power, likely in Roman Africa during the Imperial period.

EDCS-09300559.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-09300559.text_edition: \n Genio Gholaiae pro salute Auggustorum Caius Iuulius sic Dignus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae piae vindicis qui primo die quo ad locum ventum est ubi domini nnnostri castra fieri iusserunt locum consecravit et ex p\n
EDCS-09300559.translation: For the health of the Augusti, Caius Julius Dignus, centurion of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion, consecrated the site on the first day our lords ordered the camp to be established, and from...
EDCS-09300559.summary: This inscription records Caius Julius Dignus, a centurion, consecrating a site for the health of the emperors, likely in Roman Africa during the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09400692.text_edition: \n Positum CA III Idus Iunias Saturno Augusto sacrum Caius Manius Felix Fortunatianus sacerdos in somnis monitus Saturni numine iussus Manius hic votum solvi sacrumque dicavi pro comperta fide et pro servata salute votum solvi libens animo\n
EDCS-09400692.translation: Erected on the third day before the Ides of June, sacred to Saturn Augustus. Caius Manius Felix Fortunatianus, priest, ordered by Saturn's divine power in a dream, dedicated this vow and sacred offering here, willingly fulfilling the vow for proven loyalty and preserved safety.
EDCS-09400692.summary: This is a dedication to Saturn Augustus by the priest Caius Manius Felix Fortunatianus, who was inspired by a dream, likely in Roman Africa during the Imperial period.

EDCS-09400700.text_edition: \n CA CIBOS NAO agricolae in spl(endidissima) re publica Bihensi Bilta conductori pariatori restitutori fundi Aufidiani et praeter cetera bona quae in eodem fundo fecit steriles quoque oleastri surculos inserendo plurimas oleas instituit puteum iuxta viam pomarium cum trichilis post collectarium vineas novellas sub silva aeque instituit uxor marito incomparabili fecit\n
EDCS-09400700.translation: CIBOSNAO. Bilta, a farmer and conductor of the splendid Republic of Bihensi, an unparalleled husband to his wife, established many olive trees by grafting shoots onto barren oleasters, a well near the road, an orchard with a summerhouse, and young vineyards under the woods, in addition to other improvements he made on the Aufidian estate; his wife made [this] for her incomparable husband.
EDCS-09400700.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Bilta, a farmer and estate manager in Roman Africa, set up by his wife, highlighting his agricultural achievements and their devoted relationship, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-09400701.translation: Victoria, may you be in peace.
EDCS-09400701.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely for a woman named Victoria, dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-09401242.text_edition: \n Magno et invicto principi restitutori orbis sui terrarum Flavio Valerio Constantino Invicto Pio Felici Augusto Asadi devota numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-09401242.translation: To Magnus and the unconquered prince, restorer of his world, Flavius Valerius Constantinus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, Asadi, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-09401242.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Emperor Constantine I, likely erected by an individual named Asadi, dating to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-09401251.text_edition: \n Paragorius bixit sic in pace annos IIII\n
EDCS-09401251.translation: Paragorius lived thus in peace for 4 years.
EDCS-09401251.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a child named Paragorius who died at the age of 4, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-09401252.text_edition: \n Spesinilla innocens fidelis requievit in pace\n
EDCS-09401252.translation: Spesinilla, innocent and faithful, rested in peace.
EDCS-09401252.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Spesinilla, emphasizing her virtues, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-09401255.text_edition: \n Fortunata nurus Sirici sic fidelis vixit in pace annos XXII menses III dies VII\n
EDCS-09401255.translation: Fortunata, daughter-in-law of Siricus, lived faithfully thus in peace for 22 years, 3 months, and 7 days.
EDCS-09401255.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fortunata, detailing her age at death and relationship to Siricus, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-09401256.text_edition: \n Memoria Fortunatais sic infanti in pace\n
EDCS-09401256.translation: In memory of Fortunata, an infant, thus in peace.
EDCS-09401256.summary: This is a funerary inscription for an infant named Fortunata, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-09401257.summary: This is a funerary inscription for an individual named Innocentius, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-09401261.text_edition: \n Memoria Safratis in pace die VIII Idus Nobembres sic\n
EDCS-09401261.translation: In memory of Safratis, in peace, on the 8th day before the Ides of November.
EDCS-09401261.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Safratis, recording the date of death, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-09401262.text_edition: \n Hic iacet Sitifis qui vixit anno mensibus III\n
EDCS-09401262.translation: Here lies Sitifis, who lived for 1 year and 3 months.
EDCS-09401262.summary: This is a funerary inscription for an individual named Sitifis, recording their age at death, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-09401601.text_edition: \n Nomina quae hoc taurobolio sacrum acceperunt Iulius Felicissimus et Iulia Ianuaria eius et Iulius \n
EDCS-09401601.translation: The names of those who received this sacred taurobolium are Iulius Felicissimus and his Iulia Ianuaria and Iulius...
EDCS-09401601.summary: This is an inscription recording recipients of a taurobolium (a ritual sacrifice of a bull), likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09700369.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus ddominis nnostris Philippis Auggustis Marco Aurelio Cominio Cassiano legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro \n
EDCS-09700369.translation: To our Lords Emperors, the Augusti, Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, clarissimus vir, governor with the authority of the Augusti and propraetor.
EDCS-09700369.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the governor Cassianus to the emperors Philip. It is likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09700371.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely the beginning of a name and potentially a dedication or epitaph.

EDCS-09700372.text_edition: \n legionis III Augustae Vicrius Verus |(centurio) [legionis III Augustae] praepositus vexillationis \n
EDCS-09700372.translation: Vicrius Verus, centurion of the Legio III Augusta, commander of a detachment.
EDCS-09700372.summary: This inscription records the titles and positions held by Vicrius Verus, a centurion in the Legio III Augusta, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09700378.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulia Privata vixit annis LXX\n
EDCS-09700378.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulia Privata lived for 70 years.
EDCS-09700378.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulia Privata, recording her name and age at death.

EDCS-09700765.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Decimus Volasenna Dorotheus pius vixit nnis LXXX mensibus XI diebus VII hic situs est D A pi vixit annis LX mensibus diebus hic sit-- est\n
EDCS-09700765.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Decimus Volasenna Dorotheus, dutiful, lived 80 years, 11 months, 7 days. He is buried here. DA, lived 60 years, months, days. He is buried here.
EDCS-09700765.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the life spans of at least two individuals, Decimus Volasenna Dorotheus and another whose name is incomplete.

EDCS-09701230.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Domitio Aureliano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo Germanico maximo Gothico maximo tribuniciae potestatis patri patriae consuli II municipes Uzappenses devoti numini maiestatique eius decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-09701230.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, greatest German, greatest Goth, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul II. The citizens of Uzappa, devoted to his divine will and majesty, by decree of their decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-09701230.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the citizens of Uzappa to Emperor Aurelian, celebrating his titles and victories, likely from the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09701236.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Otacilia Tertia vixit annis LXXX\n
EDCS-09701236.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Otacilia Tertia lived for 80 years.
EDCS-09701236.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Otacilia Tertia, recording her name and age at death.

EDCS-09701237.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Stagio Lustianus vixit annis XVIII\n
EDCS-09701237.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Stagio Lustianus lived for 18 years.
EDCS-09701237.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Stagio Lustianus, recording his name and age at death.

EDCS-09701238.text_edition: \n Marcus Licinius Marci filius Galeria Lugduno Fidelis militavit eques in legione III Augusta nnos XVI factus duplicarius in ala nnoniorum menses IV vixit nnos XXXII hic situs est Quintus Iulius Atticus et Titus nicius Saecularis equites legionis III Augustae heredes eius posuerunt\n
EDCS-09701238.translation: Marcus Licinius, son of Marcus, from the tribe Galeria, from Lugdunum, a loyal soldier, served as an equestrian in the Legio III Augusta for 16 years, became a duplicarius in the ala Norica for 4 months, lived for 32 years. He is buried here. Quintus Iulius Atticus and Titus Iulius Saecularis, equites of the Legio III Augusta, his heirs, placed this.
EDCS-09701238.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Licinius, detailing his military service in the Legio III Augusta and his age at death, set up by his heirs.

EDCS-09701239.text_edition: \n Lucius Iulius Luci filius Quirina Tarvillus Avarici miles legionis III Augustae centuria Anchisae vixit nnos XXV militavit annos V hic situs est\n
EDCS-09701239.translation: Lucius Iulius, son of Lucius, from the tribe Quirina, from Tarvillus Avarici, soldier of the Legio III Augusta, in the century of Anchisa, lived for 25 years, served for 5 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-09701239.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Iulius, detailing his military service in the Legio III Augusta and his age at death.

EDCS-09701240.text_edition: \n Sextus Alfius Sexti filius Arnensis Primus Carthagine miles legionis III Augustae centuria Rutili Secundi vixit \n
EDCS-09701240.translation: Sextus Alfius Primus, son of Sextus, of the Arnensis tribe, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, in the century of Rutilius Secundus, lived [unknown duration].
EDCS-09701240.summary: This is a tombstone for Sextus Alfius Primus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701241.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aesopo vikario sic vixit annos XXVI Adventus verna ex dispensatore legionis III Augustae\n
EDCS-09701241.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Aesopus, the deputy official, lived 26 years. Adventus Verna, from the supply officer of the III Augusta legion, [dedicated this].
EDCS-09701241.summary: This is a tombstone for Aesopus, a deputy official, dedicated by Adventus Verna, a supply officer of the III Augusta legion, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701242.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Asiaticus Felix paedagogus vixit annos LXXX qui se vivo domum aeternam fecit hic situs est\n
EDCS-09701242.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caius Asiaticus Felix, the teacher, lived 80 years, who made his eternal home while still alive; he is buried here.
EDCS-09701242.summary: This is a tombstone for Caius Asiaticus Felix, a teacher, who built his own tomb, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701243.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulia Silleha vixit annos LXXV hic sita est Marius Gaetulicus matri karissimae fecit\n
EDCS-09701243.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulia Silleha lived 75 years; she is buried here. Marius Gaetulicus made this for his dearest mother.
EDCS-09701243.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Silleha, dedicated by her son Marius Gaetulicus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701244.text_edition: \n Caius Aelius Faustus vixit annos LV menses VI hic situs est Achilleus patri\n
EDCS-09701244.translation: Caius Aelius Faustus lived 55 years and 6 months; he is buried here. Achilleus for his father.
EDCS-09701244.summary: This is a tombstone for Caius Aelius Faustus, dedicated by his son Achilleus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701245.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Annia Hilara vixit annos LXXV hic sita est Sulpicius Pompeius uxori karissimae fecit\n
EDCS-09701245.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Annia Hilara lived 75 years; she is buried here. Sulpicius Pompeius made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-09701245.summary: This is a tombstone for Annia Hilara, dedicated by her husband Sulpicius Pompeius, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701246.text_edition: \n Quinto Aurelio Quinti filio Pio Po militi legionis III Augustae centuria Mestri Vi beneficiario tribuni vixit nnos XXVIII mil[itavit ------\n
EDCS-09701246.translation: To Quintus Aurelius Pius, son of Quintus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, in the century of Mestrius Vi, an assistant to the tribune, lived 28 years, served [unknown duration].
EDCS-09701246.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Aurelius Pius, a soldier and assistant tribune of the III Augusta legion, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701247.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caecilius Rogatianus vixit annos LXX Aemilia Aurelia marito pio Quadrubio sic\n
EDCS-09701247.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caecilius Rogatianus lived 70 years. Aemilia Aurelia for her devoted husband Quadrubius, thus [lived].
EDCS-09701247.summary: This is a tombstone for Quadrubius, dedicated by his wife Aemilia Aurelia, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701248.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Aelius Sabinus vixit annos XCI hic situs est\n
EDCS-09701248.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caius Aelius Sabinus lived 91 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-09701248.summary: This is a tombstone for Caius Aelius Sabinus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701249.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Fructuosa vixit annos XX hic sita\n
EDCS-09701249.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Fructuosa lived 20 years; she is buried [here].
EDCS-09701249.summary: This is a tombstone for Fructuosa, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701250.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Fructo carissimo filius fecit\n
EDCS-09701250.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. His son Fructus made this for his dearest son.
EDCS-09701250.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Fructus, made by his son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701251.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Helvius Germanus vixit annos LXX fili posuerunt\n
EDCS-09701251.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Helvius Germanus lived 70 years. His sons erected this.
EDCS-09701251.summary: This is a funerary monument for Helvius Germanus, who lived to 70, erected by his sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701252.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulia Quintula vixit annis XXX\n
EDCS-09701252.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulia Quintula lived 30 years.
EDCS-09701252.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Iulia Quintula, who lived to 30, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701253.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulia Respectula vixit annos XXXV menses VI Iulius Extricatus coniugi piae fecit hic sita est\n
EDCS-09701253.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulia Respectula lived 35 years and 6 months. Iulius Extricatus made this for his pious wife, who is buried here.
EDCS-09701253.summary: This funerary monument records the lifespan of Iulia Respectula and was erected by her husband Iulius Extricatus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701254.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulius Viator vixit annis LXXII filius patri carissimo fecit\n
EDCS-09701254.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulius Viator lived 72 years. His son made this for his dearest father.
EDCS-09701254.summary: This is a funerary monument for Iulius Viator, who lived to 72, erected by his son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701255.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Laelius Terentianus vixit annis XX mensibus XI diebus XXV hic situs est Laelius Mercurius filio dulcissimo fecit\n
EDCS-09701255.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caius Laelius Terentianus lived 20 years, 11 months, and 25 days. He lies here. Laelius Mercurius made this for his sweetest son.
EDCS-09701255.summary: This funerary dedication records the life of Caius Laelius Terentianus and was made by his father Laelius Mercurius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701256.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Rutilia Marcella vixit nnis duobus mensibus decem hic sita est\n
EDCS-09701256.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Rutilia Marcella lived two years and ten months. She lies here.
EDCS-09701256.summary: This is a funerary monument for Rutilia Marcella, who died at two years and ten months old, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701257.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum ego Prima Feliciano marito rarissimo memoriam feci et dedicavi qui vixit annos LXX\n
EDCS-09701257.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. I, Prima, made and dedicated this memorial to Feliciano, my most unique husband, who lived 70 years.
EDCS-09701257.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Feliciano, erected by his wife Prima, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701302.text_edition: \n Memoriae Simpliciae coniugis quae vixit annos XXXIII recessit VI Idus Maias (anno) provinciae CCXXVI item Exupi fili vixit annos VI menses VIII dies VI recessit XIII Kalendas supra scriptas (anno) provinciae supra scripto et Monnicae filiae vixit nnos V menses VIIII recessit XIIII Kalendas Iulias (anno) provinciae supra scripto Iulius Exupius piissimis fecit\n
EDCS-09701302.translation: To the memory of my wife Simplicia, who lived 33 years and passed away on the 6th day before the Ides of May in the 226th year of the province; also of my son Exupius, who lived 6 years, 8 months, and 6 days, and passed away on the 13th day before the Kalends of [August, inferred from 'supra scriptas'] in the aforementioned year of the province; and of my daughter Monnica, who lived 5 years, 9 months, and passed away on the 14th day before the Kalends of July in the aforementioned year of the province. Iulius Exupius made this for his most devoted [children and wife].
EDCS-09701302.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Iulius Exupius for his wife Simplicia and two children, Exupius and Monnica, with specific death dates and ages, dating to the provincial year 226 (likely 249 CE).

EDCS-09701715.text_edition: \n Neroni Claudio divi Claudi filio Germanici Caesaris nepoti Tiberi Caesaris Augusti pronepoti divi Augusti abnepoti Caesari Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate imperatori consuli designato II patri patriae Marcus Pompeius Silvanus Staberius Flavinus XVvir sacris faciundis proconsul III dedicavit Quinto Cassio Grato praetore proconsule Cretae et Cyrenarum legato pro praetore Africae III\n
EDCS-09701715.translation: To Nero Claudius, son of the deified Claudius, grandson of Germanicus Caesar, great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, great-great-grandson of the deified Augustus, to Caesar Augustus Germanicus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, Imperator, consul-designate for the second time, father of the country. Marcus Pompeius Silvanus Staberius Flavinus, fifteen-man board for performing sacred rites, proconsul for the third time, dedicated this, with Quintus Cassius Gratus as praetor, proconsul of Crete and Cyrene, and legate with proconsular power of Africa for the third time.
EDCS-09701715.summary: This is a dedication by Marcus Pompeius Silvanus Staberius Flavinus, detailing the extensive imperial titles of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus and mentioning the officials involved in the dedication, likely from the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-09701716.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XVI consule III et Imperatore Caesare Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto pontifice tribunicia potestate II consule II divi Antonini Pii filiis divi Hadriani nepotibus divi Traiani Parthici pronepotibus \n
EDCS-09701716.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 3rd time, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, sons of the deified Antoninus Pius, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Trajan Parthicus.
EDCS-09701716.summary: This is a formal preamble to an inscription, likely a dedication or honorary monument, dating to the joint reign of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, around 161-169 AD.

EDCS-09701718.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Felix Augusti pius vixit nnos XIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-09701718.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Felix, devoted to the Emperor, lived 13 years; here he lies.
EDCS-09701718.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a boy named Felix, who died at the age of 13, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09701719.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Rustica Augusti serva pia vixit annis LX hic sita est\n
EDCS-09701719.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Rustica, a devoted slave of the Emperor, lived 60 years; here she lies.
EDCS-09701719.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Rustica, a slave of the emperor, who died at the age of 60, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09701720.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Cnaeus Tannonius Ingenuus pius vixit nnis XVI mensibus III diebus XVIIII Gavia Sassa mater huius pia vixit nnis XLV mensibus VI hic siti sunt\n
EDCS-09701720.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gnaeus Tannonius Ingenuus, devoted, lived 16 years, 3 months, and 19 days. Gavia Sassa, his devoted mother, lived 45 years and 6 months. Here they lie.
EDCS-09701720.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gnaeus Tannonius Ingenuus and his mother Gavia Sassa, recording their ages at death, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09701749.text_edition: \n Dis Mauris Castoribus Auggustis sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis patris patriae et Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis et Publi Septimi Getae nobilissimi Caesaris Augggustorum et Iuliae Domnae et Fulviae Plautillae Auggustarum Publius Perellius Saturninus IIvir sua pecunia fecit et dedicavit\n
EDCS-09701749.translation: Sacred to the Divine Castores of the Emperors. For the health of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax, father of the country, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix, and Publius Septimius Geta, most noble Caesar, Augusti, and Julia Domna, and Fulvia Plautilla, Augustae. Publius Perellius Saturninus, as aedile, made and dedicated this at his own expense.
EDCS-09701749.summary: This is a dedication to the Divine Castores (Castor and Pollux), likely a public building or monument, by Publius Perellius Saturninus for the health of the Severan imperial family, dating to their reign (c. 198-205 AD).

EDCS-09701768.text_edition: \n En perfecta cito baiarum grata voluptas undantesque fluunt aquae saxi de rupe sub ima nisibus hic nostris prostratus libor sic anhelat quisquis amat fratrum veniat mecumque laetetur\n
EDCS-09701768.translation: Here, pleasure, welcome and abundant, flows from the rocks, springing forth. Whoever loves his brothers, let him come and rejoice with me.
EDCS-09701768.summary: This is a short, possibly poetic, inscription likely associated with a public bath or pleasure resort, possibly at Baiae, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701777.text_edition: \n Diorus Treboni spectavit Kalendis Decembribus Caio Furnio Caio Silano\n
EDCS-09701777.translation: Diorus, son of Trebonius, watched on the Kalends of December, in the consulship of Gaius Furnius and Gaius Silanus.
EDCS-09701777.summary: This is a record of a date or an event witnessed by Diorus, linked to a specific consular pair, Gaius Furnius and Gaius Silanus, likely dating to their consulship in 45 BC.

EDCS-09701779.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Caelius Saturninus pius vixit annis XVIIII hic situs\n
EDCS-09701779.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Caelius Saturninus, devoted, lived 19 years; here he lies.
EDCS-09701779.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Caelius Saturninus, who died at the age of 19, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09701782.text_edition: \n Marcus Seius Ianuarius vixit nnos V Seius Ianuariusmia Fortunata filio piissimo\n
EDCS-09701782.translation: Marcus Seius Ianuarius lived 5 years. To their most devoted son, Seius Ianuarius and Fortunata.
EDCS-09701782.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy named Marcus Seius Ianuarius, dedicated by his parents, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09701783.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus sacrum Publius Sextilius Libycus hic situs vixit pius nnos V\n
EDCS-09701783.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Sextilius Libycus, here he lies, lived 5 devoted years.
EDCS-09701783.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Sextilius Libycus, who died at the age of 5, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09701784.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Publius Minucius Saturninus vixit nnos L hic situs est\n
EDCS-09701784.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Minucius Saturninus lived 50 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-09701784.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death and burial of Publius Minucius Saturninus, dated to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09701787.text_edition: \n Iussu Csacrum Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Invicti Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi tribunicia potestate VIII imperatoris XII consulis II patris patriae decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-09701787.translation: By order of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Invictus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, in his eighth tribunician power, twelfth acclamation as Imperator, second consulship, father of the fatherland, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-09701787.summary: A monumental inscription detailing the titles and honors of Emperor Septimius Severus, likely commemorating a public work funded by the state, dated to his imperial reign.

EDCS-09800351.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Armeniaco Medico Parthico Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXVIII imperatori consuli patri patriae arcus ex HS CXX milibus nummum ab Avilio Casto in eum et statuas legatis praeter HS quae de publico adiecta sunt dedicatus Caio Septimio Severo proconsule Lucio Septimio Severo legato pro praetore\n
EDCS-09800351.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus Germanicus, Pontifex Maximus, in his twenty-eighth tribunician power, Emperor, Consul, Father of the Fatherland. An arch, at a cost of 120,000 sesterces from Avilius Castus, in addition to the statues of the legates and the funds added from public money, was dedicated while Gaius Septimius Severus was proconsul and Lucius Septimius Severus was legate pro praetore.
EDCS-09800351.summary: An inscription commemorating the dedication of an arch and statues to Emperor Caracalla, funded by Avilius Castus and public money, with extensive consular and legatine officials named, likely dated to the reign of Caracalla (early 3rd century AD).

EDCS-09800352.text_edition: \n Caio Fulvio Plautiano praefecto praetorio clarissimo viro comiti et necessario dominorum nostrorum Imperatorum Auggustorum Marcus Calpurnius Geta Attianus et Marcus Calpurnius Attianus filius numini devotissimi posuerunt\n
EDCS-09800352.translation: Marcus Calpurnius Geta Attianus and his son Marcus Calpurnius Attianus, most devoted to the divine will, erected [this monument] to Gaius Fulvius Plautianus, praetorian prefect, a most distinguished man, companion and confidant of our lords the Emperors Augusti.
EDCS-09800352.summary: A dedication by Marcus Calpurnius Geta Attianus and his son to Gaius Fulvius Plautianus, a prominent figure and praetorian prefect, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09800353.text_edition: \n Opus doliare ex praediis Lucillae Veri Severo et Sabiniano consulibus\n
EDCS-09800353.translation: Pottery works from the estates of Lucilla, under the consulship of Severus and Sabinianus.
EDCS-09800353.summary: A record of pottery production from the estates of Lucilla, dated by the consulship of Severus and Sabinianus (likely 206 AD).

EDCS-09800354.text_edition: \n Ex praediis Domitiae Lucillae opus doliare Fortunati Lucillae\n
EDCS-09800354.translation: Pottery works from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, belonging to Fortunatus and Lucilla.
EDCS-09800354.summary: An inscription indicating pottery production from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, possibly for her ownership or management, dated to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800355.text_edition: \n Opus doliare ex praediis Lucillae Veri Quinti Flavi Apri\n
EDCS-09800355.translation: Pottery works from the estates of Lucilla, belonging to Quintus Flavius Aper.
EDCS-09800355.summary: An inscription documenting pottery production from the estates of Lucilla, associated with Quintus Flavius Aper, dated to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800356.text_edition: \n Barbaro et Regulo consulibus ex officina Faviana Flavi Apri opus doliare Fadi Euhelpisti\n
EDCS-09800356.translation: Pottery works from the workshop of Flavius Aper, belonging to Fadius Euhelpistus, under the consulship of Barbarus and Regulus.
EDCS-09800356.summary: An inscription recording pottery production from the workshop of Flavius Aper, attributed to Fadius Euhelpistus, dated by the consulship of Barbarus and Regulus (likely 241 AD).

EDCS-09800357.text_edition: \n Opus doliare ex praediis Caesaris nostri Claudi Secundini\n
EDCS-09800357.translation: Pottery works from the estates of our Caesar Claudius Secundinus.
EDCS-09800357.summary: An inscription indicating pottery production from the estates of Claudius Secundinus, who held the title of Caesar, dated to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800358.text_edition: \n Opus doliare ex praediis Aureli Caesaris et Faustinae Augustae Mai servi\n
EDCS-09800358.translation: Pottery works from the estates of Aurelius Caesar and Faustina Augusta Maius Servus.
EDCS-09800358.summary: An inscription detailing pottery production from estates belonging to individuals named Aurelius Caesar and Faustina Augusta Maius Servus, dated to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800360.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marius Ianuarius miles legionis III Augustae vixit annis XXXX\n
EDCS-09800360.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Marius Ianuarius, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, lived for 40 years.
EDCS-09800360.summary: This is a tombstone for Marius Ianuarius, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-09800361.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulius Rogatianus filius Rogatiani cornicinis vixit annos XVI menses II dies mo\n
EDCS-09800361.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Iulius Rogatianus, son of Rogatianus the cornicen (horn blower), lived for 16 years, 2 months, and some days.
EDCS-09800361.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulius Rogatianus, son of a cornicen, who died at the age of 16.

EDCS-09800362.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Claudius Maxime miles legionis III Augustae stupendiorum sic viginti dua vixit annos XXXX pater ANA fecit\n
EDCS-09800362.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Claudius Maxime, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, a stupendior (rank/description) of 22, lived for 40 years. His father ANA made this.
EDCS-09800362.summary: This is a tombstone for Claudius Maxime, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, erected by his father, dating to his life at age 40.

EDCS-09800363.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Grania Hospita vixit annos XXI hic sita est\n
EDCS-09800363.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Grania Hospita lived for 21 years; she lies here.
EDCS-09800363.summary: This is a tombstone for Grania Hospita, who died at the age of 21.

EDCS-09800467.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-09800467.text_edition: \n Baeatissimorum martyrum speranti EV R DATVA corpora qui A TA saeculi nequitia corona immarcescibile consecuti sunt via Christo regnare hic interim quiescerA scribtura sic declarat fortis Hinna Faustula et Gaudiosa fratres sic fecerunt\n
EDCS-09800467.translation: The bodies of the most blessed martyrs, who, by Christ's grace, attained the unfading crown, left behind by the wickedness of this age, rest here, awaiting reign with Christ; the inscription declares this: Fortis, Hinna, Faustula, and Gaudiosa, siblings, made this.
EDCS-09800467.summary: This is a funerary inscription for martyrs, commemorating their heavenly reward and noting that the tomb was built by siblings.

EDCS-10300034.translation: In memory of Abedeunis, who lived for seven years.
EDCS-10300034.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Abedeunis at the age of seven.

EDCS-10300146.text_edition: \n Quinto Caelio Maximo aedili auguri IIviro quod in magg(istrato) suo ludorum et circensium spectacula exhibuerit munus etiam gladiatorium de suo ediderit et hoc amplius in praesentis tempore HS MMMMMMMMMMMundecim milia rei publicae donaverit ex cuius summae usuris quinssto sic quoque anno semper universis \n
EDCS-10300146.translation: To Quintus Caelius Maximus, aedile, augur, and duovir, for exhibiting games and circus spectacles during his magistracy, for also presenting a gladiatorial munus at his own expense, and furthermore for donating 11,100,000 sesterces to the Republic in the present time, from the interest of which sum each year, forever, to all [the expenses are to be paid].
EDCS-10300146.summary: This is a public inscription honoring Quintus Caelius Maximus for his generosity as aedile and duovir, particularly for funding games, gladiatorial contests, and a significant donation to the state.

EDCS-10300161.translation: For Flavia Faenu, an incomparable woman, for her many distinguished merits towards me, Caus made this for his sweetest wife.
EDCS-10300161.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Caus to his wife Flavia Faenu, praising her virtues and merits.

EDCS-10300172.translation: Tertia, daughter of Ammicaris and Botonis, lived [for...]
EDCS-10300172.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone for Tertia, daughter of Ammicaris and Botonis, recording her lifespan.

EDCS-10300173.translation: Publius Veius Celer, son of Publius, of the Arnensian tribe, lies here; he lived for 32 years.
EDCS-10300173.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Veius Celer, son of Publius, who died at the age of 32.

EDCS-10300204.translation: To Aurelius Aristobulus, a man of the highest distinction, proconsul, who discovered and dedicated this monument, [to a man] of incomparable [virtue] and [unparalleled] spirit.
EDCS-10300204.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription from a public monument dedicated to the proconsul Aurelius Aristobulus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10300208.translation: Lucius Pomponius Secundus willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-10300208.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Lucius Pomponius Secundus fulfilling a vow, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10300258.text_edition: \n Neptuno Augusto sacrum Lucius Caecilius Maximinus ex summa honoraria flamoni sui posuit idemque dedicavit decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-10300258.translation: Dedicated to Neptune Augustus. Lucius Caecilius Maximinus erected this with funds from his high priestly office and also dedicated it by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-10300258.summary: This is a dedication to Neptune Augustus by Lucius Caecilius Maximinus, funded by his priestly office and authorized by local council, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10300290.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the age of Decimia Annia Felix, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10300299.translation: To Iulia Victoria, daughter of Caius. She lived 35 years. Caius Cornelius Cassi erected this for his wife, a virtuous woman well-deserving and an example of piety to their daughter.
EDCS-10300299.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Iulia Victoria, erected by her husband Caius Cornelius Cassi, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10300301.text_edition: \n Antonio miles legionis III Augustae centuria Sextili vixit nnos XLIV militavit nnos XXV hic situs est Marcus Antonius miles legionis XXI centuria Valeri Prisci frater posuit\n
EDCS-10300301.translation: To Antonius, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, Centuria Sextili. He lived 44 years and served 25 years. Marcus Antonius, a soldier of the XXI legion, Centuria Valeri Prisci, his brother, erected this. He lies here.
EDCS-10300301.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Antonius, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, erected by his brother, also a soldier, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10300302.text_edition: \n Titi filius Sergia Heridianus tribunus militum legionis X fr<e=I>tensis \n
EDCS-10300302.translation: Heridianus, son of Titus, of the Sergia tribe, tribune of soldiers of the X Fretensis legion.
EDCS-10300302.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely identifying Heridianus, a military tribune, probably from the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-10300303.translation: Sextus Metilius Livi, of the Stellatina tribe, lived 20 years and served 2 years. Tiberius Quintius [erected this].
EDCS-10300303.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Sextus Metilius Livi, who lived 20 years and served 2 years, erected by Tiberius Quintius, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10300306.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. [Iulia Rogatina] lived 25 years, she lies here. Her parents erected [this] for their dearest daughter. // Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Inventa lived 30 years, she lies here. Her parents erected [this] for their dearest daughter. // Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Magnius Satur, a hardworking man, not deserving [of such an end], died away from home on the Hadrumetine road. He lived 55 years. Iulia Quinta, his dearest wife, erected [this] for her husband. He lies here.
EDCS-10300306.summary: This is a collection of funerary inscriptions for Iulia Rogatina, Iulia Inventa, and Lucius Magnius Satur, erected by their parents or wives, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10300489.text_edition: \n Saturno et Opi pro salute Impperatorum Caesarum Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Pii [et Publi Septimi Getae nobilissimi Caesaris] totiusque divinae domus victoriaque eorum et Iulia Victorina uxor Secundi mater\n
EDCS-10300489.translation: For Saturn and Ops, for the safety of the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius [and Publius Septimius Geta Nobilissimus Caesar], and of their entire divine family, and for their victory, and for Iulia Victorina, wife of Secundus, mother [of Antoninus].
EDCS-10300489.summary: This is a dedication for the safety and victory of the emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, and their family, including Iulia Victorina, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10300552.text_edition: \n Iussu domini Aesculapi Lucius Numisius Luci filius Vitalis podium de suo fecit quisquis intra podium adscendere volet a muliere a suilla a faba a tonsore a balineo commune custodiat triduo cancellos calciatus intrare nolito\n
EDCS-10300552.translation: By order of our Lord Aesculapius, Lucius Numisius Vitalis, son of Lucius, built the podium at his own expense. Whoever wishes to ascend within the podium must guard it from women, pigsties, beans, and barbers; do not enter the public baths with shoes on for three days.
EDCS-10300552.summary: An inscription from the Roman period recording building rules for a podium dedicated to Aesculapius, likely constructed by Lucius Numisius Vitalis.

EDCS-10300564.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti Lucius Romanius Publi filius Arnensi Gallus voto posuit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-10300564.translation: Lucius Romanius Gallus, son of Publius, of the Arnensis tribe, dedicated this sacred offering to Diana Augusta for the health of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, fulfilling a vow.
EDCS-10300564.summary: A dedication to Diana Augusta by Lucius Romanius Gallus for the health of Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-10300565.text_edition: \n Frugifero Augusto sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii Lucius Decianus Marci filius Arnensis Extricatus praefectus iure dicundo sacerdos Cererum annorum CLXXIII nomine suo et Iuliae uxoris et Decianorum Extricati Maniliani Honorati filiorum omnibus ornamentis sua pecunia fecit donum dedit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-10300565.translation: Lucius Decianus Extricatus, son of Marcus, of the Arnensis tribe, a former prefect, priest of Ceres, aged 173 years, has made and dedicated this entire offering with his own money in his name and that of his wife Julia, and his sons Decianus Manilianus Honoratus, to the fruitful Augustus.
EDCS-10300565.summary: A dedicatory inscription to 'Frugifer Augustus' (the fruitful Augustus), likely Hadrian, by Lucius Decianus Extricatus, a prominent local official, in the reign of Antoninus Pius (138-161 AD).

EDCS-10300607.translation: For Publius Attius Extricationus, son of Publius, of the Arnensis tribe, flamen of the deified Titus, priest of Aesculapius of the colony of Julia Karthago, twice admitted to the equestrian order by the Emperors Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augusti Germanici Sarmatici, the public funds paid for this at the decree of the decurions, in honor of his mother, Julia Bassilia, perpetual flaminica.
EDCS-10300607.summary: An inscription honoring Publius Attius Extricationus, a prominent local magistrate and priest, funded by public money in honor of his mother, dating to the joint reign of Marcus Aurelius and Commodus (177-180 AD).

EDCS-10300622.text_edition: \n Iuliae Domnae Augustae matri Auggustorum castrorumque nnue victoriis eorum Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi fortissimi felicissimi coniugi civitas Giufitana decreto decurionum dedit posuit eademque dedicavit\n
EDCS-10300622.translation: The citizenship of Giufi has granted and set up this monument to Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the Augusti, of the camps, and of their victories, wife of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, most brave and most fortunate, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-10300622.summary: A civic dedication to Julia Domna, mother of Caracalla and Geta and wife of Septimius Severus, by the city of Giufi, likely erected during the reign of Septimius Severus (193-211 AD).

EDCS-10300623.translation: Marcus Areius Optatus, son of Victoris, and Quintus Minucius Secundus, son of Secundus, veterans, having multiplied the honorary sum for their aedileship, and furthermore on the day of dedication, gave a feast and distributions to the decurions and the people. The site was granted by decree of the decurions. Dedicated on the 3rd Ides of May in the consulship of Pompeianus and Paelignianus.
EDCS-10300623.summary: A record of benefactions by two former officials, Marcus Areius Optatus and Quintus Minucius Secundus, who provided a feast and distributions to the local council and populace upon the completion of their duties, dedicated in 198 AD.

EDCS-10300630.translation: For the deified Hadrian, father of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, Germanus, son of Passus Germanus, from Sutunurca, on account of the honor of perpetual flaminatus for his fifth daughter Quintae, with 4,000 sesterces, with an additional 1,525 sesterces, has made the statue of the deified Hadrian and of Caesar Lucius Aelius with public money by decree of the decurions. And for the dedication, he gave entrails and a gymnasium to the people.
EDCS-10300630.summary: An inscription recording the erection of statues of Emperors Hadrian and Lucius Aelius, and the donation of a gymnasium, by Germanus in honor of his daughter's perpetual priesthood, likely during the reign of Antoninus Pius (138-161 AD).

EDCS-10300631.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filio divi Commodi fratri divi Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate IIII imperatori XI consuli III proconsuli patri patriae cives Romani pagani veterani pagi Fortunalis quorum parentes beneficio divi Augusti [] Sutunurca agros acceperunt pecunia publica faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-10300631.translation: The Roman citizens, pagans and veterans of the district of Fortunalis, whose parents received land in Sutunurca by the favor of the deified Augustus, have taken care to have this made with public money, for the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Aurelius Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, emperor for the 11th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, father of the country.
