EDCS-13002578.summary: A short inscription likely referring to a table, possibly part of a funerary monument.

EDCS-13002581.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Iulia Castula made this for her husband Donatus, who lived 72 years.
EDCS-13002581.summary: A funerary inscription for Donatus, commissioned by his wife Iulia Castula.

EDCS-13002582.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Iulia Ianuaría lived 23 years, 2 months, and 25 days. Gaius Roius Emeritus made this for his most devoted wife and she is buried here.
EDCS-13002582.summary: A funerary inscription for Iulia Ianuaría, detailing her lifespan and erected by her husband Gaius Roius Emeritus.

EDCS-13002584.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Iulia Vitalis lived 45 years. She is buried here. Her sons made this for their most devoted mother.
EDCS-13002584.summary: A funerary inscription for Iulia Vitalis, stating her age and that her sons commissioned the monument.

EDCS-13002586.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Lepidius Congius lived 75 years. His devoted wife made this and he is buried here.
EDCS-13002586.summary: A funerary inscription for Lepidius Congius, recording his lifespan and erected by his devoted wife.

EDCS-13002587.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Liberalis lived for 11 years.
EDCS-13002587.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Liberalis at the age of 11.

EDCS-13002588.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Licinia Claudia lived for 12 months. Lucius Licinius Speratus made this for his pious daughter.
EDCS-13002588.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Licinia Claudia at 12 months old, dedicated by her father Lucius Licinius Speratus.

EDCS-13002589.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Licinia Quartula lived for 43 years. Her daughter made this for her mother.
EDCS-13002589.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Licinia Quartula at 43 years old, made by her daughter.

EDCS-13002590.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marius Felix, also known as Cucio.
EDCS-13002590.summary: A funerary inscription commemorating Marius Felix, who was also known as Cucio.

EDCS-13002592.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Petronius Victor lived for 60 years. His son made this willingly.
EDCS-13002592.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Petronius Victor at 60 years old, made by his son.

EDCS-13002594.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Pomponia Firmana Mascaradiana lived for 33 years and 4 months.
EDCS-13002594.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Pomponia Firmana Mascaradiana at 33 years and 4 months old.

EDCS-13002596.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Porcius Felix lived for 92 years. Porcia Iuliana and Zaba, his heirs, made this; he is buried here.
EDCS-13002596.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Porcius Felix at 92 years old, made by his heirs Porcia Iuliana and Zaba.

EDCS-13002597.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Pothus lived for 31 years. His wife Ianuaria made this for her pious husband; he is buried here.
EDCS-13002597.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Pothus at 31 years old, made by his wife Ianuaria.

EDCS-13002598.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Publicius Successus lived for 14 years, 3 months, and 15 days. Successus, his father, made this for his pious son.
EDCS-13002598.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Lucius Publicius Successus at 14 years, 3 months, and 15 days old, made by his father.

EDCS-13002599.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quinquatralis lived for 18 years, 5 months, and 23 days. His sister Tsidina made this.
EDCS-13002599.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Quinquatralis at 18 years, 5 months, and 23 days old, made by his sister.

EDCS-13002601.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Sextilius Donatus lived 30 years; he is buried here. His sons made this for their dearest father.
EDCS-13002601.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Sextilius Donatus, set up by his sons, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13002602.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sextus, son of..., made this for Rogata Valentia, who lived 45 years.
EDCS-13002602.summary: A funerary epitaph for Rogata Valentia, set up by her son Sextus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13002604.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Valerius lived 50 years.
EDCS-13002604.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Valerius, noting his age, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13002605.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Valerius Fortis lived 60 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13002605.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Valerius Fortis, noting his age and burial, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13002607.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Vibius Geminus lived 19 years. Vibius Postumius(?) made this for his most devoted son.
EDCS-13002607.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Vibius Geminus, set up by his father Vibius Postumius(?), likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13002608.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Victoria for father and daughter Q[...]
EDCS-13002608.summary: A funerary epitaph recording Victoria for a father and daughter, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13002609.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Victorinus lived 22 years.
EDCS-13002609.summary: A funerary epitaph for Victorinus, noting his age, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13002610.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. [This tomb is for] Evasia, wife of Roi(?), most holy, lived 20 years, 2 months, 15 days; she is buried here.
EDCS-13002610.summary: A funerary epitaph for Evasia, the wife of Roi(?), noting her age and burial, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13002622.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-13002622.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Iulius Felix lived 58 years and 7 months. Iulius Felix, his son, made this for his most devoted father; he is buried here. // Mensa Sisatiu.
EDCS-13002622.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Iulius Felix, set up by his son, noting his age and burial, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13002625.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudius Annius Honoratianus, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, lived 25 years, 2 months, 25 days. Marcus Ann(i)us Octavianus, his unhappy father, made this; he is buried here.
EDCS-13002625.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudius Annius Honoratianus, set up by his father, noting his age and burial, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13002626.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Valeria Severa lived 28 years, 7 months; she rests here. Julius Constans made this for his most beloved wife.
EDCS-13002626.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria Severa, who died at the age of 28 years and 7 months, erected by her husband Julius Constans.

EDCS-13002661.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulius Elianus Pulneus lived 35 years. His wife (?) Felicita (?) made this.
EDCS-13002661.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of Iulius Elianus Pulneus as 35 years, with a partially restored dedication by his wife.

EDCS-13002662.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Getula lived approximately 30 years.
EDCS-13002662.summary: A brief funerary epitaph stating that Getula lived for approximately 30 years.

EDCS-13002664.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulius [3]nius lived 25 years. Ponponia Ianuaria, his wife, made and dedicated this at her own expense. He rests here.
EDCS-13002664.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulius [3]nius, who died at 25, erected by his wife Ponponia Ianuaria.

EDCS-13002666.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Victoria lived 3 years, 5 months. Her dearest parents made this for their sweetest daughter.
EDCS-13002666.summary: A funerary epitaph for Victoria, who died at 3 years and 5 months, erected by her parents.

EDCS-13002773.translation: During the reign of Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus. Sacred to Victoriae Augustae. Lucius Granius Honoratus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, soldier of the III Augusta legion, century of Marcus Antonius, had this built at his own expense upon completing his term as decurion.
EDCS-13002773.summary: An inscription from the reign of Emperor Nerva recording the dedication of a monument by Lucius Granius Honoratus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, for his service as a decurion.

EDCS-13003655.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Cai filius Quirina Lugudunolus Augustiduno sic centuria Valeri Fidelis vixit annis LV militavit annis XXII heres eius Statutus Popius Saturninus centuria eadem libens merito titulum consecravit\n
EDCS-13003655.translation: Caius Iulius Lugudunolus, son of Caius, of the Quirina tribe, from Augustidunum, of the century of Valerius Fidelis, lived 55 years and served 22 years. His heir, Statutus Popius Saturninus, of the same century, lovingly and deservedly dedicated this monument.
EDCS-13003655.summary: A tombstone erected by an heir for Caius Iulius Lugudunolus, a soldier who lived 55 years and served 22 years.

EDCS-13003657.text_edition: \n Quintus Lutatius Quinti filius Quirina Viator Autrico miles legionis III Augustae centuria Plaetori vixit annos XXV militavit annos III Titus Flavius centuria Nasidi et Caius Iulius centuria Bruttieni heredes monumentum posuerunt\n
EDCS-13003657.translation: Quintus Lutatius Viator, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, from Autricum, soldier of the III Augusta legion, century of Plaetorius, lived 25 years and served 3 years. Titus Flavius of the century of Nasidius and Caius Iulius of the century of Bruttienus, his heirs, erected this monument.
EDCS-13003657.summary: A tombstone erected by the heirs Titus Flavius and Caius Iulius for Quintus Lutatius Viator, a soldier who lived 25 years and served 3 years.

EDCS-13003658.translation: [...]ius Martialis, veteran of the III Augusta legion, lived 90 years. He rests here.
EDCS-13003658.summary: A funerary epitaph for Martialis, a veteran of the III Augusta legion, who died at the age of 90.

EDCS-13003659.text_edition: \n Sextus Sulpicius Sexti filius Quirina Senilis miles legionis III Augustae centuria Caesoni beneficiarius Tetti Iuliani et Iavoleni Prisci legatorum Augusti vixit annos XXV militavit nnos VI hic situs est Marcus Aurelius Candidus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae heres eius posuit\n
EDCS-13003659.translation: Sextus Sulpicius Senilis, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, soldier of the III Augusta legion, century of Caesoni, beneficiary of the legates of Augustus Tettius Iulianus and Iavolenus Priscus, lived 25 years and served 6 years. Marcus Aurelius Candidus, a centurion of the III Augusta legion, his heir, erected this.
EDCS-13003659.summary: A tombstone erected by Marcus Aurelius Candidus for Sextus Sulpicius Senilis, a soldier and beneficiary, who lived 25 years and served 6 years.

EDCS-13003661.translation: A knight from the ala [of ...], erected by will.
EDCS-13003661.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a soldier of the cavalry, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-13003662.translation: Vita[lis ...], veteran, lived 80 years. [Family name?]ria MA [erected/dedicated?] this monument; [responsible person?] curavit.
EDCS-13003662.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a veteran named Vitalis who lived to be 80 years old, likely from the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-13003663.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Adaucta lived 21 years. [Date?] XV [erected?].
EDCS-13003663.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Adaucta who died at the age of 21, likely from the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-13003664.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Titus Aelius Princeps, freedman of the Emperor Augustus, lived 77 years; he lies here. Martialis [erected/dedicated].
EDCS-13003664.summary: This funerary inscription honors Titus Aelius Princeps, a freedman of Emperor Augustus, who lived to be 77, and was set up by Martialis, likely from the 2nd-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-13003665.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aemilia Marcella, daughter of [VI?], made this.
EDCS-13003665.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aemilia Marcella, made by her father, dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-13003667.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Appius Maximu(s) Saturninus lived 30 years. Gaius Lucius Appius Saturninus made this for his dearest brother; he lies here.
EDCS-13003667.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Appius Maximu(s) Saturninus, who died at 30, and was honored by his brother, likely from the 2nd-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-13003668.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aponia Polla lived 35 years; she lies here. Cornelius Saturninus made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-13003668.summary: This funerary inscription records Aponia Polla, who died at 35, and was honored by her husband, Cornelius Saturninus, likely from the 2nd-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-13003669.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Butis made this for her dearest husband. Paris lived 10 years; he lies here.
EDCS-13003669.summary: This funerary inscription honors Butis's husband, Paris, who died at 10 years old, likely from the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-13003671.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Caecilius Felicior lived 55 years. Caecilius Nundinarius made this for his brother.
EDCS-13003671.summary: This funerary inscription records Quintus Caecilius Felicior, who lived 55 years, and was honored by his brother, likely from the 2nd-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-13003672.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. [Name?] Caecilius Gaetulicus, also known as Pistor, lived 24 years and 9 months. Lucius Caecilius Gaetulus (father) and Caecilia Spesina (mother) made this for their most dutiful son; he lies here.
EDCS-13003672.summary: This funerary inscription records Caecilius Gaetulicus (Pistor), who died at age 24 years and 9 months, honored by his parents, likely from the 2nd-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-13003673.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Caecilius Thevestinus lived 52 years. His father made this for his dearest son. [He was] well, happy, and strong.
EDCS-13003673.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Caecilius Thevestinus, who lived 52 years, dedicated by his father.

EDCS-13003674.translation: To the departed spirits. Caecilia Cinitia lived 51 years. Caecilius Fortunatus made this monument for his freedwoman and dearest wife. She is buried here.
EDCS-13003674.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life of Caecilia Cinitia (51 years) and stating that Caecilius Fortunatus erected this monument for her as his freedwoman and wife.

EDCS-13003675.translation: To the departed spirits. Caecilia Fortunata lived 42 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-13003675.summary: A funerary inscription for Caecilia Fortunata, who lived 42 years.

EDCS-13003677.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Iulius Octavus lived forty years. His wife Tevestina dedicated this.
EDCS-13003677.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Iulius Octavus, who lived 40 years, dedicated by his wife Tevestina.

EDCS-13003679.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording a soldier of a Roman legion, with his name partially restored.

EDCS-13003680.translation: To the departed spirits. Erucius lived 13 years.
EDCS-13003680.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for Erucius, who lived 13 years.

EDCS-13003682.translation: To the departed spirits. Felix lived 14 years, 5 months, and 19 days. Lixestinus made this for his dearest son.
EDCS-13003682.summary: A funerary inscription for Felix, who lived 14 years, 5 months, and 19 days, dedicated by his father Lixestinus.

EDCS-13003683.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavia Aeregia lived 31 years and 8 months. She is buried here. Gaius Caecilius Cinnas made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-13003683.summary: A funerary inscription for Flavia Aeregia, who lived 31 years and 8 months, erected by her husband Gaius Caecilius Cinnas.

EDCS-13003685.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Helvius Saturninus, dutiful, lived 50 years. He is buried here. All his sons took care to have this made by Cornelius Optatus.
EDCS-13003685.summary: A funerary inscription for Quintus Helvius Saturninus, who lived 50 years, arranged by his sons and executed by Cornelius Optatus.

EDCS-13003689.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Iulius Fortunatianus lived 17 years and 25 days, 8 hours, after his pious mother Fabia Fortunata, who lived 3 years, 6 months, and 21 days, [and] 1 hour. He is buried here.
EDCS-13003689.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Iulius Fortunatianus, who lived 17 years and 25 days, and his mother Fabia Fortunata, who lived 3 years, 6 months, and 21 days.

EDCS-13003690.translation: To the departed gods. Gaius Julius Ianvarius lived 40 years. Prima, his wife, made this.
EDCS-13003690.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Gaius Julius Ianvarius, erected by his wife Prima.

EDCS-13003692.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum ut merito in his titulis merenti et generositati suae in sepulcro et extremo loco sit Iulia Perna\n
EDCS-13003692.translation: To the departed gods. So that Iulia Perna may rightfully have a burial place and a final resting spot in these inscriptions, deserving of her nobility.
EDCS-13003692.summary: A funerary inscription intended for Iulia Perna, emphasizing her noble status and right to burial.

EDCS-13003693.translation: To the departed gods. Marcus Iunius Victor lived 80 years. Ianuaria made this for her husband. He is buried here.
EDCS-13003693.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Iunius Victor, erected by his wife Ianuaria, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13003694.translation: To the departed gods. Iunia Optatula lived 35 years. Herennia Ianuaria made this for her daughter. She is buried here.
EDCS-13003694.summary: A funerary inscription for Iunia Optatula, erected by her mother Herennia Ianuaria.

EDCS-13003696.translation: To the departed gods. Megatia lived 22 years.
EDCS-13003696.summary: A brief funerary epitaph recording the death of Megatia at the age of 22.

EDCS-13003698.translation: To the departed gods. Sada made this. Valeria Quarta lived 30 years.
EDCS-13003698.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Valeria Quarta, possibly erected by a person named Sada.

EDCS-13003700.translation: To the departed gods. Sittia Gemellina lived 21 years. Nutrius Ianvarius, her devoted husband, made this. She is buried here.
EDCS-13003700.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sittia Gemellina, erected by her husband Nutrius Ianvarius.

EDCS-13003701.translation: To the departed gods. Vaberius Victor(?)...
EDCS-13003701.summary: A fragment of a funerary inscription, possibly for Vaberius Victor.

EDCS-13003702.translation: To the departed gods. Gaius Valerius Valerianus lived 60 years. Valerius Valens made this for his dearest father. He is buried here. D().
EDCS-13003702.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Valerius Valerianus, erected by his son Valerius Valens.

EDCS-13003703.translation: To the departed gods. Velleia Respecta lived 50 years. She is buried here. Novellianus, her son, made this for his devoted mother.
EDCS-13003703.summary: A funerary epitaph for Velleia Respecta, erected by her son Novellianus.

EDCS-13003704.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Vibius Celer lived 20 years. He rests here. Gaius Vibius Novellus made this for his brother, with devotion.
EDCS-13003704.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Gaius Vibius Celer, erected by his brother Gaius Vibius Novellus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003705.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Lutatius Victor lived 60 years.
EDCS-13003705.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Quintus Lutatius Victor, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003706.translation: To the departed spirits. Cotisa(?) lived 21 years. To the departed spirits. Victoria lived 20 years.
EDCS-13003706.summary: A tombstone with two inscriptions, recording the deaths of Cotisa(?) and Victoria, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003707.translation: To the departed spirits. Umbria Victorina lived 35 years. She rests here. Umbrius Adventus made this for his dearest mother.
EDCS-13003707.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Umbria Victorina, erected by her son Umbrius Adventus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003729.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Annius Seranus, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, lived 18 years, 11 months, and 12 days. He rests here.
EDCS-13003729.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Marcus Annius Seranus, including detailed age at death, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003736.summary: An inscription dedicating something to the god Saturn Augustus, possibly from a public or private shrine, date uncertain but likely imperial period.

EDCS-13003781.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Armeniaci Medici Parthici maximi liberorumque eius Iuliani Bitinenses posuerunt\n
EDCS-13003781.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus Maximus and his children. The people of Bitinienses set this up.
EDCS-13003781.summary: A dedication for the well-being of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and his children, set up by the inhabitants of Bitinienses, likely mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-13003801.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavius Vindex lived 80 years. He rests here.
EDCS-13003801.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Flavius Vindex, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003806.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulius Ianuarius lived 36 years.
EDCS-13003806.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Iulius Ianuarius, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003807.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulius Mamari lived 26 years.
EDCS-13003807.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Iulius Mamari, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003808.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulia Saturnina lived 70 years.
EDCS-13003808.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Saturnina, recording her name and age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13003809.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Olia Rustica lived 71 years, 10 months, and 11 days; she lies here.
EDCS-13003809.summary: This is a tombstone for Olia Rustica, recording her name, lifespan, and burial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13003868.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Nervae Traiani Caesaris Augusti Germanici Dacici consulis VI imperatoris XIII Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus legatus Augusti pro praetore inter Musulamios et Madaurenses\n
EDCS-13003868.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Nerva Traianus Caesar Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, consul for the 6th time, Imperator for the 13th time. Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus, imperial legate with praetorian power, between the Musulamii and Madaurenses.
EDCS-13003868.summary: This inscription records an official act by the legate Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus, under Emperor Trajan, concerning boundary disputes between tribes, likely from the early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-13100001.text_edition: \n Helvius Fuscus Luci filius Quirina Emerita militavit legione III Augusta vixit annos militavit annos XXXIIII hic situs est EIus ProculusAVI \n
EDCS-13100001.translation: Helvius Fuscus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, from Emerita, served in the III Augusta legion, lived 34 years, served 34 years, lies here. His [relative] Proculus [made this].
EDCS-13100001.summary: This is a military tombstone for Helvius Fuscus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, recording his tribal affiliation, origin, service duration, and age, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13100004.text_edition: \n eus Cai filius Arnensis miles legionis III Augustae centuria Curti vixit annosXVI militavit nnos hic situs est R\n
EDCS-13100004.translation: [Name], son of Caius, of the Arnensis tribe, soldier of the III Augusta legion, [in the] century of Curtius, lived 16 years, served [number] years, lies here. R [made this].
EDCS-13100004.summary: This is a tombstone for a soldier of the III Augusta legion, recording his tribal affiliation, unit, age, and service duration, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13100005.text_edition: \n lusio O vixsit sic nnos XLIII militavit nnos XXIII hic situs est curam egerunt Caius Catellius \n
EDCS-13100005.translation: [Name], lived 43 years, served 23 years, lies here. Caius Catellius [made this] with care.
EDCS-13100005.summary: This is a tombstone for an individual who served in the military, recording their age and service years, with Caius Catellius noted as the one who arranged it, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13100006.text_edition: \n s Cai filius Celer --- mil(es) legionis III Augustae \n
EDCS-13100006.translation: [Name], son of Caius Celer, soldier of the III Augusta legion.
EDCS-13100006.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription identifying a soldier, Caius Celer, of the III Augusta legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13100007.translation: [Name], soldier of the III Augusta legion, [in the] century of [name], lived 67 years, served 24 years.
EDCS-13100007.summary: This is a tombstone for a soldier of the III Augusta legion, recording his age and service years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13100023.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aris Storacis filius vixit annos LXX hic situs est Dis Manibus sacrum Arest pia vixit nnos\n
EDCS-13100023.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aris, son of Storax, lived 70 years, lies here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Arest [pious, made this], lived [years].
EDCS-13100023.summary: This is a tombstone for Aris, son of Storax, recording his age and burial, with a partial second inscription possibly for Arest, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13200001.text_edition: \n Caio Iunio Serio Augurino Marco Trebio Sergiano consulibus Caius Caelius Saturninus structor ab Avitinis sic votum solvit columnas duas in cella Caelestis deae sua pecunia fecit? et dedicavit\n
EDCS-13200001.translation: During the consulship of Caius Iunius Serius Augurinus and Marcus Trebius Sergianus, Caius Coelius Saturninus, a builder from Avitinis, fulfilled a vow and made and dedicated two columns in the cella of the goddess Caelestis with his own money.
EDCS-13200001.summary: This inscription records a religious dedication by Caius Coelius Saturninus, a builder, who fulfilled a vow by providing two columns for the temple of the goddess Caelestis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13300154.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Augusto tribunicia potestate XVIII Lucius Asprenas \n
EDCS-13300154.translation: To Emperor Caesar Augustus, in his eighteenth tribunician power. Lucius Asprenas.
EDCS-13300154.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Augustus, initiated by Lucius Asprenas, likely on a public monument, dated to his 18th tribunician power.

EDCS-13302432.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Annio Floriano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo fortissimo adque sic indulgentissimo principi restitutori orbis potestate consuli II patri patriae procons(uli) res publica coloniae Hipponensium Regiorum\n
EDCS-13302432.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Annius Florianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, most brave and most indulgent prince, restorer of the world, consul for the second time, father of the country, proconsul. The Republic of the colony of Hippona Regia.
EDCS-13302432.summary: This is a dedication from the city of Hippona Regia to Emperor Annius Florianus, praising his virtues and titles, likely an honorific inscription on a public building.

EDCS-13302452.translation: To Quintus Domitius Marsianus, son of Lucius, of the voting tribe Quirina, procurator of the Emperor for the patrimony of the province of Narbonensis, procurator of the Emperor for iron mines, procurator of the Emperor for receiving census in Gaul for the provinces of Belgica through the regions of the Tungri and Frisavones and Lower Germany and the Batavi, prefect of soldiers, admitted to the order of equestrians at public expense by the Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Caesar, for whom when the equestrian order decreed that a monument be erected at public expense, his brother Lucius Domitius Fabianus, remitting the public expense, erected it at his own cost, following the example of the imperial rescripts. Caesar Antoninus Augustus to his Marsianus greeting: I have long wished to advance you to the splendor of the ducenary procuratorship and am using the opportunity that now presents itself. Succeed Marius Pudentius, with as much hope of my perpetual favor as you have maintained conscientiousness, diligence, and experience. Farewell, my dearest Marsianus, my friend.
EDCS-13302452.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Quintus Domitius Marsianus, a high-ranking imperial official, erected by his brother who took over the expense after the state had planned to fund it, and includes a rescript from Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus. The inscription is dated to the co-reign of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, before Marsianus' death.

EDCS-13302578.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Nervae filio Nervae Traiano Augusto Germanico Dacico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XVII imperatori VII consuli VI patri patriae civitates LXIIII pagi Thuscae et Gunzuzi pecunia sua fecerunt idemque dedicaverunt curatore Victore Martialis sic praefecto earum e |(centurione)\n
EDCS-13302578.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, pontifex maximus, in his seventeenth tribunician power, imperator for the seventh time, consul for the sixth time, father of the country. Sixty-four communities and the pagi of Thusca and Gunzuzi made this at their own expense and dedicated it. Victor Martialis, curator and prefect of them, acted as supervisor.
EDCS-13302578.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Trajan by sixty-four communities and pagi, financed and dedicated by them, with Victor Martialis serving as supervisor, dated to Trajan's 17th tribunician power.

EDCS-13302609.text_edition: \n Lutatius Gai filius Iulius vixit annos XLI\n
EDCS-13302609.summary: This is a simple epitaph recording the name and age of the deceased, Gaius Lutatius Iulius.

EDCS-13302735.text_edition: \n Germanico maximo tribunicia potestate XVI imperatori XV consuli VII patri patriae proconsuli Caius Servilius Marsus consul curator rei publicae regionis Tripolitanae devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-13302735.translation: To Germanicus Maximus, in his sixteenth tribunician power, imperator for the fifteenth time, consul for the seventh time, father of the country, proconsul. Caius Servilius Marsus, consul, curator of the Republic of the Tripolitana region, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-13302735.summary: This is a dedication by Caius Servilius Marsus to Germanicus Maximus, likely a general or imperial figure, in his capacity as curator of the Tripolitana region, dated to Germanicus' 16th tribunician power.

EDCS-13400131.summary: This is a very brief dedication to a person named Septidonius, likely on a small monument or marker.

EDCS-13400167.text_edition: \n ------ cons]uli VIIviro epulonum fetiali sodali Augustali proconsuli Africae legato Augustorum pro praetore provinciarum Moesiae item Dalmatiae curatori operum publ[icorum ------\n
EDCS-13400167.translation: To consul for the seventh time, member of the college of pontiffs, fetialis, member of the college of Augustales, proconsul of Africa, legate of the Augusti, governor with praetorian authority of the provinces of Moesia and also Dalmatia, curator of public works.
EDCS-13400167.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription listing the high offices held by an unnamed individual, including consulship, proconsulship of Africa, and legateship of Moesia and Dalmatia.

EDCS-13400181.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae Publius Cornelius Dolabella consul VIIvir epulonum sodalis Titiensis proconsul occiso Tacfarinate posuit\n
EDCS-13400181.translation: To Victoria Augusta. Publius Cornelius Dolabella, consul, member of the college of pontiffs, member of the college of Titius, proconsul, erected this after killing Tacfarinate.
EDCS-13400181.summary: This is a dedication to Victoria Augusta by Publius Cornelius Dolabella, a Roman official and military figure, commemorating his victory over Tacfarinate, and likely erected after his proconsulship in Africa.

EDCS-13400182.text_edition: \n Dolabella Rom proconsul Tacfarinate debellato civitas sic Oeam restituit pace conservata POP APHR PROT et consecravit\n
EDCS-13400182.translation: Dolabella to Rome, proconsul. After conquering Tacfarinate and preserving peace, the city of Oeam restored and consecrated this.
EDCS-13400182.summary: This inscription records that the city of Oeam was restored and consecrated by Dolabella, proconsul, after he defeated Tacfarinate and secured peace, likely referring to Dolabella's actions in North Africa.

EDCS-13400211.text_edition: \n Matri Magnae Ideae sacrum [[]] [[]] [[]] [[]] [] Quintus Latinius Victor et Quintus Latinius Egregius filius eius dendrofori cerno et criobolio de suo acceptis aram pecunia sua fecerunt et consacraverunt tradente Caio Raecio Aprile et Pompeia Satria Fortunata eius sacerdotibus Matris Deum Magnae Ideae coloniae Utikensis astantibus dendroforis et sacratis ministrante Caio Rombio Felice dendroforo atparatore sic dedicata X Kalendas Iulias\n
EDCS-13400211.translation: Sacred to the Great Mother Goddess Cybele. Quintus Latinius Victor and his son Quintus Latinius Egregius, dendrophores (tree-bearers), having paid the cost of a ram and bull sacrifice from their own funds, made and consecrated this altar with their own money, with Gaius Raecius Aprilis and his wife Pompeia Satria Fortunata, priests of the Great Mother Goddess Cybele of the colony of Utica, presiding, with the dendrophores and sacred attendants standing by, and Gaius Rombius Felix, a dendrophore and attendant, officiating, was dedicated thus on the tenth day before the Kalends of July.
EDCS-13400211.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to the Great Mother Goddess Cybele by two dendrophores in Utica, dated to June 22nd.

EDCS-13600155.summary: This is a brief dedication to the goddess Diana, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13600240.text_edition: \n Dominis sanctis martyribus Silvano et Fortunato T Bonifatius votum solvit a quibus petimus ut orationes nostras in mente habeatis martyres cum comites vestros\n
EDCS-13600240.translation: To the holy lords Silvanus and Fortunatus. Bonifatius fulfilled his vow and asks that you, martyrs, remember our prayers, along with your companions.
EDCS-13600240.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to the martyrs Silvanus and Fortunatus, requesting their intercession.

EDCS-13600241.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-13600241.translation: The memorial of Victorinus, who died a martyr in peace, having held the office of 'professus' (likely a religious or administrative role), on the eighth day before the Ides of May, on a Sunday, at the eighth hour, in the province in the year 271.
EDCS-13600241.summary: This inscription marks the memorial of Victorinus, a martyr, and provides details about his death date and possibly a provincial dating system.

EDCS-13700309.text_edition: \n Africanus Fabius Quinti filius Maximus consul VIIvir epulonum pro consule XXCVII\n
EDCS-13700309.translation: Africanus Fabius Maximus, son of Quintus, consul, member of the college of seven epulones (priests who supervised banquets), served as proconsul [for the province of X].
EDCS-13700309.summary: This inscription records the career of Africanus Fabius Maximus, a Roman official who held several important positions.

EDCS-13700372.text_edition: \n ------ legato iu]ridico dioceseos Hipponensis curatori rei publicae Hipponensium regiorum item municipiorum Thubursicensium Kalamensium Tipasitanorum Marcus Ulpius Spenicus Iunior cliens domus eius locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-13700372.translation: To Marcus Ulpius Spenicus Junior, client of the imperial household, who was legal official of the diocese of Hippo, curator of the public affairs of the people of Hippo Regius, and also of the municipalities of Thubursicum, Kalamenses, and Tipasa. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13700372.summary: This inscription grants land to Marcus Ulpius Spenicus Junior, a high-ranking imperial official, by decree of the local councils.

EDCS-13700407.text_edition: \n Dei Herculis simulacrum Marcus Pacatus Amil\n
EDCS-13700407.translation: The statue of the god Hercules. Marcus Pacatus [erected it].
EDCS-13700407.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue of the god Hercules by Marcus Pacatus.

EDCS-13900059.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Iulio Constantio Pio Felici Augusto Furnos devota XXXVI\n
EDCS-13900059.translation: To our lord Flavius Julius Constantius, the Pious, Fortunate, August. Thirty-six offerings from Furnos.
EDCS-13900059.summary: This is a dedication to the emperor Constantius, possibly related to a religious cult or offering.

EDCS-13900455.text_edition: \n Flabius sic Saicham bn Macharcum Sonmon tribynus bymysiyrir aban Machrus useb\n
EDCS-13900455.translation: Flabius erected this. Saicham, son of Macharcus Sonmon, tribunes.
EDCS-13900455.summary: This inscription records a dedication or construction by Flabius, mentioning military tribunes named Saicham and others.

EDCS-13900478.text_edition: \n Sabinae Augustae Hadriani Augusti decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-13900478.translation: To Sabina Augusta, wife of Hadrian Augustus. By decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-13900478.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument to Empress Sabina at public expense, by order of the local council.

EDCS-14200004.translation: To Aurelius Caesar, son of Antoninus Augustus Pius. Vitalis, his freedman (?), home-born, X months old.
EDCS-14200004.summary: This is a dedication to Aurelius Caesar, likely a young imperial prince, by a freedman's household slave.

EDCS-14200010.text_edition: \n Dis sacrum Manibus Claudius Stiddin monimentum sic fecit se vivo posterius suis Dis sacrum Manibus Claudius Ladas monimentum sic fecit se vivo posterisque suis\n
EDCS-14200010.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Claudius Stiddin made this monument for himself while alive, and for his descendants later. Claudius Ladas made this monument for himself while alive, and for his descendants.
EDCS-14200010.summary: This is a funerary monument inscription recording the construction of a tomb by Claudius Stiddin and Claudius Ladas for themselves and their families.

EDCS-14200016.text_edition: \n Incomparabilis virtutis et innocentiae viro Lucio Clodio Clodiano veterano ex decurione qui vixit annis XLVIIII mensibus VIII Valeria Processa uxor et Clodius Victor Clodianus Processus V emeritus patri piissimo\n
EDCS-14200016.translation: To a man of incomparable virtue and integrity, Lucius Clodius Clodianus, a veteran ex-decurion, who lived 49 years and 8 months. His wife Valeria Processa and his son Clodius Victor Clodianus Processus V emeritus, to their most devoted father.
EDCS-14200016.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Lucius Clodius Clodianus, a veteran, set up by his wife and son.

EDCS-14200020.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus sacrum Balynae vixsit sic annorum sic XXXVIII Caius Caecilius Lupus uxori suae obsequentissimae feccit sic structorique sic\n
EDCS-14200020.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Balyna lived 38 years. Caius Caecilius Lupus made this for his most obedient wife and for the builder.
EDCS-14200020.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Balyna, set up by her husband Caius Caecilius Lupus, also mentioning the builder.

EDCS-14200021.text_edition: \n IV militabo in laboro obsequius per quae ista omnia perfecta sunt a me sic FV ARE felix Vrbanus Donatinaq(ue) eius qui patrem exuperant\n
EDCS-14200021.translation: I will serve and labor diligently, by which all these things were accomplished by me. Felix Urbanus and his Donatina. (These children) surpass their father.
EDCS-14200021.summary: This is a dedication, possibly to a deity or for a public work, by Felix Urbanus and his wife Donatina, celebrating their children who surpass their father.

EDCS-14200131.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto tribunicia potestate VIIII imperatori consuli II patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate IIII et Lucio Septimio [Getae] Caesari Augusto Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augustorum consulare legionis III Augustae piae vindicis\n
EDCS-14200131.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, and to Lucius Septimius [Geta] Caesar Augustus. To Quintus Anicius Faustus, consular legate of the 3rd Augustan Legion, pious and avenging.
EDCS-14200131.summary: This is a dedication to the emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, and to the legate Quintus Anicius Faustus, likely on a military monument or building.

EDCS-14200135.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepote Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate IIII consule III Quintus Servilius Candidus sua inpensa aquam quaesitam et elevatam in coloniam perduxit\n
EDCS-14200135.translation: While Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, nephew of the deified Nerva, was pontifex maximus, consul for the 3rd time, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, Quintus Servilius Candidus, at his own expense, brought water, which had been sought and raised, into the colony.
EDCS-14200135.summary: This inscription records that Quintus Servilius Candidus funded the bringing of a water supply to a colony during the reign of Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-14200138.text_edition: \n Heraclii Dignissimo principali innocentissimo puero Tito Flavio Vibiano Iuniori pontifici duumviro filio ac collegae Titi Flavi Frontini Heraclii in parvulis annis exhibenti aequaliter voluptatum genera patris sui studiis populi sufragio et decreto ordinis Divina stirpe progenito domino nostro fortissimo principi Valentiniano victori Pio Felici ac triumphatori semper Augusto Flavius Vivius Benedictus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Tripolitanae numini maiestatique eius semper devotus\n
EDCS-14200138.translation: To Heraclius, the most worthy, preeminent, and most innocent boy, pontiff and duumvir, son and colleague of Titus Flavius Frontinus Heraclius, showing in his young years the same pleasures as his father, by the people's suffrage and the decree of the order. Descended from a divine line, to our lord, the most brave prince, Valentinian, victorious, pious, happy, triumphant, always Augustus. Flavius Vivius Benedictus, the most perfect man, governor of the province of Tripolitana, always devoted to his divinity and majesty.
EDCS-14200138.summary: This is a dedication to a young boy, Titus Flavius Vibianus Iunior, who held priestly and civic offices, by the governor Flavius Vivius Benedictus, honoring him and the emperor Valentinian.

EDCS-14200141.text_edition: \n <T=I>(ito) Axio Quirina pro liberis Celeri Tito Axio Titi filio Quirina \n
EDCS-14200141.translation: Titus Axios Quirinus, for his children, Celer, Titus Axios, son of Titus, Quirinus.
EDCS-14200141.summary: This inscription likely refers to the dedication of a monument or tomb for children named Celer and Titus Axios, by their father Titus Axios Quirinus.

EDCS-14200148.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto patre patriae et Imperatore Caesare Lucio Aurelio Vero Armeniaco Augusto Servius Cornelius Scipio Salvidienus Orfitus proconsul cum Uttedio Marcello legato suo dedicavit Caius Calpurnius Celsus curator muneris publici munerarius IIvir quinquennalis flamen perpetuus arcum pecunia sua solo publico et fundavit et marmore solido fecit\n
EDCS-14200148.translation: While Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, father of the fatherland, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Armeniacus Augustus were in office, Servius Cornelius Scipio Salvidienus Orfitus, proconsul, with his legate Uttedius Marcellus, dedicated. Caius Calpurnius Celsus, curator of the public duty, twice munerarius, duumvir for five years, perpetual priest, founded and built the arch from his own money on public land with solid marble.
EDCS-14200148.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an arch, built and funded by Caius Calpurnius Celsus, during the joint reign of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, and the proconsulship of Servius Cornelius Scipio Salvidienus Orfitus.

EDCS-14200155.translation: To Gaius Caecilius Claudius Aelianus, perpetual suffete and flaminius, honored for his excellent service in public office and the curial position with integrity, and for his patriotism and diligent service in Rome, which brought him the highest praise from consular witnesses of distinguished repute; the town council of Gigthi, pleased by his gratuitous services, decreed that a statue should be erected, but he himself, content with the honor of the decree, arranged for its construction at his own expense.
EDCS-14200155.summary: This is a decree from the town council of Gigthi honoring Gaius Caecilius Claudius Aelianus for his integrity in public office and his service in Rome, and recording their decision to erect a statue in his honor.

EDCS-14400007.text_edition: \n Hic posuit cineres genetrix castissima prolis Augustine tui altera lux meriti qui servans pacis caelestia iura sacerdos commissos populos moribus instituis gloria vos maior gestorum laude coronat virtutum mater felicior suboles\n
EDCS-14400007.translation: Here lies the ashes of Augustine, your most chaste mother, the other light of your merit; who, as a priest observing the heavenly laws of peace, instructed the entrusted peoples in morals; your glory, greater than the rewards of deeds, crowns you, happier mother, with the praise of virtues, you, the more fruitful offspring.
EDCS-14400007.summary: This is a funerary epigram, likely from an epitaph, praising a deceased mother, Augustine, and her son, a priest, highlighting their virtues and spiritual roles.

EDCS-14900008.translation: With the aqueduct brought, under the administration of Annius Rufus, a most distinguished man, who manages Thysdrus through the Emperor's generosity, supplying the colony and the public squares, and distributing water to cisterns, and granting it to homes under specific conditions, this was dedicated through the provident foresight of a happy age and the inspiration of the powerful Mercury, patron and protector of the colony Thysdrus.
EDCS-14900008.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an aqueduct system in Thysdrus, completed under the administration of Annius Rufus and attributed to divine inspiration.

EDCS-14900018.text_edition: \n Lucio Terentio Aquilae Grattiano quaestori provinciae Africae amici ob parem in universos aequitatem et proprium in singulos honorem\n
EDCS-14900018.translation: To Lucius Terentius Aquila Gratianus, quaestor of the province of Africa, from his friends, for his fairness towards all and his particular regard for each individual.
EDCS-14900018.summary: This is a dedication, likely on a monument or statue base, from friends to Lucius Terentius Aquila Gratianus, a quaestor in Africa, honoring his fairness and individual regard.

EDCS-14900019.translation: From Hydatius: To Titus Flavius Umbricius Antistius Saturninus Fortunatianus, a most distinguished man and patron, from Aemilius M[e]sis and his associates.
EDCS-14900019.summary: This is a dedication to Titus Flavius Umbricius Antistius Saturninus Fortunatianus, a distinguished patron, likely from Aemilius M[e]sis and his group.

EDCS-14900021.translation: Deodatus erected this to the memory of Cassia, his most worthy wife and mother of his sons.
EDCS-14900021.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Deodatus to his wife Cassia, emphasizing her virtues as a spouse and mother.

EDCS-14900026.text_edition: \n Publio Sulpicio Quirinio Cai Valgio consulibus senatus populusque civitatium stipendiariorum pago Gurzenses hospitium fecerunt quom sic Lucio Domitio Cnaei filio Luci nepoti Ahenobarbo proconsuli eumque et postereis sic eius sibi posterisque sueis sic patronum cooptaverunt isque eos posterosque eorum in fidem clientelamque suam recepit faciundum coeraverunt Ammicar Milchatonis filius Cynasyn Boncar Azzrubalis filius Aethogursensis Muthunbal Saphonis filius Cui Nas Uzitensis\n
EDCS-14900026.translation: In the consulship of Publius Sulpicius Quirinius and Caius Valgius, the senate and people of the allied communities of the Gurzenses district established a guest-friendship with Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus, son of Gnaeus, grandson of Lucius, proconsul, and adopted him and his descendants as patron for themselves and their descendants, and he received them and their descendants into his protection and patronage; Ammicar, son of Milchaton; Cynasyn; Boncar, son of Azzrubal; Aethogursensis; Muthunbal, son of Saphon; Cui Nas; Uzitensis undertook its construction.
EDCS-14900026.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a guest-friendship and patronage relationship between the Gurzenses district and the proconsul Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus, detailing the agreements and the individuals responsible for its construction.

EDCS-14900078.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Gaius Mummius Maximus lived 56 years. May your bones rest well, under the care of Aelius Saturninus. Aelius Saturninus lived 59 years, under the care of [---].
EDCS-14900078.summary: This is a double tombstone inscription recording the ages of Gaius Mummius Maximus and Aelius Saturninus, with a wish for their peaceful rest, and indicating the person who arranged the burials.

EDCS-14900165.text_edition: \n Sint licet exiguae fugentia tempora vitae parvaque raptorum cito transeat hora dierum mergat et Elysiis mortalia corpora terris adsidue rupto Lachesis male conscia penso iam tamen inventa est blandae rationis imago per quam prolatos homines in tempora plura longior excipiat memoratio multaque servet secum per titulos mansuris fortius annis ecce recens pietas omni placitura favore ingentem famae numerum cum laude meretur exemplo iam plena novo quam Flavius alto more Secundus agens patrio signavit honore quis non iam pronis animi virtutibus adsit quis non hoc miretur opus fusasque videndo divitias stupeat tantos se cernere census per quos aetherias surgunt monimenta per auras haec est fortunae melius laudanda facultas sic sibi perpetuas faciunt impendia sedes sic immortales scit habere pecunia mores aeterno quotiens stabilis bene figitur usu viderit ille furor nimio qui ducitur auro quem trahit argenti venalis sanguine candor viderit et fusae vanis in amoribus errans gloria luxuriae peregrinas quaerere magno quae didicit vestes gemmasque nitore placentes aut ab Aeruthreo venientia munera fluctu quam laedunt gentes vario certamine rerum Graecia cum pueris Hispania Pallados usu venatu Lybiae tellus orientis amomo Aegyptos Phariis levitatibus artibus actis Gallia semper ovans dives Campania vino haec cito deficiunt et habent breve munus amoris momentis damnata suis sed si quis ad omnes respiciat vitae casus hominemque laboret metiri brevitate sua tunc credere discet nil aliut sic melius fieri nisi viribus aevi quot sic possit durare diu sub honore deorum nunc ego non dubitem tacitis Acherontos in umbris si post Fata manent sensus gaudere parentem saepe Secunde tuum reliquas et spernere turmas quod sciat hic tantam faciem superesse sepulchri perpetua novitate sui sic stare nitentes consensus lapidum sic de radice levatos in melius crevisse gradus ut et angulus omnis sic quasi mollitae ductus sit stamine cerae mobilibus signis hilaris scalptura novatur et licet atsidue sic probet hos vaga turba decores lucentes stupeat pariter pendere columnas quit sic cum militiae titulos ipsumque parentem numinibus dederis haec gaudia saepe videntem quae quondam dedit ipse loco dum munera Bacchi multa creat primasque cupit componere vites et nemus exornat revocatis saepius undis permittant mihi fata loqui noctisque timendae regnator Stygius sic immortalis haberi iam debet pater ecce tuus Ditisque relicti tristem deservisse domum dum tempore toto mavolt sic haec monumenta sequi scriptisque per aevom vivere nominibus solitis insistere lucis adsidue patrias hinc cernere dulciter arces quosque dedit natis prope semper habere Penates forsitan haec multi vano sermone ferentes venturae citius dicant praesagia mortis si quis dum vivit ponat monimenta sic futuris temporibus mihi non tales sunt pectore sensus set sic puto securos fieri quicumque parare aeternam volvere domum certoque rigore numquam lapsuros vitae defigere muros fatis certa via est neque se per stamina mutat Atropos ut primo coepit decurrere filo crede Secunde mihi pensatos ibis in annos set sic securus eris set sic toto pectore dives dum nulli gravis esse potes nec plena labore testamenta facis tuus hoc dum non timet heres ut sic aedificet iam nunc quodcumque relinques totum perveniet tua quo volet ire voluntas sed revocat me cura operis celsique decores stat sublimis honor vicinaque nubila pulsat et solis metitur iter si iungere montes forte velint oculi vincuntur in ordine colles si videas campos infra iacet abdita tellus non sic Romuleas exire colossos in arces dicitur aut circi medias obeliscus in auras nec sic sistrigeri demonstrat pervia Nili dum sua perspicuis aperit Pharos aequora flammis quid non docta facit pietas lapis ecce foratus luminibus multis hortatur currere blandas intus apes et cerineos componere nidos ut semper domus haec Thymbraeo nectare dulcis sudet florisapos dum dant nova mella liquores huc iterum pietas venerandas erige mentes et mea quo nosti carmina more fove ecce Secundus adest iterum qui pectore sancto non monimenta patri sed nova templa dedit quo nunc Calliope gemino me limite cogis quas iam transegi rursus adire vias nempe fuit nobis operis descriptio magni diximus et iunctis saxa polita locis circuitus nemorum currentes dulciter undas atque sic reportantes mella frequenter apes hoc tamen hoc solum nostrae puto defuit arti dum cadis ad multos ebria Musa iocos in summo tremulas galli non diximus alas altior extrema qui puto nube volat cuius si membris vocem natura dedisset cogeret hic omnes surgere mane deos et iam nominibus signantur limina certis cernitur et titulis credula vita suis opto Secunde geras multos feliciter annos et quae fecisti tu monimenta legas Titus Flavius Secundus filius fecit Tito Flavio Secundo patri pio militavit annos XXXIII vixit annos CX hic situs est Flaviae Urbanae matri piae vixit annos CV hic sita est Flaviae Secundae sorori piae vixit annos XX hic sita est Tito Flavio Marcello fratri pio vixit annos XX hic situs est Tito Flavio Martiali fratri militavit annos XII vixit annos XXXV hic situs est Flaviae Speratae sorori piae vixit annos XXXVI hic sita est Aemiliae Sexti filiae Pacatae uxori piae flaminicae perpetuae vixit annos LIII hic sita est Titus Flavius Titi filius Papiria Secundus ipse flamen perpetuus vixit annos LX hic situs est Flaviae Titi filiae Pacatae flaminicae perpetuae coloniae Theleptensis filiae piae Flavia Libera mater statuam posuit vixit annos XV menses X hic sita est Flavia Libera Titi Flavi Secundi uxor pia vixit annos LXXXVIII hic sita est Flavia Faustina pia vixit annos XXXVIII hic sita est Titus Flavius Faustinus et Flavia Victoria parentes posuerunt Flavia Libera pia vixit annos XVI menses VI Titus Flavius Faustinus et Flavia Victoria parentes posuerunt hic sita est Titus Flavius Papiria Receptus aedilicius quaestor aerarii decurio coloniae Theleptensis pius vixit annos XXXVI hic situs est Titus Flavius Faustinus et Flavia Victoria parentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-14900165.translation: Though life's fleeting moments be brief, and the hour of swift days pass quickly, and though the body sinks to the Elysian fields, and Lachesis incessantly, unaware, snaps the unjust thread, yet now an image of gentle reason has been found, through which those men, granted longer time, might be received into future eras, and much might be preserved within, stronger than years through lasting inscriptions. Behold, a recent piety, pleasing to all with favor, earns immense fame and praise through a new example, which Flavius Secundus, following his father's custom, has marked with honor. Who would not then incline to his noble virtues? Who would not admire this work and, seeing the poured-out wealth, marvel at such fortunes by which monuments rise to the heavens through the air? This is fortune's faculty, better to be praised, thus they make eternal seats for themselves, thus money knows how to have immortal qualities, whenever it is firmly fixed to eternal use. Let him see that madness which is led by excessive gold, let him see that which is drawn by the venal brightness of silver, let him see and the glory of luxury, wandering in vain loves, seeking foreign garments and gems shining with brilliance, or gifts from the Erythraean waves, how they harm nations in varied competition of affairs: Greece with its boys, Spain with its use of Pallas, Libya with its hunting, the East with its perfume, Egypt with its fleeting tricks, Gaul always rejoicing, wealthy Campania with wine. These quickly pass and have a brief reward of love, condemned by moments. But if anyone looks at the chances of life and labors to measure man by his brevity, then he will learn that nothing else is so well done as to endure through the forces of age. Whoever can last so long under the honor of the gods. Now I shall not doubt, in the silent shades of Acheron, if feeling remains after death, that your father, Secundus, rejoices and scorns the other crowds, knowing that such a splendor of tomb survives through its perpetual novelty, that the gleaming stones stand thus, raised from their foundation, that the steps have grown better, as if every corner were shaped from softened wax, and is renewed by mobile designs and cheerful carving. And although the wandering crowd may constantly approve these ornaments, marvel at the shining columns, yet you, Secundus, when you give these honors to the gods, you will often see these joys, which he himself once gave, creating many gifts of Bacchus, arranging vines, and adorning the grove with frequently recalled waters. May the fates permit me to speak, and may the ruler of the dreaded night and Stygian realm grant that your father may now be considered immortal. Behold, he has left the sad home of Dis, preferring throughout all time to follow these monuments and live through inscriptions for the ages, to stand by familiar names, to see his ancestral citadels with delight, and to always have near him the Penates granted to his children. Perhaps many, speaking vainly, will call these omens of approaching death, if someone erects monuments while alive. But I have no such feelings in my heart; rather, I think that whoever prepares to build an eternal home and, with certain rigor, to establish walls of life that will never fall, becomes secure. Fate has a certain path and Atropos does not change herself through the threads. Believe me, Secundus, you will go into calculated years, but you will be secure and rich in heart, as long as you can displease no one and make your wills not full of labor, so that your heir does not fear that whatever you leave will be built. Everything will go where you wish your will to go. But the care of the work calls me back, and the lofty adornments. The honor stands sublimely, touching the neighboring clouds, and measures the sun's course. If eyes wished to join the mountains, the hills are overcome in order. If you see the plains, the earth below lies hidden. Not thus do the colossal structures of Rome emerge into citadels, nor does the obelisk in the center of the Circus reach the sky, nor does the lighthouse of the Nile, while revealing the seas with its clear flames, show the way. What does learned piety not achieve? Behold, this stone, pierced with many lights, urges bees to run inside and arrange their honeycombs, so that this house may always flow sweet with Thymbraean nectar, while flowers give new honeyed liquids. Here again, piety, lift your venerable minds, and foster my songs in the familiar way. Behold, Secundus is here again, who in his holy heart gave not monuments to his father, but new temples. To which path do you, Calliope, compel me now to return with a double path? Indeed, we described a great work, and polished stones joined in places, surrounding groves, flowing waters, and bees frequently carrying honey. Yet, I think only this was missing from my art, while my drunken Muse indulged in many jests; we did not describe the trembling wings of the hawk on the summit, which, I think, flies above the highest cloud, whose voice, if nature had given it limbs, would compel all the gods to rise in the morning. And now the thresholds are marked with names, and credulous life is discerned by its own titles. I wish you, Secundus, to live many happy years, and to read the monuments you have made. Titus Flavius Secundus, son, made this for his pious father Titus Flavius Secundus, who served in the army for 33 years and lived 110 years; he lies here. For his pious mother Flavia Urbana, who lived 105 years; she lies here. For his pious sister Flavia Secunda, who lived 20 years; she lies here. For his pious brother Titus Flavius Marcellus, who served in the army for 12 years and lived 35 years; he lies here. For his pious sister Flavia Sperata, who lived 36 years; she lies here. For his pious wife, the perpetual flamninica, Aemilia Pacata, daughter of Sextus, who lived 53 years; she lies here. Titus Flavius Secundus Papirius, himself perpetual flamen, lived 60 years; he lies here. For Flavia Pacata, daughter of Titus, perpetual flamninica of the colony of Thelepte, a pious daughter; Flavia Libera, mother, erected the statue; she lived 15 years and 10 months; she lies here. Flavia Libera, wife of Titus Flavius Secundus, a pious woman, lived 88 years; she lies here. Flavia Faustina, a pious woman, lived 38 years; she lies here. Titus Flavius Faustinus and Flavia Victoria, parents, erected this. Flavia Libera, a pious woman, lived 16 years and 6 months; Titus Flavius Faustinus and Flavia Victoria, parents, erected this; she lies here. Titus Flavius Papirius Receptus, of the aediles' rank, quaestor of the treasury, decurion of the colony of Thelepte, a pious man, lived 36 years; he lies here. Titus Flavius Faustinus and Flavia Victoria, parents, erected this.
EDCS-14900165.summary: This extensive funerary monument commemorates multiple members of the Flavius Secundus family, including the father, son (who likely commissioned the monument), wife, siblings, and children, detailing their lives, military service, and religious offices, with elaborate verses praising the piety and legacy of the family, especially Titus Flavius Secundus.

EDCS-14900270.text_edition: \n Senatus consultum de nundinis saltus Beguensis in territorio Casensi descriptum et recognitum ex libro sententiarum in senatu dictarum Kani Iuni Nigri Cai Pomponi Camerini consulum in quo scripta erant Africani iura et id quod infra scriptum est Idibus Octobribus in comitiorum sic in curia Iulia scribundo adfuerunt Quintus Gargilius Quinti filius Quirina Antiquus Tiberius Claudius Tiberi filius Palatina Quartinus Caius Oppius Cai filius Velina Severus Caius Herennius Cai filius Palatina Caecilianus Marcus Iulius Marci filius Quirina Clarus Publius Cassius Publi filius Claudia Dexter quaestor Publius Nonius Marci filius Oufentina Macrinus quaestor in senatu fuerunt CC senatus consultum per discessionem factum quod Publius Cassius Secundus Publius Delphius Peregrinus Alfius Alennius Maximus Curtius Valerianus Proculus Marcus Nonius Mucianus conssules verba fecerunt desiderio amicorum Lucili Africani clarissimi viri qui petunt ut ei permittantur in provincia Africa regione Beguensi territorio Musulamiorum ad Casas nundinas IIII Nonas Novembres et XII Kalendas Decembres ex eo omnibus mensibus IIII Nonas et XII Kalendas sui cuiusque mensis instituere habere quid fieri placeret de ea re ita censuerunt permittendum Lucilio Africano clarissimo viro in provincia Africa regione Beguensi territorio Musulamiorum ad Casas nundinas IIII Nonas Novembres et XII Kalendas Decembres et ex eo omnibus mensibus IIII Nonas et XII Kalendas sui cuiusque mensis instituere et habere eoque vicinis advenisque nundinandi dumtaxat causa coire convenire sine iniuria et incommodo cuiusquam liceat actum Idibus Octobribus Publio Cassio Secundo Marco Nonio Muciano eodem exemplo de eadem re duae tabellae signatae sunt signatores Titi Flavi Comini scribae Cai Iuli Fortunati scribae Marci Caesi Helvi Euhelpisti Quinti Metili Onesimi Cai Iuli Periblepti Luci Verati Philerotis Titi Flavi Crescentis\n
EDCS-14900270.translation: A decree of the Senate concerning the market days of the Beguensis estate in the territory of Casensi, inscribed and reviewed from the record of decisions made in the Senate during the consulship of Kanius Junius Niger and Caius Pomponius Camerinus. In it were written the rights of Lucilius Africanus and what is written below. On the Ides of October, in the Julian Curia, the following were present for the recording: Quintus Gargilius Antiquus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe; Tiberius Claudius Quartinus, son of Tiberius, of the Palatina tribe; Caius Oppius Severus, son of Caius, of the Velina tribe; Caius Herennius Caecilianus, son of Caius, of the Palatina tribe; Marcus Julius Clarus, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe; Publius Cassius Dexter, son of Publius, of the Claudia tribe, quaestor; Publius Nonius Macrinus, son of Marcus, of the Oufentina tribe, quaestor. There were 200 senators present. A decree of the Senate was made by division, because Publius Cassius Secundus, Publius Delphius Peregrinus, Alfius Alennius Maximus, Curtius Valerianus Proculus, and Marcus Nonius Mucianus, consuls, spoke at the request of the friends of Lucilius Africanus, a most distinguished man, who asks that he be permitted to establish two market days in the province of Africa, in the Beguensis region, in the territory of the Musulamii, near Casas: the Nones of November and the Kalends of December, and thereafter on the fourth day before the Nones and the twelfth day before the Kalends of each month, and to hold them. Regarding this matter, they decreed as follows: It shall be permitted to Lucilius Africanus, a most distinguished man, in the province of Africa, in the Beguensis region, in the territory of the Musulamii, near Casas, to establish and hold market days on the fourth day before the Nones of November and the twelfth day before the Kalends of December, and thereafter on the fourth day before the Nones and the twelfth day before the Kalends of each month, and that neighbors and visitors may come together for the sole purpose of trading, without injury or inconvenience to anyone. This was acted upon on the Ides of October. Two tablets were signed by Publius Cassius Secundus and Marcus Nonius Mucianus, on the same principle concerning the same matter. Signatories: Titi Flavi Comini, scribe; Cai Iuli Fortunati, scribe; Marci Caesi Helvi Euhelpisti; Quinti Metili Onesimi; Cai Iuli Periblepti; Luci Verati Philerotis; Titi Flavi Crescentis.
EDCS-14900270.summary: This inscription records a Senate decree from the consulship of Kanius Junius Niger and Caius Pomponius Camerinus, granting permission to Lucilius Africanus to establish two market days per month in a specific region of the province of Africa, detailing the process, participants, and signatories.

EDCS-14900319.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Festula lived 24 years, 9 months, and 18 days. Herennius Victor, with his sons, placed this for his most beloved wife. She is laid here.
EDCS-14900319.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelia Festula, placed by her husband Herennius Victor and sons, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14900320.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Fortunata lived 43 years and 8 months. She is laid here. Lucius Cascellius Rufus placed this for his most pious and chaste wife. To the Spirits of the Departed.
EDCS-14900320.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelia Fortunata, erected by her husband Lucius Cascellius Rufus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14900321.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Aelius Naphamos lived 38 years. His devoted wife placed this.
EDCS-14900321.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Aelius Naphamos, erected by his wife, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14900322.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Rogatus lived 66 years. He is laid here. Aelius Rogatus placed this for his devoted father.
EDCS-14900322.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius Rogatus, erected by his son Aelius Rogatus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14900324.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Hilara lived 22 years. Annius Severus placed this for his most unique wife. She is laid here. To the Spirits of the Departed. Annius Severus lived 32 years, and 11 days. He is laid here.
EDCS-14900324.summary: A double funerary epitaph recording the lives of Flavia Hilara and her husband Annius Severus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14900326.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Modia Saturnina lived 90 years. She is laid here. Her daughters placed this for their dearest mother. To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Atilius Macedo lived 70 years. He is laid here. Lucius Atilius Extricatus and Antilia placed this for their devoted father.
EDCS-14900326.summary: A double funerary epitaph for Modia Saturnina, erected by her daughters, and for Lucius Atilius Macedo, erected by his son and daughter, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14900327.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Antonia Hilara Modiana, daughter of Quintus, lived approximately 30 years. Magnianus placed this for his sister.
EDCS-14900327.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antonia Hilara Modiana, erected by her brother Magnianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14900328.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Camurenus Victoricus lived 70 years. Publius, his son, made this. To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Victorinus lived 22 years. His devoted brother made this. To the Spirits of the Departed. Antonia lived 55 years. Her devoted son made this.
EDCS-14900328.summary: A triple funerary epitaph recording the lives and dedications for Gaius Camurenus Victoricus, Gaius Victorinus, and Antonia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14900329.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Aquilius Suavis, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 75 years. His son placed this for his devoted father. He is laid here.
EDCS-14900329.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Aquilius Suavis, erected by his son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14900330.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Arria Sperata lived 53 years. She is laid here. Apuleius Licinianus placed this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-14900330.summary: A funerary epitaph for Arria Sperata, erected by her husband Apuleius Licinianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14900331.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Alia Victoria lived 55 years. Assidonius Victoricus set this up for his excellent mother. She is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Assidonius Ingenuus lived 18 years and 6 months. Assidonius Liberalis, a devoted father, set this up for his excellent son. He is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Assidonius Liberalis lived about 60 years. Assidonius Victoricus set this up for his dearest father. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900331.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the deaths and ages of three individuals: Alia Victoria, Assidonius Ingenuus, and Assidonius Liberalis, with familial relationships and dedications noted.

EDCS-14900332.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Assidonius Liberalis lived 47 years, 4 months, and 5 days. He is buried here. Assidonius Calvus set this up for his dearest brother, my lord.
EDCS-14900332.summary: This tombstone records the death of Assidonius Liberalis, who lived 47 years, 4 months, and 5 days, and was commemorated by his brother, Assidonius Calvus.

EDCS-14900333.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Assidonia Aerope, devout, lived 25 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-14900333.summary: This tombstone records the death of Assidonia Aerope, who lived 25 years, and was noted as devout.

EDCS-14900334.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aufidius Marius Castulus lived 25 years. He is buried here. A devoted father set this up.
EDCS-14900334.summary: This tombstone records the death of Aufidius Marius Castulus, who lived 25 years, and was commemorated by his father.

EDCS-14900337.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Aurelius Impetratus lived 50 years. His sons set this up for their devoted father. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900337.summary: This tombstone records the death of Quintus Aurelius Impetratus, who lived 50 years, and was commemorated by his sons.

EDCS-14900339.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Numisia Donata lived 34 years. She is buried here. Lucius Bolanus Felix set this up for his devout wife. Sacred to the departed spirits. Bolana Primula lived 52 years. She is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Bolanus Caelestinus lived 8 years. Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Bolanus lived about 3 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900339.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the deaths of four individuals from the Bolanus family: Numisia Donata, Bolana Primula, Lucius Bolanus Caelestinus, and Lucius Bolanus, with ages and familial relationships indicated.

EDCS-14900341.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Caelia Donata, devout, lived 100 years. Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Caecilius Ianuarius lived 90 years.
EDCS-14900341.summary: This inscription records the extreme longevity of two individuals, Caelia Donata (100 years) and Marcus Caecilius Ianuarius (90 years), likely on a shared monument or as separate entries.

EDCS-14900342.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Helvia Gemellina lived 50 years. Matrona set this up for her mother. Sacred to the departed spirits. Caelius Datus, a boy, lived 2 years. Matrona set this up. Sacred to the departed spirits. Helvia Maior lived 31 years. Matrona set this up.
EDCS-14900342.summary: This monument commemorates three individuals: Helvia Gemellina, Caelius Datus, and Helvia Maior, with ages and dedications by Matrona, suggesting family relationships.

EDCS-14900343.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Caelius Epicurus, devout, lived 32 years and 6 months. He is buried here. Publius Caelius Numisianus for his excellent brother.
EDCS-14900343.summary: This tombstone records the death of Lucius Caelius Epicurus, who lived 32 years and 6 months, and was commemorated by his brother, Publius Caelius Numisianus.

EDCS-14900344.translation: Marcus Caelius Quadratus, son of Publius, of the Arnensis tribe, lived 50 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900344.summary: This tombstone records the death of Marcus Caelius Quadratus, son of Publius, of the Arnensis tribe, who lived 50 years.

EDCS-14900346.translation: To the departed spirits. Sacred. Calpurnia Victorina, also called Faeda, lived 80 years. Gaius Julius Proculus, her husband, placed this for his dutiful wife. She is buried here. To the departed spirits. Sacred. Gaius Julius Proculus lived 101 years, 7 months. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900346.summary: A double tombstone recording the deaths of a wife, Calpurnia Victorina, and her husband, Gaius Julius Proculus, who both died and were buried in this location.

EDCS-14900349.translation: To the departed spirits. Sacred. Lucius Carvilius Albinus lived 100 years. To the departed spirits. Sacred. Carvilia Albina lived 18 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-14900349.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Carvilius Albinus and Carvilia Albina, likely father and daughter, recording their ages at death and burial.

EDCS-14900351.translation: To the departed spirits. Sacred. Lucius Cassius Faustus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 44 years, a good man and indispensable to people and affairs. He is sought by his country, the greatest people; the earth covers him. He left a happy but ungrateful life. Lucius Cassius, his brother, dutifully placed this.
EDCS-14900351.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Cassius Faustus, a man of importance to his community, erected by his brother.

EDCS-14900352.translation: To the departed spirits. Sacred. Claudius Lucanus Tzaninus lived 17 years, 10 months, and 5 days. Claudius Sextilius Lucanus and Claudia Novella placed this for their dearest son.
EDCS-14900352.summary: A tombstone erected by Claudius Sextilius Lucanus and Claudia Novella for their son, Claudius Lucanus Tzaninus, who died at the age of 17.

EDCS-14900353.translation: To the departed spirits. Sacred. Tiberius Claudius Victor lived 70 years. Agria Rogata placed this for her sweetest husband. He is buried here. Whoever lives will live forever.
EDCS-14900353.summary: A tombstone for Tiberius Claudius Victor, erected by his wife Agria Rogata, with a pious inscription about eternal life.

EDCS-14900354.translation: To the departed spirits. Sacred. Cluvius Donatus lived 31 years. His parents placed this for their son. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900354.summary: A tombstone for Cluvius Donatus, erected by his parents after his death at the age of 31.

EDCS-14900358.translation: To the departed spirits. Sacred. Cornelia Clodiana lived 3 years, 8 months, and 25 days. Her dutiful father placed this for his sweetest daughter.
EDCS-14900358.summary: A tombstone erected by a father for his young daughter, Cornelia Clodiana, who died at the age of three.

EDCS-14900359.translation: To the departed spirits. Sacred. Cornelia Crispina lived 52 years. She is buried here. Gaius Pinarius Vitalis placed this for his dutiful mother.
EDCS-14900359.summary: A tombstone for Cornelia Crispina, erected by her son Gaius Pinarius Vitalis, recording her age at death.

EDCS-14900360.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Cornelia Licinia sacerdos Cererum publica vixit annos LXV hic sita est filii pii posuerunt\n
EDCS-14900360.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Cornelia Licinia, priestess of Ceres, lived 65 years. She is buried here. Her dutiful sons placed this.
EDCS-14900360.summary: A tombstone for Cornelia Licinia, a priestess of Ceres, erected by her sons, recording her age at death.
