EDCS-17700377.text_edition: \n Hymetii Caio Iulio Regino decurioni Karthaginis aedili IIviro quinquennalicio gentis Severianae Alexandrianae Augustae flamini flamini perpetuo curatori splendidissimae reipublicae Thimidensium Regiorum ordo decurionum ex sportulis suis ob merita dono dedit\n
EDCS-17700377.translation: To Gaius Julius Reginus of Hymetii, councilor, aedile, duovir for five years, of the Severian Alexandrian Augustan tribe, perpetual priest, curator of the most distinguished republic of the Thimidensian Regians, the council of decurions gave this gift from their sportulae for his merits.
EDCS-17700377.summary: A dedicatory inscription honoring Gaius Julius Reginus, a high-ranking local official in Thimidis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700378.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Prince of Youth, with tribunician power for the second time, consul, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and great-great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva. Gnaeus Cal[---] [---]
EDCS-17700378.summary: A fragmentary imperial titulature inscription, likely recording titles of Caracalla and possibly Severus, dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-17700408.translation: Public vows. For the two-year term, administered by Quintus Mycius Endulcentius, perpetual priest, curator of the republic of the Biienses. [---] a temple begun many ages ago, from the ground up, in the two-year term of Gaius Aurelius Galosus and Marcus Favonius Macedon, completed in the year of the duovirate of Splendon Restutianus and Publius Aurelius Robur. Expenses partly from public funds, partly from offerings. [---] dedicated by Victorinus, a man of consular rank, governor of the province of Valeria Byzacena, with the care of [---] Arrius Secundianus. By sacred indulgence of our lords Constantius and Constans, the greatest Augusti.
EDCS-17700408.summary: A lengthy inscription detailing the completion and dedication of a public temple in Biienses, funded by public money and offerings, completed under the reign of Constantius and Constans, dating to the mid-4th century AD.

EDCS-17700470.translation: During the consulship of Gaius Caesar, Imperator, for the fourth time. Lucius Pomponius Malchus (?), freedman of Lucius, duovir, oversaw the construction of the entire town wall made of squared stone.
EDCS-17700470.summary: An inscription recording the construction of a town wall by the duovir Lucius Pomponius Malchus, likely dating to the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-17700471.translation: During the consulship of Marcus Appuleius and Publius Silius. As duovir for five years, Gnaeus Domitius Malchio. As aediles, Lucius Sertorius Alexander and Lucius Vitruvius Alexander constructed a permanent platform, two porticoes, a sundial, [---] and improved the road.
EDCS-17700471.summary: An inscription recording public works, including a permanent platform, porticoes, a sundial, and road improvement, funded by the duovir Gnaeus Domitius Malchio and aediles Lucius Sertorius Alexander and Lucius Vitruvius Alexander, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-17700472.text_edition: \n Publius Attius Publi filius Vaarus sic legatus pro praetore Caius Considius Cai filius Longus legatus pro praetore murum turres posteicuus sic fossam faciundum coeraverunt Titus Tettius Titi filius Velina praefectus\n
EDCS-17700472.translation: Publius Attius Varus, son of Publius, legate pro praetore. Gaius Considius Longus, son of Caius, legate pro praetore. They oversaw the construction of the wall, towers, and rear fosse. Titus Tettius Velina, son of Titus, prefect.
EDCS-17700472.summary: An inscription recording the construction of a defensive wall, towers, and fosse by two legates pro praetore and a prefect, likely in a frontier context, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700839.translation: To our Lord Flavius Constantinus Maximus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus. Domitius Latronianus, clarissimus vir, proconsul of the province of Africa, and Vettius Piso Severus, clarissimus vir, curator of the republic of Carthage, ever most devoted to his divinity.
EDCS-17700839.summary: An inscription dedicated to Emperor Constantine the Great by the proconsul of Africa and the curator of Carthage, dating to the 4th century AD.

EDCS-17700849.translation: To the departed spirits. Restored by T. Nepius Velocis, his heir, for his freedman Rufrius Adiutor, son of Decimus, of the Oufentina tribe, from Milan. He served in the 13th urban cohort, in the century of Pubilius, for 15 years. He lived for 40 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-17700849.summary: This is a tombstone for Rufrius Adiutor, a soldier in the urban cohort, paid for by his heir.

EDCS-17700850.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Vilanius Quinti filius Voltinia Nepos Philippis |(centurio) cohortis XIII urbanae donis donatus a Domitiano ob bellum Dacicum item ab eodem ob bellum Germanicum item torquibus armillis ob bellum Dacicum vixit nnos L militavit annos XXXII Marcus Silius Quintianus optio bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-17700850.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Vilanius Nepos, son of Quintus, of the Voltinia tribe, a centurion in the 13th urban cohort, honored with gifts by Domitian for the Dacian War and also for the German War, and also with torques and armillae for the Dacian War. He lived for 50 years and served for 32 years. Marcus Silius Quintianus, optio, erected this for the deserving.
EDCS-17700850.summary: This tombstone commemorates Quintus Vilanius Nepos, a decorated centurion of the urban cohort, erected by his optio.

EDCS-17700851.translation: To the departed spirits. Antigona will live and feast. Vitalis, the courier of our Emperor, will live and feast. While I, Vitalis, am alive and well, I made this tomb; and as I pass through and read these lines, I myself, having circumnavigated the whole land region with diplomas and caught hares and foxes with dogs, and afterward willingly enjoyed drinks and feasts, having done many things in youth because I am about to die, whoever of you young men is wise, erect a tomb for yourself while alive.
EDCS-17700851.summary: This is an unusual funerary epigram by Vitalis, a courier, who erected his own tombstone and urges the living to do the same.

EDCS-17700854.translation: To the departed spirits. Aelia Rufina lived for 43 years.
EDCS-17700854.summary: This is a simple tombstone for Aelia Rufina, recording her name and age at death.

EDCS-17700855.translation: To the departed spirits. For Lucius Antonius Martialis, most devoted brother. [Restored text implies a dedication.]
EDCS-17700855.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated to Lucius Antonius Martialis, their most devoted brother, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17700857.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Aufidius Maximus, pious, lived for 70 years. May the earth be light for you, may your bones rest well.
EDCS-17700857.summary: This is a tombstone for Caius Aufidius Maximus, recording his name, piety, age at death, and a standard closing formula.

EDCS-17700859.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Caecilius Paulinus, son of Marcus, of the Arnensis tribe, pious, lived for 23 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-17700859.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Caius Caecilius Paulinus, a young man, including his tribe and age.

EDCS-17700860.translation: To the departed spirits. Commorianus, the pious, lived for 75 years. He is buried here. May your bones rest well, may the earth be light for you.
EDCS-17700860.summary: This is a tombstone for Commorianus, noting his piety and age at death, with a common closing formula.

EDCS-17700861.translation: To the departed spirits. Caelia Prima lived for 40 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-17700861.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Caelia Prima, giving her name and age at death.

EDCS-17700863.translation: Caesetia Ingenua, daughter of Marcus. [This monument] for Caesetia Ingenua, daughter of Marcus, who is also buried here. She lived for 13 years, whose bones are in the altar of the monument within the enclosure. The rest is [illegible] outside, where he lies. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-17700863.summary: This is a tombstone for a young girl, Caesetia Ingenua, indicating the location of her remains and a standard closing.

EDCS-17700864.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sacred. To Caria Galla, daughter of Quintus. She lived seven years, ten months, and twelve days.
EDCS-17700864.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Caria Galla, who died at a young age, approximately in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700865.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sacred. [3]dia Cerealis, [3] pious, lived seventeen years.
EDCS-17700865.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the death of a woman named [3]dia Cerealis, who lived to be seventeen, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700867.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sacred. Concordia Quieta, pious, lived twenty-two years. She rests here. May her bones rest well.
EDCS-17700867.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Concordia Quieta, who died at age twenty-two, approximately in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700868.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sacred. Daphnis Candida, pious, lived thirty-five years. She rests here. Nicetes made this for his most pious companion.
EDCS-17700868.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Daphnis Candida, who lived to be thirty-five, and was set up by her companion Nicetes, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700869.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sacred. [3]s Felicio, lived [---] years.
EDCS-17700869.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the death of a person named Felicio, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700873.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sacred. Iulia Durmi[ana] lived twenty-one years. She rests here.
EDCS-17700873.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Iulia Durmi[ana], who lived to be twenty-one, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700874.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sacred. Licinius Crescens, pious, lived thirty-five years.
EDCS-17700874.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Licinius Crescens, who lived to be thirty-five, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700875.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sacred. Mania Certula, pious, lived thirty years. She rests here.
EDCS-17700875.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Mania Certula, who lived to be thirty, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700876.translation: Maximilla, daughter of Bassus, pious, lived nineteen years. She rests here.
EDCS-17700876.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Maximilla, daughter of Bassus, who died at the age of nineteen, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700878.translation: [3] Octavius Primus, innocent, lived twenty years and seven months.
EDCS-17700878.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the death of Octavius Primus, who lived twenty years and seven months, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700879.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Primigenius lived piously for 22 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-17700879.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age and burial of Primigenius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700882.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Rufus Bamseus lived for 60 years; he is buried here. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Secunda, daughter of Rogatianus, lived for 70 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-17700882.summary: A double funerary epitaph recording the ages and burials of Rufus Bamseus and Secunda, daughter of Rogatianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700886.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Saturninus lived for 50 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-17700886.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age and burial of Lucius Saturninus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700887.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Saturninus [died...]
EDCS-17700887.summary: An incomplete funerary epitaph that begins with a dedication to the spirits of the departed and the name Publius Saturninus.

EDCS-17700889.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Saturninus, son of Rufus Faustus, lived piously for 65 years; he is buried here. May your bones rest well. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-17700889.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age and burial of Saturninus, son of Rufus Faustus, and including a wish for his peaceful rest.

EDCS-17700890.translation: Marcus Silius Maximus made this tomb for his dearest wife. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Vibia Saturnina lived for 33 years, two months, and sixteen days.
EDCS-17700890.summary: A funerary inscription where Marcus Silius Maximus dedicates a tomb to his wife, Vibia Saturnina, who died at the age of 33.

EDCS-17700891.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sulvia Castula lived...
EDCS-17700891.summary: An incomplete funerary epitaph beginning with a dedication to the spirits of the departed and the name Sulvia Castula.

EDCS-17700892.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Sufenas Felix lived piously for [3?] years and [3?] days. He is buried here. May your bones rest well. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-17700892.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Sufenas Felix, recording his age in years and days, and including a wish for his peaceful rest.

EDCS-17700894.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Volussienus Pacatus Saturnianus, son of Proculus, lived for 2 years and 4 months. May it happen for you as you wish, that you do not violate this sacred vow.
EDCS-17700894.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Volussienus Pacatus Saturnianus, who died at a young age, with a curse against anyone who would disturb the grave.

EDCS-17700902.translation: [He] is buried piously. Lived for 15 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-17700902.summary: An incomplete funerary epitaph recording the age and burial of an individual, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17700915.translation: May Victorinus rest in peace and in harmony.
EDCS-17700915.summary: A short epitaph, likely from a tombstone, wishing peace and harmony for Victorinus, possibly dating to the Late Roman or Early Christian period.

EDCS-17701040.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pertinaci Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli designato II patri patriae civitas Ucres pecunia publica fecit et dedicavit anno Corneli Anullini proconsulis clarissimi viri et Valeri Festi legati eius\n
EDCS-17701040.translation: The city of Ucres built and dedicated this with public money to Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul-designate for the second time, father of the country, in the year of the proconsul Cornelius Anullinus, a most distinguished man, and of Valerius Festus, his legate.
EDCS-17701040.summary: This is a public building inscription from the city of Ucres dedicated to Emperor Septimius Severus, dated by the consuls of the year, likely the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-17701044.translation: To Sextus Caecilius Crescentius Volusianus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, former praefectus fabrum, priest of the Arval Brethren responsible for sacred rites, advocate of the imperial treasury in Rome, procurator of inheritances and of the private correspondence of the deified Antoninus and of the emperors, patron of the municipality, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-17701044.summary: This is a funerary and honorary inscription for Sextus Caecilius Crescentius Volusianus, detailing his distinguished career in imperial administration and his patronage of a municipality, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-17701050.text_edition: \n Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus pro consule trimodium posuit qua civitates salem emetirentur\n
EDCS-17701050.translation: Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus, proconsul, erected this three-modius measure so that the communities could measure out salt.
EDCS-17701050.summary: This inscription records the erection of a public measure for salt by Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus, a proconsul, likely dating to the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-17701090.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filio divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XVII imperatori XII consuli III patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filio divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto tribunicia potestate XI imperatori II consuli III et Publio Septimio Severo Getae [nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis et Iuliae Domnae Augustae] matri Auggustorum et castrorum colonia Septimia Vaga nomini et auspiciis divinis eorum inlustrata per Titum Flavium Decimum proconsulem clarissimum virum colonia deducta arcum fecit et numini Augusti eorum dicavit\n
EDCS-17701090.translation: Colonia Septimia Vaga, in the name and under the divine auspices of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 17th time, imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country; and of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Aurelius Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, holding tribunician power for the 11th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time; and of Publius Septimius Severus Geta, most noble Caesar, prince of youth; and of Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the emperors and of the camps, illuminated by their divine names and auspices, through Titus Flavius Decimus, a most distinguished proconsul, erected this arch and dedicated it to their divine majesty.
EDCS-17701090.summary: This is a monumental inscription by Colonia Septimia Vaga commemorating the construction of an arch dedicated to the emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, Geta, and Empress Julia Domna, likely erected during Severus' reign (193-211 CE).

EDCS-17701124.translation: Sacred to the gods of the underworld. Capras lived 10 years.
EDCS-17701124.summary: A brief funerary inscription dedicated to the gods of the underworld for a child named Capras who lived for 10 years.

EDCS-17701135.translation: The pious wife of Quintus Volcius Ianuarius, Gargilia Tiburtina, daughter of Quintus Volcius Ianuarius, lived 102 years. She lies here. Sacred to the gods of the underworld.
EDCS-17701135.summary: A funerary inscription for Gargilia Tiburtina, wife of Quintus Volcius Ianuarius, who lived 102 years, dedicated to the gods of the underworld.

EDCS-17701277.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Severus Pius, grandson of the deified Severus Pius, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country; and of [Julia Mammaea] Augusta, mother of the emperors and of the camps and of the senate and of the fatherland, the municipality of Septimium Aurelium Antoninianum [Alexandrianum] Herculeum frugiferum Thignica, devoted to their divine majesty and power, rebuilt the macellum, which had collapsed from age, from the ground with public money and dedicated it.
EDCS-17701277.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a market (macellum) in the municipality of Septimium Aurelium Antoninianum by public funds during the reign of Severus Alexander and his mother Julia Mammaea, likely around 224-227 CE.

EDCS-17701278.translation: For the safety(?) of our lords Diocletian and Maximian Augusti and Constantius and Maximian, most noble Caesars. [Name/title?] Caecilianus [title?] performed the taurobolium (?)
EDCS-17701278.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication, possibly a taurobolium (a ritual sacrifice), for the safety of the Tetrarchic emperors (Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius), with the name of the dedicator partially preserved.

EDCS-17900348.text_edition: \n Sexto Cocceio Anicio Fausto Paulino proconsuli provinciae Africae res publica coloniae [][] Augustae \n
EDCS-17900348.translation: The Republic of the colony [ Colonia Augusta?] to Sextus Cocceius Anicius Faustus Paulinus, proconsul of the province of Africa.
EDCS-17900348.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the 'Republic' (i.e., the city government) of Colonia Augusta to Sextus Cocceius Anicius Faustus Paulinus, proconsul of Africa, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-17900382.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, and Minerva, the August ones. For the health of the Emperors Caesars Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, conquerors of Armenia, Media, and Parthia, and of their divine house, Lucius Marcius Simplex and Lucius Marcius Simplex Regillianus made this at their own expense. // Lucius Marcius Simplex and Lucius Marcius Simplex Regillianus made this at their own expense.
EDCS-17900382.summary: This inscription is a dedication of a religious shrine to several gods for the health of Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, funded by Lucius Marcius Simplex and Lucius Marcius Simplex Regillianus, likely dating to their joint reign (161-169 AD).

EDCS-17900384.text_edition: \n Pietati Augustae sacrum Pompeius Rogatus testamento Cai Pompei Nahani fratris sui ex HS XXX milibus nummum solo suo exstruxit itemque dedicavit curatoribus Marco Morasio Donato Caio Pompeio Cossuto\n
EDCS-17900384.translation: Sacred to Pietas Augusta. Pompeius Rogatus built and dedicated this, as per his will, from his brother Caius Pompeius Nahani's 30,000 sesterces, using his own land, with Marcus Morasius Donatus and Caius Pompeius Cossutus as curators.
EDCS-17900384.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a monument to Pietas Augusta, funded by Pompeius Rogatus in memory of his brother and completed by appointed curators.

EDCS-17900389.text_edition: \n Imperatori Tiberio Claudio Caesari Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli imperatori II Lucius Iulius Luci filius Cornelia Crassus aedilis ornamentarius tribunus militum legionis XXI rapacis in Germania praefectus fabrum IIvir augur IIvir quinquennalis designatus? patronus pagi dedit Caius Caesetius Cai filius Arnensi perpetuus sacerdos patronus pagi Thuggensis nomine suo et fili et perpetui arcum de sua pecunia faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-17900389.translation: Lucius Iulius Crassus, son of Lucius of the Cornelia tribe, an ornamental aedile, tribune of the soldiers of the XXI Rapax legion in Germany, prefect of engineers, twice duovir, augur, designated quinquennial duovir, and patron of the village, dedicated to Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul for the second time. Caius Caesetius Arnensis, son of Caius of the Arnensi tribe, perpetual priest and patron of the Thugga village, on his own behalf and that of his son and perpetual successors, saw to the construction of this arch at his own expense.
EDCS-17900389.summary: This inscription details a dedication of an arch to Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus by Lucius Iulius Crassus, with Caius Caesetius Arnensis also noted as a patron and priest who oversaw its construction at his own expense, likely from the 1st century AD.

EDCS-17900394.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Britannici maximi filii divi Marci Antonini Germanici Sarmatici nepotis divi Antonini Pii pronepotis divi Hadriani abnepotis divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepotis Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Parthici maximi Britannici maximi Germanici maximi pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate XVII imperatoris III consulis IIII patris patriae proconsulis et Iuliae Domnae Piae Felicis Augustae matris Augusti et castrorum et senatus et patriae totiusque divinae domus eorum templum Victoriae Augustae domini nostri quod Gabinia Hermiona testamento suo ex |(sestertium) C milibus nummum fieri iussit perfectum et dedicatum est suo testamento die dedicationis et deinde quodannis epulum decurionibus ab heredibus suis dari praecepit item agrum qui appellatur circus ad voluptatem populi rei publicae remisit\n
EDCS-17900394.translation: For the health of Emperor Caesar Septimius Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 17th time, imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, proconsul, and of Julia Domna Pia Felix Augusta, mother of the Augusti, of the camp, of the senate, and of the whole fatherland, and of their entire divine house, the temple of Victoria Augusta, our lord, which Gabinia Hermiona ordered to be built by her will from 100,000 sesterces, was completed and dedicated according to her will. On the day of dedication, and thereafter every year, she ordered a feast to be given to the decurions by her heirs. She also remitted the land called Circus to the Republic for the pleasure of the people.
EDCS-17900394.summary: This extensive inscription records the dedication of a temple to Victoria Augusta, along with associated provisions for public enjoyment and distributions, funded by Gabinia Hermiona through her will for the health of Emperor Septimius Severus and his family, likely in the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-17900398.translation: The colony of Licinia Septimia Aurelia Alexandriana Thuggensis, devoted to the divine majesty of Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus Germanicus Pius Felix Augustus, father of the country, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 10th time, imperator for the 10th time, consul for the 2nd time, designated for the 5th time, proconsul, dedicates this.
EDCS-17900398.summary: This inscription records the colony of Thuggensis dedicating itself to the divine majesty of Emperor Gallienus, likely dating to his reign between 253-268 AD.

EDCS-17900404.translation: For the health of Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus. Aulus Gabinius Datus, patron of the village and city, and Marcus Gabinius Bassus, perpetual flamen of Augustus, patron of the village and city, built from their own land and at their own expense, from the foundations, the temples of Concordia, Liber Pater, along with other temples and colonnades. They promised 50,000 sesterces for this work and completed it with increased funds, and dedicated it with Fortunatus, Lucius Instanius Fortunatus, and Marcus Vibius Gemellus as curators.
EDCS-17900404.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of several temples and porticoes by Aulus Gabinius Datus and Marcus Gabinius Bassus, patrons and a flamen of Augustus, for the health of Emperor Hadrian, funded significantly by their own wealth, likely in the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-17900406.translation: The people of Thuggenses to Asicia Victoria, flaminica. For her munificence and singular generosity to the public affairs, for which she promised 100,000 sesterces for the priesthood of her daughter Vibia Asician(a), from whose income theatrical games and gifts to the decurions were to be provided. The decurions of both orders erected this.
EDCS-17900406.summary: This inscription honors Asicia Victoria, a flaminica, for her generous donation to the public which funded games and gifts for the decurions, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17900412.text_edition: \n Quintus Pacuvius Saturus flamen perpetuus augur coloniae Iuliae Karthaginis et Nahania Victoria uxor eius flaminica perpetua opus templi Mercuri quot sic Marcus Pacuvius Felix Victorianus filius eorum codicillis suis ex HS L milibus fieri iussit amplius ipsi ob honorem flamonii perpetui ex HS LXX milibus pollicitis summis templum Mercuri et cellas duas cum statuis et porticibus et ornamentis omnibusae exstruxerunt et excoluerunt item civitati Thuggae HS XXV milia Quintus Pacuvius Saturus flamen perpetuus daturum se pollicitus est ex cuius summae reditu quotannis decurionibus sportulae darentur et ob diem dedicationis ludos scaenicos et sportulas decurionibus utriusque ordinis et universo populo dedit\n
EDCS-17900412.translation: Quintus Pacuvius Saturus, perpetual flamen and augur of the colony of Iulia Karthago, and Nahania Victoria, his wife, perpetual flaminica, ordered the work of the temple of Mercury to be carried out by their son Marcus Pacuvius Felix Victorianus in his codicils from 50,000 sesterces. Furthermore, for the honor of his perpetual priesthood, he promised an additional 70,000 sesterces, constructed and embellished the temple of Mercury with two chapels, statues, porticoes, and all ornaments. Quintus Pacuvius Saturus, perpetual flamen, also promised to give 25,000 sesterces to the city of Thugga, from the income of which gifts were to be provided annually to the decurions. On the day of dedication, he gave theatrical games and gifts to the decurions of both orders and to the entire populace.
EDCS-17900412.summary: This inscription details a substantial construction project for the temple of Mercury and related amenities, funded by Quintus Pacuvius Saturus and his family, with significant public distributions and games, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17900414.translation: For the health of Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus Germanicus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, conqueror of Germany, with tribunician power for the 10th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, proconsul, and of Cornelia Salonina Augusta, and of their entire divine house. Botria Fortunata, daughter of Victor, perpetual flaminica, built and embellished the temple of Tellus from her own funds from the ground up, in honor of her high priestly office, and dedicated it, providing gifts to the decurions and a feast to the entire populace.
EDCS-17900414.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a temple to Tellus by Botria Fortunata, a perpetual flaminica, funded by her own wealth and dedicated to the imperial family, likely during the reign of Gallienus (253-268 AD).

EDCS-17900416.translation: Sacred to the shades below. Dolabellius Felix, son of Candidus, pious, lived 43 years, is buried here.
EDCS-17900416.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death and burial of Dolabellius Felix, providing his age and indicating his piety.

EDCS-17900419.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Volceia Victoria lived 65 years, she lies here. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Cornelius Solutor, a pious man, lived 33 years, he lies here. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Botuvia Victoria pious lived [years]
EDCS-17900419.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument recording the lives of at least three individuals, Volceia Victoria, Quintus Cornelius Solutor, and Botuvia Victoria.

EDCS-17900442.text_edition: \n Fortunae Augustae sacrum Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Armeniaco et Imperatori Caesari Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto Armeniaco templum quod Caius Iulius Cai filius Cornelia Galba |(centurio) legionis XXII Primigeniae hastatus ex HS XXX milibus nummum testamento suo fieri iussit Lucius Iulius Luci filius Cornelia Rogatus Kappianus frater patruelis et heres eius adiectis ob honorem flamonii perpetui sui HS X milibus nummum et amplius quae professus est HS XXX milibus nummum cum fratribus Potito Natale Honorata faciendum curavit Lucius Iulius Titisenus Rogatus Kappianus filius sororis et heres eius consummavit et cum Aulo Titiseno Honorato Kappiano filio suo dedicavit et ob dedicationem triduo ludos decurionibus sportulas populo epulum et gymnasium dedit\n
EDCS-17900442.translation: Sacred to Fortuna Augusta. Caius Iulius Galba, son of Caius, a centurion of the 22nd Legion Primigenia, ordered a temple to be built in his will for 30,000 sesterces. Lucius Iulius Rogatus Kappianus, son of Lucius, of the Cornelia tribe, his cousin and heir, added 10,000 sesterces for the honor of his perpetual priesthood, and more than 30,000 sesterces which he declared, supervised its construction with his brothers Potitus, Natale, and Honorata. Lucius Iulius Titisenus Rogatus Kappianus, son of his sister and his heir, completed it and dedicated it with Aulus Titisenus Honoratus Kappianus, his son. For the dedication, he gave games for three days, gifts to the decurions, a feast for the people, and a gymnasium.
EDCS-17900442.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a temple to Fortuna Augusta, financed and overseen by multiple family members including Lucius Iulius Rogatus Kappianus and his son Lucius Iulius Titisenus Rogatus Kappianus, honoring the emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus.

EDCS-17900447.translation: To the most invincible and most fortunate Emperor Augustus Caesar, pacifier of the world, by decree of the decurions of the Musticenses.
EDCS-17900447.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring an unnamed Emperor Augustus Caesar, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, by the local council (decurions) of Musticenses.

EDCS-18300018.text_edition: \n Marco Flavio Marci filio Sabarro Vettio Severo clarissimo viro proconsuli provinciae Africae decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-18300018.translation: To Marcus Flavius Vettius Severus, son of Marcus, of the Sabarra family, a most distinguished man, proconsul of the province of Africa, by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-18300018.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Flavius Vettius Severus, proconsul of Africa, with a dedication by the decurions using public funds.

EDCS-18300231.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Aelius Laetianus lived 18 years. In memory of Caius Iulius Saturninus, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, Cilonianus, who held all offices in the colony of Sufetula, and [his] grandsons.
EDCS-18300231.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument commemorating Caius Iulius Saturninus, a prominent local official in Sufetula, and also recording the life of Quintus Aelius Laetianus.

EDCS-20900090.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Fortunatus qui et Samracius vixit annos XVII\n
EDCS-20900090.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Fortunatus, also called Samracius, lived 17 years.
EDCS-20900090.summary: This is a short funerary inscription recording the death of Fortunatus Samracius at the age of 17.

EDCS-20900091.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Ael Antulla vixit annis XXV piissime Honorata vixit annis IX\n
EDCS-20900091.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Ael Antulla lived 25 years, most pious Honorata lived 9 years.
EDCS-20900091.summary: This funerary inscription records the lives of two individuals, Ael Antulla and Honorata.

EDCS-20900092.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus fixit sic Iluma Mathlicis monimento sic FACM anno(s LXXXXV\n
EDCS-20900092.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iluma Mathlicis set up this monument, lived 95 years.
EDCS-20900092.summary: This funerary inscription records that Iluma Mathlicis erected a monument for herself and lived to the age of 95.

EDCS-21000545.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Flavius Constantius vixit nnos XVIIII menses IIII\n
EDCS-21000545.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Flavius Constantius lived 19 years and 4 months.
EDCS-21000545.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the life of Marcus Flavius Constantius, who died at the age of 19 years and 4 months.

EDCS-21000573.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Commodi Augusti Quintus Volusius Maximus Maximianus sacerdos quod nania Maxima uxor sua hoc munus fieri una secum voverat id Volusius Maximianus votum solvit\n
EDCS-21000573.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Augustus. Quintus Volusius Maximus Maximianus, a priest, fulfilled a vow that his wife Nania Maxima had pledged to make together with him; Volusius Maximianus fulfilled the vow.
EDCS-21000573.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by the priest Quintus Volusius Maximus Maximianus and his wife Nania Maxima for the well-being of Emperor Commodus.

EDCS-21000585.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, conqueror of Arabia and Adiabene...
EDCS-21000585.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely honoring Emperor Septimius Severus, dating to his reign (late 2nd/early 3rd century CE).

EDCS-21000597.text_edition: \n Titi Camidieni Atimeti doliare ex praediis Plotinae Augustae\n
EDCS-21000597.translation: Doliar work from the estates of Plotina Augusta, made by Titus Camidienus Atimetus.
EDCS-21000597.summary: This records dolia (large earthenware jars) produced from the estates of Plotina Augusta, likely during the Hadrianic period (mid-2nd century CE).

EDCS-21000598.text_edition: \n Opus doliare ex praediis Domitiae Lucillae Flavi Probi Pontiano et Atiliano consulibus\n
EDCS-21000598.translation: Doliar work from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, by Flavius Probus and Pontianus and Atilianus, consuls.
EDCS-21000598.summary: This inscription records dolia produced from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, dating to the consulship of Flavius Probus and Pontianus and Atilianus, likely in the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-21000604.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto sacrum Marcus Manlius Felix sacerdos dono dedit dedicavit\n
EDCS-21000604.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus. Marcus Manlius Felix, the priest, gave and dedicated this offering.
EDCS-21000604.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus Augustus by the priest Marcus Manlius Felix, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-21000610.text_edition: \n Areti Quinto Apronio Longino Mamiano clarissimo puero Quinti Aproni Mamiani Marciani egregi viri filio Cai Aproni Fortunati Mamiani egregi viri nepoti ordo coloniae Marianae Augustae Alexandrianae Uchitanorum Maiorum patrono et alumno suo\n
EDCS-21000610.translation: To Aretus, Quintus Apronius Longinus Mamianus, a most distinguished boy, son of Quintus Apronius Mamianus Marcianus, an excellent man, grandson of Caius Apronius Fortunatus Mamianus, an excellent man; to the patron and pupil of the town council of Colonia Mariana Augusta Alexandriana Uchitanorum Maiorum.
EDCS-21000610.summary: This is a dedication to a young boy named Aretus by the town council of Colonia Mariana Augusta Alexandriana Uchitanorum Maiorum, honoring him as a patron and pupil, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-21000613.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Aurelio Commodo Augusto Imperatoris Antonini Augusti filio divi Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae atnepoti Germanico Sarmatico tribunicia potestate IIII imperatori IIII consuli II patri patriae res publica Uchitanorum Maiorum sua pecunia fecit itemque dedicavit decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-21000613.translation: The Republic of the Uchitanorum Maiorum, by decree of the decurions and with public funds, made and also dedicated this to Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus, son of Emperor Antoninus Augustus, grandson of divine Pius, great-grandson of divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of divine Parthicus Trajan, great-great-great-grandson of divine Nerva, Germanicus, Sarmaticus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, emperor for the 4th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country.
EDCS-21000613.summary: This inscription records the public works funded and dedicated by the town of Uchitanorum Maiorum to Emperor Commodus, celebrating his imperial titles and lineage, dating to his 4th tribunician power (179 CE).

EDCS-21000614.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto principi iuventutis tribunicia potestate V consuli proconsuli patri patriae Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Parthici maximi Arabici Adiabenici fortissimi felicissimi principis sena et Iuliae Domnae Augustae matris Augustorum et castrorum filio et Publi Septimi Getae nobilissimi Caesaris fratri divi Marci Antonini Pii nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani et divi Nervae a<d=T>nepoti res publica Uchitanorum Maiorum decreto decurionum pecunia publica \n
EDCS-21000614.translation: The Republic of the Uchitanorum Maiorum, by decree of the decurions and with public funds, [dedicated this] to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, prince of youth, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, proconsul, father of the country, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Parthicus Maximus Arabicus Adiabenicus, the most valiant and fortunate prince, and of Iulia Domna Augusta, mother of the emperors and of the camp, and brother of Publius Septimius Geta nobilissimus Caesar, grandson of divine Marcus Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of divine Trajan and divine Nerva.
EDCS-21000614.summary: This inscription records public works funded by the town of Uchitanorum Maiorum dedicated to Emperor Caracalla, celebrating his titles and imperial ancestry, dating to his 5th tribunician power (202 CE).

EDCS-21000615.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Valerio Diocletiano Pio Felici Augusto et \n
EDCS-21000615.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletian Pius Felix Augustus and...
EDCS-21000615.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring Emperor Diocletian, likely dating to his reign (284-305 CE).

EDCS-21000617.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Tuscilius Luci filius Quintianus miles cohortis XI urbanae stipendiorum trium pius vixit annis XXIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-21000617.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Quintus Tuscilius Quintianus, son of Lucius, a soldier of the 11th Urban Cohort, with three years of service, pious, lived 23 years, is buried here.
EDCS-21000617.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Quintus Tuscilius Quintianus, a soldier of the 11th Urban Cohort, who died at the age of 23, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22000652.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto tribunicia potestate IX imperatori consuli II patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate IIII et Lucio Septimio [Getae] Caesari Augusto Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augustorum consulari [legionis III Augustae piae vindicis]\n
EDCS-22000652.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, emperor for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, and to Lucius Septimius [Geta] Caesar Augustus, by Quintus Anicius Faustus, consular legate of the Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex.
EDCS-22000652.summary: This inscription records the dedication of something by Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of Legio III Augusta, to Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, dating to Severus's 9th tribunician power (201 CE) and Caracalla's 4th (201 CE).

EDCS-22000655.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Caesari Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consul II imperator III Quintus Marcius Cai filius Barea consul XVvir sacris faciundis fetialis proconsul dedicavit\n
EDCS-22000655.translation: To Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the fifth time, consul for the second time, hailed as emperor for the third time, Quintus Marcius Barea, son of Gaius, consul for the fifteenth time, member of the college of fifteen for sacred rites, fetial priest, proconsul, dedicated this.
EDCS-22000655.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Quintus Marcius Barea, honoring Emperor Claudius.

EDCS-22000675.text_edition: \n Quinto Egrilio Plariano legato pro praetore patrono Gigthenses publice\n
EDCS-22000675.translation: To Quintus Egrilius Plarianus, governor with praetorian power, their patron, by public decree of the people of Gigthis.
EDCS-22000675.summary: This inscription records a public dedication by the people of Gigthis to their patron, Quintus Egrilius Plarianus.

EDCS-22400639.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Nonia Faustina, daughter of Gaius, lived for 5 years.
EDCS-22400639.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Nonia Faustina, a child of five years old.

EDCS-22700720.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, with tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the country, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-22700720.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Gordian III, funded by public money and decreed by the local council.

EDCS-23200247.text_edition: \n Iuliae Flaviae Herenniae Caeciliae Honoratianae Optatae clarissimae puellae filiae Flavi Pollionis Flaviani clarissimi viri curatoris rei publicae praetoris kandidati decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-23200247.translation: By decree of the decurions, at public expense, to Julia Flavia Herennia Caecilia Honoratiana Optata, most illustrious girl, daughter of Flavius Pollio Flavianus, most illustrious man, curator of the state, candidate for praetor.
EDCS-23200247.summary: This inscription records a public dedication to Julia Flavia Herennia Caecilia Honoratiana Optata, a young girl, daughter of a prominent official.

EDCS-23200257.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Aecia the pious lived 14 years. Mapalicus set this up for his daughter. She rests here.
EDCS-23200257.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Aecia, a 14-year-old girl, set up by her father Mapalicus.

EDCS-23200258.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Aelia Cyrpe, also called Eutycia, lived 42 years. Her husband set [this up] for his wife.
EDCS-23200258.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Aelia Cyrpe (also known as Eutycia), who lived 42 years, set up by her husband.

EDCS-23200261.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Aemilia Marcella the pious lived 7 years. [...] Romanus [...] rests here.
EDCS-23200261.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Aemilia Marcella, a child of seven years, with partial information about a relative.

EDCS-23200262.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Marcus Antonius Concessus lived 95 years. He rests here. // Sacred to the departed gods. [...] lived [...] years. Marcus Antonius Concessus set [this up] for his most beloved wife.
EDCS-23200262.summary: This inscription is a double tombstone, first recording the death of Marcus Antonius Concessus at 95, and then a second dedication by him to his wife.

EDCS-23200264.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Arrania Sperata lived [3 years, 3 months] [...]. [...] her most beloved and pious daughter made this. She rests here.
EDCS-23200264.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Arrania Sperata, with partial details of her age and a dedication by her daughter.

EDCS-23200265.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Asidonia Victoria lived 65 years and is buried here; her sons placed this. To the Spirits of the Departed. Asidonius December lived 70 years and is buried here; his sons placed this.
EDCS-23200265.summary: This inscription records two separate burials for Asidonia Victoria and Asidonius December, with their respective sons acting as dedicants, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200266.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Assidonius Marianus, pious, lived [X] years and is buried here; Iu[3]O placed [this] for [his] pious [father].
EDCS-23200266.summary: This funerary inscription honors Marcus Assidonius Marianus, who lived an unknown number of years and was memorialized by his son, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200267.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Atilius Maioricus lived 40 years. Atilius Faustianus placed this for his dearest brother; he is buried here. To the Spirits of the Departed. [X] Atilius Clodianus lived 23 years; his son placed [this]; he is buried here.
EDCS-23200267.summary: This inscription contains two funerary notices, one for Lucius Atilius Maioricus commemorating his brother Atilius Faustianus, and another for Atilius Clodianus memorialized by his son, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200271.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. C() Pescenia lived 2 years and is buried here; Ianuarius placed this for his daughter. To the Spirits of the Departed. Augendus lived 4 years and is buried here.
EDCS-23200271.summary: This inscription records the burial of two young children, C() Pescenia and Augendus, with Ianuarius dedicating the former and likely a parent the latter, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200272.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Caecilius Macer, son of Caius, of the tribe Quirina, lived 20 years and is buried here.
EDCS-23200272.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Caecilius Macer, a young man who died at the age of 20, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200274.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aemilia Restutula lived 21 years and is buried here. To the Spirits of the Departed. Caelius Marius lived 60 years; Claudia Donata placed this for her husband.
EDCS-23200274.summary: This inscription records two burials: Aemilia Restutula and Caelius Marius, the latter memorialized by his wife Claudia Donata, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200276.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Caelia Octavia, who lived [X]3 years and 10 months, and is buried here, [3]ius Aq[3]/[3]s placed [this] for [his] most chaste and dearest wife.
EDCS-23200276.summary: This funerary inscription honors Caelia Octavia, who died at the age of 30-something and was memorialized by her husband, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200277.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caninia Verecunda Italica lived 21 years and is buried here; Pompeius Aemilianus placed this for his incomparable wife. To the Spirits of the Departed. Pompeia Fortunata lived 27 years and is buried here; her pious father placed [this].
EDCS-23200277.summary: This inscription records two burials: Caninia Verecunda Italica, memorialized by her husband, and Pompeia Fortunata, memorialized by her father, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200278.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Cutullus lived 48 years and is buried here; his pious sister placed [this].
EDCS-23200278.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Cutullus, who died at the age of 48, and was memorialized by his sister, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200280.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Memoria lived 25 years and is buried here; Iulius Secundianus placed [this] for his dearest mother. To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Cornelius Massuricus lived 3 years and 5 months; Caius Massuricus, his pious father, placed [this] for his son. Aelia Crescentia lived 70 years and is buried here; Cornelius Massuricus placed [this].
EDCS-23200280.summary: This inscription records three burials: Iulia Memoria memorialized by her son, Publius Cornelius Massuricus by his father Caius Massuricus, and Aelia Crescentia by Cornelius Massuricus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200281.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Publius Cornelius Secundianus lived 5 years and is buried here. Iulius Saturninus placed [this] for his pious son. Sacred to the departed spirits. Publius Cornelius Victor, also called Massuricus, lived 46 years and is buried here. Iulia Saturnina placed [this] for her pious husband. Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulia Prima lived 15 years and is buried here. Iulia Saturnina placed [this] for her devoted foster-daughter.
EDCS-23200281.summary: This is a triple tombstone recording the deaths of three individuals, Publius Cornelius Secundianus, Publius Cornelius Victor, and Iulia Prima, with dedications by family members.

EDCS-23200282.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Concordia Pia lived 7 years and 7 months. Ianuarius placed [this] for his pious daughter and she is buried here.
EDCS-23200282.summary: This tombstone records the death of Concordia Pia, who lived 7 years and 7 months, and was dedicated by her father Ianuarius.

EDCS-23200284.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Clodia Secunda lived 20 years and is buried here. Iulius Maximus Barda placed [this] for his pious wife.
EDCS-23200284.summary: This tombstone records the death of Clodia Secunda, who lived 20 years, and was dedicated by her husband Iulius Maximus Barda.

EDCS-23200286.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Steia Donatula and Rupila lived 50 years and are buried here. Titus Lartius Quintulus placed [this] for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-23200286.summary: This tombstone records the death of Steia Donatula (with a partial name Rupila), who lived 50 years, and was dedicated by her husband Titus Lartius Quintulus.

EDCS-23200287.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Fabia Secunda lived 16 years and is buried here. Her parents placed [this] for their sweetest daughter. Na[---] lived [---] years.
EDCS-23200287.summary: This tombstone records the death of Fabia Secunda, who lived 16 years, and was dedicated by her parents, with a second, incomplete inscription for another individual.

EDCS-23200288.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulia Aemiliana lived 48 years and is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Fannius Longinus lived 52 years and is buried here.
EDCS-23200288.summary: This is a double tombstone recording the deaths of Iulia Aemiliana (aged 48) and Quintus Fannius Longinus (aged 52).

EDCS-23200289.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Flavia Dativa lived 60 years. Flavia Restuta placed [this] for her dearest sister. I, Flavia Restuta, and Flavia Pulla [dedicated this].
EDCS-23200289.summary: This tombstone records the death of Flavia Dativa, who lived 60 years, and was dedicated by her sister Flavia Restuta and Flavia Pulla.

EDCS-23200290.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. [Munatius Quintasius lived ---] years and is buried here. [---] placed [this] for his pious [---]. Sacred to the departed spirits. Flavia Cai Piscenila lived 25 years and 4 months and is buried here. Munatius Quintasius placed [this] for his dearest wife.
EDCS-23200290.summary: This is a double tombstone recording the deaths of an unnamed individual who died sometime after Munatius Quintasius and Flavia Cai Piscenila, who lived 25 years and 4 months and was dedicated by her husband Munatius Quintasius.

EDCS-23200291.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Geminius Felix lived 51 years and 12 [months---].
EDCS-23200291.summary: This tombstone records the death of Geminius Felix, who lived 51 years and 12 months, with an incomplete dedication.

EDCS-23200292.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Germanus lived [---] years.
EDCS-23200292.summary: This tombstone records the death of Germanus, with an incomplete lifespan and dedication.

EDCS-23200293.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Helvius Donatus lived for [3] years; he is buried here. His most devoted sons erected this.
EDCS-23200293.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Helvius Donatus, erected by his sons, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200294.text_edition: \n Barasi X X X Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Iulius Bucianus vixi interim annis LXXII de bonis X\n
EDCS-23200294.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Quintus Iulius Bucianus lived for 72 years. [Erected] from his own property.
EDCS-23200294.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Quintus Iulius Bucianus, who lived to 72 and whose monument was funded by his own means, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200295.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Caius Iulius Donatus lived for 22 years; his parents placed him here, a pious son.
EDCS-23200295.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Iulius Donatus, who died at 22 and was commemorated by his parents, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200296.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Pudentilla lived for 85 years. Her dearest daughter made [this] for her mother. Sacred to the Divine Shades. Caius Iulius Felix lived for 82 years and 7 months. Unworthily, Felix, you left life; you should have lived for almost 100 years. May the earth be light on you, if the shades exist. He is buried here. His dearest wife.
EDCS-23200296.summary: This complex inscription records the deaths of Pudentilla, commemorated by her daughter, and Caius Iulius Felix, whose monument was erected by his wife, with laments about his premature death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200303.translation: Iulia Casta lived for 36 years and 9 months. Iuli[...]. Vic[...]
EDCS-23200303.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary epitaph for Iulia Casta, recording her lifespan of 36 years and 9 months, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200337.translation: [...]uriia [...]onilla [lived for] 1 year and 11 months. She is buried here.
EDCS-23200337.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the burial of a child named possibly Uriia/Onilla who lived for 1 year and 11 months, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200445.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-23200445.text_edition: \n Pro restituta salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Armeniaci Parthici Medici pontificis maximi patris patriae tribunicia potestate XXIII consulis III imperatoris V civitas Mactaritanorum extruxit anno Sexti Laterani proconsulis clarissimi viri Lucius Rupilius Au legatus eius dedicavit\n
EDCS-23200445.translation: For the restored health of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Parthicus Medicus, Pontifex Maximus, Father of the Fatherland, with tribunician power for the 23rd time, consul for the 3rd time, and Imperator. The city of Mactaritanorum erected [this] in the year of Sextus Lateranus, most illustrious proconsul. Lucius Rupilius Au, his legate, dedicated it.
EDCS-23200445.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the city of Mactaritanorum to commemorate the health of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, under the proconsulship of Sextus Lateranus, likely dating to the late 2nd century CE.

EDCS-23200457.translation: To Marcus Valerius Quadratus, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, decemvir for judging lawsuits, broad-striped tribune of the Legio III Augusta, quaestor of the Emperor, patron of the municipality, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-23200457.summary: This is a dedication of a public monument to Marcus Valerius Quadratus, detailing his distinguished career in various civic and military offices, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200467.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Caius Mulceius Maximus lived for 30 years. Sacred to the Divine Shades. Sextus Aurelius F[...]-nus lived for 40 years.
EDCS-23200467.summary: This is a funerary inscription that appears to be a composite monument, originally an epitaph for a freedman who rose to wealth and status through agriculture and public service, with additional, later funerary dedications to Caius Mulceius Maximus and Sextus Aurelius F[...]-nus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23400751.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus. The city of Uzappa erected [this] by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-23400751.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the city of Uzappa in honor of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus (Caracalla), funded by public decree, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-23400753.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, conqueror of the Germans, conqueror of the Sarmatians, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, the citizens of Uzappa.
EDCS-23400753.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Probus with a full titulature and indicates it was erected by the citizens of Uzappa.

EDCS-23400833.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Britannici maximi fili divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepotis divi Antonini Pii pronepotis divi Hadriani abnepotis divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepotis Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis principis iuventutis Augusti Parthici maximi Brittannici maximi pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate XV imperatoris II consulis III patris patriae et Iuliae Domnae Augustae Piae Felicis matris Augusti et castrorum et senatus totiusque domus divinae Publius Opstorius Saturninus flamen perpetuus sacerdos Mercuri cum patriae suae Vazitanae triplicata summa flamoni perpetui HS III milibus nummum aedem Mercurio Sobrio pollicitus fuisset ampliata liberalitate eandem aedem cum pronao et ara fecit et ob dedicationem aepulum sic et gymnasium dedit loco dato decreto decurionum idem iam ante hoc ob honorem XI primatus aedem Aesculapio deo promissam bassilicam sic cohaerentem multiplicata pecunia fecit\n
EDCS-23400833.translation: For the well-being of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix, prince of the youth, Augustus, conqueror of the Parthians, conqueror of the Britons, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva; and of Julia Domna Augusta Pia Felix, mother of the Augusti and of the camp and of the entire divine house, Publius Opstorius Saturninus, perpetual flamen, priest of Mercury, with his homeland Vazitana, having promised 3,000 sesterces for the triple sum of the perpetual flamonic office, built the same temple with a pronaos and altar, having amplified his generosity; and for the dedication, he gave a banquet and a gymnasium, in a place granted by decree of the decurions; he had already previously, for the honor of his 11th primary office, promised a temple to the god Aesculapius, and with multiplied funds, built an adjoining basilica.
EDCS-23400833.summary: This long inscription records the extensive dedications made by Publius Opstorius Saturninus, a perpetual flamen, including a temple to Mercury and a basilica to Aesculapius, for the health of Emperor Caracalla and Empress Julia Domna, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-23500042.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae ducatu instantiaque Claudi Constantis procuratoris Augusti contigit des et Musulamios gentesque aliasnminmerumptest anno usque\n
EDCS-23500042.translation: To Augusta Victoria, by the command and at the instance of Claudius Constans, procurator of Augustus, the Cetus and Musulamii and numerous other peoples...
EDCS-23500042.summary: This inscription, likely from North Africa, records a dedication to Augusta Victoria by Claudius Constans, a procurator, and mentions various peoples, possibly related to military achievements or administrative divisions.

EDCS-23500043.translation: Rasinia Secunda returned on the 17th day before the Kalends of November in the 157th year.
EDCS-23500043.summary: This short inscription records the return of a person named Rasinia Secunda on a specific date, possibly in reference to a public office or a historical era.

EDCS-23500057.translation: Samaita and Immi made this for their mother.
EDCS-23500057.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription recording that Samaita and Immi erected a memorial for their mother.

EDCS-24100006.text_edition: \n Aurellio Nazmuri parenti et matri Aurelli Maior et Magnus et Arcadius fili parentibus piissimis fecerunt\n
EDCS-24100006.translation: Aurellius Nazmur, to his parents, and to his mother, Aurellius Maior, Magnus, and Arcadius, his sons, made this for their most devoted parents.
EDCS-24100006.summary: This is a funerary inscription where sons Aurellius Maior, Magnus, and Arcadius honor their parents, including Aurellius Nazmur and his mother.

EDCS-24100007.text_edition: \n x templum de sua pecunia Titus Flavius Quirina Capito Io \n
EDCS-24100007.translation: Titus Flavius Quirina Capito Io made a temple at his own expense.
EDCS-24100007.summary: This inscription records Titus Flavius Quirina Capito Io dedicating a temple built at his own expense.

EDCS-24100009.text_edition: \n Mercurio et Minervae Animosa Symmachi filia dedit dedicavit\n
EDCS-24100009.translation: Animosa, daughter of Symmachus, gave and dedicated this to Mercury and Minerva.
EDCS-24100009.summary: This is a dedication to the gods Mercury and Minerva by Animosa, daughter of Symmachus.

EDCS-24100011.text_edition: \n [Fulviae Plau][tillae Augustae] Antonini Augusti Pii sponsae dedicante Flavio Celere procuratore liberti et familia splendidissimi vectigalis IIII publicorum Africae\n
EDCS-24100011.translation: To Fulvia Plautilla Augusta, wife of Antoninus Augustus Pius, dedicated by Flavius Celer, procurator, freedmen and staff of the most distinguished tax collectors of Africa.
EDCS-24100011.summary: This inscription records the dedication to Fulvia Plautilla Augusta, wife of Emperor Antoninus Pius, by Flavius Celer, a procurator, and his associated staff.

EDCS-24100012.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis decem sic et octies imperator duodecies consul ter pater patriae proconsul coepit et ex maiore parte perfecit Imperator Caesar divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Britannici maximi filius divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini Pii pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepos Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurellius sic Antoninus Pius Augustus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus Germanicus maximus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis decem sic novies imperator ter consul quater pater patriae proconsul perfici curavit\n
EDCS-24100012.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 18th time, imperator for the 12th time, consul for the third time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, began and for the most part completed. Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus Germanicus maximus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 19th time, imperator for the third time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, had completed.
EDCS-24100012.summary: This inscription details the construction of a public work initiated by Emperor Septimius Severus and completed under Emperor Caracalla, outlining their extensive imperial titles and consulships.

EDCS-24100014.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Vespasiano Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate III imperatore X consule IIII designato V patre patriae Quintus Manlius Ancharius Tarquitius Saturninus proconsul patronus dedicavit Volumnius Memor Felix legatus pro praetore Iddibal Balsillecis filius Annobalis nepos Asmunis proneposus templum Matris Magnae et exornavit ex HS CC milibus nummum de sua pecunia dedit is et Passienus Maris E\n
EDCS-24100014.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, Imperator for the 10th time, Consul for the 4th time, designated Consul for the 5th time, Father of the Country, Quintus Manlius Ancharius Tarquitius Saturninus, Proconsul, Patron, dedicated this temple to the Great Mother. Volumnius Memor Felix, legate with praetorian authority, son of Iddibal, grandson of Balsilles, great-grandson of Asmunis, also adorned it and gave it from his own funds, 200,000 sesterces. He also and Passienus Maris...
EDCS-24100014.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple to the Great Mother by the proconsul Quintus Manlius Ancharius Tarquitius Saturninus and its adornment by the legate Volumnius Memor Felix, dating to the reign of Vespasian (AD 69-79).

EDCS-24100015.text_edition: \n NI OMM honestissim cognoscendi perpenso qu MEATO ob qurem publicam exquisitisisionum generibus fecerit ampliorem instauratori moenium publicorum quod eius innumera circa se ac suos officia supra genitalis civis affectum Lepcis magna inclita fide devotione praestans multifariam senserit meritorum eius tenacissime memor per ordinis sui et populi viros Flavio Victori Calpurnio viro perfectissimo praesidi provinciae Tripolitanae patrono suo statuam decrevit et ob individuum mutui amoris affectum eamdem sic se propter constituit ac dedicavit\n
EDCS-24100015.translation: The most honest men, after careful consideration of the public records and examination of various issues, decided to erect a statue for him, the restorer of public works, because of his countless services to the city and to his family, and because, above the natural affection of a citizen, Lepcis Magna, outstanding in loyalty and devotion, has recognized his merits in many ways. They decreed a statue to Flavius Victor Calpurnius, a most accomplished man, governor of the province of Tripolitana, their patron, and, because of the indivisible affection of mutual love, they erected and dedicated the same statue in his presence.
EDCS-24100015.summary: This decree from Lepcis Magna records the erection of a statue to the provincial governor Flavius Victor Calpurnius by the local elites and people, honoring his services and their mutual affection, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24100028.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aelia Arisuth vixit annos sexaginta plus minus Dis Manibus sacrum Aelios Ma Iurathani bixit sic annosaginta quinque plus minus\n
EDCS-24100028.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Aelia Arisuth lived sixty years, more or less. Sacred to the departed gods. Aelios Ma Iurathani lived sixty-five years, more or less.
EDCS-24100028.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the ages at death of two individuals, Aelia Arisuth and Aelios Ma Iurathani, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24100043.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-24100043.text_edition: \n Quintus Servaeus Quinti filius Quirina Macer adlectus in quinque decurias a divo Hadriano lupam aeream quam ad ornamentum municipii promiserat cum Romulo et Remo dedit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-24100043.translation: Quintus Servaeus Macer, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, admitted to the five decuries by the divine Hadrian, dedicated the bronze she-wolf, which he had promised for the adornment of the municipality, along with Romulus and Remus. He also dedicated it himself.
EDCS-24100043.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a bronze she-wolf statue, along with Romulus and Remus, to the municipality by Quintus Servaeus Macer, who had been appointed to a judicial role by Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to Hadrian's reign (AD 117-138).

EDCS-24100078.text_edition: \n Marco Servilio Publi filio Quirina Draconi Albuciano IIviro flamini perpetuo quod super multa in rem publicam merita et amplissimum munificentiae studium legationem urbicam gratuitum ad Latium maius petendum duplicem susceperit tandemque feliciter renuntiaverit ordo publice ponendam censuit et cum is honore contentus pecuniam rei publicae remisisset populus de suo posuit\n
EDCS-24100078.translation: For Marcus Servilius Draconi, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, Albucianus, twice chief magistrate (duumvir), and perpetual priest, because of his many services to the state and his most ample zeal for munificence, he undertook the urban legation to seek the greater Latium region twice, and finally successfully announced its return. The council decreed that a statue should be publicly erected. Since he was content with this honor and remitted the money to the state, the people erected it at their own expense.
EDCS-24100078.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue to Marcus Servilius Draconi, a local official honored for his public services and munificence, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period when such municipal offices were common.

EDCS-24100137.text_edition: \n Legio III Augusta leimitavit sic Caio Vibio Marso proconsule III dextra decumanum LXX ultra kardinem CCLXXX\n
EDCS-24100137.translation: Legio III Augusta marked out [the boundary] as follows, under the proconsul Gaius Vibius Marsus for the third time: seventy paces on the right, 280 paces beyond the cardo.
EDCS-24100137.summary: A boundary marker inscription set up by Legio III Augusta during the proconsulship of Gaius Vibius Marsus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200153.translation: To the departed gods. Marcus Arruntius Felix rests here. He lived 14 years and one day. His most devoted father made this.
EDCS-24200153.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Arruntius Felix, who died at age 14, dedicated by his father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200156.translation: To the departed gods. Caecilius Maximus rests here. He lived 86 years.
EDCS-24200156.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caecilius Maximus, who died at age 86, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200166.translation: To the departed gods. Gaius Clodius Maximus rests here. He lived 7 years. Gaius Iulius Donatus made this for his most devoted grandson.
EDCS-24200166.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Clodius Maximus, who died at age 7, dedicated by his grandfather Gaius Iulius Donatus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200171.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Cornelius Constantitus lived 17 years, 7 months, and 12 days. Cornelia Assuria, his sister, made this for her dearest brother.
EDCS-24200171.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cornelius Constantitus, who died at age 17 years, 7 months, and 12 days, dedicated by his sister Cornelia Assuria, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200172.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, sacred. Demetrius lived for 60 years and 3 months. Iulia Afrodite, his most devoted wife, made this.
EDCS-24200172.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the lifespan of Demetrius, made by his wife.

EDCS-24200177.translation: …and Festa Cyrilla lived for 3 years and 9 months. Titus Flavius Secundus made this for his most devoted wife and daughter.
EDCS-24200177.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the lifespan of Festa Cyrilla, made by her father.

EDCS-24200179.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Titus Flavius Polybius, his freedman, who deserved well. Titus Flavius Epit…ync…anus, patron, made this.
EDCS-24200179.summary: This is an epitaph for a freedman, Titus Flavius Polybius, dedicated by his patron.

EDCS-24200180.translation: Gavillia Amanda rests here. She lived for 35 years. Lucius Gavillius Peregrinus made this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-24200180.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the lifespan of Gavillia Amanda, made by her husband.

EDCS-24200183.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, sacred. Herennia Tampia lived for 63 years. Her son made this for his dearest mother.
EDCS-24200183.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the lifespan of Herennia Tampia, made by her son.

EDCS-24200186.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, sacred. Iunia Optata rests here. She lived for about 60 years. Iunia Peregrina, her freedwoman, made this.
EDCS-24200186.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the lifespan of Iunia Optata, made by her freedwoman.

EDCS-24200188.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Magnius Setinus rests here. He lived for 82 years. Marcus Magnius Capito, his son, made this for his most devoted father.
EDCS-24200188.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the lifespan of Lucius Magnius Setinus, made by his son.

EDCS-24200194.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, sacred. Lucius Mevius Petronianus rests here. He lived for 20 years, 8 months, and 8 days. Aelia Tyche, his mother, made this for her most devoted son.
EDCS-24200194.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the lifespan of Lucius Mevius Petronianus, made by his mother.

EDCS-24200199.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Pomponius Fortunatus rests here. He lived for 33 years. Cornelia Maxima made this for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-24200199.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the lifespan of Publius Pomponius Fortunatus, made by his wife.

EDCS-24200200.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, sacred. Postumia Donata, also called Pincula, rests here. She lived for 17 years and 8 months. Her husband made this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-24200200.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the lifespan of Postumia Donata, made by her husband.

EDCS-24200205.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Terentia Donata lived for 15 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-24200205.summary: This is a tombstone for Terentia Donata, who died at age 15, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200207.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Vatronia Fortunata lived for 40 years.
