EDCS-24200207.summary: This is a tombstone for Vatronia Fortunata, who died at age 40, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200210.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Vitalis lived for 4 months. Felix, his father, had him placed here.
EDCS-24200210.summary: This is a tombstone for Vitalis, who died at 4 months old, erected by his father Felix, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200233.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ausityche, expressing a peaceful sentiment, likely from the Late Roman or Early Christian period.

EDCS-24200244.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Veneria, expressing a peaceful sentiment, likely from the Late Roman or Early Christian period.

EDCS-24200649.summary: This records a votive offering by Substantius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200656.translation: [...] ordered games with twelve palm fronds for the birthdays of his daughters Victorina and Macedonia, and also prize baskets and a feast for the people, which he commanded to be given annually, from his own money, as a good citizen.
EDCS-24200656.summary: This inscription, likely from a public monument, records the endowment by a citizen for games and distributions to the populace in honor of his daughters, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200676.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, sacred. Ianuarius lived for 50 years. To Rufia Callicora, his dearest wife.
EDCS-24200676.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Ianuarius at age 50, erected by his wife Rufia Callicora, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200677.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Pomponius Amandulus lived for 1 year, 6 months, and 11 days. Lucius Pomponius Urbanus for his dearest son.
EDCS-24200677.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Lucius Pomponius Amandulus at age 1 year, 6 months, and 11 days, erected by his father Lucius Pomponius Urbanus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200699.text_edition: \n Lucio Silicio Luci filio Claudia Saturnino militi legionis III Augustae centuria Iuli Liguris vixit annos XL militavit annos XVIIII defunctus in pugna sub Lucilio centurione inter Aras et Vatari Rerrichae Rustici Romani filiae Silicius Luci filius Felix matri et fratri de sua inpensa fecit\n
EDCS-24200699.translation: To Lucius Silicius, son of Lucius, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, from the century of Iulius Liguris. He lived 40 years and served 19 years, died in battle under Centurion Lucilius between Aras and Vataris. Silicius Felix, son of Lucius, made this for his mother and brother at his own expense.
EDCS-24200699.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Silicius, a soldier of the III Augusta legion who died in battle, erected by his surviving son Silicius Felix for his mother and brother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24300174.text_edition: \n Caius Geminius Rufus pius vixit annos LVII hic situs est\n
EDCS-24300174.translation: Gaius Geminius Rufus, dutiful, lived 57 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-24300174.summary: A tombstone recording the life and burial of Gaius Geminius Rufus, likely dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-24300189.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Caecilius Silvanus, son of Quintus, lived 6 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-24300189.summary: A tombstone for a young child, Lucius Caecilius Silvanus, who died at the age of six, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-24300193.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, sacred. Lucius Rasinius Celer lived 85 years. Quintus Rasinius Saturninus, his most dutiful son, made this for his father. He is buried here.
EDCS-24300193.summary: A tombstone dedicated by a son to his deceased father, Lucius Rasinius Celer, who lived to the age of 85, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-24300194.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, sacred. Vibia Donata lived [...]
EDCS-24300194.summary: A partial tombstone for Vibia Donata, recording her name and the beginning of her lifespan, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-24300201.text_edition: \n VI Kalendas Iulias Valeriano et Lucilo consulibus domino Augusto sacrum Concessurus Luci Memnonis votum solvit\n
EDCS-24300201.translation: On the sixth day before the Kalends of July, in the consulship of Valerianus and Lucilus, Concessurus, a freedman of Lucius Memnon, fulfilled his vow to the divine emperor.
EDCS-24300201.summary: A dedication to the emperor, made by a freedman named Concessurus, dated to the consulship of Valerianus and Lucilus, likely in the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24300229.translation: [...], II, [consul], designated IX, X, father of the fatherland, to Lucius Iavolenus Priscus, imperial legate pro praetore. The community of the Thigenses.
EDCS-24300229.summary: A fragmentary inscription honoring Lucius Iavolenus Priscus, an imperial legate, by the community of the Thigenses, with a date indicated by consular titles, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-24300230.text_edition: \n Imperatore Nerva Caesare Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate consule III Quinto Fabio Barbaro Valerio Magno Iuliano legato Augusti pro praetore castellus sic Thigensium\n
EDCS-24300230.translation: Under Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus, pontifex maximus, in his third tribunician power, consul, with Quintus Fabius Barbarus Valerius Magnus Iulianus, imperial legate pro praetore: the fort of the Thigenses.
EDCS-24300230.summary: An inscription marking a location as the fort of the Thigenses, dated to the consulship of Emperor Nerva and Quintus Fabius Barbarus Valerius Magnus Iulianus, likely from the late 1st century CE.

EDCS-24300245.text_edition: \n Genio dddominorum nnnostrorum Diocletiani et Maximiani Auggustorum et Constanti et Maximiani nobillissimorum Caessarum Varius Flavianus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Valeriae Byzacenae\n
EDCS-24300245.translation: To the genius of our lords Diocletian and Maximian Augusti, and of Constantius and Maximian, most noble Caesars, Varius Flavianus, a most accomplished man and governor of the province of Valeria Byzacena.
EDCS-24300245.summary: A dedication by Varius Flavianus, governor of Valeria Byzacena, to the Augusti Diocletian and Maximian, and the Caesars Constantius and Maximian, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-24400040.translation: To Saturn, sacred to Achaea, Augustus. For the safety of Emperor Caesar Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland. The gens Bacchuiana built the temple with their own money and likewise dedicated it. Candidus, son of Balsamon, from the first eleven, donated additional space in which the temple was built.
EDCS-24400040.summary: A dedication to Saturn and for the health of Emperor Antoninus Pius, recording the construction and endowment of a temple by the gens Bacchuiana and a donation of space by Candidus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-24400142.translation: To Faustina Augusta, wife of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, pontifex maximus, in his fifteenth tribunician power, consul for the third time, by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-24400142.summary: An inscription honoring Faustina Augusta, wife of Marcus Aurelius, erected by public funds as decided by the local council, dated to Marcus Aurelius's 15th tribunician power and 3rd consulship, likely around 175 CE.

EDCS-24501002.text_edition: \n Extricata Imr serva Felicis saltuari filia pia vixit annis IIII hic sita\n
EDCS-24501002.translation: Extricata, the devoted slave girl of Felix, daughter of Saltuarius, lived for 4 years and is buried here.
EDCS-24501002.summary: This is a tombstone for a young slave girl named Extricata, dating from antiquity.

EDCS-24501023.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Pullaienus Papias pius vixit annos XVIII hic situs est Primigenius pater posuit\n
EDCS-24501023.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Pullaienus Papias, devoted, lived for 18 years and is buried here. His father Primigenius erected this.
EDCS-24501023.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Pullaienus Papias, erected by his father, dating from antiquity.

EDCS-24501052.text_edition: \n Amodb Secundi IAI ASAE pia vixit annos XVI Lucius Secundus Mutthuni LIMBENE pius vixit annos LXXXV Felix filius parentibus fecit\n
EDCS-24501052.translation: Amodb Secundi (devoted), lived for 16 years. Lucius Secundus Mutthuni (devoted), lived for 85 years. Felix, their son, erected this for his parents.
EDCS-24501052.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Secundus Mutthuni and his unnamed spouse, erected by their son Felix, dating from antiquity.

EDCS-24501099.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Commodo Antonino Augusto V Manio Acilio Glabrione II consulibus pridie Kalendas Februarias et possessorum civium ibi Victor Callitiosi filius et Honoratus filius um quaestos quod agri suorum pecoribus ovium devastarentur et tem conrosas sic esse quod ipsum initium honoris nostri at sit facta etiam mentione sacrarum litterarum ONnostramque denuntiationem initium honoris nostrit nec debeat eis contumacia eorum prodesse et aliter ea res s secundum sacras constitutiones actum fuerit sic quae decreto subiciendam existimavimus et aecum mihi desiderium vestrum videtur et exemplo adiuvari --- in? agrum vestrum invitis vobis pecora pascendi gratia induccare quod si ignorante domino servus induxerrit pecora in ipsum servum proconsul severe constituet si iusso sic domini etiam praetium sic servi ex forma censoria |(denarios) D dominus dare debebit si sua sponte id admiserint a proconsule flectentur sic ita ut in relegentur quit sic fieri placeret de ea re universi censuerunt passim in territorio unius cuiuscumque pecora pascendi facultatemncentur et cum etiam post ex constitutione \n
EDCS-24501099.translation: Under the emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus, and Manius Acilius Glabrio, consuls for the second time, on the day before the Kalends of February...[This inscription appears to be a legal decree or dispute regarding grazing rights and penalties, dating to the consulship of Commodus and Acilius Glabrio in the late 2nd century CE.]
EDCS-24501099.summary: This inscription is a legal text concerning grazing rights and penalties, dated to the consulship of Commodus and Acilius Glabrio (c. 185 CE).

EDCS-24501126.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-24501126.text_edition: \n Mercurio Augusto sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aurelli sic Severi Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti pontificis maximi patris patriae tribunicia potestate XII proconsulis et Iuliae Mamaeae Augustae matris Augusti et senatus et patriae totiusque domus divinae eorum Quintus Cervius Luci filius Lucretius Maximus et Caius Geminius Cai filius Victoricus aediles sua pecunia fecerunt et ob dedicationem epulum decurionibus et missilia populo dederunt et fistula plumbea cum epitonio aereo ad labrum lapideum aqua ut saliret aeque sua pecunia curaverunt locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-24501126.translation: Sacred to Mercury Augustus for the health of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, Father of the Fatherland, holder of tribunician power for the 12th time, proconsul, and Julia Mamaea Augusta, Mother of Augustus and the Senate and of the whole Fatherland, and of their divine household. Quintus Cervius Lucretius Maximus, son of Lucius, and Caius Geminius Victoricus, son of Caius, aediles, made this at their own expense and for the dedication, they provided a feast for the decurions and distributed gifts to the people, and they also took care, at their own expense, to have a lead pipe with a bronze fitting so that water would flow to a stone basin. The site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-24501126.summary: This is a dedication of a public work, likely a fountain, to Mercury Augustus for the health of Emperor Severus Alexander and Julia Mamaea, funded by the aediles Quintus Cervius Lucretius Maximus and Caius Geminius Victoricus, dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24501163.text_edition: \n Adoni Augusto sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Augusti et Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti et Iuliae Domine sic Augustae\n
EDCS-24501163.translation: Sacred to Adonis Augustus for the health of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus and the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus and Julia Domna Augusta.
EDCS-24501163.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Adonis Augustus for the health of Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Julia Domna, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24501229.text_edition: \n Thoraci mirae integritatis et innocentiae inimitabilis exempli viro Caelio Severo viro clarissimo patricio consulari curatori rei publicae et patrono coloniae Puppitanorum qui solus sua liberalitate forum vetustate conlapsum cum aedibus et capitolio et curia meliori cultu restituit et dedicavit ordo Puppitanorum patrono perpetuo dedicata VIIII Kalendas Iunias Imperatore Probo Augusto V et Victorino consulibus\n
EDCS-24501229.translation: To Thorac, a man of wondrous integrity and inimitable example of innocence, to Caelus Severus, a most distinguished man, a patrician, curator of the Republic, and patron of the colony of Puppita, who alone, with his own generosity, restored and dedicated the forum, which had collapsed from age, along with the buildings, the Capitol, and the curia, with better craftsmanship. The ordo of the Puppitans to their perpetual patron. Dedicated on the 9th day before the Kalends of June, when Emperor Probus Augustus was consul for the 5th time and Victorinus was consul.
EDCS-24501229.summary: This inscription honors Caelus Severus, a distinguished patron of the colony of Puppita, for his restoration of the forum and public buildings, dating to the reign of Emperor Probus (276-282 CE).

EDCS-24501257.translation: In the consulship of Marullus and Aelianus, he fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-24501257.summary: This is a brief votive inscription, recording the fulfillment of a vow during the consulship of Marullus and Aelianus.

EDCS-24501260.translation: In the second consulship of Fuscianus Silanus, he fulfilled his vow willingly and with good spirit.
EDCS-24501260.summary: This is a brief votive inscription, recording the fulfillment of a vow during the second consulship of Fuscianus Silanus.

EDCS-24501266.translation: He fulfilled his vow willingly and with good spirit on the 4th day before the Kalends of May, in the consulship of Tertullus and Clemens.
EDCS-24501266.summary: This is a brief votive inscription, recording the fulfillment of a vow on the 4th day before the Kalends of May, during the consulship of Tertullus and Clemens.

EDCS-24501482.text_edition: \n Caio Arrio Cai filio Quirina Calpurnio Longino clarissimo viro consuli legato Karthaginis auguri praetori urbano quaestori tribuno legionis triumviro auro argento flando feriundo Avioccalenses civi et patrono\n
EDCS-24501482.translation: To Gaius Arrius Calpurnius Longinus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, most distinguished man, consul, legate of Carthage, augur, urban praetor, quaestor, tribune of the legion, triumvir for striking gold and silver coinage, as a citizen and patron of the Avioccalenses.
EDCS-24501482.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Arrius Calpurnius Longinus, a prominent Roman official and patron, by the people of Avioccalenses.

EDCS-24501906.translation: Public property of the municipality of the Sufetulenses, from the Musunii. Marcus Junius Rufinus Sabinianus, most distinguished man.
EDCS-24501906.summary: This inscription records a public work funded by the Musunii for the municipality of Sufetulenses, mentioning Marcus Junius Rufinus Sabinianus as a distinguished man.

EDCS-24501908.translation: Public property of the municipality of the Sufetulenses, from the Musunii. Marcus Junius Rufinus Sabinianus, most distinguished man.
EDCS-24501908.summary: This inscription records a public work funded by the Musunii for the municipality of Sufetulenses, mentioning the distinguished man Marcus Junius Rufinus Sabinianus (with a partial restoration).

EDCS-24501911.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Domitia Prima, daughter of Gaius, dutiful, lived 60 years, is buried here.
EDCS-24501911.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Domitia Prima, recording her age and burial place.

EDCS-24501913.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Honorata Fortunata lived [approximate age] years.
EDCS-24501913.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Honorata Fortunata, likely recording her name and lifespan.

EDCS-24501939.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Cai filius Galeria Lugduno Mansuetus miles legionis III Augustae centuria Veti vixit annis XXXIIX militavit annis XIIX hic situs est Caius Iulius PVIVS \n
EDCS-24501939.translation: Gaius Julius Mansuetus, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, from Lugduno, soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, under Centurion Vetus, lived 38 years, served 18 years, is buried here. Gaius Julius PVIVS.
EDCS-24501939.summary: This is a soldier's tombstone for Gaius Julius Mansuetus, detailing his military service, age, and burial, with an additional name.

EDCS-24501950.text_edition: \n Epithymetus Sergia Corneli Cethegi proconsulis servus hic situs est vixit nnos X\n
EDCS-24501950.translation: Epithymetus, slave of Sergia Cornelia Cethega, proconsul's wife, is buried here. Lived 10 years.
EDCS-24501950.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Epithymetus, a slave, recording his age and owner.

EDCS-24501960.translation: Petronius Gallianus, who is also called Harpaxius, lived 45 years, is buried here. Pompeia Maxima placed this for her devoted husband.
EDCS-24501960.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life and burial of Petronius Gallianus, erected by his wife Pompeia Maxima.

EDCS-24501977.text_edition: \n AES Impperatoribus ddominis nnostris Diocletiano III et Maximiano II conssulibus hheredes Sulpicii Felicis istut sic opus plateae quam Felix pater eques Romanus ob aedilitatis suae honorem promisit civitati nostrae cum gradibus III propriis sumptibus perfecerunt per Sulpicium Primum tutorem\n
EDCS-24501977.translation: For the safety of our lords the Emperors Diocletian Augustus for the third time and Maximian Augustus for the second time, consuls. The heirs of Sulpicius Felix completed this work on the street, which Felix, his father, a Roman knight, promised for the honor of his aedilship, for our city, with three flights of steps, at their own expense, under the guardianship of Sulpicius Primus.
EDCS-24501977.summary: This inscription records the completion of a public works project, a street with steps, funded by the heirs of Sulpicius Felix in honor of his aedilship, during the consulship of Diocletian and Maximian.

EDCS-24502012.text_edition: \n Pro salutem sic domini nostri cultores Iovis Optimi Maximi paganicum suum sua pecunia fecerunt magistri ATVCI \n
EDCS-24502012.translation: For the well-being of our lord, the worshippers of Jupiter Optimus Maximus made their pagan festival at their own expense, the magistrates ATVCI.
EDCS-24502012.summary: This inscription records a religious festival funded by the magistrates ATVCI for the well-being of their lord and in honor of Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-24502091.translation: Sacred to the Great Mother of the Gods, Idaea Augusta. For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus Pius Felix Augustus and his entire divine family. Quintus Arellius Optatianus, a Roman knight and priest, having completed the sacred rites of the lamb and bull sacrifices, with the approval of the order of his colony of Mactaris, has been approved as chief priest, at his own expense, with Rannius Salvio, a Roman knight and pontiff, and Claudius Faustus, priests, handing over the proceedings, along with all the dendrophori and consecrated persons of both sexes. He has willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-24502091.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Quintus Arellius Optatianus, a Roman knight and priest, to the Great Mother of the Gods, Idaea Augusta, for the well-being of Emperor Probus, and was approved by the local council of Mactaris.

EDCS-24502092.text_edition: \n Matri Deum Magnae Ideae Augustae sacrum pro salute Impperatorum Caessarum Cai Valeri Diocletiani Pii Felicis Augusti et Marci Aureli Valeri M[axi][miani] Pii Pii Felicis Augusti totiusque divinae domus eorum Quintus Minthonius Fortunatus sacerdos perfectis ritae sic sacris cernorum crioboli et tauroboli suffragio ordinis coloniae suae Mactaritanae comprobatus antistes sumptibus suis tradente Claudio Bono sacerdote una cum universis dendroforiis sic et sacratis utriusque sexus votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-24502092.translation: Sacred to the Great Mother of the Gods, Idaea Augusta. For the safety of the Emperors Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletian Pius Felix Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian Pius Pius Felix Augustus and their entire divine family. Quintus Minthonius Fortunatus, a priest, having completed the sacred rites of the lamb and bull sacrifices, with the approval of the order of his colony of Mactaris, has been approved as chief priest, at his own expense, with Claudius Bonus, priest, handing over the proceedings, along with all the dendrophori and consecrated persons of both sexes. He has willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-24502092.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Quintus Minthonius Fortunatus, a priest, to the Great Mother of the Gods, Idaea Augusta, for the well-being of Emperors Diocletian and Maximian, with the approval of the council of Mactaris.

EDCS-24700295.text_edition: \n Karthagini Augustae sacrum ex testamento Quinti Aproni Quinti filii Arnensi Vitalis honestae memoriae viri qui rei publicae Uchitanorum maiorum at sic statuam milia et in epulationem sui HS II milia ndam adfectionem amplificationem HMIL epulationis concessit et ex HS V milibus fieri curavit Caius Apronius Extricatus ad exornandam munificentiam fratris basem cum impensa perferendae et constituendae statuae suo sumptu et cura posuit donum dedit\n
EDCS-24700295.translation: Sacred to Carthage Augusta. From the will of Quintus Apronius Vitalis, son of Quintus, of the Arnensian tribe, a man of honorable memory, who granted 2,000 sesterces for a statue and 2,000 sesterces for a banquet for himself for the public affairs of the people of Uchita. Caius Apronius Extricatus, to adorn the generosity of his brother, erected the base for the statue and its expenses at his own expense and care, and presented it as a gift.
EDCS-24700295.summary: This inscription records a posthumous dedication by Quintus Apronius Vitalis to Carthage Augusta, with his brother Caius Apronius Extricatus completing the monument and statue base.

EDCS-24700297.text_edition: \n Saturno Augusto pro salute Imperatoris Nervae Quintus Urvinius Quinti libertus Callistus templum a solo donum dedit pecunia publica fecit\n
EDCS-24700297.translation: To Saturn Augustus. For the safety of the Emperor Nerva. Quintus Urvinius Callistus, freedman of Quintus, dedicated a temple from the ground up; he also constructed it with public funds.
EDCS-24700297.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple to Saturn Augustus by Quintus Urvinius Callistus, freedman of Quintus, for the safety of Emperor Nerva, built with public funds.

EDCS-24700300.text_edition: \n Victoriae Parthicae Britannicae Germanicae maximae Augustae Imperatoris Caesaris divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Britannici maximi fili divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepotis divi Antonini Pii pronepotis Marci Aureli Severi Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Parthici maximi Britannici maximi Germanici maximi pontificis maximi tribuniciae potestatis XVII imperatoris II consulis IIII patris patriae pecunia publica Uchitanorum maiorum decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-24700300.translation: To Victoria Parthica Britannica Germanica Maxima Augusta, daughter of Emperor Caesar divus Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, granddaughter of divus Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-granddaughter of divus Antoninus Pius, great-great-granddaughter of Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 17th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Country. Erected with public funds by decree of the decurions of the people of Uchita.
EDCS-24700300.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Victoria Augusta, commemorating military victories under Emperor Caracalla, and was funded by the people of Uchita.

EDCS-24700303.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 22nd time, Armenian, Mede, Parthicus Maximus, Imperator for the 5th time, Consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Country, son of divus Antoninus, grandson of divus Hadrianus, great-grandson of divus Traianus Parthicus, great-great-grandson of divus Nerva.
EDCS-24700303.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Caracalla, listing his numerous titles and lineage, likely dating to his 22nd year of tribunician power.

EDCS-24700305.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of divus Antoninus, brother of divus Verus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of divus Hadrianus, great-grandson of divus Traianus Parthicus, great-great-grandson of divus Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 31st time, Imperator for the 8th time, Consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Country. Fortunatus, by virtue of his many offices, [at his own expense]...
EDCS-24700305.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Caracalla, listing his titles and tribunician power, with a partial mention of Fortunatus and his extensive magisterial duties.

EDCS-24700308.translation: [For the safety(?)] of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, Imperator for the 4th time, Consul for the 2nd time, Father of the Country. The public administration of the people of Uchita Maiora made it at its own expense and dedicated it by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-24700308.summary: This inscription records a dedication, likely for the safety of Emperor Commodus, erected by the public administration of Uchita Maiora at their own expense.

EDCS-24700309.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, Imperator for the 4th time, Consul for the 2nd time, Father of the Country. The public administration of Uchita Maiora made it at its own expense and dedicated it by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-24700309.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Commodus, listing his titles and tribunician power, erected by the public administration of Uchita Maiora.

EDCS-24700310.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pert<i=E>naci Augusto Parthico Arabico Parthico Adiabenico tribunicia potestate V imperatori VIIII consuli II patri patriae divi Marci filio divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti res publica Uchitanorum maiorum pecunia publica posuit in quam rem Caius Lucilius Cai filius Athenaeus sacerdos Cereris coloniae Iuliae Karthaginis anni CCXXXV flamen perpetuus depensis in curam supra scriptam quam ipse gessit HS XII milibus nummum summae suae honorariae et amplius pecunia publica erogata basem cum ornamentis suis sua pecunia fecit et epulo decurionibus dato dedicavit decreto decurionum \n
EDCS-24700310.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Parthicus Arabicus Parthicus Adiabenicus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, Imperator for the 9th time, Consul for the 2nd time, Father of the Country, son of divus Marcus, brother of divus Commodus, grandson of divus Antoninus, great-grandson of divus Hadrianus, great-great-grandson of divus Traianus, great-great-great-grandson of divus Nerva. The public administration of the people of Uchita Maiora placed it with public funds. Caius Lucilius Athenaeus, son of Caius, priest of Ceres of the colony of Iulia Karthago in the year 235, perpetual flamen, contributed 12,000 sesterces from his own funds for the aforementioned care which he himself managed, in addition to public funds, and made the base with its ornaments at his own expense and dedicated it after providing a banquet for the decurions, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-24700310.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument to Emperor Septimius Severus by the people of Uchita Maiora, with significant personal contributions from Caius Lucilius Athenaeus, a local priest and flamen.

EDCS-24700311.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Augustus, father of the fatherland, pontiff, maximum, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, imperator for the 8th time, consul for the 2nd time, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Saramaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, and great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-24700311.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus, likely on a public building or monument, as indicated by the phrase 'by decree of the decurions, at public expense'.

EDCS-24700312.translation: To Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camps, wife of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Augustus, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-24700312.summary: This is a dedication to Julia Domna, wife of Septimius Severus, likely on a public building or monument, as indicated by the phrase 'by decree of the decurions, at public expense'.

EDCS-24700314.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, destined Emperor, son of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Augustus, father of the fatherland, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-24700314.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), son of Septimius Severus, likely on a public building or monument, as indicated by the phrase 'by decree of the decurions, at public expense'.

EDCS-24700319.translation: Under the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-24700319.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription indicating a date during the reign of Emperor Claudius Gothicus.

EDCS-24800764.text_edition: \n VS SILVEVS Lammiani et Domitiani Thusdritano iunctae sunt ius Marinus et Doryphorus Primigenio suo salutem exemplum epistulae scriptae nobis a Tutilio Pudente egregio viro ut notum haberes et id quod subiectum est celeberrimis locis propone nulla VII proximis annis scriptam iubeas sermo procuratorum Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti quia Caesar noster infatigabili cura sua per quam adsidue pro humanis utilitatibus excubat omnes partes agrorum quae tam oleis quam frumentis aptae sunt excoli iubet idcirco permissum providentiae eius potestas fit omnibus etiam eas partes occupandi quae in centuriis elocatis saltus Blandiani et Udensis et in illis partibus sunt quae ex saltu Lamiano et Domitiano iunctae Thusdritano sunt nec a conductoribus exercentur isque qui occupaverint possidendi ac fruendi heredique suo relinquendi id ius datur quod et lege Hadriana comprehensum de rudibus agris et iis qui per decem annos continuos inculti sunt nec ex Blandiano et Udensi saltu maiores partes fructuum exigentur a possesoribus quam quartas ideo qua cetera omnia iussa Caesaris nostri augeri quam ullo m diu minui sinis si quis in ea loca neglecta ab conductoribus occupaverit quae rigari solent si tertias partes fructuum dabit de his quoque regionibus quae ex Lamiano et Domitiano saltu iunctae Thusdritano sunt tantundem dabit de oleis quas quisque aut in scrobibus posuerit aut oleastris inseruerit captorum fructuum nulla decem proximis exigetur sed nec de pomis septem annis proximis nec alia poma in divisionem umquam cadent quam quae venibunt a possessoribus quas partes aridas fructuum quisque debebit dare eas proximo quinquennio ei dabit in cuius conductione agrum occupaverit post it tempus rationi Caesaris nostri in fsis e lege relocan\n
EDCS-24800764.translation: VS SILVEVS to Lammianus and Domitianus, joined to Thusdritus, sends greetings from Marinus and Doryphorus Primigenius. Let it be known by you, as we have been informed by an example of an epistle written to us by Tutilius Pudens, an excellent man, and let it be proposed in the most frequented places. In the last seven years, because our Caesar, with his tireless care, through which he unceasingly watches over human interests, orders all parts of the lands, which are suitable for both olives and grains, to be cultivated. For this reason, his foresight permits the power to extend to everyone, even to occupy those parts which are in the leased centuriae of the Blandian and Udens pastures, and in those parts which are joined from the Lamian and Thusdritan pastures, and are not worked by contractors. And to him who shall occupy them, the right is given to possess and enjoy, and to leave to his heir, which is also comprehended in the Hadrianic law concerning uncultivated lands, and those which have been uncultivated for ten continuous years. Nor shall larger portions of the fruits be exacted from the possessors than a quarter from the Blandian and Udens pastures. Therefore, as all other commands of our Caesar are to be increased, do not allow any to be diminished for a long time. If anyone shall occupy in those places, neglected by the contractors, which are usually irrigated, he shall give a third part of the fruits. He shall give the same from those regions which are joined from the Lamian and Domitian pastures to Thusdritus. For the olive trees which anyone has planted either in pits or grafted from wildings, no fruits shall be exacted for the nearest ten years, but also for the fruits of apples for the nearest seven years, nor shall other apples ever fall into division than those which are sold by the possessors. The proportions of dry fruits which anyone shall owe, he shall give them in the next five years to him in whose lease he occupied the land. After that time, they will be relocated to the accounts of our Caesar according to the law. The area is 100 iugera.
EDCS-24800764.summary: This is a rescript from Emperor Trajan Hadrian to Tutilius Pudens, concerning the cultivation and occupation of lands in the Blandian and Udens pastures, outlining terms for settlers and harvest shares.

EDCS-24800810.translation: Having undergone the taurobolium, the priest dedicated (it).
EDCS-24800810.summary: This is a short dedication by a priest who has undergone the taurobolium, a ritual sacrifice.

EDCS-24900150.translation: Sacred to the deified spirits. Licinius Germullus, soldier of the I Urbana cohort, served 19 years, lived [---].
EDCS-24900150.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Licinius Germullus, a soldier of the I Urbana cohort, recording his service and lifespan.

EDCS-24900288.text_edition: \n Lucius Atilius Luci libertus Hiero furnarius Valeria |(mulieris) liberta Euterpe furnaria vivit Caius Valerius Cai libertus Dionisius sic triarius vivit\n
EDCS-24900288.translation: Lucius Atilius Hiero, freedman of Lucius, baker. Valeria Euterpe, freedwoman of [---], baker, lives. Caius Valerius Dionisius, freedman of Caius, a veteran, lives.
EDCS-24900288.summary: This is a funerary inscription for several individuals, including bakers and a veteran, indicating their familial relationships and occupations.

EDCS-24900289.translation: Asyllia Polla, daughter of Lucius, physician, lies here. She lived 65 years. Euscius, her freedman, made this at his own expense.
EDCS-24900289.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Asyllia Polla, a physician, recording her age at death and stating it was paid for by her freedman.

EDCS-24900292.translation: To the deified spirits. Quintus Julius Ingenuus, soldier of Legion VII Gemina Felix, lived 43 years, served 21 years. Flavius Marinus, his heir, erected this index.
EDCS-24900292.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Quintus Julius Ingenuus, a soldier of the VII Gemina Felix legion, recording his age at death and length of service, and noting it was erected by his heir.

EDCS-24900293.translation: To Cornelius Marcellus, soldier of the 13th Urban Cohort. Cornelius Crescens lived 30 years, deserving of his patron.
EDCS-24900293.summary: A funerary inscription for Cornelius Marcellus, a soldier in the 13th Urban Cohort, set up by Cornelius Crescens.

EDCS-24900296.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Felix Caesaris nostri cursor servus pius vixit nnos XVIII collegium mulionum ob merita\n
EDCS-24900296.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Felix, a faithful servant and courier of our emperor, lived 18 years. The guild of muleteers [set this up] for his merits.
EDCS-24900296.summary: A funerary inscription for Felix, a servant and courier of the emperor, honored by the guild of muleteers.

EDCS-24900299.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aurelius [name illegible], a faithful attendant born in the imperial household, lived 34 years and 8 months and 9 days. He lies here.
EDCS-24900299.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius, a domestic attendant to the emperors, who died at the age of 34.

EDCS-24900300.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Romanus, a faithful land surveyor of the emperor, lived 50 years. He lies here.
EDCS-24900300.summary: A funerary inscription for Romanus, an imperial land surveyor, who died at the age of 50.

EDCS-24900302.text_edition: \n Lascivus Augusti servus nomenclator pius vixit nnos XXII hic situs est\n
EDCS-24900302.translation: Lascivus, a faithful servant of the emperor and a nomenclator (official list-keeper), lived 22 years. He lies here.
EDCS-24900302.summary: A funerary inscription for Lascivus, an imperial servant and nomenclator, who died at the age of 22.

EDCS-24900306.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Felix, a faithful servant and attendant of our emperor, lived 48 years. He lies here. Aelia Melpomene made this for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-24900306.summary: A funerary inscription for Felix, an imperial servant, set up by his wife Aelia Melpomene.

EDCS-24900311.translation: Primus, servant of Caesar, lived 3 years. He lies here. Nicodicus, servant of Caesar and a messenger, made this for his devoted son.
EDCS-24900311.summary: A funerary inscription for Primus, son of Nicodicus, a servant and messenger of Caesar.

EDCS-24900314.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Aelius Optatus, a faithful freedman of the emperor and a scribe, lived 30 years. He lies here. His sister made this.
EDCS-24900314.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Optatus, an imperial freedman and scribe, set up by his sister.

EDCS-24900316.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Cladus, a faithful servant of the emperor and assistant to the scribe, lived 27 years and 4 months. He lies here. His devoted wife Quietula made this.
EDCS-24900316.summary: A funerary inscription for Cladus, an imperial servant and scribe's assistant, set up by his wife Quietula.

EDCS-24900318.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Saturninus, a faithful servant and assistant to the scribe of our emperor, lived 40 years and 7 months and 5 days. He lies here.
EDCS-24900318.summary: A funerary inscription for Saturninus, an imperial servant and scribe's assistant, who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-24900325.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Agatho, a devoted slave, lived 45 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900325.summary: A tombstone for Agatho, a slave, who lived 45 years, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900326.translation: Agathopus, vicarius and slave of the Emperor, lived 24 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900326.summary: A tombstone for Agathopus, a vicarius and slave of the Emperor, who lived 24 years, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900330.translation: Attia Data lived 1 year and 10 months. She is buried here. Her parents made this for their devoted daughter.
EDCS-24900330.summary: A tombstone for Attia Data, who lived 1 year and 10 months, erected by her parents, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900331.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Attis, a slave of the Emperor, lived 11 years. She is buried here. Spendo for his most sweet fellow freedwoman.
EDCS-24900331.summary: A tombstone for Attis, a slave of the Emperor, who lived 11 years, possibly erected by Spendo, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900338.translation: Campanus, a devoted slave of Caesar, lived 14 years. He is buried here. Gargilia Veneria, his wife, made this for her devoted husband.
EDCS-24900338.summary: A tombstone for Campanus, a slave of Caesar, who lived 14 years, erected by his wife Gargilia Veneria, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900342.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Chrestus, a most devoted slave of the Emperors, lived 5 years, 11 months, and 14 days. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900342.summary: A tombstone for Chrestus, a slave of the Emperors, who lived 5 years, 11 months, and 14 days, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900346.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Claudia Eugenia lived 24 years and 6 months. Claudia Cratiste lived 15 years. They are buried here.
EDCS-24900346.summary: A tombstone for Claudia Eugenia and Claudia Cratiste, who lived 24 years, 6 months, and 15 years respectively, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900348.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. [---]fio, freedman of the Emperor, [---]fo Cocceia.
EDCS-24900348.summary: A funerary monument fragment recording a freedman of the Emperor and Cocceia, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900353.translation: Crescens lived 2 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900353.summary: A tombstone for Crescens, who lived 2 years, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900354.translation: Crito, a devoted slave of Caesar, lived 40 years. He is buried here. His devoted sons made this.
EDCS-24900354.summary: A tombstone for Crito, a slave of Caesar, who lived 40 years, erected by his sons, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900355.text_edition: \n Daphnis ego Hermetis coniunx sum libera facta cum dominus vellet primus Hermes liber ut esset fato ego facta prior fato ego rapta prior quae tuli quod gemui gemitus viro saepe reliqui quae domino invito vitam dedi proxime nato nunc quis alet natum quis vitae longa ministrat me Styga sic quod rapuit tam cito eni<m=A> a superos sic pia vixit annis XXV hic sita est \n
EDCS-24900355.translation: I, Daphnis, wife of Hermes, was freed, being freed by my master's wish that Hermes should be freed first. Fate freed me sooner, fate snatched me away sooner. What I bore and what I groaned for, I often left my husband. Forcing my life away from my master, for my son recently born. Who will now nourish my child? Who will provide for his life? As the one whom Death snatched so quickly, so did I, who lived piously, lie here for 25 years.
EDCS-24900355.summary: A funerary epitaph for Daphnis, a freedwoman, recounting her life and premature death after her husband Hermes was freed, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900357.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Dionysia, a pious slave of Caesar, lived for 3 years. She is buried here. Her mother Tertia made this.
EDCS-24900357.summary: A tombstone for Dionysia, a pious slave of Caesar, who died at the age of three, erected by her mother Tertia, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900360.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Elegans lived piously for 40 years.
EDCS-24900360.summary: A funerary inscription for Elegans, who lived piously for 40 years, likely dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900362.translation: Erucia Vitalis lived piously for 34 years. She is buried here. Vitalis, a slave of our Caesar, made this for his pious wife.
EDCS-24900362.summary: A tombstone for Erucia Vitalis, erected by her husband Vitalis, a slave of Caesar, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900363.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Eutaxia, a pious slave of our Caesar, lived for 9 years and 4 months. She is buried here. May your bones rest well. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-24900363.summary: A funerary epitaph for Eutaxia, a pious slave of Caesar, who died at the age of 9 years and 4 months, with standard wishes for the deceased, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900365.translation: Publius Fabius Secundus lived piously for 75 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900365.summary: A tombstone for Publius Fabius Secundus, who lived piously for 75 years, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900367.translation: Faustilla, a pious slave of our Caesar, lived for 60 years. Her daughter Helena for 30 years. Felix lived for 2 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900367.summary: A funerary inscription commemorating Faustilla, her daughter Helena, and a child named Felix, all associated with Caesar's household, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900370.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Faustus and Saturninus Secundus, brothers and pious slaves of our Caesar, lived for 18 and 6 years respectively. They are buried here. Saturninus and Ianuaria, parents, made this for their most pious sons.
EDCS-24900370.summary: A tombstone for two brothers, Faustus and Saturninus Secundus, slaves of Caesar, erected by their parents, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900373.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Felicula, a pious slave of Augustus, lived for 25 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-24900373.summary: A tombstone for Felicula, a pious slave of Augustus, who died at the age of 25, dated to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-24900376.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Felix lived piously for 90 years and 5 months. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900376.summary: A funerary inscription for Felix, who lived a pious life for 90 years and 5 months, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900382.translation: Flavia Magnia Callippina, devout, lived for 2 years and 4 months; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900382.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Flavia Magnia Callippina at a young age.

EDCS-24900383.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Flavia Safsula lived for 22 years, 25 days, and 9 hours; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900383.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Flavia Safsula at the age of 22.

EDCS-24900384.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Flavia Tertia, freedwoman of Augustus, devout, lived for 80 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900384.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Flavia Tertia, a freedwoman of the emperor, at the age of 80.

EDCS-24900386.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Fuficius Nobilis, devout, lived for 46 years, 7 months, and 9 days. Maxima made this for her most devout husband; he is buried here.
EDCS-24900386.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Gaius Fuficius Nobilis, erected by his wife Maxima.

EDCS-24900388.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. [Name missing]us, devout, lived for 40 years. Gemellus made this for his most devout brother; he is buried here.
EDCS-24900388.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of an unnamed man who lived 40 years, erected by his brother Gemellus.

EDCS-24900390.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Heraclida, devout, lived for 62 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900390.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Heraclida at the age of 62.

EDCS-24900391.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Hilarus, servant of our Caesar, devout, lived for 11 years; likewise Elix, his brother, devout, lived for 2 years. Elix, their father, made this for his most devout sons; they are buried here.
EDCS-24900391.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the deaths of two brothers, Hilarus and Elix, who lived to ages 11 and 2 respectively, erected by their father.

EDCS-24900392.translation: [Name missing] Hygia, devout, lived for 30 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900392.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Hygia at the age of 30.

EDCS-24900394.translation: Ianuarius, servant of Augustus, lived for 5 years.
EDCS-24900394.summary: This is a fragment of a tombstone inscription recording the death of Ianuarius, a servant of the emperor, at the age of 5.

EDCS-24900396.translation: [Name missing]us Inginus F[---], lived for [---] years, [---] days, 5 days.
EDCS-24900396.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone inscription that likely records the name and lifespan of an individual.

EDCS-24900400.translation: Iulia Tertulla the pious lived 55 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900400.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Iulia Tertulla, recording her name, piety, age at death, and burial place.

EDCS-24900401.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Licinia Tertulla, daughter of Marcus, the pious, lived 28 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900401.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Licinia Tertulla, recording her name, parentage, piety, age at death, and burial place.

EDCS-24900402.translation: Lucinus the pious lived 5 years, 2 months, and 10 days.
EDCS-24900402.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Lucinus, recording his name, piety, and age at death in years, months, and days.

EDCS-24900410.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Munatia Apsens, daughter of Lucius, the pious, lived 15 years, 10 months, and 24 days; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900410.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Munatia Apsens, recording her name, parentage, piety, age at death in years, months, and days, and burial place.

EDCS-24900411.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Murrania Maia, the pious, lived 50 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900411.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Murrania Maia, recording her name, piety, age at death, and burial place.

EDCS-24900413.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Norbania Saturnina the pious. As fame proves, I surpassed twice six hundred years of light and also the part of the year we mentioned, twice three combined with ten days. I kept chastity and modesty throughout my life. Happy now I rest in the African soil. You too, passing by, whoever reads this tomb, spare my ashes from being trampled by reckless feet. Thus may much light overflow for you into the heavenly air.
EDCS-24900413.summary: This is a metrical tombstone inscription for Norbania Saturnina, recording her name, piety, a poetic recounting of her lifespan (24 years) and virtues, her burial in Africa, and a plea to passersby.

EDCS-24900417.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Optata Piata, the pious, lived 2 years, 3 months, and 8 days; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900417.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Optata Piata, recording her name, piety, age at death in years, months, and days, and burial place.

EDCS-24900423.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Phaenippus, a pious servant of Augustus, lived 33 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-24900423.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Phaenippus, a servant of Augustus, recording his name, piety, age at death, and burial place.

EDCS-24900424.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Philo, a servant of Augustus, lived 33 years. Laetina, his sister, made this. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900424.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Philo, a servant of Augustus, recording his name, age at death, and that it was erected by his sister, Laetina.

EDCS-24900426.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Prepusa, a servant of Caesar, the pious, lived 7 years and 3 months; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900426.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Prepusa, a servant of Caesar, recording her name, piety, age at death in years and months, and burial place.

EDCS-24900429.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Prima, dutiful, lived for 1 year, 9 months, and 14 days; she rests here.
EDCS-24900429.summary: This is a tombstone for Prima, who died in infancy, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900434.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Romanus, a dutiful slave of our Caesar, lived for 15 years; he rests here.
EDCS-24900434.summary: This is a tombstone for Romanus, a slave of the emperor, who died as a youth, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900435.translation: Salvia, a dutiful slave of Caesar, lived for 30 years; she rests here.
EDCS-24900435.summary: This is a tombstone for Salvia, a slave of the emperor, who died in adulthood, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900436.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Salvia, a dutiful slave of Augustus, lived for 38 years; she rests here. Titus Flavius Salvius had this made out of duty.
EDCS-24900436.summary: This is a tombstone for Salvia, a slave of Augustus, with a dedication by Titus Flavius Salvius, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900438.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Salvius, a slave of our Caesar, lived for two years and eight months; he rests here. Salvius, his father, had this made for his deserving son.
EDCS-24900438.summary: This is a tombstone for Salvius, the son of Salvius, a slave of the emperor, who died in early childhood, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900439.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Sannacrio, dutiful, lived for 7 years; he rests here.
EDCS-24900439.summary: This is a tombstone for Sannacrio, who died in childhood, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900440.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Saturnina, a dutiful slave of our Caesar, lived for 10 years; she rests here.
EDCS-24900440.summary: This is a tombstone for Saturnina, a slave of the emperor, who died in childhood, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900441.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Saturnina, a dutiful slave of our Caesar, lived for 23 years; she rests here.
EDCS-24900441.summary: This is a tombstone for Saturnina, a slave of the emperor, who died in young adulthood, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900445.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Secundus Gallus, a dutiful slave of Augustus, lived for 72 years. Satura, his dutiful wife, lived for 68 years; they rest here.
EDCS-24900445.summary: This is a double tombstone for Secundus Gallus and his wife Satura, both slaves of Augustus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900447.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Servilius Dorus, dutiful, lived for 70 years; he rests here.
EDCS-24900447.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Servilius Dorus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900449.translation: Titus Sextius Extricatus lived for 2 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-24900449.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Titus Sextius Extricatus at the age of two.

EDCS-24900453.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Speratus, servant of Augustus, lived 85 years; he is buried here. Claudia Extricata made this for her devoted husband.
EDCS-24900453.summary: A tombstone set up by Claudia Extricata for her deceased husband, Speratus, a freedman of Augustus.

EDCS-24900454.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. [Name] pia lived [number] years; she is buried here. Sterceius made this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-24900454.summary: A tombstone for a deceased wife, set up by her husband Sterceius, with much of the text missing.

EDCS-24900458.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Syria lived 3 years. Nymphas made this for her devoted daughter.
EDCS-24900458.summary: A tombstone set up by Nymphas for her daughter Syria, who died at the age of three.

EDCS-24900460.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Terentius Pudens, dutiful, lived 19 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-24900460.summary: A tombstone recording the life and burial of Gaius Terentius Pudens, who died at the age of nineteen.

EDCS-24900461.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Tertulla pia lived 40 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900461.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Tertulla at the age of forty.

EDCS-25000608.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Trachalus pius lived 37 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-25000608.summary: A tombstone recording the life and burial of Trachalus, who died at the age of thirty-seven.

EDCS-25000609.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Triumphalis, servant of Augustus, lived 25 years and 4 months; he is buried here. May your bones rest well. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-25000609.summary: A tombstone for Triumphalis, a freedman of Augustus, who died at age twenty-five years and four months, with a wish for peace in the afterlife.

EDCS-25000612.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Valeria Prepusa lived 32 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25000612.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Valeria Prepusa at the age of thirty-two.

EDCS-25000643.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. [Name] pius lived [number] years.
EDCS-25000643.summary: A fragment of a tombstone for a deceased man named [Name], with most of the text missing.

EDCS-25000646.translation: Gaius Accius Tiro, son of Spurius, is buried here.
EDCS-25000646.summary: This is a tombstone recording the burial of Gaius Accius Tiro, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000647.translation: Aebutia Felicula lived for 16 years; may her bones rest well here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-25000647.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Aebutia Felicula, who died at 16, and includes a common funerary formula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000648.translation: [---]lia, [---]bi, freedwoman of Marcus Bennius, lived for 4 years.
EDCS-25000648.summary: This is a fragment of a tombstone for a freedwoman of Marcus Bennius, recording her name and age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000649.translation: Communis, slave of Marcus Vibbius Martialis, is buried here.
EDCS-25000649.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Communis, a slave belonging to Marcus Vibbius Martialis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000650.translation: Helena, slave of Procula Benni, lived for 22 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25000650.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Helena, a slave of Procula Benni, who died at 22, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000651.translation: Manlia Rufilla, daughter of Decimus, is buried here.
EDCS-25000651.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Manlia Rufilla, daughter of Decimus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000652.translation: Masclus, son of Athymus of Bennius, is buried here.
EDCS-25000652.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Masclus, son of Athymus of Bennius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000653.translation: [---]elissa, of Hedulus, is buried here; she lived for [---] years.
EDCS-25000653.summary: This is a fragment of a tombstone recording the burial of a woman named Melissa (or similar), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000655.translation: Gnaeus Munatius Diogenes, freedman of a woman, lived for 70 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-25000655.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Gnaeus Munatius Diogenes, a freedman of a woman, who died at 70, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000657.summary: This inscription likely records the name of a freedman named Tryphaenis, possibly on a tombstone or a similar marker, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000658.text_edition: \n Lucius Vergilius LLuciorum et mulieris libertus Ru<f=E>io hic situs <e=H>(st) \n
EDCS-25000658.translation: Lucius Vergilius, freedman of Lucius and of his wife, is buried here.
EDCS-25000658.summary: A funerary epitaph for a freedman named Lucius Vergilius, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000659.text_edition: \n Vergilia Luci liberta Pia hic sita est\n
EDCS-25000659.translation: Vergilia Pia, freedwoman of Lucius, is buried here.
EDCS-25000659.summary: A funerary epitaph for a freedwoman named Vergilia Pia, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000660.translation: Hysis, slave of Marcus Vibbius Martialis, lived 45 years.
EDCS-25000660.summary: A funerary epitaph for a slave named Hysis, belonging to Marcus Vibbius Martialis, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000669.translation: Sacred to the Deities of the Dead. Marcus Bruttius Felix lived 60 years; he is buried here. Bruttia Secunda, daughter of Marcus, lived [years]; she is buried here. Bruttia Quarta, daughter of Marcus, lived 18 years; she is buried here. Sallustia Quarta, daughter of Lucius, lived [years]; she is buried here.
EDCS-25000669.summary: A multi-part funerary monument recording the lives and burials of Marcus Bruttius Felix and three women named Bruttia and Sallustia, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000672.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph, likely for a person named Caecilius Candidus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000673.translation: Quintus Caecilius Rufus, son of Quintus, pious, lived 4 years.
EDCS-25000673.summary: A funerary epitaph for a child named Quintus Caecilius Rufus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000680.translation: Sacred to the Deities of the Dead. Gaius Clodius [....] lived [...] Gaius Cl[odius
EDCS-25000680.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for a person named Gaius Clodius, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000681.summary: A very fragmentary funerary epitaph, possibly for a woman named Clodia, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000689.translation: Sacred to the Deities of the Dead. Decum[...]
EDCS-25000689.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph, possibly for a person named Decumus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000697.translation: Felicula, slave of Publius Iurius Dexter, pious, lived 25 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25000697.summary: A funerary epitaph for a slave woman named Felicula, belonging to Publius Iurius Dexter, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000700.translation: Quintus Figilius Diodorus, freedman of Quintus; Publius Fufius Dama, freedman of a woman; Fufia Helena, freedwoman of Publius.
EDCS-25000700.summary: This inscription lists the names of four freedmen and freedwomen, likely for a funerary monument.

EDCS-25000702.translation: Marcus Fulvius Rufus, dutiful, lived 24 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-25000702.summary: This is a tombstone recording the name, piety, lifespan, and burial of Marcus Fulvius Rufus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000716.translation: Iulius [name illegible], dutiful, lived [number illegible] years, [number illegible] months.
EDCS-25000716.summary: This is a fragmented tombstone that records the name and lifespan of a deceased individual, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000725.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Longeia Flavia Laurentia, a chaste virgin, lived 17 years, 4 months, 23 days.
EDCS-25000725.summary: This is a tombstone for Longeia Flavia Laurentia, a virgin who died at the age of 17 years, 4 months, and 23 days, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000731.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Malia Nic[...]
EDCS-25000731.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription beginning with a dedication to the departed spirits and the name Malia Nic[...], likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000744.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Perellia Eulymen[ia?], dutiful, lived 22 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25000744.summary: This tombstone records the name and lifespan of Perellia Eulymen[ia?], who lived 22 years and is buried here, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000762.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Sallustia Exorn[ata] [...]
EDCS-25000762.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the departed, with the name Sallustia Exorn[ata], likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000784.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. [Name illegible] Venustus, he is buried here; he lived dutifully for 28 years.
EDCS-25000784.summary: This is a tombstone recording the name Venustus and his lifespan of 28 years, with a dedication to the departed spirits, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000786.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Vibius Psellus, dutiful, lived more or less 28 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-25000786.summary: This tombstone records the name Vibius Psellus, his approximate age of 28 years, and his burial, with a dedication to the departed spirits, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000788.translation: Dedicated to the divine Manes. Victor lived 18 years.
EDCS-25000788.summary: This tombstone records the name Victor and his lifespan of 18 years, with a dedication to the divine Manes, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000792.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Volcacia Charis, dutiful, lived 70 years; she rests here.
EDCS-25000792.summary: A funerary epitaph for Volcacia Charis, recording her age at death and indicating she is buried here, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25000981.text_edition: \n Sexto Caecilio Sexti filio Quirina Aemiliano Xviro stlitibus iudicandis VIIviro epulonum quaestori Augusti candidato legato pro praetore provinciae Africae tribuno plebis praetori patrono decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-25000981.translation: To Sextus Caecilius Aemilianus, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, member of the college of the Xviri stlitibus iudicandis, member of the college of the VIIviri epulonum, quaestor of the Emperor, candidate for the consulship, governor with praetorian power of the province of Africa, tribune of the plebs, praetor, patron, honoured by decree of the local council with public funds.
EDCS-25000981.summary: An honorary inscription honoring Sextus Caecilius Aemilianus, detailing his extensive career in Roman public life and political offices, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001151.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Sextila Sabina, dutiful, lived 35 years; she rests here.
EDCS-25001151.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sextila Sabina, recording her age at death and indicating she is buried here, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001152.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Sextius Adiectus, dutiful, lived 13 years; he rests here.
EDCS-25001152.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Sextius Adiectus, recording his age at death and indicating he is buried here, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001153.translation: Titus Sextius Felicio, dutiful, lived 70 years; he rests here.
EDCS-25001153.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Sextius Felicio, recording his age at death and indicating he is buried here, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001154.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Sextius Festus, dutiful, lived 33 years; he rests here. Faustina, mother, made (this).
EDCS-25001154.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Sextius Festus, recording his age at death and indicating he is buried here, erected by his mother Faustina, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001155.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Sextius Hilarisus, dutiful, lived 30 years; he rests here.
EDCS-25001155.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Sextius Hilarisus, recording his age at death and indicating he is buried here, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001156.translation: Sextia Sperata, freedwoman of Donatus, dutiful, lived 55 years. [Sextia ...] to [her sisters ...] Speratus [...]. They rest here.
EDCS-25001156.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph possibly honoring multiple individuals, including Sextia Sperata and her sisters, indicating their burial place and age at death, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001161.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Sura, a servant of the Emperor, lived 40 years; she rests here. Faustus made (this) for his wife.
EDCS-25001161.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sura, an imperial servant, recording her age at death and indicating she is buried here, erected by her husband Faustus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001166.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria, a servant of the Emperor, dutiful, lived 25 years and three months; she rests here.
EDCS-25001166.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria, an imperial servant, recording her age and lifespan at death and indicating she is buried here, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001170.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Victor lived piously for 8 years. Ianuaria lived piously for 12 years.
EDCS-25001170.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the ages of two children, Victor and Ianuaria, dedicated to the Divine Shades.

EDCS-25001180.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Urbana, daughter, lived for 19 years, here she lies.
EDCS-25001180.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age of Urbana, daughter, dedicated to the Divine Shades.

EDCS-25001186.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Zosime lived piously for 85 years, here she lies.
EDCS-25001186.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age of Zosime, dedicated to the Divine Shades.

EDCS-25001205.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Caecilia Felicitas lived piously for 21 years, here she lies. Viator, servant and assistant of the Emperor's record office, made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-25001205.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caecilia Felicitas, made by her husband Viator, a servant and assistant in the imperial record office.

EDCS-25001206.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Chrestus, custodian of the Emperor's grain warehouses, lived piously for 80 years.
EDCS-25001206.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age of Chrestus, a custodian of imperial grain warehouses.

EDCS-25001218.translation: [---]. Perelius lived piously for [---] years, here he lies.
EDCS-25001218.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the life of Perelius.

EDCS-25001224.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Veneria lived piously for 70 years, here she lies.
EDCS-25001224.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age of Veneria, dedicated to the Divine Shades.

EDCS-25001242.translation: Artoria Harmonia lived piously for 2 years, here she lies.
EDCS-25001242.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age of the child Artoria Harmonia.

EDCS-25001269.translation: Egrilia Romana, daughter of Aulus, lived piously for 20 years, here she lies.
EDCS-25001269.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age of Egrilia Romana, daughter of Aulus.

EDCS-25001278.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Fulvia Donata.

EDCS-25001291.translation: Farsuleia Victrix, devout, lived 38 years; she rests here.
EDCS-25001291.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Farsuleia Victrix, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001298.translation: Iulia Quieta, devout, lived 35 years; she rests here.
EDCS-25001298.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Quieta, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001306.translation: Modia Grata, freedwoman of Caius, devout, lived 40 years; she rests here.
EDCS-25001306.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Modia Grata, a freedwoman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001313.translation: Quintus Pompeius Naus, devout, lived 18 years; he rests here. Quintus Pompeius Quietus, devout, lived 10 years; he rests here.
EDCS-25001313.summary: This is a paired funerary inscription recording the deaths of Quintus Pompeius Naus and Quintus Pompeius Quietus, likely father and son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001328.translation: Marcus Rutilius Liber, devout, lived sixty years.
EDCS-25001328.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Rutilius Liber, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001351.translation: Valeria Secunda, daughter of Quintus, devout, lived 22 years; she rests here.
EDCS-25001351.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valeria Secunda, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001466.text_edition: \n Caio Memmio Cai filio Quirina Fido Iulio Albio consuli sodali Titio legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Noricae curatori viae Flaminiae praefecto Miniciae proconsuli provinciae Baeticae legato Augusti legionis VII Claudiae iuridico per Italiam regionis Transpadanae praetori legato pro praetore provinciae Africae aedili Ceriali quaestori provinciae Asiae tribuno laticlavio legionis II Augustae Caius Annius Iulius Secundus [][] amico rarissimo ob eximiam eius erga se benivolentiam sua pecunia posuerunt et dedicaverunt decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-25001466.translation: Caius Annius Iulius Secundus erected and dedicated this at his own expense with a decree of the decurions, for Gaius Memmius Fidus Iulius Albius, son of Caius, of the Quirina tribe, consul, Titian sodalis, legate of the emperor as proconsul of Noricum, curator of the Flaminian Way, prefect of the Minicia, proconsul of Baetica, legate of Emperor Augustus with the VII Claudia legion, iuridicus for the Transpadane region in Italy, praetor, legate as proconsul of Africa, cereal aedile, quaestor of the province of Asia, broad-striped tribune of the II Augusta legion, his very rare friend, because of his exceptional kindness towards him.
EDCS-25001466.summary: This is a public monument erected by Caius Annius Iulius Secundus to honor Caius Memmius Fidus Iulius Albius, a prominent Roman official, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-25001475.text_edition: \n Asterius filius Rustici archosinagogi sic margaritarii domus dei partem portici tesselavit\n
EDCS-25001475.translation: Asterius, son of Rusticus, the archosinagogus, a pearl merchant, tiled a part of the portico of the house of God.
EDCS-25001475.summary: This inscription records the donation of Asterius, son of Rusticus, a synagogue official and pearl merchant, to a Jewish community building, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001540.text_edition: \n Raniae Flaviae Iulianae Optatae clarissimae feminae matri Flaviorum Flaviani clarissimi pueri et Optatae clarissimae filiae et Flavianillae clarissimae puellae colonia Ammaedarensium sua pecunia fecit\n
EDCS-25001540.translation: The colony of Ammaedarum, at its own expense, made this for Rania Flavia Iuliana Optata, most distinguished woman, mother of Flavius Flavianus, most distinguished boy, and of Optata, most distinguished daughter, and of Flavianilla, most distinguished girl.
EDCS-25001540.summary: This is a public dedication by the colony of Ammaedarum honoring Rania Flavia Iuliana Optata and her children, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001592.translation: Caius Iulius Rogatus, veteran, devout, lived 80 years; he rests here.
EDCS-25001592.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Iulius Rogatus, a veteran, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001596.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Titus Flavius Laetus, freedman of the Emperor, a clerk, dutiful, lived for 70 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001596.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Laetus, a freedman and clerk, who lived to be 70 years old.

EDCS-25001597.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Titus Flavius Tertius, freedman of the Emperor, a clerk, dutiful, lived for 70 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001597.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Tertius, a freedman and clerk, who lived to be 70 years old.

EDCS-25001599.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Donatus, freedman of the Emperor, assistant clerk, dutiful, lived for 50 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001599.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Donatus, a freedman and assistant clerk, who lived to be 50 years old.

EDCS-25001600.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. To Eminenti, assistant clerk of the Emperor, who lived for 30 years, 9 months, and 6 days. His brother Phaenippus erected this.
EDCS-25001600.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Eminenti, an assistant clerk, erected by his brother Phaenippus.

EDCS-25001601.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Eugramus, a slave and assistant clerk of the Emperors, dutiful, lived for 33 years and 5 months. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001601.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Eugramus, an imperial slave and assistant clerk, who lived to be 33 years old.

EDCS-25001602.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Julius Terminalis, dutiful, lived for 81 years and 5 months and 4 days. This was arranged by Terminalis his grandson, through Phaenippus, assistant clerk of our Emperor. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001602.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julius Terminalis, who lived to be 81 years old, arranged by his grandson Terminalis via an imperial assistant clerk.

EDCS-25001603.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Primus, a slave and assistant clerk of the Emperor, dutiful, lived for 43 years. Victoria, his dutiful daughter, lived for 8 years. Primigenia, his dutiful daughter, lived for 3 years. Fortunata, freedwoman of the Emperor, erected this for her deserving husband and children. They are buried here.
EDCS-25001603.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Primus, his two daughters, and his wife Fortunata, erected by Fortunata for her family, all of whom were associated with imperial service.

EDCS-25001604.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Priscus, a slave of our Emperor, assistant clerk, lived for 22 years, 3 months, and 7 days. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001604.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Priscus, an imperial slave and assistant clerk, who lived to be 22 years old.

EDCS-25001605.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Quietus, a slave of the Emperor, assistant clerk, dutiful, lived for 43 years and 7 months. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001605.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quietus, an imperial slave and assistant clerk, who lived to be 43 years old.

EDCS-25001606.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. Salvius, a slave of the Emperor, assistant clerk, lived for 40 years. He is buried here.
