EDCS-25601179.translation: Quintus Maedius Severus, patron of the district and of the citizenry, in his own name, consecrated this temple, which he had promised, enlarged with money, to Fortuna Augusta and Venus Concordia Augusta, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, father of the fatherland, from its foundations.
EDCS-25601179.summary: This is a dedication by Quintus Maedius Severus for the construction of a temple to Fortuna Augusta and Venus Concordia Augusta, intended to ensure the safety of Emperor Hadrian, and records his patron status and the funding of the project.

EDCS-25601193.summary: A short dedication to the god Mercury Silvio.

EDCS-25601198.translation: To Neptune Augustus, sacred. Gaius Helvius Suavis and his wife Cassia Faustina fulfilled their vow. Helvius said, 'I undertook these gifts of the god by vow and established the household gods in my father's house. My wife Faustina placed these same things with me.' To the lord of the waves and father of the Nereids.
EDCS-25601198.summary: A dedication to Neptune by Gaius Helvius Suavis and his wife Cassia Faustina, recording their fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-25601221.text_edition: \n Divo Augusto sacrum et Tiberio Claudio Caesari Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VIII imperatori XVI consuli IV patri patriae censori Caius Artorius Bassus pontifex aedilis IIvir curator Lucus<t=I>ae patronus pagi dedicavit Iulius Venustus Thinobae filius honoribus peractis flamen divi Augusti et Gabinia Felicula uxor et Faustus filius eius huic senatus et plebs ob merita patris omnium portarum sententiis ornamenta sufetis gratis decrevit suo et Fausti Thinobae patris honoribus peractis flaminis divi Augusti et Firmi qui sic civitas ornamenta sufetis ob merita sua decrevit et Saturi sufetis II qui a civitate et plebe suffragio creatus est et Institoris honoribus peractis flamen sic divi Augusti fratrum suorum nomine sua pecunia fecerunt curatore Iulio Firmo filio \n
EDCS-25601221.translation: Sacred to the deified Augustus and to Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 8th time, imperator for the 16th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, censor. Gaius Artorius Bassus, priest, aedile twice, duumvir, curator of Lucusta, patron of the district, dedicated it. Julius Venustus, son of Thinobas, after completing his honors as priest of the deified Augustus, and his wife Gabinia Felicula, and his son Faustus, to whom the senate and people decreed ornaments and fasces as a gift for his father's merits with unanimous votes, honored his father Thinobas and himself, as priest of the deified Augustus. And Firmius, who by virtue of his citizenship received ornaments and fasces from the state and people by vote, and Saturus, who was created a fasces-bearer by the state and people by suffrage, and Institor, after completing his honors as priest of the deified Augustus, his brothers, in their name, made it with their own money, with Julius Firmo, his son, as curator.
EDCS-25601221.summary: A dedication of a monument by Gaius Artorius Bassus and Julius Venustus, honoring the emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus and the deified Augustus, with extensive details about the honors bestowed on various individuals.

EDCS-25601222.text_edition: \n Imperatori Tiberio Caesari Augusto sacrum curatore Lucio Vergilio Publi filio RufoG dato Viriae Publi filiae Rusticae aviae Marci Licini Rufi flaminis perpetui Augusti coloniae Concordiae Iuliae Karthaginis Marcus Licinius Marci libertus Tyrannus patronus pagi igne consumptas restituit aedem et statuas corruptas exornavit opus intestinum Priscillo filio\n
EDCS-25601222.translation: Sacred to the emperor Tiberius Caesar Augustus. With Lucius Vergilius Rufus, son of Publius, as curator, and given to Viria Rustica, daughter of Publius, grandmother of Marcus Licinius Rufus, perpetual priest of Augustus of the colony of Colonia Concordia Iulia Karthago. Marcus Licinius Tyrannus, freedman of Marcus, son of Marcus, patron of the district, rebuilt the temple, consumed by fire, and adorned the damaged statues, with Priscillus, his son, handling the internal work.
EDCS-25601222.summary: A dedication by Marcus Licinius Tyrannus, a freedman and patron of the district in Karthago, who rebuilt a temple and adorned statues dedicated to Emperor Tiberius Caesar Augustus.

EDCS-25601227.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-25601227.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii liberorumque eius Quintus Gabinius Marci filius Quirina Felix Faustianus cum Dato et Processa filiis suis porticus fori columnis et contignatione et lacunaribus omnique cultu parietum sua pecunia ornatas pago patriae dedit\n
EDCS-25601227.translation: For the health of Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, and his children. Quintus Gabinius Felix Faustianus, son of Marcus of the Quirina tribe, with his sons Dato and Processa, dedicated the portico of the forum, adorned with columns, beams, coffers, and all wall decorations, with his own money, to his fatherland district.
EDCS-25601227.summary: A dedication by Quintus Gabinius Felix Faustianus and his sons, funding the decoration of the forum portico for the health of Emperor Antoninus Pius and his family.

EDCS-25601228.translation: To the deified Antoninus Pius, father of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus, the district and city of Thugga, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-25601228.summary: A dedication to the deified Emperor Antoninus Pius by the district and city of Thugga.

EDCS-25601235.translation: To Faustina Augusta, daughter of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus, and deified Antoninus Pius, the district and city of Thugga, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-25601235.summary: A dedication to Empress Faustina, daughter of Marcus Aurelius, by the district and city of Thugga.

EDCS-25601253.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. The state of the colony of Thugga, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-25601253.summary: A dedication to Emperor Maximian by the state of the colony of Thugga.

EDCS-25601256.text_edition: \n Fortissimo ac nobilissimo Caesari virtute etiam ac pietate praecipuo Flavio Valerio Constantio tribunicia potestate III consuli I patri patriae proconsuli res publica coloniae Thuggensis anno proconsulatus Postumi Titiani clarissimi viri curante Egnatio Tucciano clarissimo viro numini eius maiestatique dicatissimo\n
EDCS-25601256.translation: To the most valiant and noble Caesar, outstanding also in virtue and piety, Flavius Valerius Constantius, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 1st time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. The state of the colony of Thugga, in the year of the proconsulship of Postumius Titianus, a most distinguished man, with Egnatius Tuccianus, a most distinguished man, most devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-25601256.summary: A dedication to Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius by the state of the colony of Thugga, during the proconsulship of Postumius Titianus.

EDCS-25601269.translation: To Appia Alexandria, a most distinguished lady, and Pompeia Appia Cinthia Agathoclia.
EDCS-25601269.summary: A dedication to two women, Appia Alexandria and Pompeia Appia Cinthia Agathoclia.

EDCS-25601271.text_edition: \n Passieno Rufo tribuno militum legionis XII fulminatae et Passieno Rufi filio Thuggenses pro amicitia quae eis cum patre est libentes dederunt\n
EDCS-25601271.translation: Passienus Rufus, a military tribune of the Twelfth Legion Fulminata, and his son Passienus, have gladly given this to the people of Thugga, in consideration of the friendship they have with the father.
EDCS-25601271.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the people of Thugga to Passienus Rufus and his son, likely a former official associated with the region, possibly dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-25601273.text_edition: \n Honorati Aulo Vitellio Papiria Felici Honorato equiti Romano fisci advocato at sic vehicula per Flaminiam fisci advocato Transpadum et partem Norici fisci advocato at sic fusa per Numidiam fisci advocato at sic patrimonium Karthaginiense praeposito agenti per Campaniam Calabriam Lucaniam Picenum annonam curanti militibus Augusti nostri sacerdoti Lanuvino pro libertate publica volumtaria sic et gratuita legatione functo ob merita et obsequia eius in patriam et in cives amorem res publica colonia Licinia Thugga decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-25601273.translation: The Republic of the colonia Licinia Thugga, by decree of the decurions and at public expense, has erected this monument to Honoratus, fiscal advocate for Aulus Vitellius Papirius Felix Fortunatus, an equestrian of Rome; fiscal advocate for the Imperial Treasury in the Flaminian region, Transpadana, and part of Noricum; fiscal advocate for the Imperial Treasury's grain supply; fiscal advocate for the Imperial Treasury in Numidia; administrator of the Carthaginian patrimony and agent for Campania, Calabria, Lucania, and Picenum; curator of the grain supply for our Emperor's soldiers; and priest of Lanuvium, who served voluntarily and freely as an ambassador for public liberty, in recognition of his merits and obedience, and his love for his homeland and citizens.
EDCS-25601273.summary: This inscription is a public dedication by the colony of Thugga to Honoratus, a prominent fiscal advocate and former priest, acknowledging his service and love for his homeland, likely dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-25601281.text_edition: \n Res publica municipii Septimi Aureli Liberi Thuggae cancellos aereos quos Asicia Victoria flaminica perpetua H Imperatoris ad ornamentum rostrorum ex pollucto HS XXviginti milibus nummum fieri voluit additis a se quattuor cancellis ampliata pecunia dedit\n
EDCS-25601281.translation: The Republic of the municipium of Septimius Aurelius Liber of Thugga has given this, having expanded it with additional funds, of the bronze railings which Asicia Victoria, perpetual flaminica of the Emperor, wished to be made from the privy purse for 20,000 sesterces, to adorn the Rostra, adding four additional railings at her own expense.
EDCS-25601281.summary: This inscription records the completion and expansion of bronze railings for the Rostra in Thugga, funded by Asicia Victoria and supplemented by the local municipality, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-25601289.text_edition: \n Lucio Instanio Papiria Commodo Asicio Adiutori splendidissimae coloniae Thuggae duumviralicio aedilicio libentissime adque abstinentissime ad sacras aures sine onere rei publicae\n
EDCS-25601289.translation: To Lucius Instanius Papirius Commodus Asicius Adiutor, most willingly and with the greatest integrity, without burdening the Republic, for his duumviral and aedilician office, to the sacred ears.
EDCS-25601289.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Lucius Instanius Papirius Commodus Asicius Adiutor, honoring his public offices (duumvir and aedile) in the colonia Thugga, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-25700352.translation: The Emperors Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, Imperator for the 5th time, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, Princeps of the Youth, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, paved the road from Carthage [...].
EDCS-25700352.summary: This inscription records the paving of a road from Carthage by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximus, with the completion date indicated by their titles and imperial powers, likely around 236-238 CE.

EDCS-25700358.translation: Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, Consul for the 3rd time, paved the road from Carthage to Theveste, with the 85th mile marker, through Legio III Augusta, under Publius Metilius Secundus, legate of the Emperor pro praetore.
EDCS-25700358.summary: This inscription records the construction of the road from Carthage to Theveste by Emperor Hadrian, completed under the governorship of Publius Metilius Secundus by Legio III Augusta, likely around 123 CE.

EDCS-25700389.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII consul III viam a Carthagine Thevestem milia passuum CXCIcentum nonaginta unum miliaDCCXXXXseptingentos quadraginta stravit Publio Metilio Secundo legato Augusti pro praetore consule designato per legionem III Augustam\n
EDCS-25700389.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, Consul for the 3rd time, paved the road from Carthage to Theveste, 191 miles and 740 paces, through Legio III Augusta, under Publius Metilius Secundus, legate of the Emperor pro praetore, consul-designate.
EDCS-25700389.summary: This inscription details Emperor Hadrian's paving of the road from Carthage to Theveste, specifying the length, and attributing its construction to Legio III Augusta under Publius Metilius Secundus, dating to around 117-119 CE.

EDCS-25700391.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Vespasiano Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate VII imperatore XVII consule VII qui primusitionem flumin aperuit Imperatore Tito Vespasiano Augusti filio imperatore XI pontifice tribunicia potestate V consule V Caesare Augusti filio Domitiano consule IIII pontifice Quinto Egnatio Cato legato Augusti pro praetore legio III Augusta\n
EDCS-25700391.translation: During the reign of Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, Imperator for the 17th time, Consul for the 7th time, who first opened the course of the river, and Emperor Titus Vespasian Augustus, son of Augustus, Imperator for the 11th time, Pontifex, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, Consul for the 5th time, and Caesar Augustus' son Domitian, Consul for the 4th time, Pontifex, under Quintus Egnatius Catus, legate of the Emperor pro praetore, with Legio III Augusta.
EDCS-25700391.summary: This inscription records public works, including the opening of a river course, carried out under Emperors Vespasian and Titus, and Caesar Domitian, with the involvement of Legio III Augusta and legate Quintus Egnatius Catus, likely during the 70s CE.

EDCS-25900101.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Quinti filius Felix Aurunculeianus aedilis ob honorem aedilitatis signum Marsyae quod ex HS IICCCCduobus milibus quadringentis nummum cum legitima summa taxaverat adiecta amplius pecunia posuit et dedicavit decreto decurionum idemque primus ludos dedit\n
EDCS-25900101.translation: Gaius Julius Felix Aurunculeianus, son of Quintus, as aedile, for the honor of his aedileship, has placed and dedicated the statue of Marsyas, which he had valued at 2400 sesterces with the legal sum, adding more money from his own funds, and he himself was the first to present games.
EDCS-25900101.summary: This inscription records Gaius Julius Felix Aurunculeianus, as an aedile, dedicating a statue of Marsyas and presenting games at his own expense, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-25900233.text_edition: \n At deas Caelestes argenteas fabricandas II ex HS milibus nummum quae Aburnius Avillius Felix testamento suo ab heredibus suis praestari voluit rei publicae dederunt templum deae Caelestis Augustae, ex pollicitatione Gabini Rufi patris ob honorem flamonii perpetui itemque Iuliae Gallitae matris ex HS LX milibus nummum coeptum est inlatis quoque rei publicae Thuggensium ante diem dedicationis HS XXX milibus nummum die dedicationis rei publicae numeratis ex testamento Avilliae filiae Venustae ex quorum reditu sportulae et ludi praestentur Quintus Gabinius Rufus Felix Beatianus multiplicata a se pecunia ob honorem parentum suorum HS milibus nummum ad ornamentum patriae suae sua liberalitate constitutis perfecit excoluit et cum statuis ceterisque solo privato dedicavit ludis editis datis sportulis et epulo et gymnasio\n
EDCS-25900233.translation: To the goddesses, silver statues of whom are to be made for 10,000 sesterces, which Aburnius Avillius Felix wished to be provided by his heirs in his will, the Republic gave. The temple of the goddess Caelestis Augusta was begun from the promise of Gabinius Rufus Felix Beatianus, father, for the honor of his perpetual priesthood, and similarly from that of Julia Gallita, mother, for 60,000 sesterces. Furthermore, 30,000 sesterces were paid to the Republic of Thugga before the dedication day, from the will of Avillia Venusta, daughter. From the income of these, distributions and games are to be provided. Quintus Gabinius Rufus Felix Beatianus, having multiplied the money for the honor of his parents, established, perfected, and adorned it with statues and other items on private land, dedicating it with games presented, distributions given, a feast, and a gymnasium.
EDCS-25900233.summary: This complex inscription details a series of dedications and constructions in Thugga, involving multiple generations of the Gabinius and Avillius families, related to religious honors and public amenities, likely spanning the late 2nd to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26000920.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Lucius Julius Crassus, son of Lucius, of the Cornelia tribe, having held the curule aedileship, military tribune of the XXI Rapax legion in Germany, prefect of craftsmen, twice a city magistrate, augur, designated quinquennial magistrate, patron of the district, dedicated this; Gaius Pomponius Restitutus, son of Lucius, saw to its construction at his own expense.
EDCS-26000920.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Jupiter, with Lucius Julius Crassus as the primary dedicant and Gaius Pomponius Restitutus overseeing its construction, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE based on the legionary service.

EDCS-26001074.translation: Sacred to the Augustan Empress. Gaius Pacuvius Felix, son of Gaius, made and dedicated this at his own expense, in his own name and that of his wife Tullia Primula.
EDCS-26001074.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a sacred monument to the divine Augusta (likely a member of the imperial family) by Gaius Pacuvius Felix for himself and his wife, dating possibly to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-26001077.translation: To the Lord of the Triumph of Liberty and our Rescuer through his invincible labors, for the welfare of private citizens and the public, the state of the devoted colony of Uchitanorum Maiorum dedicates this eternally to Lucius Flavius Valerius Constantinus, perpetual Augustus, to his divine majesty.
EDCS-26001077.summary: This inscription is a formal dedication by the colony of Uchitanorum Maiorum to Emperor Constantine, praising him as a deliverer and worshipping his divine authority, likely dating to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-26001089.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Sextus Manlius Fortunatus pius vixit annos LXXI hic situs est\n
EDCS-26001089.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Sextus Manlius Fortunatus, dutiful, lived for 71 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-26001089.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name, piety, age, and burial place of Sextus Manlius Fortunatus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26100452.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription that records a father's action and a provincial designation or date, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26200964.summary: This is a very short inscription likely identifying the owner or subject of an object or monument, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26300053.translation: On these estates of Marcus Hortensius Gaudentis and his sons, Gaudentiis. Restitutus. Province 239.
EDCS-26300053.summary: This inscription seems to record property ownership or a transaction related to the estates of Marcus Hortensius Gaudentis and his sons, with a reference to a province or date, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26300070.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Julius Saturninus, identified as a dearest father, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26300072.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Modia Saturnina, identified as a dearest mother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26400650.translation: To Lucius Egnatuleius Sabinus, son of Publius, of the Galeria tribe, pontiff of the Palatual College, procurator of the Emperor for the Revenue Office of Gallia, procurator of the Emperor for the strategos of the Thebaid, procurator of the Emperor for the collection of taxes in Macedonia, prefect of the Cinithian people, tribune of the IV Scythica Legion, [flamen of the] Emperor of the colony of Thysdritis. Egnatuleia Sabina, daughter of Publius. Lucius Egnatuleius Sabinus [son of...]
EDCS-26400650.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription honouring Lucius Egnatuleius Sabinus and his family members, listing his extensive imperial administrative and military career across various provinces, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26400669.translation: Gaius Sulpicius Lupercus lived 49 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-26400669.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Gaius Sulpicius Lupercus.

EDCS-26500696.text_edition: \n Tiberius Caesar divi Augusti filius Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XXXI consul III Caius Vibius Marsus pro consul III dedicavit\n
EDCS-26500696.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 31st time, consul for the 3rd time, Gaius Vibius Marsus, proconsul for the 3rd time, dedicated this.
EDCS-26500696.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Gaius Vibius Marsus, proconsul, under the authority of Tiberius Caesar.

EDCS-26500719.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar, consul for the 2nd time, son of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-26500719.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar, son of Antoninus Pius, funded by public money and decreed by local officials.

EDCS-26500720.translation: To the deified Publius Helvius Pertinax Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-26500720.summary: This is a dedication to the deified Emperor Pertinax, funded by public money and decreed by local officials.

EDCS-26500882.text_edition: \n Caius Marius Cai filius aedilis signum Aequitatis sacrum de sua pequnia sic ex decreto decurionum faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-26500882.translation: Gaius Marius, son of Caius, as aedile, commissioned this sacred statue of Equity to be made from his own funds, in accordance with the decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26500882.summary: This inscription records the commissioning of a statue of Equity by Gaius Marius, an aedile, using his own money as per a local decree.

EDCS-26500886.summary: This incomplete inscription likely records the name of Sextius, son of Aurelius Antoninus, possibly related to a dedication or funerary monument.

EDCS-26500906.text_edition: \n D equo publico ab Imperatore Caesare Hadriano Augusto flamen perpetuus ob liberalitatem HS CCCLmilium Syllae posuit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-26500906.translation: He erected this from public funds, by decree of the decurions, with 350,000 sesterces from the Emperor Caesar Hadrianus Augustus, perpetual priest, for his generosity with public horses.
EDCS-26500906.summary: This inscription records a public monument erected with imperial funds and a decree of local officials to honor a perpetual priest for his generosity with public horses.

EDCS-26500953.translation: From the public treasury of Annius Clodius.
EDCS-26500953.summary: This inscription likely indicates the source of funds from the public treasury of Annius Clodius for a monument.

EDCS-26500956.summary: This inscription records the ownership or dedication by Quintus Granius Marcianinus.

EDCS-26500957.summary: This inscription records the ownership or dedication by Quintus Granius Pelagius.

EDCS-26500960.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly from a tombstone or dedication, wishing good fortune to an individual.

EDCS-26500972.translation: and of a most honorable man
EDCS-26500972.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely part of a longer text praising an honorable man.

EDCS-26500979.translation: and of a most honorable man
EDCS-26500979.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely part of a longer text praising an honorable man.

EDCS-26500982.summary: This fragment likely refers to two individuals named Octavius, possibly indicating a familial or collegial relationship.

EDCS-26500983.translation: and the duovirate, thus the Memmii
EDCS-26500983.summary: This fragment seems to mention the office of duovir and refers to individuals named Memmius, possibly related to local governance.

EDCS-26500985.translation: of Marcus CucuIIus, a most honorable man
EDCS-26500985.summary: This fragment praises Marcus CucuIIus as a most honorable man, likely part of a dedication or epitaph.

EDCS-26500986.text_edition: \n Luci M L Dogasi honestissimi viri\n
EDCS-26500986.translation: of Lucius Marius Dogasus, a most honorable man
EDCS-26500986.summary: This fragment praises Lucius Marius Dogasus as a most honorable man, likely part of a dedication or epitaph.

EDCS-26800220.translation: Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis made this tilework from the kilns of our Caesar, when Propinquus and Ambibulus were consuls.
EDCS-26800220.summary: This inscription records a tilemaker, Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis, and the production of tilework from imperial kilns during the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.

EDCS-26800225.translation: Tilework from the kilns of Asinia Quadratilla, made by Gaius Nannius Fortunatus for Lucius Quadratus, when he was consul.
EDCS-26800225.summary: This inscription records tile production from the kilns of Asinia Quadratilla, made by Gaius Nannius Fortunatus for Lucius Quadratus during his consulship.

EDCS-26800226.translation: Tilework from the kilns of Asinia Quadratilla, made by Gaius Nannius Fortunatus for Lucius Quadratus, when he was consul.
EDCS-26800226.summary: This inscription records tile production from the kilns of Asinia Quadratilla, made by Gaius Nannius Fortunatus for Lucius Quadratus during his consulship.

EDCS-26800227.translation: From the pottery works of Asinia Quadrata, products of Caesar our Lord; by Unnus Fortunatus, when Lucius Quadratus was consul.
EDCS-26800227.summary: This inscription records ceramic products from the workshops of Asinia Quadrata, made by Unnus Fortunatus, and dated by the consulship of Lucius Quadratus.

EDCS-26800498.translation: When Gaius Furnius and Gaius Silanus were consuls.
EDCS-26800498.summary: This inscription records the names of the consuls Gaius Furnius and Gaius Silanus.

EDCS-26800507.text_edition: \n IM Caio Pansa Aulo Hirtio cnsulibus Gerilianianum\n
EDCS-26800507.translation: In the consulship of Gaius Pansa and Aulus Hirtius, the Gerilianum [was built/dedicated].
EDCS-26800507.summary: This inscription records the Gerilianum, dated by the consulship of Gaius Pansa and Aulus Hirtius.

EDCS-26800510.text_edition: \n AR F VI Lucio Volcatio Lucio Autronio consulibus ALO Caius Caesar MACK\n
EDCS-26800510.translation: When Lucius Volcatius and Lucius Autronius were consuls, Gaius Caesar [did something]...
EDCS-26800510.summary: This inscription records the names of consuls Lucius Volcatius and Lucius Autronius, and mentions Gaius Caesar.

EDCS-26800512.translation: When Emperor Caesar Marcus Crassus was consul.
EDCS-26800512.summary: This inscription records the name of the consul Marcus Crassus, who was also Emperor Caesar.

EDCS-26800513.translation: In the consulship of Lucius Q. Saenius.
EDCS-26800513.summary: This inscription records the name of the consul Lucius Q. Saenius.

EDCS-26800514.translation: When Lucius Arruntius and Marcus Marcellus were consuls.
EDCS-26800514.summary: This inscription records the names of the consuls Lucius Arruntius and Marcus Marcellus.

EDCS-26800516.text_edition: \n Quinto Lepido Marco Lollio consulibus AP vinum Mesopotamium Lucio Afranio Silvio\n
EDCS-26800516.translation: Mesopotamian wine from Quintus Lepidus and Marcus Lollius; to Lucius Afranius Silvius.
EDCS-26800516.summary: This inscription records Mesopotamian wine associated with consuls Quintus Lepidus and Marcus Lollius, and dedicated to Lucius Afranius Silvius.

EDCS-26800521.translation: When Lucius Domitius and Lucius Tarius were consuls.
EDCS-26800521.summary: This inscription records the names of the consuls Lucius Domitius and Lucius Tarius.

EDCS-26801057.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-26801057.translation: For the health of Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, acclaimed imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the country, proconsul, and of Cornelia Salonina Augusta; the curator of the res publica provided the portico of the forum, as planned, with 50,000 sesterces, which is also called a sportula, from the generosity of the most holy Emperor, [giving] benevolence, favor, and liberality. Papiria Felix Iulianus, an equestrian of Rome, perpetual priest, holder of the duumviral office, from his perpetual priesthood, gave the sportulae and the banquet for the decurions, adding to the public treasury 50,000 sesterces and on the day of dedication [provided] a banquet and staged theatrical games for the entire populace.
EDCS-26801057.summary: This extensive inscription records a significant public dedication including a portico, a cash donation (sportula), a banquet, games, and offerings, made by Papiria Felix Iulianus in honor of Emperor Gallienus and Empress Cornelia Salonina, likely dating to the period of Gallienus' reign.

EDCS-26801190.text_edition: \n M Fydel et F Thesylgum pater et mater M Metusanis qui eis hec sic memoriam fecit discussi ratiocinio ad ea erogatum est sumptos mercedes in numo follis m milia praeter cibarias operantibus feliciter legant et vissitent filios et nepotes meos et tales faciant\n
EDCS-26801190.translation: M. Fydelius and M. Thesylgus, his parents, set up this memorial for M. Metusanus, for whom the expenditure was granted after careful calculation; the costs, including wages and food for the workers, amounted to ten thousand coins, apart from the food. May those who read and visit live happily and make their children and grandchildren like these.
EDCS-26801190.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating M. Metusanus, erected by his parents, likely from the Roman period, detailing the expenses of its construction and expressing wishes for future generations.

EDCS-26900659.text_edition: \n Iunoni Liviae Augusti sacrum Lucio Passieno Rufo imperatore Africam obtinente Cnaeus Cornelius Cnaei filius Cornelia Rufus et Maria Cai filia Galla Cnaei conservati vota libentes merito solvont sic\n
EDCS-26900659.translation: To Juno. Cnaeus Cornelius Rufus, son of Cnaeus, of the Cornelia tribe, and Maria Galla, daughter of Caius, together with Cnaeus, give thanks for their deliverance and willingly fulfill their vows, with Lucius Passienus Rufus as governor of Africa.
EDCS-26900659.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Juno, recording the fulfillment of vows by Cnaeus Cornelius Rufus and Maria Galla during the governorship of Lucius Passienus Rufus in Africa, likely dating to the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-27100462.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Titus Ovinius Privatus, the priest, has willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27100462.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Saturn Augustus, recording a vow fulfilled by Titus Ovinius Privatus, a priest, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-27600038.translation: To our lord and emperor Constantine the Great, Invictus, Pious, Happy Augustus, founder of the public good. To our lord Magnus Decentius Caesar. (Dated) 8.
EDCS-27600038.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Emperor Constantine the Great and Caesar Magnus Decentius, likely a milestone or boundary marker from the 4th century AD.

EDCS-27600045.translation: To our lord Constantine the Great, Victor, Ever Augustus, founder of the public good. (Dated) 10.
EDCS-27600045.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Emperor Constantine the Great, likely a milestone or boundary marker from the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-27600050.translation: To Flavius Constantius, most noble and most fortunate Caesar, founder of the public good. (Dated illegible).
EDCS-27600050.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Caesar Flavius Constantius, likely dating to the period of the Tetrarchy or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-27600091.translation: To our lord Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius, Pious, Happy, Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 14th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. (Damaged).
EDCS-27600091.summary: This is a formal inscription detailing the titles and powers of Emperor Constantius I, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-27600095.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, greatest conqueror of Britain, greatest conqueror of Germany, with tribunician power for the 19th time, consul for the 3rd time, restored. (Dated) 19.
EDCS-27600095.summary: This inscription records the titles and accomplishments of Emperor Caracalla, including his victories and dating his tribunician power and consulship, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-27600114.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, grandson of the divine Gordian, son of the sister of the divine Gordian, Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul, proconsul, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-27600114.summary: This inscription details the full imperial titles and lineage of Emperor Gordian III, likely dating to his reign in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-27600141.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, grandson of the divine Nerva, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, paved the road from Carthage to Theveste by means of the Legio III Augusta, under Publius Metilius Secundus, governor. (Dated) 82.
EDCS-27600141.summary: This inscription records Emperor Hadrian's paving of a road from Carthage to Theveste by Legio III Augusta, under the governorship of P. Metilius Secundus, likely dating to the 2nd century AD and possibly referencing a distance of 82 miles.

EDCS-27600148.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae nepos divi Traiani Parthici filius Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII consul III viam a Karthagine Thevestem stravit per legionem III Augustam Publio Metilio Secundo legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-27600148.translation: Emperor Caesar, grandson of the divine Nerva, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, chief priest, with the tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, paved the road from Carthage to Theveste, through Legio III Augusta, with Publius Metilius Secundus as his legate with the power of a propraetor.
EDCS-27600148.summary: This inscription records the paving of a road by Emperor Hadrian, undertaken by Legio III Augusta.

EDCS-27600149.translation: Emperor Caesar, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, with tribunician power.
EDCS-27600149.summary: This is a titulary inscription of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, listing his adopted names and honors, with an incomplete record of his tribunician power.

EDCS-27600150.translation: Emperor Caesar, Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Trajanus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, [greatest of the greatest], father of the fatherland, [in his] 85th year.
EDCS-27600150.summary: This is a titulary inscription of Emperor Decius, listing his titles and noting a date related to his reign, likely an era or year count.

EDCS-27600151.translation: Of Emperor Caesar, Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Trajanus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, father of the fatherland, with tribunician power, [in his] 86th year.
EDCS-27600151.summary: This is a titulary inscription of Emperor Decius, listing his titles and noting a date related to his reign, likely an era or year count.

EDCS-27600232.translation: To the Great and Unconquered Emperor Caesar Valerius Diocletian, Unconquered, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul for the 2nd time; and to Valerius Maximianus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, proconsul.
EDCS-27600232.summary: This inscription honors Emperors Diocletian and Maximian, likely commemorating joint consular or proconsular terms.

EDCS-27600599.summary: This is a simple ownership or dedication inscription, identifying Gaius Junius Alexius.

EDCS-27601206.summary: This is a simple ownership or dedication inscription, identifying Lucius Munatius Adiectus.

EDCS-27800149.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filio divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VI imperatori XI consuli II proconsuli optimo maximoque principi patri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti principis iuventutis et Publi Septimi Getae nobilissimi Caesaris pagus Thibaritanus decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-27800149.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the divine Commodus, grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, descendant of the divine Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 6th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, the best and greatest prince, father of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, prince of youth, and of Publius Septimius Geta, most noble Caesar, by decree of the decurions of the pagus Thibaritanus, at public expense.
EDCS-27800149.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the pagus Thibaritanus to Emperor Septimius Severus and his son Caracalla (as Caesar Geta), commemorating his imperial titles and paternal role.

EDCS-27800403.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XV imperatori II consuli IIII patri patriae Lepcitani publice\n
EDCS-27800403.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 15th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, by the people of Lepcis.
EDCS-27800403.summary: This inscription records a public dedication by the people of Lepcis to Emperor Antoninus Pius, listing his significant imperial titles.

EDCS-27900251.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Underworld. Castus, son of Catagius, pious, lived 85 years.
EDCS-27900251.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name, parentage, piety, and age of the deceased, Castus.

EDCS-27900253.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Corm[...]
EDCS-27900253.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the beginning of a dedication to the gods of the underworld for Gaius Corm[...].

EDCS-27900255.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Domit[...]
EDCS-27900255.summary: This is a funerary inscription beginning with a dedication to the gods of the underworld for Domit[...].

EDCS-27900270.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sallustia Felicisima, pious, lived 45 years, rests here.
EDCS-27900270.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Sallustia Felicisima, who lived 45 years and is buried here.

EDCS-28000291.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Anconius Felix, pious, lived 29 years, rests here.
EDCS-28000291.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Anconius Felix, who lived 29 years and is buried here.

EDCS-30100060.text_edition: \n Quintus Titleis Cai filius Sarapi Isidi dedit\n
EDCS-30100060.translation: Quintus Titleis, son of Cai, gave [this] to Isis Sarapis.
EDCS-30100060.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Quintus Titleis dedicating something to the deity Isis Sarapis.

EDCS-30100061.text_edition: \n Claudius Syrapiacus votum solvit Claudius Syrapiacus votum solvit\n
EDCS-30100061.translation: Claudius Syrapiacus fulfilled [his] vow. Claudius Syrapiacus fulfilled [his] vow.
EDCS-30100061.summary: This is a votive inscription where Claudius Syrapiacus states that he fulfilled his vow, likely to a deity associated with Syrapiacus.

EDCS-30100065.text_edition: \n Dignae meritae ac obsequentissimae coniugi Felicitati Gaudentius fecit in pace\n
EDCS-30100065.translation: Gaudentius made [this] for his worthy, deserving, and most obedient wife, Felicitas, in peace.
EDCS-30100065.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Gaudentius dedicated a monument to his wife, Felicitas.

EDCS-30100066.text_edition: \n Dulcissimo compari Iulio Ianuario qui vicsit sic cum suis annis XXXII plus minus in pace requievit\n
EDCS-30100066.translation: Rest in peace, [my] dearest companion, Julius Ianuarius, who lived about 32 years with his own.
EDCS-30100066.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julius Ianuarius, who lived approximately 32 years and is buried in peace.

EDCS-30100101.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Gaius Gelasius vixit annos LXXII\n
EDCS-30100101.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Gelasius lived 72 years.
EDCS-30100101.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Gaius Gelasius, who lived 72 years.

EDCS-30100102.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caerellius Sextilius pius vixit annos XXII Faustinus pater\n
EDCS-30100102.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Caerellius Sextilius, pious, lived 22 years. His father Faustinus [dedicated this].
EDCS-30100102.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Faustinus for his son, Caerellius Sextilius, who lived 22 years.

EDCS-30100103.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Fausti Acerotis fecit sibi et coniugi suae se vivente annis LXX sibi et coniugi suae annis LX\n
EDCS-30100103.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Faustus Acerotis made this for himself and his wife while he was alive, at age 70, and for himself and his wife, at age 60.
EDCS-30100103.summary: This funerary inscription records Faustus Acerotis dedicating a tomb for himself and his wife, specifying their ages at death.

EDCS-30100104.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Minucius Fla Dis Manibus sacrum Minucia Iulia\n
EDCS-30100104.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Minucius Fla[...]. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Minucia Iulia.
EDCS-30100104.summary: This fragmented funerary inscription mentions Gaius Minucius and Minucia Iulia.

EDCS-30100105.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Namfamo vixit annos XXXVI \n
EDCS-30100105.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Namfamus lived for 36 years.
EDCS-30100105.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Namfamus.

EDCS-30100106.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Munnia Saturnina vixit annis XXXX Lucius Caelius Praestans filius curavit\n
EDCS-30100106.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Munnia Saturnina lived for 40 years. Her son, Lucius Caelius Praestans, took care of this.
EDCS-30100106.summary: This funerary inscription records Munnia Saturnina's lifespan and states that her son, Lucius Caelius Praestans, arranged her burial.

EDCS-30100107.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Gaius Cannius Aris vixit annis LXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-30100107.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Cannius Aris lived for 75 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-30100107.summary: This funerary inscription records Gaius Cannius Aris's lifespan and burial.

EDCS-30100109.summary: This short inscription records the name of the person who carved it, Gaius Allius Maialicus.

EDCS-30100110.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III imperator V et Caius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus pontes vetustate conlabsos sic et iter longa incuria corruptum restituerunt et pro sua infatigabili providentia pervium commeantibus reddiderunt IIVX\n
EDCS-30100110.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, imperator for the 5th time, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, Germanicus maximus, Sarmaticus maximus, Dacicus maximus, restored the bridges which had collapsed from age and the road which had been corrupted by long neglect, and, by their tireless foresight, made them passable for travelers.
EDCS-30100110.summary: This inscription records the restoration of collapsed bridges and a neglected road by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus Caesar, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100111.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III consul IIII Domino nostro Flavio Theudossius sic Augusto IIII\n
EDCS-30100111.translation: Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, and our Lord Flavius Theodosius, Augustus for the 4th time.
EDCS-30100111.summary: This inscription lists the titles and honors of Emperor Gallienus and Emperor Theodosius II, likely dating from the mid-4th century AD.

EDCS-30100113.text_edition: \n Caio Iunio Faustino Placido Postumiano clarissimo viro cons(uli) adlecto inter comites Auggustorum nnostrorum sacerdoti Flaviali Titiali legato Auggustorum pro praetore provinciae Mysiae sic inferioris legato Auggustorum pro praetore provinciae Belgicae legato Auggustorum pro praetore provinciae Lusetaniae! legato Auggustorum legionis primae adiutricis piae fidelis iuridico per Aemiliam et Etruriam et Tusciam praetori candidato legato provinciae Africae dioeceseos Karthaginiensis tribuno plebis candidato quaestori provinciae Africae decemviro stlitibus iudicandis patrono perpetuo \n
EDCS-30100113.translation: To Gaius Junius Faustinus Placidas Postumianus, most distinguished man, consul, appointed among the companions of our Augusti, priest of the Flavian and Titian families, legate of the Augusti with praetorian power of the province of Lower Mysia, legate of the Augusti with praetorian power of the province of Belgica, legate of the Augusti with praetorian power of the province of Lusitania, legate of the First Adjutant Legion, pious and faithful, legal officer for Aemilia, Etruria, and Tuscia, praetor-designate, legate of the province of Africa in the diocese of Carthage, tribune of the plebs-designate, quaestor of the province of Africa, decemvir for judging lawsuits, perpetual patron.
EDCS-30100113.summary: This lengthy senatorial career inscription honors Gaius Junius Faustinus Placidas Postumianus, detailing his extensive public service and dignities, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100114.text_edition: \n Baeatissimo saeculo Cuttilulenses Macrini Sossiani legati clarissimi viri\n
EDCS-30100114.translation: To the most blessed age of Cuttilulenses. Macrinus Sossianus, most distinguished legate.
EDCS-30100114.summary: This inscription honors Macrinus Sossianus, identified as a distinguished legate associated with Cuttilulenses, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100124.text_edition: \n Et tu exuri sartor quod disperabas explicitu est sede in catena\n
EDCS-30100124.translation: And you, tailor, have been made whole, what you despaired of has been completed; the seat is in chains.
EDCS-30100124.summary: This fragmentary inscription, likely from a tomb, records a recovery from illness or despair and mentions a seat in chains, possibly a metaphor.

EDCS-30100127.translation: To the memory of the most distinguished man, with money...
EDCS-30100127.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription commemorating a distinguished man, with a lacuna indicating further details about the cost or dedication.

EDCS-30100136.text_edition: \n Parthici di Auspici Felicis cultu ornavit e[t ------\n
EDCS-30100136.translation: With the worship of the fortunate Parthian god, he adorned...
EDCS-30100136.summary: This inscription fragment refers to the worship and adornment associated with a deity named the 'fortunate Parthian god', likely part of a dedicatory or honorific text.

EDCS-30100141.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Oppius Felix pius vixit annos XXXIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-30100141.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Marcus Oppius Felix, dutiful, lived 33 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-30100141.summary: This is a standard funerary epitaph recording the name, pietas, age at death, and burial place of Marcus Oppius Felix.

EDCS-30100142.text_edition: \n Sexti filius Arnensi Fortunatus pius vixit annos V sit tibi terra levis ossa tibi bene quiescant hic situs est\n
EDCS-30100142.translation: Fortunatus, son of Sextus, of the Arnensian tribe, dutiful, lived 5 years. May the earth lie lightly on you, may your bones rest well. He is buried here.
EDCS-30100142.summary: This is a touching epitaph for a young boy named Fortunatus, recording his parentage, tribe, age, and expressing wishes for his peaceful afterlife.

EDCS-30100143.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-30100143.text_edition: \n Genti Valeriae aeternae ddominorum nnostrorum Imperatoris Caesaris Cai Valeri Diocletiani Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti et Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Valeri Maximiani Pii Felicis Inuicti Augusti et Flavi Valeri Constanti et Galeri Valeri Maximini fortissimorum felicissimorumque Caesarum templum cum omni cultu plebs municipii Mariani Thibaritani devota numini maiestatique ipsorum et s eorum cumulata participantibus secum civibus suis votivae devotionis magno exemplo incohatum perfecit et perfectum ac dedicatum consecravit [ex auctoritate] [[[Luci Aeli Helvi Dionysi ------\n
EDCS-30100143.translation: To the eternal Genti Valeriae of our Lords, the Emperor Caesar Caius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and the most brave and fortunate Caesars Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, the people of the municipality of Mariani Thibaritani, devoted to their divine power and majesty, having completed a temple with all worship, and having consecrated it when perfect and dedicated, with the participation of their fellow citizens as a great example of votive devotion, initiated [under the authority] of Lucius Aelius Helvius Dionysius...
EDCS-30100143.summary: This lengthy inscription records the construction and dedication of a temple by the people of Mariani Thibaritani to the Tetrarchic emperors, highlighting their devotion and the grand scale of the project.

EDCS-30100149.text_edition: \n Memoriae Syluuli sacerdotis inter lapsos vixit annos XXV in fide\n
EDCS-30100149.translation: To the memory of Sylvulus, priest, among the fallen, lived 25 years, in faith.
EDCS-30100149.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sylvulus, a priest, who died at 25 years of age, possibly in battle or during a period of turmoil, and is remembered for his faith.

EDCS-30501140.text_edition: \n Abienii eximiae integritatis viro ac mire bonitatis exemplo Postumio Rufio Festo amplissimo proconsuli clarissimo viro vice sacr[a iudicanti ------\n
EDCS-30501140.translation: To Postumius Rufus Festus, most eminent proconsul, a most distinguished man, an example of outstanding integrity and admirable goodness, vice sacred[ly] judging...
EDCS-30501140.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription honoring Postumius Rufus Festus, a proconsul, recognizing his integrity and goodness during his judicial duties.

EDCS-30501141.text_edition: \n Marco Tettio Gallico Clodiano equiti Romano flamini perpetuo et Tettiae Pomponiae Cosconianae filiae eorum Iucundus servus actor\n
EDCS-30501141.translation: Iucundus, the steward, [erected this] for Marcus Tettius Gallicus Clodianus, Roman equestrian, perpetual flamen, and for their daughter Tettia Pomponia Cosconiana.
EDCS-30501141.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by a steward for Marcus Tettius Gallicus Clodianus, a Roman equestrian and perpetual flamen, and his daughter.

EDCS-30501142.text_edition: \n ae Marci Tetti Gallici Clodiani equitis Romani flaminis perpetui et Marco Tettio Gallicano Clodiano filio eorum Iucundus servus actor\n
EDCS-30501142.translation: Iucundus, the steward, [erected this] for Marcus Tettius Gallicus Clodianus, Roman equestrian, perpetual flamen, and for their son Marcus Tettius Gallicanus Clodianus.
EDCS-30501142.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by a steward for Marcus Tettius Gallicus Clodianus, a Roman equestrian and perpetual flamen, and his son.

EDCS-30501164.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus et Imperator Caesar Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus et Flavius Valerius Constantius et Galerius Valerius Maximianus nobbilissimi Caessares a Tipasa milia passuum IIII\n
EDCS-30501164.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and Emperor Caesar Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesars, [set this up] 4 miles from Tipasa.
EDCS-30501164.summary: This is a milestone indicating a distance of 4 miles from Tipasa, set up by the Tetrarchs, including Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius, likely in the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-31800269.translation: Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis made this tiling work from the Emperor's tiles, with Propinquus and Ambibulus as consuls.
EDCS-31800269.summary: This inscription records a tiling work made by Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis using imperial tiles, dating to the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.

EDCS-31800270.translation: Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis made this tiling work from the Emperor's tiles, with Propinquus and Ambibulus as consuls.
EDCS-31800270.summary: This inscription records a tiling work made by Lucius Bruttidius Augustalis using imperial tiles, dating to the consulship of Propinquus and Ambibulus.

EDCS-33500319.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-33500319.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto divi Veri fratri Armeniaco Parthico Medico Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXVII imperatori VI cons(uli) III patri patriae proconsuli pagus Uchitanorum Maiorum aedem vetustate corruptam ex auctoritate Iuni Sabiniani proconsulis clarissimi viri petente procuratore transtulit et aquam induxit itemque fontem fecit curatore ato magistro pagi\n
EDCS-33500319.translation: The pagus Uchitanorum Maiorum, at the request of the procurator and under the authority of the most distinguished man, the proconsul Iunius Sabinianus, transferred a temple corrupted by age to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, brother of the deified Verus, conqueror of Armenia, Parthia, and Media, Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 27th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, proconsul, and brought water to it, and also made a fountain, with Ato as the curator and master of the pagus.
EDCS-33500319.summary: This inscription details the relocation of an old temple and the introduction of a water supply and fountain by the pagus Uchitanorum Maiorum, authorized by proconsul Iunius Sabinianus and dedicated to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.

EDCS-33500451.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pertinaci Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II imperatori IIII consuli II proconsuli patri patriae civitas Limisa decreto decurionum pecunia publica fecit anno Publi Corneli Anullini proconsulis clarissimi viri \n
EDCS-33500451.translation: The city of Limisa, by decree of the decurions and with public funds, made this for Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, imperator for the 4th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, father of his country, in the year of Publius Cornelius Anullinus, most distinguished man, as proconsul.
EDCS-33500451.summary: This inscription records the construction of a monument by the city of Limisa using public funds in honor of Emperor Septimius Severus Pertinax, during the proconsulship of P. Cornelius Anullinus.

EDCS-33500455.text_edition: \n Palladi Lucio Iunio Publi filio Papiria Proculo Feliciano flamini Augusti perpetuo municipii Limisensium cui cum ordo ob eximia eius merita publice statuam de sportulis ponendam censuisset honore muneris oblati contentus sua pecunia posuit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-33500455.translation: Palladius Lucius Junius Publius's son Papirius Proculus Felicianus, perpetual flamen of Augustus of the municipality of Limisa, who, when the council had decreed that a statue should be erected to him at public expense in recognition of his exceptional merits, was content with the honor offered and erected it at his own expense, the site having been granted by decree of the council.
EDCS-33500455.summary: This inscription records that Palladius Lucius Junius Publius Proculus Felicianus, a local magistrate and priest in Limisa, erected his own statue, despite the council's decree to fund it, in recognition of his merits.

EDCS-33600238.summary: This is a very brief inscription likely referring to a statue or image dedicated to Silvanus.

EDCS-34000171.text_edition: \n Isidi Augustae sacrum Publius Aelius Privatus et Cocceia Bassa uxor eius aram ex voto libentes animo posuerunt et dedicaverunt\n
EDCS-34000171.translation: Sacred to Augusta Isis. Publius Aelius Privatus and his wife Cocceia Bassa placed and dedicated this altar willingly with devoted hearts, fulfilling a vow.
EDCS-34000171.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication of an altar to the goddess Isis Augusta by Publius Aelius Privatus and his wife Cocceia Bassa.

EDCS-34200064.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that simply names Paulus Gentilis Saturninus.

EDCS-34200080.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo tribunicia potestate VII imperatori IX consuli II patri patriae Quintus Lollius Quinti filius Quirina Saturninus equo publico exornatus IIvir quinquennalis praeter alia in rem publicam et erga cives beneficia ob honorem quinquennalitatis inlatis rei publicae HS VI milibus sedem quoque domino nostro Severo Augusto designato ab ordine loco sua pecunia posuit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-34200080.translation: Quintus Lollius Saturninus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, honored with the equestrian order, quinquennial duovir, placed and dedicated this seat at his own expense, at his own location, for our lord Severus Augustus designated by the council, in addition to other benefits to the state and citizens, for the honor of his quinquennial term, with 6,000 sesterces contributed to the state, for Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 9th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of his country.
EDCS-34200080.summary: This inscription records Quintus Lollius Saturninus dedicating a seat in honor of Emperor Septimius Severus, funded by himself and with public contributions, during his term as quinquennial duovir.

EDCS-34200081.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Pio principi iuventutis Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filio divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti praeterquam quod [] statuam togatam posuit item rei publicae summam duumviratus legitimam intulit Marcus Agrius Ulpius Primanus IIvir ex reliquiis et liberalitate pollicitus basem posuit\n
EDCS-34200081.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius, prince of the youth, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva. Marcus Agrius Ulpius Primanus, as twice-magistrate, who had promised a togate statue and also the full inheritance of the office of twice-magistrate to the state, erected the base from remaining funds and his own generosity.
EDCS-34200081.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue base by Marcus Agrius Ulpius Primanus, a local magistrate, to honor Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Caracalla) and fulfill a public vow, dating to his reign (c. 198-217 AD).

EDCS-34200082.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Pio principi iuventutis Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filio divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti sedemL cum statua togata et filius eius Quintus Domitius Luci filius Quirina Pudens IIvir liberalitate pollicitus sua pecunia summam quoque honorariam IIviratus\n
EDCS-34200082.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius, prince of the youth, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva. Quintus Domitius Pudens, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, twice-magistrate, who promised a seat with a togate statue and also the full honorarium for the office of twice-magistrate, erected it with his own money, as well as his son.
EDCS-34200082.summary: This inscription records the erection of a seat with a statue by Quintus Domitius Pudens and his son, local magistrates, to honor Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Caracalla), funded by their own generosity, dating to his reign (c. 198-217 AD).

EDCS-34200083.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Nervae Traiani Caesaris Augusti Germanici Dacici consulis VI imperatoris XIIII Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus legatus Augusti pro praetore inter Musulamios et Siccenses\n
EDCS-34200083.translation: By the authority of Emperor Nerva Trajan Caesar Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, Consul VI, Imperator XIIII. Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus, imperial legate with praetorian power, between the Musulamii and the Siccenses.
EDCS-34200083.summary: This inscription records an administrative decision or boundary settlement made by the Roman legate Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus, acting under the authority of Emperor Trajan, likely in North Africa, dating to Trajan's reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-34600048.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-34600048.text_edition: \n Ex indulgentia domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris divi Severi Pii nepotis divi Magni Antonini fili Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate VIIII consulis III patris patriae proconsulis colonia Alexandriana Augusta Uchi maius sub eius nomine auspicioque deducta per Caesonium Lucillum clarissimum virum partes proconsulis PONTM vices administrantem monumentum ad aeternum testimonium reciperatae libertatis erexit pecunia publica\n
EDCS-34600048.translation: By the indulgence of our lord Emperor Caesar, grandson of the deified Severus Pius, son of the deified Magnus Antoninus, Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, Consul III, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul. The colony of Alexandria Augusta Uchi Maius, founded under his name and auspices, was established by Caesennius Lucillus, a most distinguished man, administering the duties of the proconsul, erected this monument at public expense as an eternal testimony to its recovered liberty.
EDCS-34600048.summary: This inscription records the foundation of the colony of Alexandria Augusta Uchi Maius under the auspices of Emperor Severus Alexander, with a monument erected by Caesennius Lucillus to commemorate the city's restored liberty, dating to Severus Alexander's reign (222-235 AD).

EDCS-34800167.translation: May 16. Total 59. Of these: 1 scribe, 1 optio, 21 quintanarii, 3 stationarii, 5 for the bath water, 1 for the straps, 1 for Boinag(?), 7 sick. The rest are working. 19 [personnel]. [Bath]house. Sick: Aurelius Carinas, Julius [---]
EDCS-34800167.summary: This inscription appears to be a daily report or roster, possibly for a military unit or public works crew, detailing personnel assigned to various duties including bath maintenance and listing sick individuals, dated to May 16th, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-34800170.translation: June 16. Total 86. Recruits: 64. Of these: 3 equites, 22 quintanarii, 8 for the bathhouse, 1 from the cistern, 1 for the overseer, 1 for the camelry station, 1 optio. Also Rufus: 2 sick. 28 are excused. The remaining 26 are diligent. The bathhouse receives.
EDCS-34800170.summary: This inscription is a daily report detailing the numbers and assignments of recruits and other personnel, including those assigned to the bathhouse and excused duties, dating to June 16th, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-34800173.translation: August 1. Total 44. Of these: 1 scribe, 1 optio, 1 proculcator, 7 for the bath water, 5 stationarii, 1 miller, 1 furnace worker, 1 for the prison. Sick: Paccius Maximus, 1 with 3 camels. The remaining 22 are working. The bathhouse receives 16 [personnel] and 1 camel driver. The furnace receives 5.
EDCS-34800173.summary: This inscription is a daily report listing personnel assigned to various duties, including bath operations, a mill, a furnace, and a prison, along with sick individuals and those managing camels, dated to August 1st, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-34800177.translation: September 21. Total 58. Of these: 9 equites, 1 optio, 14 quintanarii, 6 for the bath water, 1 from the cistern, 1 for the overseer, 1 sent to Esuba, 1 craftsman. 3 sick: Julius Ianuarius, Julius the sesquiplicarius, Caecilius Rogatus. The remaining 21 are working. The bathhouse receives 16. The furnace receives 5.
EDCS-34800177.summary: This inscription is a daily report detailing personnel, including cavalrymen and specialists, assigned to various tasks and listing three sick individuals, with numbers for bathhouse and furnace staff, dated to September 21st, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-34800182.translation: November 11. Total 45. Of these: 1 sesquiplicarius Aurelius Celestinus, [3] equites Caecilius Rogatus, 1 optio Aurelius Quirinus, [3] quintanarii, 2 for the bathhouse, [3] from the cisterns. [---]
EDCS-34800182.summary: This inscription is a partial daily report listing personnel, including a sesquiplicarius, equites, and an optio, along with numbers for bathhouse and cistern assignments, dated to November 11th, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-34800189.translation: [---] Total 44 [personnel]. Of these: 1 scribe, 1 optio, 1 proculcator [---], 3 for the bathhouse, 13 quintanarii, 4 stationarii. 2 sick. Paccius Maximus, Julius Secundarius: 2 [individuals]. The rest are working, a total of 19. The bathhouse receives 12, the furnace worker 7.
EDCS-34800189.summary: This inscription is a fragmented daily report detailing personnel assignments, including scribes, optiones, and quintanarii, with numbers for bathhouse and furnace staff, and listing sick individuals, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-34800190.translation: XV quintanarii (soldiers serving for a 5-year term?), 6 to the bath water, III stationarii (stationed soldiers?), 1 to Esuba, 1 to the treasury, 2 sick. Pacius Maximus, Pompeus Datius. The rest await(?), XV. Bath 3, furnace.
EDCS-34800190.summary: This inscription is a list of personnel and their assignments, possibly related to military or administrative duties, including mentions of baths and a furnace, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34800192.translation: II stationarii, 1 sent with the Garamantes, 1 guard, 3 or 4 sick. Farbius [3]s [[Aurel 3]]. Aurelius Felix, Julius Priscus, Julius Optatus. The rest await(?), XXIIII.
EDCS-34800192.summary: This inscription appears to be a roll call or roster listing soldiers and their statuses, including an engagement with the Garamantes and mentioning sick personnel, likely from the Roman military.

EDCS-34800194.translation: 1 to the prefect, 1 craftsman, 2 sick. Aurelius Celestinus, Cecil(ius) Rogatus. The rest await(?), XVI. The bath receives [3]. Furnace.
EDCS-34800194.summary: This inscription records personnel assignments and counts, including a craftsman and sick individuals, with references to a bath and furnace, suggesting a logistical or administrative context from the Roman period.

EDCS-34800195.translation: 1 sick. Cecil(ius) Rogatus. The rest await(?), XVI. The bath receives XII. Furnace IIII.
EDCS-34800195.summary: This inscription details the number of sick individuals and the capacity of a bath and furnace, likely related to the upkeep or operation of Roman public facilities.

EDCS-34800196.translation: 1 optio (officer), XV quintanarii, 6 to the bath water, 1 from the watch posts, 1 to the standards, 1 to the prefect, 1 to the rods (punishment?), 2 sick. Cornelius Hannibal, Aurelius Celestinus. The rest await(?).
EDCS-34800196.summary: This inscription lists various military personnel ranks and duties, including quintanarii and an optio, with references to water for the baths and military standards, dating to the Roman Imperial era.

EDCS-34800197.translation: III sick. Cornelius Hannibal, Aurelius Celestinus, Caecilius Rogatus. The rest await(?), XVIII. Bath XIIII, furnace IIII.
EDCS-34800197.summary: This inscription records counts of sick individuals and the capacity of a bath and furnace, alongside names of prominent figures, likely from a Roman military or administrative context.

EDCS-34800198.translation: 1 to the prefect, 1 to the bath. III sick. Cornelius Hannibal, Aurelius Celestinus, Caecilius Rogatus. The rest await(?), XVIII. The bath receives(?) VII. Furnace VI.
EDCS-34800198.summary: This inscription details personnel assignments, including those reporting to a prefect and a bath, along with counts of sick individuals and the capacity of a bath and furnace, likely from Roman administrative records.

EDCS-34800199.translation: Aurelius Donatus [3]. The rest await(?), X. The bath receives VIIII. Furnace II.
EDCS-34800199.summary: This inscription lists a name, Aurelius Donatus, and the capacities of a bath and furnace, likely part of a record of supplies or operational status from the Roman period.

EDCS-34800200.translation: 1 sent for bath wood, 1 guard, IIII sick. Julius Ianuarius, Caecilius [].
EDCS-34800200.summary: This inscription records personnel involved in providing wood for the baths and a guard, along with the number of sick individuals, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34800204.translation: Aurelius Restutus, Julius Donatus, Aurelius Felicianus, Terentius Rogatus, Caecilius Rogatus.
EDCS-34800204.summary: This inscription is a list of names, likely Roman citizens or officials, possibly part of a longer inscription or record.

EDCS-34800206.translation: To the camels... Aurelius Celestinus reports thirty-one remaining; the bathhouse takes twenty-one; the furnace, ten.
EDCS-34800206.summary: This inscription appears to be a record of accounts related to a bathhouse, possibly a military or public facility, detailing remaining items, capacity, and furnace operations.

EDCS-34800207.translation: The bathhouse takes seventeen; the furnace, four. Sick: Aurelius Carinas, Iulius Maximus, Iulius Victorinus, Iulius (sesquiplicarius). Number of... five-yearly seventeen.
EDCS-34800207.summary: This inscription lists individuals, possibly associated with a military unit or facility, noting numbers related to a bathhouse and furnace, and indicating a count of sick individuals and 'quintanarii'.

EDCS-34800212.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records the name of an individual, Caecilius Fortunatus, possibly as a dedication or memorial.

EDCS-34800214.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions names, Musanus and Restutus, and the word 'reliqui' (remaining), suggesting a record of property or goods.

EDCS-34800217.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription that preserves parts of Roman names (Aurelius, Iulius, Caecilius), possibly part of a longer text.

EDCS-34800225.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the name Felicianus followed by 'I', possibly indicating a quantity or a single item attributed to him.

EDCS-34800227.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription that preserves parts of names, including Aurelius, Chrisogonus, and Locrianus, likely part of a list or dedication.

EDCS-34800228.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription that preserves parts of names like Bubeius and a place name or gentilician 'Ipalacen', likely part of a list or geographical reference.

EDCS-34800230.summary: This fragmentary inscription lists two names, Cornelius and Felsinus, each followed by the numeral four, possibly indicating a division of goods or a count.

EDCS-34800231.translation: To the crossing... and we went out... Malchus and Iulius Felix... deserted after the bundles... Celius Fortunatus, Iulius Dona[...], Sulpicius Donatus... Pompeus Datius and they returned... at the fifth hour of the night. Sulpicius Donatus, the aforementioned, was sent with letters... Iulius Rufinus (cavalryman).
EDCS-34800231.summary: This inscription records an event where certain individuals deserted, possibly after delivering goods, and later returned, with one person being sent on a mission with letters, likely detailing troop movements or administrative matters.

EDCS-34800232.translation: Optatus... Aemilius Aemilianus Cornelius Hannibal and his associate... Rufus sent... Licinius Fortunatus and Paccius Maximus, Julius Ianuarius, Julius Censorinus, Aurelius Marinus were sent to Hyeru/zeria, Julius Rogatianus, Julius Bonilla...
EDCS-34800232.summary: This is a fragment of a document likely recording the dispatch of individuals, possibly officials or messengers, on a specific date, with some names and destinations partially preserved.

EDCS-34800233.translation: ...came with a token... and... and... Julius Baltun... our... Titus Buzuris... Aurelius Felicianus...
EDCS-34800233.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription that appears to list names and possibly some official or military titles, with little context remaining.

EDCS-34800235.translation: ...November(?), the Garamantes entered, leading 4 donkeys and 2 Egyptians carrying letters to you and to Opter, servant(?) as a fugitive(?).
EDCS-34800235.summary: This inscription records the arrival of Garamantian envoys leading donkeys and Egyptians carrying letters, possibly related to the extradition of a fugitive.

EDCS-34800236.translation: ...on the day mentioned above, the Garamantes(?) entered bringing 3 mules and 4 donkeys on the day mentioned above... Julius Ianuarius Octavius Fortunatus... Horatianus.
EDCS-34800236.summary: This inscription records the arrival of Garamantian(?) individuals with mules and donkeys, possibly carrying goods like barley, on a specific date.

EDCS-34800240.translation: To Octavius Festus, my decurion and prefect, Aemilius Aemilianus, soldier, greetings. I have sent to you, lord, by camel drivers from Iddibalis, 17.5 measures, which amounts to 90 modii, to be the consuls 12 days before the Kalends of February, after Thuscus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-34800240.summary: This is a letter from a soldier, Aemilius Aemilianus, to his superior, Octavius Festus, reporting the dispatch of 90 modii of grain via camel drivers, dated shortly before February in the consulship of Thuscus and Bassus.

EDCS-34800241.translation: To Octavius Festus, my decurion and prefect, Aemilius Aemilianus, soldier, greetings. I have sent to you, lord, by the camel driver Iassucthan, 17.9 measures, which amounts to 108 modii, to be the consuls 12 days before the Kalends of February, after Thuscus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-34800241.summary: This is a letter from a soldier, Aemilius Aemilianus, to his superior, Octavius Festus, reporting the dispatch of 108 modii of grain via a camel driver, dated shortly before February in the consulship of Thuscus and Bassus.

EDCS-34800242.translation: To Octavius Festus, my decurion and prefect, Aemilius Aemilianus, soldier, greetings. I have sent to you, lord, by the camel driver Iaremaban, 30 measures, which amounts to 60 modii, to be the consuls... days before the Kalends of February, after Thuscus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-34800242.summary: This is a letter from a soldier, Aemilius Aemilianus, to his superior, Octavius Festus, reporting the dispatch of 60 modii of grain via a camel driver, dated shortly before February in the consulship of Thuscus and Bassus.

EDCS-34800243.translation: ...greetings... I have sent to you, lord... son of Fezinis... 7.3 measures... three... the consuls...
EDCS-34800243.summary: This is a fragmented letter reporting the dispatch of a quantity of goods, possibly grain, by someone identified as the son of Fezinis, with a reference to the future consuls.
