EDCS-34800244.translation: ...to the decurion and prefect, by Aurelius Donatus, soldier, you suspect... from Glareo... two and a half measures, making thirty modii, for the use of the soldiers stationed at Golas, three days before the Kalends of August, in the consulship of Aemilianus and Bassus.
EDCS-34800244.summary: This inscription records a supply of 30 modii of grain sent by Aurelius Donatus to soldiers stationed at Golas, addressed to a decurion and prefect, dated shortly before August in the consulship of Aemilianus and Bassus.

EDCS-34800245.translation: To Octavius Festus... greetings... soldier... in the consulship of Aemilianus and Bassus.
EDCS-34800245.summary: This is a fragment of a letter addressed to Octavius Festus, mentioning a soldier and the consulship of Aemilianus and Bassus, likely related to official business.

EDCS-34800249.translation: To Aurelius Secundus, my lord, greetings from Licinius Fortunatus. I have sent them to you, my lord, also through your boys, two measures and a half, which you will sign for me.
EDCS-34800249.summary: This is a personal letter from Licinius Fortunatus to Aurelius Secundus, concerning the transfer of grain and requesting a signature, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-34800252.translation: [...] Emeritus and Poppidius V[---] to [---], greetings. You should know that two ships [---] arrived with [---] diversas, and two more [---] with [---] and a half load, which [---] did not meet them [---] may he receive the burdens, and in the seventh year [---].
EDCS-34800252.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragment of a logistical report or letter concerning the arrival of ships and their cargo, likely related to trade or military supply in Roman North Africa, possibly from the 3rd or 4th century CE.

EDCS-34800253.translation: [...] to [...]bio, greetings to our Lords [---] publicus niger sen[---] [---]brum and cor[---] [---] with asylum.
EDCS-34800253.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a dedication or a record of some sort, possibly mentioning a public official and a place of refuge, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-34800258.translation: Pomponius Sillvanus, decurion, to Iulius Varus, equestrian of the VIII Cohort Fida, greetings. I am inquiring about the tesserae (tokens) for the place called Secedi. I sent various tesserae to Secedi to Iulius Ianuarius on the 8th day before the Kalends of May. Received on the 5th day before the Kalends of May through Pano, the grain official.
EDCS-34800258.summary: This is a military correspondence concerning the delivery of identification tokens (tesserae) to a specific location and individual, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-34800260.translation: [---] arrived [---] [---] Aurelius Celestinus [---] on the aforementioned day arrived [---] Optantus cor[---] [---] a token from [---] for barley [---] ships [---] through Volumnius [---] farewell. Gallus [---] because [---] deserters.
EDCS-34800260.summary: This fragmented inscription seems to be a letter or report concerning the arrival of ships, the transfer of goods like barley, and possibly the presence of deserters, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-34800263.inscription_type: tituli fabricationis, tituli operum, tituli possessionis
EDCS-34800263.translation: [---] with asses [---] [---] consuls [---] To Catulus, acting [---] Aemilius Flamininus, vice [---] principal, you should know, lord, that Aban the barbarian refugee has come to me for the third time. I have sent him to you on the Ides of February through M/[---].
EDCS-34800263.summary: This is a letter from Aemilius Flamininus to a superior, reporting the arrival of a barbarian refugee named Aban for the third time, mentioning the consuls for dating, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-34800264.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly a list of names or a record of official actions, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-34800265.translation: To Iulius [---], my superior, from the pagus [---] I have sent [---] to Aelius [---] an equestrian [---] with two letters, Donat[---] [I]ulius Maximus arrived [---] on the ninth day before the Ides of Ma[y] [---] at the first hour [---] farewell.
EDCS-34800265.summary: This is a letter from Iulius Maximus to his superior, Iulius, reporting the delivery of letters and mentioning the arrival of Aelius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-34800266.translation: You should know that the deserter Amnon Meded [---] has arrived [---] I have sent [---] to you with my letters. I hope you are well.
EDCS-34800266.summary: This inscription is a short letter reporting the arrival of a deserter named Amnon Meded and sending greetings, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-34800269.translation: Terentius [---] and another decurion [---] of them [---] most excellent [---] first [---] [---]T S LI[---]
EDCS-34800269.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly a dedication or a list of names and titles, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-34800271.translation: To my own [?], Pomponius Felix wishes you good health on the Ides of November.
EDCS-34800271.summary: A personal letter, likely from a private monument, conveying good wishes from Pomponius Felix on a specific date.

EDCS-34800272.translation: Luria Felici[...], I received letters [...] life through whom [...] nine whom [...]
EDCS-34800272.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragment of a personal letter, possibly from a tomb or a private record, mentioning receipts of letters and someone named Luria Felici.

EDCS-34800275.translation: Augurinus to his brother Urtatus, greetings [...]. I send you [...] at the first hour [...].
EDCS-34800275.summary: A fragmentary private letter or epitaph from Augurinus to his brother Urtatus, possibly dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-34800278.translation: [...to] Germano, a token [...] sixteen [...] which is in the possession of six [...] empty [...] sign [...]
EDCS-34800278.summary: This inscription, likely from a military context or a personal token, mentions a quantity of sixteen and possibly refers to empty slots or signs.

EDCS-34800280.translation: Julius [...], Vabius S[...], Aelius Rogatus [...], Aurelius Urb[...], Julius Saturninus [...], Julius Ianvarius [...]
EDCS-34800280.summary: A list of Roman names, possibly from a funerary monument, a legal document, or a list of property owners.

EDCS-34800283.summary: A very fragmentary inscription, possibly part of a longer text, its original purpose and context are unclear.

EDCS-34800286.summary: An extremely fragmented inscription containing only partial names or beginnings of words, making its context impossible to determine.

EDCS-34800288.summary: A single, possibly restored, personal name ('Valerius') from a very incomplete inscription, offering no further context.

EDCS-34800294.summary: A very fragmentary inscription, mentioning 'cator' and referencing the Corpus Inscriptionum Latinarum (CIL), indicating it is a known but damaged inscription.

EDCS-34800295.translation: Thirty and a half [...] boy [...] outstanding [...] nature [...] in military service [...]
EDCS-34800295.summary: This inscription fragment likely refers to a young soldier (puer in militia) of exceptional talent, possibly related to age or rank.

EDCS-34800296.translation: On the seventh day before the Kalends of [Month], one and a half [units?] were restored by Lucius? Cornelius? [...]
EDCS-34800296.summary: A fragmentary inscription, possibly recording a land division or construction project, with incomplete names and dates, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34800299.summary: A very fragmentary inscription, possibly a personal name, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34800302.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning 'Lupina' and an abbreviation possibly related to military service, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34800305.translation: [...] of the Martia ala [cavalry unit] / [...] Romano, a sesquiplicarius(?)...
EDCS-34800305.summary: A fragmentary inscription possibly referring to a cavalry unit ('ala Martia') and a soldier's rank ('sesquiplicarius'), dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34800306.summary: A very fragmentary inscription with isolated words and possible names, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34800309.translation: [...] of the [tribe?] / [...] us [...] / [...]iussero Lepidio, three in number [...] / [...] S [...] R EN [...] MACO [...] / [...] to where with you safe and favorable [...] / [...]e sum cum id (!) prem [...] / [...] II durense [...] / [...] on account of opinions [...] / [...]iure to come [...] / [...] in this place barbarians, forty in number [...] / [...] more or less forty [...] / [...] are greater, Garamantes [...] / [...] and [...] and Masa [...] / [...]at when intervening [...] / [...] which are Bocus Seb [...] / [...] all [...] / [...] the masters ordered [...] / [...] to come and [...] / [...] to bring V [...] pa [...] / [...] lord
EDCS-34800309.summary: A highly fragmented inscription, likely a legal document or report concerning a dispute or census, mentioning barbarians, Garamantes, and possibly a judicial decision, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34800310.translation: [...] to Atilius Iobenalis, decurion, prefect [...] / [...]nius Saturninus, decurion, greetings [...] / [...] P [...] AT pr [...] M [...] / [...] prefect A [...] E [...] A [...] / [...] NI [...]AME [...] / [...] T [...] who [...] operations [...] / [...] I do not want you to offer it, but [...] / I have sent across through Can [...] / [...]de, gold, brother [...] / when
EDCS-34800310.summary: A fragmentary letter, possibly from a superior to a subordinate, discussing official matters, greetings, and the transmission of items, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34900999.text_edition: \n Imperatore Domitiano Caesare Augusto Germanico XIIII consule iuventus civitatis Mactaritanae cultores Martis Augusti solo publico basilicam et horrea II pecunia sua faciendum curaverunt curatoribus Saturnino Arisim filio et Fortunato Lulhim filio magistri Saturninus Leptha filius Aris Chunin filius Rogatus Addunis filius Fortunatus Arsaces filius Silvanus Mutthumbalis filius Balsillec Zrumae filius Nanes Thusabiae filius Baricbal Iasuctani filius Primus Victoris filius Gudul Mazbalis filius Saturninus Fortunati filius Cittin Selidiunis filius Ninus Iassicbalis filius Baric Sappir filius Mitthun Samonis filius Aris Arisim filius Fortunatus Papi filius Sfichinnius Mercatoris filius Optatus Laeti filius Mitthun Mutthumbalis filius Baliathon ilian filius Victor Balsamonis filius Felix Fortis filius Aris Zruman filius Mustu Musthumbalis filius Rogatus Fausti filius Bassus Maximi filius Faustus Sexti filius Ninus Baricbalis filius Mitthumbal Rogati filius Crescens Baricis libertus Titus Iaraucan filius Gudul Thilbran filius Birzic Maximi filius Maximus Optati filius Rogatus ri filius Publius Fortis filius Ialival Bassi filius Maximus Iurathai filius Mustu Zrumai filius Macarsa Quinti filius Fortunatus A--- f(ilius) Rogatus Tertullae filius Rogatus Largi Africanus Ardeumai filius Satur Birissio filius Datus Thiai filius Rogatus Faustae filius Messor Tertullae filius Satur Aris filius NovMuthumbalis filius Baricbal Fidae filius Ninus Adarbalis filius Aris Cantoris filius Vassai Rogatae filius Fortunatus Oricionis filius Sfichenius Mitthunis Vindex Bassi Birzic Zrumai filius Ciri Bassi filius Martialis Acchana filius Lucrio Sexti filius Martialis Fav filius Cinsais filius Cai Chachonis filius Saturninus Zrumanis Baliathon Tafibra\n
EDCS-34900999.translation: During the consulship of Emperor Domitian Caesar Augustus Germanicus for the 14th time, the youth of the Mactaritan community, worshippers of Mars Augustus, built this public basilica and two storehouses with their own money. They were supervised by curators Saturninus, son of Arisim, and Fortunatus, son of Lulh(im), and under the magistracy of Saturninus, son of Leptha, and other community leaders and patrons, including Aris son of Chunin, Rogatus son of Addunis, Fortunatus son of Arsaces, Silvanus son of Mutthumbalis, Balsillec son of Zrumae, Nanes son of Thusabiae, Baricbal son of Iasuctani, Primus son of Victor, Gudul son of Mazbalis, Saturninus son of Fortunati, Cittin son of Selidiunis, Ninus son of Iassicbalis, Baric son of Sappir, Mitthun son of Samonis, Aris son of Arisim, Fortunatus son of Papi, Sfichinnius son of Mercatoris, Optatus son of Laeti, Mitthun son of Mutthumbalis, Baliathon son of Ilian, Victor son of Balsamonis, Felix son of Fortis, Aris son of Zruman, Mustu son of Musthumbalis, Rogatus son of Fausti, Bassus son of Maximi, Faustus son of Sexti, Ninus son of Baricbalis, Mitthum son of Rogati, Crescens freedman of Baricis, Titus son of Iaraucan, Gudul son of Thilbran, Birzic son of Maximi, Maximus son of Optati, Rogatus son of Ri, Publius son of Fortis, Ialival son of Bassi, Maximus son of Iurathai, Mustu son of Zrumai, Macarsa son of Quinti, Fortunatus son of A---, Rogatus son of Tertulla, Rogatus son of Largi Africanus, Ardeumai son of Satur, Birissio son of Datus, Thiai son of Rogatus, Faustae son of Messor, Tertullae son of Satur, Aris son of Nov, Muthumbalis son of Baricbal, Fidae son of Ninus, Adarbalis son of Aris, Cantoris son of Vassai, Rogatae son of Fortunatus, Oricionis son of Sfichenius, Mitthunis Vindex son of Bassi, Birzic son of Zrumai, Ciri son of Bassi, Martialis son of Acchana, Lucrio son of Sexti, Martialis son of Fav, Cinsais son of Cai, Chachonis son of Saturninus, Zrumanis, Baliathon son of Tafibra.
EDCS-34900999.summary: This inscription records the construction of a public basilica and storehouses by the youth of Mactaritan, dedicated to Mars Augustus, during the consulship of Domitian and lists numerous community leaders and patrons who contributed or were involved.

EDCS-35001070.translation: 13 days before the Kalends of June, a total of 3 [individuals/units?]; among them: 1 sesquiplicarius, 6 equites, 1 optio, 10 quintanarii, 2 for the water of the bathhouse, 2 for the camels, 1 for the prefect, 1 craftsman, 1 sick person. Caecilius Rogatus... the rest shall pay back... 8 bathhouse attendants, 5 furnace workers.
EDCS-35001070.summary: This is a fragmented list of personnel and their duties or numbers, likely related to the administration of a bathhouse or similar facility, with a date indicated as 13 days before the Kalends of June.

EDCS-36400004.text_edition: \n Hic Vibius Felix si tempora longa fuissent Felix epulis cognomine fortem neverim nam septem simul et viginti viximus annos nunc quoniam Parcae rumpunt breve fila sorores patris mente pia pariter karaque sepultus hoc situs in tumulo felix in mortem quiesco\n
EDCS-36400004.translation: If times had been long, I, Vibius Felix, would have wished to say that Felix, strong in his surname, had lived seven and twenty years together. But now, since the Fates break the short threads of sisters, buried by my father’s pious mind and also dear, I lie in this tomb, happy in death, I rest.
EDCS-36400004.summary: This is an epitaph for Vibius Felix, composed in elegiac couplets, lamenting his short life of twenty-seven years and expressing his contentment in death.

EDCS-36400005.text_edition: \n Hic sita est Kasta Magni rarissima coniunx quae vixit semper parce et sine fraude beata pro dolore pia est misera brevi tempore fati sancta pudicitia vixit denis quater annis o dulcis coniunx uita mihi dulcior ipsa concordes linquens generum tuumque maritum hanc aram Magnus posuit karissimae Kastae\n
EDCS-36400005.translation: Here lies Kasta Magna, his rarest wife, who lived always with restraint and without deceit, blessed. In her grief, pious, miserable, she lived a short time in fate, chaste, modest. She lived thirty-six years. Oh sweet wife, sweeter to me than life itself, leaving concord, your son-in-law and your husband, Magnus erected this altar for his dearest Kasta.
EDCS-36400005.summary: This is a funerary altar erected by Magnus for his wife Kasta, who lived a virtuous life and died at the age of 36.

EDCS-36400006.text_edition: \n ius Luci filius Arnensi Fortunatus vixit annis IV hic situs est RN teneris sic puer iacet sub pondere magno annis uixi IV dulcissumus sic parentibus in V annum ascendens hic animam posui cui puero mater mama posuisse viderus sic huic puero lapidem supremum redidit ipsa\n
EDCS-36400006.translation: Lucius, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe, Fortunatus, lived 4 years. Here he lies. Tender boy, he lies under a great weight. I lived 4 years, most sweet. Ascending into the fifth year, I surrendered my soul here. To whom, mother, it seemed, Mama placed this stone, the last one for this boy, she herself gave it.
EDCS-36400006.summary: This is a tombstone for a young boy named Fortunatus, who died at the age of 4 and was buried by his mother Mama.

EDCS-36400007.text_edition: \n F O N e a vitae tantem lus atque mo satyros perfedentis dulcia orum frequentora movit et quiq qui suas fabulas in tempore finxit easdem meditans semper elusit n se placuit ut voluit puer molliora fratri \n
EDCS-36400007.translation: An example of life, so much joy and satyrs, pleasing sweet things, and he who in his time finished his stories, always meditating on the same ones, he amused himself. He pleased himself as he wished, softer than his brother.
EDCS-36400007.summary: This fragmentary inscription seems to praise a person's life, possibly an actor or poet, known for their joyful performances and stories.

EDCS-36400008.text_edition: \n Hic sita sedibus tot tollens o Claudia co o utinam t R frui d SAV\n
EDCS-36400008.translation: Here lies, raised to the seats of the gods, oh Claudia, oh R... may you enjoy...
EDCS-36400008.summary: This is a very fragmentary epitaph for a Claudia, likely indicating her burial or transition to the divine.

EDCS-36400010.text_edition: \n VIIDA vixit annos LX Fortunatus filius posuit quid simus aut quid loquimur vitast id denique nostra cum reputem VM LE quoque dispicio vel modo SIS cum vixit homo nunc homo NEST est hominis praeter cana favilla L stat LANS et nomen tantum vestigiu N est inscriptu est hominis cetera memNI CVRTEA N VIS Crusias denique AV uerunt non sunt NOS quoque mus vivit et dum quest aetas dum VNDA voluptas non est aeternum\n
EDCS-36400010.translation: VIIDA lived 60 years. Fortunatus, his son, placed this. What we are, or what we say, life is just that. When I consider it, I see that when a man lived, now he is not a man. Nothing remains of man but white ash. L stands, and only the name, a trace of man is inscribed, the rest is memory. CVRTEA N VIS Crusias finally... they lived, we too... life is as long as pleasure lasts, it is not eternal.
EDCS-36400010.summary: This epitaph reflects on the ephemeral nature of life, stating that only a name and ashes remain after death, likely for a man named Fortunatus who lived to 60.

EDCS-36400011.text_edition: \n tas hominum mos est bene vivere et post obitum nomen habere cara pia coniux Vaninna cum Valio Victorico sancto marito periit V LVM NOSA\n
EDCS-36400011.translation: It is the custom of men's lives to live well and to have a name after death. My dear, pious wife Vaninna perished with her holy husband Valio Victoricus. V LVM NOSA
EDCS-36400011.summary: This epitaph states that it is customary for people to live well and be remembered after death, and records the passing of Vaninna with her husband Valio Victoricus.

EDCS-36400014.text_edition: \n ICR Acherusiae tenebrae et Elysii coluntur quinquaginta licet septem annos sex menses atque simum et decem dies eri quos tribuerit relicta vita cessit hic situs est\n
EDCS-36400014.translation: The darkness of Acheron and the Elysian Fields are honored. He lived fifty-seven years, six months, and ten days, then he departed from life. Here he lies.
EDCS-36400014.summary: This epitaph records the death of an individual who lived 57 years, 6 months, and 10 days, and mentions the realms of the afterlife.

EDCS-36400015.text_edition: \n Aquiliae Quinti filiae Flaccillae c(larissimae)? f(eminae) Quintus Silius Luci filius Arnensi Maurus amicus ob merita quod beneficio liberalitate eius paternis necessitatibus exsolutus dignitatis suae conservatus est decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-36400015.translation: To Aquilia Flaccilla, daughter of Quintus, a most distinguished woman. Quintus Silius Maurus, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe, as a friend, because he was freed from his paternal necessities by her merit and generosity and was preserved in his dignity, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-36400015.summary: This inscription honors Aquilia Flaccilla, a distinguished woman, and records the gratitude of Quintus Silius Maurus, who was aided by her generosity, as recognized by a decree of the decurions.

EDCS-39000050.summary: This is a very fragmentary Greek inscription, likely a patronymic or genitive case, possibly a name or ownership mark.

EDCS-39000057.summary: This is a fragmentary Greek inscription that seems to refer to something or someone as 'the younger' and possibly mentions 'God'.

EDCS-43400822.summary: This is a funerary inscription likely for Flavia Domitilla, dating to the Christian period (indicated by 'in pace').

EDCS-44200068.translation: Chadda, son of Nimirmirath, Nytros, and Felimun, son of Thubab.
EDCS-44200068.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary marker for several individuals, likely of non-Latin origin based on the names, with a possible date range from the Roman to early Byzantine period.

EDCS-44200073.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, having his tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, restored [the structure] in the 7th year.
EDCS-44200073.summary: This is a dedication by Emperor Marcus Aurelius, listing his titles and achievements, indicating he restored a structure (likely public), dating to his reign (161-180 CE).

EDCS-44200081.translation: To Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, the citizen community of Numluli, at public expense.
EDCS-44200081.summary: This is a dedication by the community of Numluli to Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely Caracalla, funded by public money, dating to his reign (211-217 CE).

EDCS-44200346.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, chief priest, having his tribunician power for the 3rd time, emperor for the 5th time, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, restored the road from Carthage to the borders of the province of Numidia, which had been corrupted and ruined by long neglect.
EDCS-44200346.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Carthage and Numidia by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximus, dating to their joint rule (c. 236-238 CE).

EDCS-44400164.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus, Germanicus...
EDCS-44400164.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription naming Emperor Trajan, listing some of his titles and likely his lineage, dating to his reign (98-117 CE).

EDCS-44400337.translation: [Emperor] Caesar Gaius [Julius Verus] Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Germanicus [Maximus], Sarmaticus [Maximus], Dacicus Maximus, chief [priest, having tribunician power for the 3rd time, emperor for the 5th time, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, [most noble] Caesar, prince of the youth, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Da[cicus] Maximus, restored the road from Carthage to the borders of the province of Numidia, which had been corrupted and ruined by long neglect, [in the 66th year].
EDCS-44400337.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Carthage and Numidia by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximus, dating to their joint rule (c. 236-238 CE).

EDCS-44400338.translation: To Perpetual Emperor Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictissimus Augustus, in the 70th year.
EDCS-44400338.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Aurelian, listing his imperial titles and indicating its completion in a specific year (possibly regnal year or a dating system), dating to his reign (270-275 CE).

EDCS-44400351.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, having tribunician power for the 3rd time, emperor for the 5th time, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, restored the bridges, fallen into ruin with age, and the road, corrupted by long neglect, and by their tireless foresight made it passable for travelers, [in the 30th year].
EDCS-44400351.summary: This inscription records the restoration of bridges and a road by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximus, highlighting their efforts to ensure public works and travel, dating to their joint rule (c. 236-238 CE).

EDCS-44500115.translation: To Emperor Caesar Publius [Li]cinius Valerianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, having tribunician power, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, father of our lord Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus Pius Felix...
EDCS-44500115.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Valerian I, listing his titles and patronage, likely referring to his son Gallienus as well, dating to Valerian's reign (253-260 CE).

EDCS-44900148.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Constantinus the Younger, most noble Caesar, 53.
EDCS-44900148.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Caesar Constantinus Junior, likely a military honorary inscription given the number 'LIII'.

EDCS-44900218.translation: To our lord Flavius Constantinus, the good ruler for the state, ever Augustus.
EDCS-44900218.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantinus Augustus, praising him as a beneficial ruler of the state.

EDCS-45600121.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, most great conqueror of Parthia, most great conqueror of Britain, most great conqueror of Germany, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 19th time, hailed as imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, restored this, 81.
EDCS-45600121.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something by Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, listing his numerous titles and honours, with a numerical marker 'LXXXI' possibly indicating the location or a measure.

EDCS-45800001.translation: [---] Yriraban son of Isicuar, BABAR, EIMSE VLH, 5NI.
EDCS-45800001.summary: This inscription is fragmented and appears to record a personal dedication or epitaph with names and possibly numerical or directional information.

EDCS-45900018.translation: To our lord, Imperator Caesar Flavius Constantinus Maximus Pius Felix Victor and Triumphant ever Augustus, born for the good of the state, and to Flavius Claudius Constantinus, the most noble Caesars.
EDCS-45900018.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantinus Maximus and Caesar Constantinus, honoring them as rulers beneficial to the state.

EDCS-46400965.text_edition: \n Pro salute Augusti nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani principis totiusque domus divinae optimi Germanici Parthici data a Licinio maximo et feliciore Augusto libertis proccuratoribus ad exemplum legis Mancianae qui eorum intra fundo villae Magnae Varianae id est Mappalia Siga habitabunt eis eos agros qui subcisiva sunt excolere permittitur lege Manciana ita ut eas sic qui excoluerit usum proprium habeat ex fructibus qui eo loco nati erunt dominis aut conductoribus vilicisve eius fundi partes e lege Manciana praestare debebunt hac condecione sic coloni fructus cuiusque culturae quos ad aream deportare et terere debebunt summas reddant arbitratu suo conductoribus vilicisve eius fundi et si conductores vilicisve sic eius fundi in assem partes colonicas daturas renuntiaverint tabelles caveant eius fructus partes quas praestare debent conductores sic vilicisve eius fundi coloni colonicas partes praestare debeant qui in fundo villae Magnae sive Mappalia Siga villas habent habebunt dominicas eius fundi aut conductoribus vilicisve eorum in assem partes fructuum et vinearum ex consuetudine Manciane sic cuiusque generis habet praestare debebunt tritici ex aream sic partem tertiam hordei ex aream sic partem tertiam fabae ex aream sic partem qu tam vinu sic de laco sic partem tertiam olei coacti partem tertiam mellis in alveis mellaris sextarios singulos qui supra haec lex scripta a Lurio Victore Odilonis magistro et Flavio Geminio defensore Felice Annobalis Birzilis quinque alveos habebit in tempore quo vindemia mellaria fuit fuerit dominis aut conductoribus vilicisve eius fundi qui in assem colunt dare debebit si quis alveos examina apes vasa mellaria ex fundo villae Magnae sive Mappaliae Sigae in octonarium agrum transtulerit quo fraus aut dominis aut conductoribus vilicisve eis sic quam fiat alvveis sic examina apes vasa mellaria mel qui in eo f(undo) erunt conductoribus sic vilicorumve in assem eius fundi erunt ficus aride arboresque extra pomario erunt qua pomarium intra villam ipsam sit ut non amplius iuger tot at colonus arbitrio suo coactorum fructuum conductori vilicisve eius fundi partem dare debebit ficeta vetera et oliveta quae ante hanc legem sata sunt e consuetudinem sic fructum conductori vilicisve eius praestare debeat sic si quod ficetum postea factum erit eius ficeti fructuctum per continuas ficationes quinque arbitrio suo eo sic qui seruerit percipere permittitur post quintam ficationem eadem legem sic qua supra scriptum est conductoribus vilicisve eius fundi praestare debebit vineas serere colere loco veterum permittitur ea condicione ut ex ea satione proxumis sic vindemis quinque fructum earum vinearum is qui ita fuerit sic suo arbitrio percipeat sic itemque post quintam vindemiam quam ita sata erit fructus partes tertias e lege Manciana conductoribus vilicisve eius in assem dare debebunt olivetum serere colere in eo loco qua quis incultum excoluerit permittitur ea condicicione ut ex ea satione eius fructus oliveti quid ita satum est per olivationes proximas decem arbitrio suo permittere debeat item post olivationes olei coacti partem tertiam conductoribus vilicisve eius fundi dare debebit qui inseruerit oleastra post annos quinque partem tertiam dare debeat q in fundo villae Magnae Varianae sive Mappaliae Sigae sunt eruntve extra eos agros qui vicias habent eorum agrorum fructuus conductoribus vilicisve dentur custodes exigere debebunt pro pecora quae intra fundum villae Magnae sic Mappaliae Sig(a)e pascentur in pecora singula aera quattus sic conductoribus vilicisve dominorum eius fundi praestare debebit si quis ex fundo villae Magnae sive Mappaliae Sigae fructus stantem pendentem maturum inmaturum caeciderit exciderit exportaverit deportaverit conbuserit sic desequerit sic dequienii detrimentum conductoribus vilicisve eius fundi coloni erit ei cui de tantum praestare debebit si qui in fundo villae Magnae sive Mappaliae Sig(a)e severunt severint liberis qui e legitimo matrimonio procreati sunt testamento relinquere licet superficies post hoc tempus lege Manciana ritu fiduciaeve data sunt dabuntur ius fiduciae lege Manciane sic servabitur qui superficiem ex inculto excoluit excoluerit ibique aedificium deposuit posuerit eive sic qui coluit postea desierit perdesierit eo tempore quo ita ea superficies coli desit desierit ea sic quo fuit fuerit ius colendi dumtaxad sic biennio proximo ex qua die colere desierit servatur servabitur post biennium conductores vilicisve sic eorum ea superficies quae proxumo sic anno F culta fuit et coli desierit conductor vilicusve eius fundi ea superficies esse dicitur denuntiet superficiem cultam denuntiationem denuntiatur sigalis testa itemque insequentem annum tat ea sine querella eius eius f(undi) post biennium conductor vilicusve colere iubeto ne quis conductor vilicusve eorum inquilinum eius fundi coloni qui intra fundum villae Magn(a)e sive Mappaliae Sigae habitabunt dominis aut conductoribus vilicisve in assem quodannis sic in hominibus singulis in arationes operas numero II et in messem operas n(umero) II et in sarritiones cuiusque generis singulas operas binas praestare debebunt coloni inquilini eius fundi intra anni nomina sua conductoribus in custodias singulas qu per genenera tam seorsum quamsum stipendiariorum qui in fundo villae Magnae sive Mappaliae Sigae habitabuntuas conductoribus vilicisvet custodibus servis dom singula partem quartam \n
EDCS-46400965.translation: For the safety of our Emperor Caesar Trajanus, princeps of the whole world, and of his divine household, the best, Germanicus, Parthicus, given by Licinius Maximus and Felix Victor Augustus, freedmen procurators, according to the example of the Mancian law. Those who will live within the estate of the Villa Magnae Varianae, that is Mappalia Siga, are permitted to cultivate the lands that are surplus, according to the Mancian law, in such a way that whoever cultivates them shall have the use of the produce that grows in that place for himself, but shall provide to the owners or their contractors or overseers their shares according to the law. On this condition, that the farmers shall render to the contractors or overseers of that estate the full amounts of produce from each crop that they should bring to the threshing floor and process, according to their own judgment. And if the contractors or overseers of that estate announce that they will give the farmer's share, the farmers shall be bound by contract, by which they must provide the farmer's share of the produce and vines to the contractors or overseers of that estate, as is customary. Those who have or will have farms in the Villa Magnae or Mappalia Siga estate must provide to the owners or their contractors or overseers their share of the produce and vines according to their judgment, namely one-third of the wheat from the threshing floor, one-third of the barley from the threshing floor, one-third of the beans from the threshing floor, one-third of the wine from the vat, one-third of the pressed oil, one sextarius of honey from each hive. The law written above this was made by Lurius Victor, son of Odilo, master, and Flavius Geminius, defender. Felice Annobalis Birzilis will have five hives. At the time of the honey harvest, he must give to the owners or contractors or overseers of that estate, those who farm for a share, if anyone moves hives, swarms, honey vessels from the Villa Magnae or Mappaliae Siga estate to an area of eight iugera, so that fraud against the owners or contractors or overseers does not occur, the hives, swarms, honey vessels, and honey that are in that estate will belong to the contractors or overseers of that estate. Figs and trees outside the orchard, which is within the villa itself, shall not exceed the number of iugera that the farmer, according to his judgment, must give to the contractor or overseer of the fruits collected. Old fig groves and olive groves planted before this law must provide their fruits to the contractor or overseer according to custom. If any fig grove is made later, its fruits may be enjoyed by the one who planted it at his own discretion for five continuous fig harvests. After the fifth harvest, the same law as written above must be provided to the contractors or overseers of that estate. It is permitted to plant and cultivate vines in place of old ones, on the condition that for the next five grape harvests, the one who planted them may enjoy the fruits of those vines at his own discretion. Likewise, after the fifth harvest, they must provide one-third of the fruits of those vines to the contractors or overseers of that estate, according to the Mancian law. It is permitted to plant and cultivate an olive grove in a place that one has cleared from uncultivated land, on the condition that for the next ten olive harvests, the one who planted it may enjoy the fruits of that olive grove at his own discretion. After the olive harvests, they must provide one-third of the pressed oil to the contractors or overseers of that estate. Those who graft wild olive trees must provide one-third after five years. The fruits of those lands in the Villa Magnae Varianae or Mappaliae Siga which are or will be outside those areas that have villages, must be given to the contractors or overseers. Guards must collect for each head of cattle grazed within the Villa Magnae or Mappaliae Siga estate, four asses to the contractors or overseers of the owners of that estate. If anyone cuts, picks, transports, burns, or otherwise removes fruits, whether standing, hanging, ripe, or unripe, from the Villa Magnae or Mappaliae Siga estate, the farmer shall owe compensation to the contractors or overseers of that estate for the loss. If those who have planted in the Villa Magnae or Mappaliae Siga estate have legitimate children born in lawful marriage, they may bequeath them by will. Superficies that have been or will be granted under the Mancian law or by trust shall be preserved. The right of trust under the Mancian law shall be observed. Whoever has cultivated a superficies from uncultivated land and has built a building thereon, or whoever cultivated it and later ceased to cultivate it, at the time when that superficies ceased to be cultivated, the right of cultivation shall be preserved for them for two full years from the day on which they ceased to cultivate. After two years, the contractors or overseers of that estate shall be deemed to own that superficies which was cultivated in the preceding year and has ceased to be cultivated. The contractor or overseer of that estate must announce the cultivated superficies. The announcement is marked by a seal. Likewise, in the following year, the contractor or overseer of that estate must order it to be cultivated without complaint, after two years. No contractor or overseer shall compel any tenant farmer living within the Villa Magnae or Mappaliae Siga estate to provide to the owners or contractors or overseers annually, for each person, two days of labor for plowing, two days of labor for harvesting, and two days of labor for weeding of any kind. The tenant farmers of that estate must provide, within the year, for their own names, to the contractors for individual guard duties, and for the stipendiaries who live in the Villa Magnae or Mappaliae Siga estate, to the contractors, overseers, guards, and servants, one-quarter of each.
EDCS-46400965.summary: This is a detailed agricultural lease agreement (lex fundi) for the Villa Magnae Varianae and Mappalia Siga estates, outlining the rights and obligations of landowners, contractors, overseers, and tenant farmers regarding cultivation, produce sharing, and labor duties, issued under the authority of Emperor Trajan and regulated by Mancian law.

EDCS-47400210.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Vindicia Victorina lived 70 years, 3 months, 12 days. Her dutiful father erected this. Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulia Maxima lived 30 years, 7 months. Julius Maximus erected this for his dutiful daughter. She rests here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Aemilius Tertullus lived 7 years, [3] months. He rests here. His father erected this. Sacred to the departed spirits. Aemilius Saturninus lived 3 years, 2 months. He rests here. His father erected this.
EDCS-47400210.summary: This is a collection of four funerary inscriptions (epitaphs) dedicated to the departed spirits, recording the names, ages at death, and familial relationships of the deceased.

EDCS-47400288.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, father of the country, consul. To Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, father of the country.
EDCS-47400288.summary: This inscription records dedications to two Roman Emperors, Trebonianus Gallus and his co-emperor Volusianus, listing their official titles and honours.

EDCS-47400346.summary: This is a fragmented inscription that mentions 'victor Constantine', likely referring to an emperor.

EDCS-48500128.translation: Unconquered divine powers. Junius Amicus, a sesquiplicarius (soldier paid one-and-a-half times the normal rate), who was also a librarius (clerk/scribe), restored part of the former bathhouse with the entire contingent of soldiers.
EDCS-48500128.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a bathhouse by Junius Amicus, a soldier of sesquiplicarius rank and a clerk, with the assistance of his military unit.

EDCS-48600014.translation: To Marcus Flavius Valerius Constantius, most noble Caesar, from the municipium Iulium Aurelium Mus(titanum), devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-48600014.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the municipium of Mus(titanum) to the Caesar Marcus Flavius Valerius Constantius, likely dating to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-51500073.translation: Iulia Severa Gaetulica, for her husband (?), ordered a statue erected to Gaius Iulius Cerealis, whom Gratillius her heir (?) [erected?].
EDCS-51500073.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue by Iulia Severa Gaetulica for her husband Gaius Iulius Cerealis, with incomplete information about the restorer, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51500074.translation: Marcus Bibius Crescens, a most distinguished man.
EDCS-51500074.summary: This is a simple epitaph for Marcus Bibius Crescens, a man of high status, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51500075.summary: This inscription labels a container of garum (fish sauce) belonging to Marcus Vibius Crescens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51500076.summary: This is a possessive inscription, likely referring to property or a tomb belonging to Marcus Vibius Crescens, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51600002.summary: This is a Christian epitaph for Indulgentia, who is described as faithful and at peace, likely from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-51600003.summary: This is a Christian epitaph for Iulia Monna, stating she is at peace, likely from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-51800020.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, conqueror of Parthia, conqueror of Britain, conqueror of Germania, holding tribunician power for the 19th time, consul for the 4th time, father of his country, restored [this monument] in the 19th year.
EDCS-51800020.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a monument by Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, dated by his tribunician power and consulships, likely to 178 CE.

EDCS-51800021.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Traianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power...
EDCS-51800021.summary: This is a fragmented inscription mentioning Emperor Decius, likely recording an official act or dedication during his reign, from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-51900490.translation: Marcus Aufidius Rufinus, son of Marcus, of the voting tribe Quirina, pious, lived for 3 years, is buried here.
EDCS-51900490.summary: This is a child's epitaph for Marcus Aufidius Rufinus, who died at the age of three, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51900492.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Publius Anicius Baric lived 115 years and returned nature's debt.
EDCS-51900492.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Publius Anicius Baric, who lived to an exceptional age, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51900496.translation: Lucius Gargilius Paetus, son of Lucius, of the Arnensis tribe, pious, lived 70 years, is buried here.
EDCS-51900496.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Gargilius Paetus, providing his name, parentage, tribal affiliation, age at death, and stating he is buried here, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51900500.translation: Tettia Prima, daughter of Lucius, pious, lived 23 years, is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-51900500.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tettia Prima, including her name, parentage, piety, age at death, burial location, and a wish for her peaceful rest, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-53500002.translation: The faith of the mind, always kept for your husband, has brought your joys of life to heaven. You remain an example for chaste women to imitate on earth. Praise has this merit, your fame preserves your modesty, surpassing the law of death as it endures into eternity. Thus do morals live, thus does time never fail. All your kin grieved for you, all chaste women grieved for you. Whoever marveled at your innocent life in time, does not die; bodies are released from fate. You conquered the narrow years of life's time. Caelestina.
EDCS-53500002.summary: A poetic epitaph celebrating the virtue and piety of a woman named Caelestina, whose exemplary life is seen as having transcended death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55701564.translation: By the authority and decision of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Antoninus Augustus Pius, a public determination was made for the Mustitani.
EDCS-55701564.summary: A public decree issued under the authority of Emperor Antoninus Pius concerning the Mustitani tribe, likely from the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-56500212.translation: To Gaius Marius Fidus, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, quaestor, prefect of justice, duovir, perpetual flamen, the people of Emerita set up this equestrian monument from the money he contributed for his games, as he promised annual, perpetual, and festive donations for his merits and generosity; and on his behalf, his equestrian sons erected the equestrian monument and the pedestrian one for himself, according to his will.
EDCS-56500212.summary: A dedication by the people of Emerita for Gaius Marius Fidus, honoring him for his public service and generosity with games, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-58200010.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Flavius Rogatianus the younger lived 16 years, 10 months, 23 days. Lucius Rogatianus for his most obedient son. Though snatched too soon by cruel fate after sweet Muses, after the groves and springs, the rivers of Virgil, this place holds me, surrounded by a dark grove. I do not grieve that I was quickly carried off to the shades below, but that my camp and flourishing military studies, having become a deserter by the deadly law of fate. Let my final supreme sin and this be after my death. I do not cast these eternal things to the resounding Muse.
EDCS-58200010.summary: A poignant epitaph for a young man named Lucius Flavius Rogatianus, who died at 16, lamenting his unfulfilled potential and interrupted studies, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-58200016.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Fulvius Fortunatianus, pious, lived 65 years, is buried here.
EDCS-58200016.summary: A funerary epitaph for Fulvius Fortunatianus, stating his name, piety, age at death, and burial location, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-58200017.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Pomponius Vitalis, pious, lived 83 years, is buried here.
EDCS-58200017.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Pomponius Vitalis, providing his name, piety, age at death, and burial location, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-58200018.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Quintus Mucius Solutor, pious, lived [number] years.
EDCS-58200018.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Quintus Mucius Solutor, recording his name and piety, with his age at death missing, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-58200020.translation: To the departed spirits. Servius Agilio lived piously for 85 years.
EDCS-58200020.summary: A funerary epitaph for Servius Agilio, who lived to be 85 years old.

EDCS-58200024.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Caecilius Vitalis lived 75 years and 2 months; he lies buried here. His sons, Caecilius Vitalianus Barbarus and Rusticulus, made this for their most excellent father, most learned in all literature and most skilled in both Greek and Latin languages, a propagator of this lineage, a good man.
EDCS-58200024.summary: A funerary monument erected by sons for their father, Quintus Caecilius Vitalis, praising his literary skills and family legacy.

EDCS-59600004.text_edition: \n Neroni Claudio divi Claudi filio Germanici nepoti Tiberi Caesaris pronepoti divi Augusti abnepoti Caesari Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VIII imperatori consuli IIII patri patriae Servius Cornelius Servi filius Lemonia Orfitus quaestor divi Claudi praetor urbanus consul pontifex sodalis Augustalis proconsul patronus dedicavit Publio Silio Celere legato columnas et super columnia et super ostia in tribus Ithymbal Arinis filius Sabinus Tapapius flamen divi Augusti sufetu faciendas curavit idemque curator pecuniae publicae eas porticus\n
EDCS-59600004.translation: Servius Cornelius Orfitus, quaestor of the deified Claudius, urban praetor, consul, pontiff, sodalis Augustalis, proconsul, patron, dedicated this for Nero Claudius, son of the deified Claudius, grandson of Germanicus, great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar, great-great-grandson of the deified Augustus Caesar Germanicus, chief pontiff, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, emperor, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland. Publius Silio Celer, legate, supervised the construction of the columns, the structures above the columns, and the doorways in the three Ithymbal Arinis. His son Sabinus Tapapius, flamen of the deified Augustus and sufes, saw to their construction and was also supervisor of public funds for these porticoes.
EDCS-59600004.summary: A dedication of porticoes, columns, and doorways by Servius Cornelius Orfitus to Emperor Nero Claudius, with details of Roman official careers and the supervision of construction by Publius Silio Celer and Sabinus Tapapius.

EDCS-59600074.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Claudius Donatus, soldier of Legion III Augusta Pia Vindex, lived 52 years.
EDCS-59600074.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Claudius Donatus, a soldier of Legion III Augusta Pia Vindex, who died at age 52.

EDCS-59600075.translation: ...legate; soldier of Legion III Augusta Pia Vindex, lived 31 years.
EDCS-59600075.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph recording the service and lifespan of a soldier, identified as a legate, from Legion III Augusta Pia Vindex.

EDCS-59600076.translation: The soldiers of the Severiana vexillation of Legion III Augusta Pia Vindex, devoted to your divine majesty and power, [erected this] for Julia Mamaea, mother of our Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, under the supervision of Aelius Crescentinus, first-rank centurion.
EDCS-59600076.summary: A dedication by soldiers of Legion III Augusta Pia Vindex to Julia Mamaea, mother of Emperor Alexander Severus, overseen by Aelius Crescentinus.

EDCS-59800042.translation: Secundio, actor for Quintus Servilius Candidus, lived 66 years, 5 months, and 12 and a half days.
EDCS-59800042.summary: A funerary epitaph for Secundio, an actor for Quintus Servilius Candidus, recording his lifespan.

EDCS-59800048.translation: Titus Iulius Crisontianus, soldier of the III Augusti LII.
EDCS-59800048.summary: A fragmented inscription for Titus Iulius Crisontianus, a soldier of Legion III Augusta.

EDCS-59800074.summary: A very fragmented epitaph recording the lifespan of an individual.

EDCS-59800083.translation: For the good memory of Magora, [she] lived [....] years.
EDCS-59800083.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph for Magora, recording her name and an incomplete lifespan.

EDCS-59800084.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Benedicta Sabina, likely a tombstone label.

EDCS-59800085.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Benedicta Roga, likely a tombstone label.

EDCS-59800086.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Benedicta Pro[---], likely a tombstone label.

EDCS-59800088.summary: This inscription likely refers to a memorial for Aurelia.

EDCS-59800093.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions a subdeacon.

EDCS-59800095.summary: This fragmentary inscription seems to record something related to Proiecta.

EDCS-59800096.summary: This inscription records a memorial dedicated to Kasianus.

EDCS-59800097.translation: Aurellia Cornelia Hammonilla lived for 25 years in peace
EDCS-59800097.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Aurellia Cornelia Hammonilla.

EDCS-59800102.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus [---] / [---] in accordance with his will [---] ordered to be made [---] / [---] Publius Ampelius, a most distinguished and eminent proconsul
EDCS-59800102.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus, mentioning a commission by a proconsul, likely from the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-59800105.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Marcus Ulpius Fortunatus, freedman and procurator of Augustus, pious, lived 42 years, is buried here. Coelia Thallusa, daughter of Aulus, his most devoted wife, made this.
EDCS-59800105.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Marcus Ulpius Fortunatus by his wife Coelia Thallusa, and dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59800108.translation: To the divine virtue and perpetual clemency of the greatest emperor, founder of eternal peace, restorer of public safety and common liberty, our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, most powerful emperor, the municipality of Iulium Aurelium Mustitanum, devoted to his divinity and majesty.
EDCS-59800108.summary: This is a civic dedication by the municipality of Mustitanum to Emperor Constantine, praising his virtues and role as a ruler, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-59800109.translation: To Iulia Domna Augusta, mother of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, and of the camps, the senate, and the fatherland, wife of the divine Severus Pius, father of our lord the Emperor Antoninus Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, Pater Patriae, the council of Mustitanum, by decree of the decurions, at public expense, has dedicated this monument to Fortuna Redux and to Fertility.
EDCS-59800109.summary: This is a dedication by the council of Mustitanum to Empress Iulia Domna, mother and wife of emperors, honoring her maternal and marital roles, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-59900161.translation: [Name fragment] from [name fragment], a descendant in the fourth generation, added this work above his father's building.
EDCS-59900161.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary or commemorative inscription recording a descendant's addition to a building, likely a tomb, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-60500082.translation: [Name fragment], son of [name fragment], lived for thirty years. He is buried here.
EDCS-60500082.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph recording the name, parentage, age at death, and burial of an individual, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-60500083.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Primigenius, slave of Flavius Av[--], lived for twenty-five years.
EDCS-60500083.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a slave named Primigenius, indicating his owner and age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-61300095.translation: [Fragmentary legal petition and imperial response concerning land rights and labor obligations, possibly related to a dispute over the saltus Burunitanus between landowners and a conductor (manager/tenant) and concerning abuses of power by officials.]
EDCS-61300095.summary: This is a very fragmentary text containing a legal petition and imperial responses, likely from the Roman Imperial period, concerning property rights, labor duties, and alleged abuses by officials in the province of Africa Proconsularis.

EDCS-61800284.translation: Sentia Vitalis [epitaph text includes a mention of an omnipotent god, her age, and a plea to her husband to live well while he can].
EDCS-61800284.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph for Sentia Vitalis, mentioning her age and religious beliefs, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64800298.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Mussius Ingenuus lived for [number] years.
EDCS-64800298.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Mussius Ingenuus, indicating his age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64800299.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Fadius Claros lived for 35 years, 3 months, and 25 days.
EDCS-64800299.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Fadius Claros, providing his age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65000109.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iudas Icositanus monumentum vivus fecit\n
EDCS-65000109.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Iudas Icositanus made this monument for himself while alive.
EDCS-65000109.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iudas Icositanus, who commissioned his own monument (a 'living tomb'), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65000121.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Gravius Romanus, likely a funerary dedication or a name panel.

EDCS-65400059.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Porcius Verus lived 47 years. Fulvius Primus placed this for his father. He is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Porcius Martialis lived 22 years. Quintus Porcius Verus placed this for his brother. He is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Fulvia Tertiola lived 4 years. Quintus Porcius Verus, her father, placed this. She is buried here.
EDCS-65400059.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Quintus Porcius Verus, his brother Porcius Martialis, and his daughter Fulvia Tertiola, with dedications from family members, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600082.translation: Aemilia Eufrosyna placed this cupola for her freedman Aemilius Dainus. He lived 35 years. Sacred to the departed spirits.
EDCS-65600082.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of the freedman Aemilius Dainus at age 35, dedicated by his patroness Aemilia Eufrosyna.

EDCS-65600083.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Agria Sabina, a chaste matron, lived 30 years. She is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Asidonius Repentinus lived [3] years. He is buried here.
EDCS-65600083.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Agria Sabina, a married woman, and Asidonius Repentinus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600084.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Clodia Venusta lived 80 years. Her most dutiful sons placed this.
EDCS-65600084.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Clodia Venusta, who lived 80 years, and was commemorated by her sons.

EDCS-65600085.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Caius Cornelius Perpetuus Vipianus, son of Caius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 6 years. He is buried here. Caius Cornelius Primus placed this for his dearest son.
EDCS-65600085.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of a young boy, Caius Cornelius Perpetuus Vipianus, who died at age 6, dedicated by his father Caius Cornelius Primus.

EDCS-65600086.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Titus Lartius Vegetus lived 80 years. He is buried here. A dutiful son placed this. Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulia Quintulla lived 60 years. She is buried here. A dutiful son placed this. Sacred to the departed spirits. Titus Lartius Pudens lived 42 years. He is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Edinia Regilla lived [3] years.
EDCS-65600086.summary: This funerary inscription records the burials of Titus Lartius Vegetus, Iulia Quintulla, Titus Lartius Pudens, and Edinia Regilla, with dedications likely from their children.

EDCS-65600087.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Caius Marius Tertulus Luciolus lived fifty years. To him, who with [3] Aquilus Luciolus wished, the sons of Marius placed this for their dearest father. He is buried here.
EDCS-65600087.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Caius Marius Tertulus Luciolus, who died at age 50, dedicated by his sons.

EDCS-65600088.translation: Numonia Candida, a woman without equal, lived 38 years. She is buried here. Her parents, whom death cruelly struck, placed this so that the memory of her name might endure through reading.
EDCS-65600088.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Numonia Candida, who lived 38 years, and was memorialized by her parents who had also passed away.

EDCS-65600090.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Tilia Concordia lived 17 years and 30 days. T. N. P., her dutiful father, placed this. Sacred to the departed spirits. Tilius [3]nus [3] lived [3] years [3] M [3].
EDCS-65600090.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Tilia Concordia, who died at age 17, dedicated by her father, and a second fragmented burial record.

EDCS-65600091.translation: To the gods, the shades. Ulpia Cata, a woman of honorable memory, lived 28 years. She lies here. Ulpius Colonus made this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-65600091.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ulpia Cata, set up by her husband Ulpius Colonus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600093.translation: To the gods, the shades. Flavius [---], lived [3] years. He lies here. Primitivus placed this for his most beloved son. // To the gods, the shades. Flavius Felix, aged 20, lived 16 years, [6] months, [6] days. He made this for his devoted son. // To the gods, the shades. Flavius Primitivus, lived 36 years, 3 months. He lies here. The Flavii placed this for their father.
EDCS-65600093.summary: This inscription appears to be a composite text with at least three funerary notices for members of the Flavius family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600094.translation: To the gods, the shades. Iulia Fortunata [---]
EDCS-65600094.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Iulia Fortunata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600121.translation: To the gods, the shades, sacred. Gaius Julius Agilis, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, [---]
EDCS-65600121.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Gaius Julius Agilis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600131.translation: [---]nius, a veteran of the III Augusta legion, served 20 years, lived [---] years. [---]
EDCS-65600131.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for a veteran of the III Augusta legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600133.translation: To the gods, the shades, sacred. Quintus Aemilius Saturninus, lived 20-29 years. He lies here.
EDCS-65600133.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Aemilius Saturninus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600134.translation: [To the gods, the shades], sacred. Appia Galla, lived 90 years. Her devoted children placed this. She lies here.
EDCS-65600134.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Appia Galla, set up by her children, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600135.translation: To the gods, the shades, sacred. Asclepiades lived [3] years. Asidonia Felicitas placed this for her devoted [---]rono. He lies here.
EDCS-65600135.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Asclepiades, set up by Asidonia Felicitas, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600136.translation: To the gods, the shades. Of Quintus Avii Quirina [---] lived 12 years. She lies here.
EDCS-65600136.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for a female, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600137.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Aurelia Matita, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600138.translation: Dius Felix lived 6 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-65600138.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span and burial of Dius Felix.

EDCS-65600141.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Fabius [---] [---] erected [this monument]. He is buried here.
EDCS-65600141.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the departed, mentioning Fabius and a monument erected by an unknown person.

EDCS-65600142.translation: Gavinnia Dorchas lived 16 years; her devoted sons erected this.
EDCS-65600142.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span of Gavinnia Dorchas, erected by her sons.

EDCS-65600143.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Geminia Spes lived 6 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-65600143.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span and burial of Geminia Spes.

EDCS-65600145.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulius Subitanus, a boy of good disposition, lived 12 years; Iulius Generosus erected this for his foster-son; he is buried here.
EDCS-65600145.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of the young Iulius Subitanus, for whom Iulius Generosus erected the monument.

EDCS-65600146.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Concordia lived 17 years and 30 days; her son erected this out of devotion.
EDCS-65600146.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Concordia, recording her age at death and erected by her son.

EDCS-65600148.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aulus Numonius Rogatus lived 75 years; he is buried here; his sons erected this for their devoted father.
EDCS-65600148.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aulus Numonius Rogatus, recording his age at death and erected by his sons.

EDCS-65600149.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Valerius Liberalis lived 30 years; he is buried here; his father erected this for his son.
EDCS-65600149.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Valerius Liberalis, recording his age at death and erected by his father.

EDCS-65600150.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Varius Restutus lived [---] years; he is buried here.
EDCS-65600150.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Varius Restutus, recording his burial.

EDCS-65600151.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Virtus Masi lived 29 years.
EDCS-65600151.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Marcus Virtus Masi.

EDCS-65600158.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Vinnius Trophimus, freedman of Gaius, lived 91 years. He rests here. Gaius Vinnius Servilius placed this for his devoted father.
EDCS-65600158.summary: This is a tombstone commemorating Gaius Vinnius Trophimus, who lived to be 91, and was set up by his son, Gaius Vinnius Servilius.

EDCS-65600161.translation: To the departed spirits. Sallustina(?) lived 16 years. She rests here. Grata placed this for her sister.
EDCS-65600161.summary: This is a tombstone for Sallustina(?), who died at age 16, and was set up by her sister, Grata.

EDCS-65600162.translation: To the departed spirits. Aventius Protagoras lived 60 years. Asidonia Buntesia made this for a good man. He rests here.
EDCS-65600162.summary: This tombstone records the death of Aventius Protagoras at age 60 and was made by Asidonia Buntesia for him.

EDCS-65600163.translation: To the departed spirits. Adventus lived 70 years. His sons placed this for their devoted father. He rests here.
EDCS-65600163.summary: This tombstone commemorates Adventus, who lived to be 70, and was set up by his sons.

EDCS-65600164.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Aelius(?).
EDCS-65600164.summary: This is a fragment of a tombstone with the name Quintus Aelius(?).

EDCS-65600165.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Aelius Catullinus lived 50 years. He rests here.
EDCS-65600165.summary: This tombstone commemorates Lucius Aelius Catullinus, who lived to be 50 and rests here.

EDCS-65600166.translation: To the departed spirits. Arrius Avicius, son of Lucius, lived 27 years, as fate permitted. To the departed spirits. Aelius Comenialis lived [3] years.
EDCS-65600166.summary: This tombstone fragment records the death of Arrius Avicius at age 27 and also mentions Aelius Comenialis.

EDCS-65600167.translation: To the departed spirits. Carvilia Antonilla lived 87 years. Her sons placed this for their most rare mother. She rests here. To the departed spirits. Quintus Aelius Saturninus lived 90 years. His sons placed this for their most rare father. He rests here.
EDCS-65600167.summary: This tombstone records two burials: Carvilia Antonilla, who lived to 87 and was commemorated by her sons, and Quintus Aelius Saturninus, who lived to 90 and was also commemorated by his sons.

EDCS-65600168.translation: To the departed spirits. Aelia Ianuaria lived 28 years. Caninius Felix placed this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-65600168.summary: This tombstone records the death of Aelia Ianuaria at age 28 and was erected by her husband, Caninius Felix.

EDCS-65600171.translation: To the departed spirits. Aemilia Rogatina, devout, lived [3] years. She rests here. Aemilius Capito placed this for his daughter with his own money.
EDCS-65600171.summary: This tombstone commemorates Aemilia Rogatina, who died at age [3], and was set up by her father, Aemilius Capito.

EDCS-65600172.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aeternius lived for 27 years and 6 months.
EDCS-65600172.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Aeternius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600173.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Allia Rogatina lived for 27 years. Here lies Lucius Geminius Orfitianus.
EDCS-65600173.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Allia Rogatina and mentions Lucius Geminius Orfitianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600174.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Antonius Baricio lived for 50 years. Here he lies. His dutiful sons placed this.
EDCS-65600174.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Marcus Antonius Baricio and states it was erected by his sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600175.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Antonius Fortunatus lived for [3] years. His son placed this. Here he lies.
EDCS-65600175.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Marcus Antonius Fortunatus and notes it was placed by his son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600176.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Antonia Marciana lived for 20 years. Here she lies. Marianus placed this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-65600176.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Antonia Marciana and states it was erected by her husband, Marianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600177.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Apria Maiga lived for 35 years. Though poor, she was a matron. Statilius Paternianus, her devoted husband, placed this. Here she lies.
EDCS-65600177.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Apria Maiga, describing her as a matron, and notes it was erected by her devoted husband, Statilius Paternianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600178.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Nina Isperata lived for 65 years. His dutiful son placed this. Here she lies. // Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Apuleius Secundinus lived for 70 years. His dutiful son placed this for his father. [Here he lies].
EDCS-65600178.summary: This monument contains two funerary epitaphs, one for Nina Isperata and another for Apuleius Secundinus, likely erected by sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600180.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Arruntia Maior lived for 105 years. Her dutiful sons placed this for their mother. Here she lies.
EDCS-65600180.summary: This funerary epitaph records the very long lifespan of Arruntia Maior and states it was erected by her sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600181.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Asidonia Asiane lived for 45 years. Here she lies. Asidonius Facilis and Athena and A[---]danus made this for their dearest mother.
EDCS-65600181.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Asidonia Asiane and notes it was erected by her children, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600182.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Redducta Victoria lived for 35 years and 5 months. Asidonius Calvus placed this for his sister. Here she lies.
EDCS-65600182.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Redducta Victoria and states it was erected by her brother, Asidonius Calvus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600183.translation: To the departed spirits. Attia Marciana lived 50 years; here she lies. Modius Victoricus placed this for his most beloved wife.
EDCS-65600183.summary: This is a tombstone for Attia Marciana, set up by her husband Modius Victoricus, dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-65600184.translation: To the departed spirits. Augustica(?) lived for [3] years and [3] months; her dutiful children placed this.
EDCS-65600184.summary: This is a tombstone for Augustica (?), set up by her dutiful children, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-65600185.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelia Victorina(?) the dutiful...
EDCS-65600185.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone for Aurelia Victorina (?), likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-65600186.translation: Publius Baebidius Victor, son of Publius, of the tribe Quirina, dutiful, lived 35 years; here he lies. Quintus Geminius Secundus placed this for his best friend.
EDCS-65600186.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Baebidius Victor, set up by his friend Quintus Geminius Secundus, dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-65600188.translation: To the departed spirits. Baebinia Donatula lived 57 years; her dutiful children placed this for their mother; here she lies.
EDCS-65600188.summary: This is a tombstone for Baebinia Donatula, set up by her dutiful children, dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-65600190.translation: To the departed spirits. Bucosus lived 21 years; here he lies. [---] placed this for his dutiful son.
EDCS-65600190.summary: This is a tombstone for Bucosus, set up by his father, dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-65600191.translation: To the departed spirits. Bolanius Liberalis lived 46 years...
EDCS-65600191.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone for Bolanius Liberalis, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-65600192.translation: To the departed spirits. Caecilia Secundula, also known as Monnula, lived 17 years and 7 months; her dutiful mother placed this. To the departed spirits. Quintus Caecilius Secundianus lived 52 years. Gavia Septimina, his dutiful wife, placed this; here he lies.
EDCS-65600192.summary: This is a double tombstone recording the deaths of Caecilia Secundula (erected by her mother) and Quintus Caecilius Secundianus (erected by his wife), likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-65600193.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone for a member of the Caecilius family, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-65600194.translation: To the departed spirits. Caecilia Donata lived [---]13 years...
EDCS-65600194.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone for Caecilia Donata, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-65600195.translation: To the Divine Shades. Caecilia Iulla, pious, lived 18 years, 6 months, and 3 days. Caecilius Amandus, father, erected this for his pious daughter. She lies here.
EDCS-65600195.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caecilia Iulla, set up by her father, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600196.translation: To the Divine Shades. [Caecilia] Tertia lived 14 years. [Quintus] Caecilius Firmianus erected [this for his daughter]. She lies here. To the Divine Shades. Sacred. Caecilia Privata lived 27 years. She lies here. Quintus Caecilius Firmianus erected [this] for his pious wife.
EDCS-65600196.summary: A funerary inscription recording the deaths of two women, Caecilia Tertia and Caecilia Privata, both of whom were set up by a Quintus Caecilius Firmianus, likely a father and husband respectively, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600197.translation: To the Divine Shades. Sacred. Lucius Caelius Germanus lived 26 years. He lies here. Lucius Caelius [son's name?] erected this for his pious brother.
EDCS-65600197.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Caelius Germanus, erected by his brother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600198.translation: To the Divine Shades. Sacred. Titus Caelius Martialis lived 51 years. He lies here.
EDCS-65600198.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Caelius Martialis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600199.translation: To the Divine Shades. Sacred. Caelia Euporia lived 38 years. She lies here. Caelius Praetor(?), erected this for his excellent wife.
EDCS-65600199.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caelia Euporia, erected by her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600200.translation: [Name] lived 51 years, [duration] months. Caelia Tertulla, his wife, erected this for her deserving and pious husband.
EDCS-65600200.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unnamed man, erected by his wife Caelia Tertulla, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600201.translation: [Name] erected this for his father Caelis. He lies here.
EDCS-65600201.summary: A funerary epitaph for a father named Caelis, erected by his son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600203.translation: To the Divine Shades. Sacred. Calventius Commodus lived 81 years.
EDCS-65600203.summary: A funerary epitaph for Calventius Commodus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600205.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gaius Camurenus Saturninus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 54 years. He lies here. [His son] Camurenus Saturninus erected this for his pious father.
EDCS-65600205.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Camurenus Saturninus, erected by his son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600207.translation: To the Divine Shades. Sacred. Titus Carmeius Nundinarius lived 11 years. He lies here. And Carmeia Augurina lived 9 years. She lies here. Carmeius Augurinus, father, erected this for his most pious children. To the Divine Shades. Sacred. Volussia Servilia lived 55 years. She lies here. Canneius Augurinus erected this for his dearest, most pious wife. To the Divine Shades. Sacred. Titus Carmeius Felix lived 87 years. He lies here. Carmeia Festa lived 75 years. She lies here. Carmeius Augurinus erected this for his most pious father and mother.
EDCS-65600207.summary: A complex funerary inscription recording the deaths and burials of multiple family members, including Titus Carmeius Nundinarius, Carmeia Augurina, Volussia Servilia, Titus Carmeius Felix, and Carmeia Festa, with epitaphs set up by Carmeius Augurinus, likely a father and husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600209.translation: Cata, daughter of Lucius, lived 10 years. She lies here.
EDCS-65600209.summary: A tombstone recording the death of a girl named Cata, who died at the age of ten.

EDCS-65600210.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Claudius Colonicus lived 75 years. He lies here. Domitia, his wife, erected this.
