EDCS-65600210.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudius Colonicus, who lived to 75, erected by his wife Domitia.

EDCS-65600211.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Titia Sallusa Candi[d]iana lived 23 years and 10 months. For my most dutiful daughter, she lies here. Gaius Titius Agrius Maximus, her father, erected this. Sacred to the departed spirits. Claudia Candida lived 59 years and 6 months. For my incomparable wife, an example of chastity, most lauded of matrons, she lies here. [Gaius Titius] Agrius Maximus, her husband, erected this.
EDCS-65600211.summary: This inscription is a double epitaph, commemorating both a daughter, Titia Sallusa Candi[d]iana, and her mother, Claudia Candida, both erected by the father and husband, Gaius Titius Agrius Maximus.

EDCS-65600212.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Claudia Tryphena lived 75 years. She lies here.
EDCS-65600212.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudia Tryphena, who lived to 75.

EDCS-65600213.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Claudia [---] lived 44 years. She lies here. Clod[ius ---]anus for his wife, pious, modest, to whom faith, integrity, and freedom never failed; who lost her, lost [it?].
EDCS-65600213.summary: A funerary epitaph for a woman named Claudia, whose husband Clodius expresses his grief over her death and her virtues.

EDCS-65600214.translation: Estenia Paulina placed [this] for her husband. Sacred to the departed spirits. Clodius Crescentianus lived 63 years, more or less.
EDCS-65600214.summary: This monument records the death of Clodius Crescentianus, who lived approximately 63 years, and was set up by his wife Estenia Paulina.

EDCS-65600215.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Clodius Tracalus lived 19 years, 7 days, and 7 hours. For his excellent son, he lies here.
EDCS-65600215.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Clodius Tracalus, who died at 19 years, 7 days, and 7 hours, erected by his father.

EDCS-65600216.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Cornelius Antinianus, dearest infant. Sacred to the departed spirits. Ulpia Sever[a] lived 33 years. She lies here.
EDCS-65600216.summary: This inscription is a double epitaph, one for a child named Cornelius Antinianus and another for Ulpia Severa, who died at 33.

EDCS-65600217.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Cornelius Felix lived 49 years. He lies here. Porcia Laeta [erected this?] for her excellent husband.
EDCS-65600217.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Cornelius Felix, who lived to 49, likely erected by his wife Porcia Laeta.

EDCS-65600218.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Cornelia Dubitata lived 54 years and 3 months. She lies here. Caecilius Amandus erected this for his most pious wife.
EDCS-65600218.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cornelia Dubitata, who died at 54 years and 3 months, erected by her husband Caecilius Amandus.

EDCS-65600219.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Cornelia Extricata lived 22 years. Larnia Sal... her daughter placed [this]. She lies here.
EDCS-65600219.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cornelia Extricata, who lived 22 years, set up by her daughter, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600220.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Cornelia Felicitas lived 36 years, 10 months, and 17 days. Lucius Sittius Maritus placed [this] for his dearest wife. She lies here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius, her son, lived 30 days. His devoted father placed [this]. He lies here.
EDCS-65600220.summary: A double funerary epitaph recording the deaths of Cornelia Felicitas and her infant son Lucius, both set up by Lucius Sittius Maritus, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600221.translation: Marcus Decumus Capito lived 48 years. Manlia Victoria placed [this] for her husband.
EDCS-65600221.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Decumus Capito, who lived 48 years, erected by his wife Manlia Victoria, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600222.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Deuterius lived 6 years, 7 months, and 19 days. He lies here. Iulius ..., Nice, and Cirus made [this] for their dearest foster-son.
EDCS-65600222.summary: A funerary epitaph for Deuterius, a child who lived 6 years, set up by his foster parents or guardians, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600223.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Publius Domitius lived 35 years. He lies here. Pursaccus placed [this] for his dearest brother.
EDCS-65600223.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Domitius, who lived 35 years, erected by his brother Pursaccus, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600224.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aeternia Feculina lived 92 years. Cornelius Felix made [this] for her out of devotion. Sacred to the departed spirits. Domitius Felix lived 65 years. He lies here. Iulius Nundinarius, his son, placed [this] for his father.
EDCS-65600224.summary: A double funerary epitaph recording Aeternia Feculina and Domitius Felix, with Cornelius Felix and Iulius Nundinarius as dedicants, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600225.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Eventius lived 5 years. His devoted father placed [this]. He lies here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Eventius lived 2 years. His devoted father made [this].
EDCS-65600225.summary: A double funerary epitaph for a child named Eventius, who died twice according to the text, with his devoted father as the dedicant, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600226.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Fabius Pirforus lived 81 years. He lies here. His sons placed [this] for their father.
EDCS-65600226.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Fabius Pirforus, who lived 81 years, erected by his sons, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600227.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Fabia Secundula lived 50 years. Iunius Fabianus, her devoted son, placed [this].
EDCS-65600227.summary: A funerary epitaph for Fabia Secundula, who lived 50 years, erected by her devoted son, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600228.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Fa[...]/hospes(?) lived 6 years. He lies here.
EDCS-65600228.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Fa[...]/hospes(?), a child who lived 6 years, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600229.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flaminius Castulus lived 40 years. To the Spirits of the Departed. Geminia Maxima lived... years. To the Spirits of the Departed. Flaminia Estricatula lived 1 year and 7 days. Vibia Urbana(?)...
EDCS-65600229.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to contain three separate funerary notices for individuals named Flaminius Castulus, Geminia Maxima, and Flaminia Estricatula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600230.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. [A name missing] Flamon lived 6 years, 3 months, and 6 days. [His parents] lovingly set this up.
EDCS-65600230.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, records the death of a child named Flamon at the age of 6 years and 12 days, set up by his parents.

EDCS-65600231.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavius Conventalis lived 36 years and is laid to rest here. To the Spirits of the Departed. Magni(a) Voluptas lived 28 years and is laid to rest here. To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Victoria lived 7 years, 3 months, and is laid to rest here. To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavius Secundus lived 13 years, 3 months, and is laid to rest here; his uncles set this up.
EDCS-65600231.summary: This inscription contains four separate funerary notices for Flavius Conventalis, Magni(a) Voluptas, Flavia Victoria, and Flavius Secundus, all from the Roman Imperial period, with two of the deceased having their monuments erected by relatives.

EDCS-65600232.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Flavius Fronto lived 2 years and is laid to rest here. Quintus Flavius Felix, the father, set this up.
EDCS-65600232.summary: This is a funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period recording the death of Titus Flavius Fronto at the age of 2, with his father Quintus Flavius Felix as the dedicator.

EDCS-65600233.translation: Flavius Valerius Falacrius lived 33 years and 2 months and is buried here.
EDCS-65600233.summary: This is a funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period recording the life and burial of Flavius Valerius Falacrius.

EDCS-65600234.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavius Romanus lived 29 years. Fidus in friendship always without [illegible] blood and [illegible] mother [illegible] ... Junius ... [illegible] ... Here he rests. His brother...
EDCS-65600234.summary: This damaged funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period commemorates Flavius Romanus, who died at 29, and mentions his brother and mother.

EDCS-65600235.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Flavius Saturninus lived 11 years and 7 months and is laid to rest here. Liberalis made [this] for his good son(?).
EDCS-65600235.summary: This funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period records the death of Lucius Flavius Saturninus at age 11 years and 7 months, with a dedication likely from his son.

EDCS-65600236.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Flavius Urbanianus the younger lived 1 year and 7 months. Flavius Urbanianus the father set [this] up for his son; here he rests.
EDCS-65600236.summary: This funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period records the death of Lucius Flavius Urbanianus the younger at 1 year and 7 months, with his father Flavius Urbanianus as the dedicator.

EDCS-65600238.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Fortitosa Peculiaris, daughter, lived 12 years; her dearest mother, two sisters, and four brothers set up the tomb out of piety; here she rests.
EDCS-65600238.summary: This funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period records the death of Fortitosa Peculiaris at age 12, with the monument erected by her mother and siblings.

EDCS-65600240.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Frumentia, daughter of Frumentius, lived 9 years and 1 month; here she rests.
EDCS-65600240.summary: This funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period records the death of Frumentia at age 9 years and 1 month, likely set up by her family.

EDCS-65600242.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Galla lived for 35 years, 6 months, and 10 days. She rests here. Liberalis placed this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-65600242.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the life span of Galla and erected by her husband, Liberalis.

EDCS-65600243.translation: Gargilia Spes, daughter of Caius, also called Daia Flavia, lived for 2 years. She rests here. Rumi­cilia Faustina took care of the burial.
EDCS-65600243.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for the infant Gargilia Spes, with care of the burial managed by Rumi­cilia Faustina.

EDCS-65600244.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Lucius Geminius... lived for 74 years. Quintus Geminius Martialis made this for his father. He rests here.
EDCS-65600244.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Geminius, erected by his son Quintus Geminius Martialis.

EDCS-65600247.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Geminia Secundula lived for 32 years. She rests here. Papius Servilianus placed this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-65600247.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Geminia Secundula, erected by her husband Papius Servilianus.

EDCS-65600248.translation: ... Nabor lived for 21 years, 4 months, and 26 days. He rests here... lived for 60... Geminius(?) N... dearest(?).
EDCS-65600248.summary: This fragmented funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Nabor and possibly part of another inscription for a Geminius.

EDCS-65600249.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Gentia Tertullica lived for 19 years. She rests here. Gentius Victorius placed this for his devoted daughter.
EDCS-65600249.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gentia Tertullica, erected by her father Gentius Victorius.

EDCS-65600250.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Golaia lived for 10 years... She rests here. Her mother placed this.
EDCS-65600250.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Golaia, erected by her mother.

EDCS-65600251.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Quintus Helvius Iovinus...
EDCS-65600251.summary: This is the beginning of a funerary epitaph for Quintus Helvius Iovinus.

EDCS-65600253.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Herennius Victorianus, their foster child, lived for 10 years. He rests here. // To the gods of the underworld. Titus Herennius Victor lived for 47 years, 10 months, and 12 days. He rests here. Considia Victoria placed this for her devoted husband.
EDCS-65600253.summary: This inscription records two funerary epitaphs: one for Herennius Victorianus, likely a foster child, and another for Titus Herennius Victor, erected by his wife Considia Victoria.

EDCS-65600255.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Hail, Hon­o­ra­tus! Why did you live so briefly after fate had decreed...?
EDCS-65600255.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, likely addressed to Hon­o­ra­tus, questioning his short life.

EDCS-65600256.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Agria Fortunata lived 116 years; her grandchildren and son-in-law, recognizing her merits, set this up; she lies here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Hortensius Dativus lived 40 years and 4 months; his devoted mother set this up. Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Hortensius Barigbal lived 67 years; his devoted son and wife set this up.
EDCS-65600256.summary: This is a funerary monument with three separate inscriptions commemorating Agria Fortunata, Gaius Hortensius Dativus, and Gaius Hortensius Barigbal, detailing their ages and who erected the memorials.

EDCS-65600257.translation: [?]lius, sacred to the departed spirits. Kalvaria lived 87 years and 5 hours for her parents. Hor() Silvana, his wife, lived 72 years and 2 hours.
EDCS-65600257.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages of Kalvaria and his wife Hor() Silvana, with Kalvaria's memorial erected for his parents.

EDCS-65600258.translation: Gaius Julius Donatus lived 90 years. Julius Donatus set this up for his devoted father. He lies here.
EDCS-65600258.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Gaius Julius Donatus' son, also named Julius Donatus, erected a memorial for his father who lived 90 years.

EDCS-65600259.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Julius Fortunatus lived [---] years.
EDCS-65600259.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Fortunatus, stating his name and that he lived for an unspecified number of years.

EDCS-65600260.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Julius Hilarius [---].
EDCS-65600260.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Julius Hilarius.

EDCS-65600261.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Julius Saturninus lived 25 years and six months. His mother set this up.
EDCS-65600261.summary: This funerary inscription records that Gaius Julius Saturninus lived 25 years and six months, and his mother erected the monument.

EDCS-65600262.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Julius Silvinus lived 40 years. He lies here. Popilia Floria set this up for her devoted husband.
EDCS-65600262.summary: This funerary inscription states that Gaius Julius Silvinus lived 40 years and was commemorated by his wife, Popilia Floria.

EDCS-65600263.translation: This is the eternal home. Julius Trifolium made for himself during his lifetime, who lived as many years as he could, not as many as he wanted. He lies here. This is the eternal home. Julia Victoria made for herself during her lifetime, who lived as many years as she could, not as many as she wanted. She lies here.
EDCS-65600263.summary: This inscription consists of two parallel texts for Julius Trifolium and Julia Victoria, describing the eternal homes they built for themselves and reflecting philosophically on the duration of their lives.

EDCS-65600264.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Julius Tutocosus lived [---]17 years. He set this up for himself during his lifetime.
EDCS-65600264.summary: This funerary inscription records that Quintus Julius Tutocosus lived 17 years and set up the monument for himself during his lifetime.

EDCS-65600265.translation: He lies here. Gaius Julius Felix set this up for his devoted son. He lived 8 years. Gaius Julius Felix, the father, set this up for his devoted daughter.
EDCS-65600265.summary: This funerary monument contains two parts: one dedicated by Gaius Julius Felix to his son who lived 8 years, and another dedicated by the same father to his daughter.

EDCS-65600267.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Primitiva lived 70 years. She lies buried here. Antonia Rogata placed this for her most dutiful mother.
EDCS-65600267.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Iulia Primitiva, with a dedication by her daughter Antonia Rogata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600268.translation: Iulia Rogata, daughter of Marcus, lived [about] 34 years. She lies buried here.
EDCS-65600268.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Iulia Rogata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600270.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Junius Ingenuus lived 40 years. He lies buried here. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iunia Sisso[..] lived 5 years. She lies buried here. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Junius Mauricius lived 2 years. He lies buried here. Viria Romana placed this for her husband and sons.
EDCS-65600270.summary: A complex funerary monument recording the deaths of at least three individuals, with a separate dedication by Viria Romana for her family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600271.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Labienus Saturus lived 35 years. He lies buried here. Iulia Pupa placed this for her husband out of devotion.
EDCS-65600271.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Lucius Labienus Saturus, with a dedication by his wife Iulia Pupa, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600272.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Laelius Maximus lived 26 years and 48 days. He lies buried here. His mother placed this for her most dutiful and dearest son.
EDCS-65600272.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Laelius Maximus, with a dedication by his mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600276.translation: [..] lived [about] 30 years. She lies buried here. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Lucretius Severus lived 45 years. He lies buried here.
EDCS-65600276.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph, with the legible portion recording the death of Gaius Lucretius Severus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600277.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Antonia Fortunata lived 60 years. She lies buried here. Her husband [dedicated this] for his wife. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Maevius Heros lived 45 years and 2 months. He lies buried here. Maevius Speratus placed this for his son. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Maevia Secunda lived 33 years. She lies buried here. Her dutiful father placed this for his dutiful daughter. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Maevius Aeros lived 114 years. He lies buried here. Lucius Maevius Speratus placed this for his father.
EDCS-65600277.summary: A complex funerary monument recording the deaths of at least four individuals, with dedications by family members, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600278.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Mevius Martialis Nepos, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 50 years and 7 months. He lies buried here. Cocceia Fortunata placed this for her dutiful son.
EDCS-65600278.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Lucius Mevius Martialis Nepos, with a dedication by his mother Cocceia Fortunata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600279.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Mevius Rusticus lived [about] 11 months. He lies buried here. Mevius Victor placed this for his dutiful son.
EDCS-65600279.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Mevius Rusticus, with a dedication by his father Mevius Victor, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600280.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Mevia Paula, daughter of Publius, lived 60 years. She lies buried here.
EDCS-65600280.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Mevia Paula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600281.translation: "To the departed spirits. Mevia Rufina, daughter of Caius, lived 68 years and is buried here. Cornelia Marcella, her daughter, placed this for her pious mother."
EDCS-65600281.summary: A funerary epitaph for Mevia Rufina, who lived 68 years, set up by her daughter Cornelia Marcella, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-65600282.translation: "To the departed spirits. Mevia Veneria lived 35 years. Rutillus placed this for his devoted daughter. She is buried here. // To the departed spirits. Mevia Novella lived 71 years. Rutillus placed this for his devoted wife. She is buried here."
EDCS-65600282.summary: A double funerary epitaph recording Mevia Veneria, who lived 35 years and was placed by Rutillus, and Mevia Novella, who lived 71 years and was also placed by Rutillus, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-65600283.translation: "Abaci I. To the departed spirits. Caius Magnius Felix Ianuarius lived 3 months and is buried here. Lampadarius and Aquilina placed this for their son."
EDCS-65600283.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Magnius Felix Ianuarius, who lived 3 months, set up by his parents Lampadarius and Aquilina, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-65600284.translation: "To the departed spirits. Lucius Magnius Saturninus lived 12 years and 2 months and is here. Magnius, his father, placed this for his devoted son."
EDCS-65600284.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Magnius Saturninus, who lived 12 years and 2 months, set up by his father Magnius, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-65600285.translation: "To the departed spirits. Magnia Quinta, a holy woman, lived 25 years and [3] days and is buried here. // To the departed spirits. Lucius Magnius Murena lived 103 years, 2 months, and 4 days and is buried here. Quintus Magnius Rufinus Fortunatus placed this for his most excellent father."
EDCS-65600285.summary: A double funerary epitaph recording Magnia Quinta, who lived 25 years, and Lucius Magnius Murena, who lived 103 years, the latter set up by his son Quintus Magnius Rufinus Fortunatus, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-65600286.translation: "To the departed spirits. Magnia Successa lived 81 years. Her devoted sons placed this."
EDCS-65600286.summary: A funerary epitaph for Magnia Successa, who lived 81 years, set up by her devoted sons, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-65600287.translation: "To the departed spirits. M[...ilia] Optata, pious, lived 66 years. Aemilius Capito placed this [for her]. She is buried here."
EDCS-65600287.summary: A funerary epitaph for M[...ilia] Optata, who lived 66 years, set up by Aemilius Capito, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-65600288.translation: "To the departed spirits. Publius Marcius Sillanus lived 30 years."
EDCS-65600288.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Publius Marcius Sillanus, who lived 30 years, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-65600289.translation: "To the departed spirits. Publius Mar[cius Ian]uarius."
EDCS-65600289.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Publius Mar[cius Ian]uarius, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-65600290.translation: "To the departed spirits. Maria Faustina lived 75 years and is buried here. Cornia Faustina placed this for her pious grandmother."
EDCS-65600290.summary: A funerary epitaph for Maria Faustina, who lived 75 years, set up by her granddaughter Cornia Faustina, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-65600291.translation: To the Infernal Gods. Silvanus (son of) Mart(a), his good wife, daughter of Marcus. Lived X years, V months. Matu[...]
EDCS-65600291.summary: This epitaph, dedicated to the Infernal Gods, records the death of Marta, wife of Silvanus, and specifies her lifespan.

EDCS-65600292.translation: Sacred to the Infernal Gods. Merula lived XXXVI years; she lies here. Marcus Flavius Secundus placed this for his devout wife.
EDCS-65600292.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the Infernal Gods and records the death of Merula, with her husband Marcus Flavius Secundus erecting the monument.

EDCS-65600293.translation: Victorius Felix lived LXV years and IIII months. Lucius Minucius Fortis lived LXXV years.
EDCS-65600293.summary: This inscription records the lives of two individuals, Victorius Felix and Lucius Minucius Fortis, likely on a shared funerary monument.

EDCS-65600294.translation: Sacred to the Infernal Gods. Munatia lived XLV years; she lies here. Valerianus placed this.
EDCS-65600294.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the Infernal Gods and records the life and burial of Munatia, with Valerianus identified as the erector.

EDCS-65600297.translation: [...] he lies here. Marcus Nonius, son, placed this for his devoted father.
EDCS-65600297.summary: This funerary inscription records Marcus Nonius placing a monument for his devoted father.

EDCS-65600298.translation: Sacred to the Infernal Gods. Quintus Octavius Antonianus lived XXIII years; he lies here. Octavius made this for his devoted father.
EDCS-65600298.summary: This funerary inscription honors Quintus Octavius Antonianus, who lived XXIII years, and was made by his father, also named Octavius.

EDCS-65600299.translation: Sacred to the Infernal Gods. Quintus Octavius Magnus lived II years; his devout mother placed this. Sacred to the Infernal Gods. Quintus Octavius Magnus lived XXXVI years; his devout wife placed this. He lies here.
EDCS-65600299.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary monument with two separate dedications for individuals named Quintus Octavius Magnus, one by his mother and another by his wife.

EDCS-65600301.translation: To the Infernal Gods. Publius Ofillius Processus lived [...] years
EDCS-65600301.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the Infernal Gods and records the name and lifespan of Publius Ofillius Processus.

EDCS-65600302.translation: To the Infernal Gods. For Aulus Orbi Saturnini, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe. [---] Orbiani. lived [...] years
EDCS-65600302.summary: This funerary inscription, dedicated to the Infernal Gods, names Aulus Orbi Saturnini and possibly Orbiani, noting their tribal affiliation and lifespan.

EDCS-65600303.translation: To the Infernal Gods. For Orbia Paulina, his most virtuous wife, daughter of Marcus Ulpius Alexander, who lived XX years, X months. She lies here.
EDCS-65600303.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the Infernal Gods for Orbia Paulina, wife of Marcus Ulpius Alexander, noting her age and burial.

EDCS-65600304.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Otacilia Quartilla lived eighteen years; she rests here.
EDCS-65600304.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Otacilia Quartilla at age 18, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600305.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Asinia Surisca, dutiful, lived thirty-five years; she rests here. Papirius Bassus. Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Papirius Bassus lived forty years; he rests here. Parents and Fortunata placed (this).
EDCS-65600305.summary: A funerary monument commemorating Asinia Surisca (died aged 35) and Quintus Papirius Bassus (died aged 40), erected by their parents and Fortunata, likely Roman Imperial.

EDCS-65600306.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Maria Faustina lived ninety years; she rests here. Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Papirius Victor lived eighty years; he rests here. His sons placed (this) for their dutiful father.
EDCS-65600306.summary: A funerary monument for Maria Faustina (died aged 90) and Quintus Papirius Victor (died aged 80), erected by the latter's sons, likely Roman Imperial.

EDCS-65600308.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Percennius Marcianus lived [age illegible]; he rests here. [Illegible text] placed (this) for his son.
EDCS-65600308.summary: A partially preserved tombstone recording Percennius Marcianus, with a dedication by his son, likely Roman Imperial.

EDCS-65600309.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Petilius Restutus lived fifty years. His wife placed (this) for [her dutiful husband?].
EDCS-65600309.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Petilius Restutus, who lived 50 years, with a partial dedication by his wife, likely Roman Imperial.

EDCS-65600310.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Petronia Honorata lived fifty years; she rests here. Her dutiful sons placed (this) for their pious mother. Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Petronius Antigonus lived sixty years; he rests here. His sons for their most dutiful father, outstanding in his devotion to his children. May your bones rest well. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-65600310.summary: A double tombstone commemorating Petronia Honorata (died aged 50) and Gaius Petronius Antigonus (died aged 60), erected by their sons, likely Roman Imperial.

EDCS-65600313.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. [Illegible name] Phonicus lived thirty-five years. Hellen() his daughter.
EDCS-65600313.summary: A fragmented tombstone recording the death of Phonicus at age 35, with a dedication by his daughter Hellen(), likely Roman Imperial.

EDCS-65600314.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Septimia Ingenua lived seventy-six years; she rests here. Gaius Pompeius Faustus placed (this) for his most dutiful mother. Gaius Pompeius Simplicus, a man of rare innocence, lived forty-two years; he rests here. Septimia Ingenua his wife and Pompeius Faustus his son placed (this) for their parent because of his outstanding kindness.
EDCS-65600314.summary: A complex funerary monument for Septimia Ingenua (died aged 76) and her son Gaius Pompeius Simplicus (died aged 42), erected by the surviving son and wife, likely Roman Imperial.

EDCS-65600315.translation: [Illegible text] he rests here. Pompeia Draca lived twenty-five years; she rests here.
EDCS-65600315.summary: A partial tombstone recording the death of Pompeia Draca at age 25, likely Roman Imperial.

EDCS-65600316.translation: Pompeia Rogata, dutiful, lived seventy years; she rests here.
EDCS-65600316.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Pompeia Rogata at age 70, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600317.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Pompeia lived for 13 years and 4 months; she rests here. Fructosa placed this for her daughter.
EDCS-65600317.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Pompeia, who died at age 13 years and 4 months, set up by her daughter Fructosa, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600320.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Postumius Festus lived for 70 years; he rests here. His sons made this tomb for their dearest father.
EDCS-65600320.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Postumius Festus, who died at age 70, and records that his sons erected the tomb, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600321.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Postumia Buntesia lived for 60 years; she rests here. Her dearest daughter placed this.
EDCS-65600321.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Postumia Buntesia, who died at age 60, set up by her daughter, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600322.translation: Lived for 90 years; she rests here. Postumia Laeta placed this for her pious mother-in-law.
EDCS-65600322.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an unnamed woman who lived to 90, set up by her mother-in-law Postumia Laeta, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600323.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Prescenius Sorex lived for 68 years; his most beloved son placed this. He rests here. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Prescenius Fortunatus lived for 65 years; he placed this for himself while alive. He rests here.
EDCS-65600323.summary: This is a double funerary epitaph recording two individuals named Prescenius: Sorex, for whom his son erected a monument, and Fortunatus, who set up his own tomb while alive; both likely date to the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600324.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Dativa lived for 81 years; she rests here. Racilius Neptunalis placed this for his pious mother. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Racilius Tibullus lived for 93 years; he rests here. Racilius Neptunalis placed this for his pious father.
EDCS-65600324.summary: This is a double funerary epitaph recording Aurelia Dativa, set up by her son Racilius Neptunalis, and Quintus Racilius Tibullus, also set up by his son Racilius Neptunalis, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600325.translation: To the departed spirits. A monument for Roscia Victoria, placed. She lived for 60 years; she rests here.
EDCS-65600325.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Roscia Victoria, who lived to 60, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600327.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Securus lived for 11 years. His parents [placed this] for their son. He rests here.
EDCS-65600327.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Securus, who died at age 11, set up by his parents, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600328.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sempronius Dubitatus lived for 100 years.
EDCS-65600328.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Sempronius Dubitatus, who lived to 100 years, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600330.translation: Lived for 51 years; his wife and his pious sons made [this]. They rest here. And for Septimius, a son, [he] lived for 11 years.
EDCS-65600330.summary: This is a double funerary epitaph recording a man who lived 51 years and his son Septimius who lived 11 years, both erected by the wife and sons, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-65600331.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Servilius Pacatus lived many years; he is buried here. Fruentius placed this for his devoted father.
EDCS-65600331.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Marcus Servilius Pacatus, with the monument dedicated by his son Fruentius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600332.translation: To the departed spirits. Sittius Aectus, his grandson, lived 8 years, 2 months, 21 days. To the departed spirits. Sittia Aecta lived 75 years.
EDCS-65600332.summary: This epitaph commemorates two individuals, Sittius Aectus (a child) and Sittia Aecta (an adult), likely related and buried together, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600333.translation: To the departed spirits. Sittia Gemellina lived 5 years. To the departed spirits. Valeria Victorilla lived 27 years. Marcus Sittius [---]nalis placed this for his wife [---].
EDCS-65600333.summary: This funerary inscription records the deaths of Sittia Gemellina and Valeria Victorilla, with the latter’s monument erected by her husband Marcus Sittius [---]nalis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600334.translation: To the departed spirits. Spesina lived 25 years, 5 months. Sambucius placed this for his most beloved wife.
EDCS-65600334.summary: This funerary monument records the life of Spesina and was dedicated by her husband Sambucius, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600336.translation: To the departed spirits. Stenia Severa lived 40 years; she is buried here. Allius Fortunatus placed this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-65600336.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Stenia Severa, with the monument erected by her husband Allius Fortunatus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600337.translation: To the departed spirits. Tadius Epicte tus lived 46 years; he is buried here. Lucius Tadius Epagatus placed this for his devoted brother.
EDCS-65600337.summary: This funerary monument commemorates Tadius Epicte tus and was erected by his brother Lucius Tadius Epagatus, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600338.translation: To the departed spirits. Terentius Augustalis lived 30 years. His devoted mother placed this; he is buried here.
EDCS-65600338.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Terentius Augustalis, with the monument erected by his mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600339.translation: Tillia Primulla, daughter of Nymphodotus, lived 50 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-65600339.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Tillia Primulla, daughter of Nymphodotus, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600340.translation: To the departed spirits. Titinius Victor lived 70 years. Titinius Victoricus placed this for his devoted father; he is buried here. To the departed spirits. Sallustia Quirilla lived 60 years. Titinius Victoricus placed this for his devoted mother; she is buried here. To the departed spirits. Aemilia Maxima lived 50 years. Iulia Felicitas placed this for her devoted mother; she is buried here. To the departed spirits. Aemilia Castula lived 6 years. Iulia Felicitas placed this for her devoted sister; she is buried here. To the departed spirits. Titinius Victoricus lived 5 years, 10 months. His devoted father placed this; he is buried here. To the departed spirits. Titinius Donatus lived two years. His father placed this for his son; he is buried here.
EDCS-65600340.summary: This is a multi-part funerary monument recording the lives and burials of at least five individuals from the Titinius and Aemilia families, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600341.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Trebbins Felix lived 31 years; he is buried here. Aemilia Amata, his devoted wife, [placed this] for her husband.
EDCS-65600341.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Caius Trebbins Felix, with the monument erected by his wife Aemilia Amata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600342.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Tullius Aquilinus lived for 15 years. He lies here.
EDCS-65600342.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Marcus Tullius Aquilinus at the age of 15, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600343.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Turrania Veneria Augustia lived for one year and 3 months. She lies here. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Turranius Sucessus lived for 7 months. Turranius Nasir lived for 14 years. P. T. A. VIII. He lies here. Turranius Saturnius and Valeria Donata, parents, set [this up].
EDCS-65600343.summary: A complex funerary monument recording the deaths of Turrania Veneria Augustia (1 year, 3 months), Turranius Sucessus (7 months), and Turranius Nasir (14 years), with parents Turranius Saturnius and Valeria Donata setting up the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600344.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Valerius Felix lies here. He lived for 18 years. Publius Valerius Fortunatus, his father, set this up for his dutiful son. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Secundula lies here. She lived for 30 years. Publius Valerius Fortunatus set this up for his most dutiful wife. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Valerius Fortunatus, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, lived for 87 years. He lies here. His sons set this up for their dutiful father.
EDCS-65600344.summary: A family funerary monument commemorating three individuals: Publius Valerius Felix (18), his wife Iulia Secundula (30), and the patriarch Publius Valerius Fortunatus (87), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600345.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Valerius Restutus lived for 32 years. He lies here. For a good friend.
EDCS-65600345.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Valerius Restutus at the age of 32, set up by a friend, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600346.translation: Lucius Valerius Vitalis, son of Lucius, lies here. Lucius Valerius, his father (?), made [this].
EDCS-65600346.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius Vitalis, with his father Lucius mentioned as the maker, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600348.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Varius Delphicus lived for 48 years. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Pompeia Calla lived for 94 years. A son set this up for his parents. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Varia Prisca lived for 65 years. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Septiminius Aprilis lived for 53 years.
EDCS-65600348.summary: A funerary monument recording the deaths of four individuals: Varius Delphicus (48), Pompeia Calla (94), Varia Prisca (65), and Septiminius Aprilis (53), with a son dedicating the monument to his parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600349.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Vereius Sebosus lived for 60 years. Vereius Felix set this up for his son. He lies here.
EDCS-65600349.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Publius Vereius Sebosus at the age of 60, set up by his son Vereius Felix, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600350.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Victorina lived for 7 years and 7 months. She lies here. Dutiful parents made [this] for their daughter. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. [Name illegible] lived for 4 years, 5 months, and 15 days. She lies here. Dutiful parents made [this] for their daughter.
EDCS-65600350.summary: A funerary monument for two young girls, Victorina (7 years, 7 months) and another unnamed child (4 years, 5 months, 15 days), both with epitaphs set up by their devoted parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600351.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Saturus lived for 35 years and 10 months. He lies here. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Vinnius Felix lived for 7 years, 8 months, and 20 days. He lies here. His mother set [this] up for her son.
EDCS-65600351.summary: A funerary monument for two individuals: Saturus (35 years, 10 months) and Vinnius Felix (7 years, 8 months, 20 days), with the latter's epitaph set up by his mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600352.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Surus Vinius Honoratus lived for 33 years, 11 months, and 6 days. Helvia Fortunata set [this] up for her dearest husband. He lies here.
EDCS-65600352.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Surus Vinius Honoratus, who lived 33 years, 11 months, and 6 days, dedicated by his wife Helvia Fortunata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65600353.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Vinnia Bocchis lived for sixteen years; she lies here. Vinnia Urbana, her most dutiful mother, placed this for her most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-65600353.summary: A funerary epitaph for Vinnia Bocchis, who died at sixteen, set up by her mother, Vinnia Urbana.

EDCS-65600354.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Volussena Salsula lived for eighty-five years; she lies here.
EDCS-65600354.summary: A funerary epitaph for Volussena Salsula, who died at eighty-five.

EDCS-65600357.translation: She lies here. [Son]inus and Maximianus placed this for their pious mother.
EDCS-65600357.summary: A funerary epitaph where sons named Maximianus and possibly Soninus placed a monument for their mother.

EDCS-68300016.translation: Sanion Anneianus, also known as Lucernarius (lamp-maker).
EDCS-68300016.summary: A funerary epitaph or designation for Sanion Anneianus, identified by his occupation as a lamp-maker.

EDCS-69000081.translation: Lucius lies here, and also his pious wife Iulia, who lived with him in equal age and love. For they delighted the citizens with their harmonious life. This couple, having ended their lives with Iulia's death, for she was the first and most dutiful, Lucius himself follows. May the remaining ashes of these two rest with flowers, and may the earth lightly cover their bones.
EDCS-69000081.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius and his wife Iulia, celebrating their harmonious and loving marriage and their shared death.

EDCS-69200158.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, father of the country, consul, and of Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, our Emperor's wife, and of the entire divine household, the citizens of the municipality of Aelius Hadrianus Thamubaiensis dedicated the Gordian baths by decree of the decurions on the sixteenth day before the Kalends of January, with Arrianus and Papus as consuls.
EDCS-69200158.summary: An official dedication by the citizens of Aelius Hadrianus Thamubaiensis of the Gordian baths in honor of Emperor Gordian III and Empress Tranquillina, dated to 19 December 217 CE (error in original text date/consuls may exist).

EDCS-69200159.translation: To good memory. Donatus, vicarius (subordinate official), lived forty years.
EDCS-69200159.summary: A funerary epitaph for Donatus, a vicarius, who died at forty years of age.

EDCS-69200160.translation: Surula, a slave (?), lived for one year and six months. Sacred to the departed spirits.
EDCS-69200160.summary: A funerary epitaph for a slave named Surula, who died at one year and six months old.

EDCS-69200161.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Fortis lived for eighty-seven years.
EDCS-69200161.summary: A funerary epitaph for Fortis, who died at eighty-seven years of age.

EDCS-69200162.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Aurelius Saturninus lived [...]
EDCS-69200162.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Aurelius Saturninus, with the duration of his life missing.

EDCS-69200166.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Faus[t]us Roga[t]us lived eighty-one years. His bones are here.
EDCS-69200166.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death and burial of Faustus Rogatus, who lived eighty-one years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200485.translation: To Gnaeus Blaesius Atticus, son of Gnaeus, of the Galeria tribe, a veteran. He served twenty-two years and lived sixty-five years. He is buried here. Attius Sedatus, Publius Masurius Evictus, and Titus Masurius Optatus, the heirs, saw to its construction.
EDCS-69200485.summary: A funerary monument dedicated to Gnaeus Blaesius Atticus, a veteran who served for twenty-two years and lived to sixty-five, erected by his heirs, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200486.translation: Hirtilia Sabina, freedwoman, and Lelia lived fifty-two years. She is buried here. Quintus Euhodius Marcellus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, centuria of Publius Iustus Hospes, and Ienticosus [---] placed this.
EDCS-69200486.summary: A funerary epitaph for Hirtilia Sabina and Lelia, erected by soldiers of the III Augusta legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200487.translation: Lucius Valerius Pastor, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, a veteran. He lived one hundred and eleven years. He is buried here. His son, Lucius Valerius Callistus, placed this.
EDCS-69200487.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius Pastor, a veteran who lived to an extraordinary age of 111, erected by his son, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200488.translation: [---]nius. Proculus, a soldier [of the III Augusta legion?], served in the XXII legion Alexandria for six years and in the III Augusta [---] lived forty-five years. [---] killed. He is buried [here].
EDCS-69200488.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph for a soldier who served in at least two legions, the XXII Alexandria and the III Augusta, and died violently at the age of forty-five, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200489.translation: [---], an evocatus of Augustus. He lived fifty years and served eighteen years. His heir made this at his own expense.
EDCS-69200489.summary: A funerary epitaph for an evocatus (a veteran recalled to service) of Augustus, who lived fifty years and served eighteen, with the monument erected by his heir, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200493.translation: [---] of the III Augusta legion. He made [this?]. He is buried here.
EDCS-69200493.summary: A very fragmentary funerary epitaph, possibly recording a soldier of the III Augusta legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200499.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Saturninus, son of Cabdollo, piously lived eighty-five years. He is buried here.
EDCS-69200499.summary: A funerary epitaph for Saturninus, son of Cabdollo, who lived eighty-five years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200503.translation: Sacred to Jupiter the August. For the health of the Emperor Caesar Traian[us Hadrianus?].
EDCS-69200503.summary: A dedication to Jupiter for the health of Emperor Trajanus Hadrianus (or a similar emperor), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200505.translation: Titus Statilius Taurus, consul for the second time.
EDCS-69200505.summary: A dedication or record of the consulship of Titus Statilius Taurus, likely from the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-69200512.translation: Noble Caesar Augustus, prince of youth, and Marcia Octacilia Severa Augusta, mother of Caesar, mother of the camps, of the senate, and of the fatherland.
EDCS-69200512.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Philip the Arab, his wife Marcia Otacilia Severa, and their son Philip II, likely dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-69200516.translation: Mucia Redemta, daughter of Lucius, of the Via tribe, pious, lived for 14 years, was buried here.
EDCS-69200516.summary: This is a tombstone for a young girl named Mucia Redemta, who died at the age of 14, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-69200518.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Probus, the Invincible, Pious, Happy, Augustus.
EDCS-69200518.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Probus, likely a part of a larger honorary inscription, dating to his reign in the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-69200520.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, grandson of the deified Marcus Antonius Gordianus, nephew of the deified Antonius Gordianus, most brave, most happy, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-69200520.summary: This inscription records the full titulature of Emperor Gordian III, detailing his honors and powers during his reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-69200531.translation: Valentius lived in peace for almost 80 years.
EDCS-69200531.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a man named Valentius, recording his peaceful life and approximate age at death, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-69200539.translation: To Perpetuus, Emperor Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, Invincible, Pious, Happy, our Augustus.
EDCS-69200539.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Aurelian, acknowledging his titles and divine favor, likely from the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-69200540.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Constantinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-69200540.summary: This inscription honors the Caesar Flavius Claudius Constantinus, likely Constantine I before his elevation to Augustus, dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-69200550.translation: Pompeia Secundula, pious, lived for 26 years, is placed here. // Nonia Bonosa, daughter of Faustus, pious, lived for 28 years, is placed here. // Areia Secunda, daughter of Laetus, pious, lived for 55 years, is placed here.
EDCS-69200550.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lives and ages of three women: Pompeia Secundula, Nonia Bonosa, and Areia Secunda, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-69200551.summary: This is a brief funerary epitaph, possibly for a Christian, indicating the deceased was honored and at peace, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-69200555.translation: To Gaius Flavius Galerius Constantius and Gaius Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-69200555.summary: This inscription dedicates honors to Galerius and Maximian, likely as Caesars in the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-69200567.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Julia Regula, also known as Stolata, lived 63 years and 6 months; she is buried here.
EDCS-69200567.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name, aliases, lifespan, and burial place of Julia Regula, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69900164.summary: A fragmentary inscription likely recording ownership, possibly of a grave or object, belonging to Caius Fadius Secundus, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70200497.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian, the Unconquered Augustus, Pious, Fortunate, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, proconsul; and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Herculius, the Unconquered [Augustus], [consul]...
EDCS-70200497.summary: A dedication honoring the emperors Diocletian and Maximian, detailing their titles and offices, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-70200503.translation: To our lords Emperors [Gaius] Valerius Diocletian and [Marcus Aurelius Valerius] Maximian and [Maximian]...
EDCS-70200503.summary: A dedication to the emperors Diocletian and Maximian, likely part of a larger inscription honoring their joint rule, late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-70200504.translation: [...] to Aurelius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-70200504.summary: A dedication to the Caesars Constantius and Maximian, likely a continuation of imperial dedications, dating to the Tetrarchic period (late 3rd/early 4th century CE).

EDCS-70300042.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Maevius Valerianus, also called Moflontius, is buried here; he lived 35 years and 3 months.
EDCS-70300042.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name, alias, and lifespan of Lucius Maevius Valerianus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700100.translation: My dear brother, whom [3] knows, send [3] with him what [3] have, [send] ten denarii. I ask that they be directed to me because I hope [3] will pay [3] from the exotic [3] and I order [3] to trade [3] for me from the oil [3] and ask [3]...
EDCS-70700100.summary: A personal letter requesting money and business instructions, written on a wax tablet or similar medium, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71100054.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. He is buried here, born from noble blood, Burpurius, superior in piety to all. He had recently completed sixty years of life, but now he obtains eternal rest in a peaceful dwelling, pleasing to the gods above, thus to the gods below. He is buried here.
EDCS-71100054.summary: A funerary epitaph for Burpurius, praising his noble birth and piety, and noting his death at sixty years of age, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71200007.summary: A short epitaph identifying Marcellina and mentioning her resting in peace, likely from a Christian context, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71200402.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Optatilia Fadilla, the pious, lived 11 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-71200402.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name, epithet, lifespan, and burial place of Optatilia Fadilla, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71200604.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-71200604.translation: For the safety of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and his children, Julia Paula Laenatiana, for the honor of her perpetual priesthood, [built] the temple of Minerva on private land and for its dedication gave gifts to the district and the community and the decurions, and to the whole people a gymnasium and a feast, overseen by the curators Asicius Adiutor and Marcus Terentius [---].
EDCS-71200604.summary: This inscription records a woman, Julia Paula Laenatiana, building a temple to Minerva and providing gifts and entertainment for the community and its officials, likely dating to the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-71200605.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-71200605.translation: For the safety of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and his children, Iulia Paula Laenati[ana], for the honor of her perpetual priesthood, [built] the temple of Minerva on private land and for its dedication gave gifts to the district and the community and the decurions, and to the whole people a gymnasium and a feast, overseen by the curators Asicio Adiuto[re] and Marcus Terentio Gell[io...].
EDCS-71200605.summary: This is a partially reconstructed version of the previous inscription, recording the same dedication by Julia Paula Laenatiana of a temple to Minerva and community gifts, likely during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-71300300.text_edition: \n [[]] no Casto tribuno militum legionis III Augustae cives b VI Lepcitani[3]\n
EDCS-71300300.translation: [...] to Castus, tribune of the soldiers of the III Augusta legion, the citizens of Lepcis B[e]n[eficiarii...].
EDCS-71300300.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a dedication to a military tribune, Castus, by citizens identified as Lepcitani, likely from Lepcis Magna.

EDCS-71300302.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Aelia Victoria lived for 40 years, cared for by her father Aelius Fortunatus.
EDCS-71300302.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Aelia Victoria at the age of 40, with her father Aelius Fortunatus listed as the one who set up the monument.

EDCS-71300303.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Aulus Cassius Sentius lived for 50 years.
EDCS-71300303.summary: This fragmentary tombstone inscription records the death of Aulus Cassius Sentius at the age of 50.

EDCS-71300306.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Valgia Ingenua, most faithful, lived for 60 years.
EDCS-71300306.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Valgia Ingenua at the age of 60, with an epithet emphasizing her faithfulness.

EDCS-71300307.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Apollonius, son of Vindicis, lived for 28 years.
EDCS-71300307.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Apollonius, son of Vindicis, at the age of 28.

EDCS-71300308.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Donata lived for 22 years. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-71300308.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Donata at the age of 22 and includes a common Latin funerary wish.

EDCS-71300309.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Fortunata lived for 2 years.
EDCS-71300309.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Fortunata at the age of 2.

EDCS-71300310.translation: Sacred to the departed gods, to the memory of Gaia Scuptis, who lived for 2[...].
EDCS-71300310.summary: This fragmentary tombstone inscription records the death of Gaia Scuptis, with the age at death partially lost.

EDCS-71300311.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed. Iovinus lived 10 months and 7 days.
EDCS-71300311.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the short life of Iovinus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300312.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed. Pious Saturninus lived 47 years; his son Nabor [erected this].
EDCS-71300312.summary: A funerary epitaph for Saturninus, set up by his son Nabor, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300315.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Gemella lived 60 years.
EDCS-71300315.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelia Gemella, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300316.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed. Cilia Fanent lived 20 years.
EDCS-71300316.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cilia Fanent, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300317.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Fuscha Audnia lived 80 years, a most dutiful and most sweet [person].
EDCS-71300317.summary: A funerary epitaph for Fuscha Audnia, describing her character, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300318.translation: Gaius Sempronius Auricinus lived 45 years; this [monument] his good son made for his parents.
EDCS-71300318.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Sempronius Auricinus, erected by his son for his parents, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300319.translation: Quintus Menius Saturninus lived 85 years and 1 day.
EDCS-71300319.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Menius Saturninus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300320.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed. Iulius Italicus lived 22 years, and his daughter Maia lived 1 month.
EDCS-71300320.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulius Italicus and his daughter Maia, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72000079.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici Maximi Britanici Maximi filius divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini Pii pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae abnepos Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Britanico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis XVIIII imperatori III consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli miliarius II\n
EDCS-72000079.translation: To Imperator Caesar, son of the divine Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, grandson of the divine Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus and the divine Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, supreme Parthicus, Britannicus, Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 19th time, imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, builder of the 2nd milestone.
EDCS-72000079.summary: An imperial titulature inscription for Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), listing his titles and lineage, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-72000084.text_edition: \n pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis consul pater patriae miliarium XXXVIIII\n
EDCS-72000084.translation: Chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, builder of the 39th milestone.
EDCS-72000084.summary: An abbreviated imperial titulature or a dedication related to a milestone, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72000085.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Britanici maximi filio et divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Britannico maximo Germanico maximo tribunicia potestate XVIIII imperatori III consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli miliarium numero LXXXIIII\n
EDCS-72000085.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, and great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 19th time, consul for the 3rd time, emperor for the 4th time, father of the country, proconsul, milemarker 84.
EDCS-72000085.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Caracalla, indicating a distance of 84 miles.

EDCS-72000088.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus miliarium numero L\n
EDCS-72000088.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, milemarker 50.
EDCS-72000088.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Gordian III, indicating a distance of 50 miles.

EDCS-72000091.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici \n
EDCS-72000091.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus.
EDCS-72000091.summary: This fragment likely belongs to a longer dedication, possibly a milestone or honorific monument, from the Severan period.

EDCS-72000094.summary: This is a fragment of a milestone inscription, likely recording a distance and referencing a proconsul.

EDCS-72000095.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Septimius Severus.
EDCS-72000095.summary: This is a fragment of a dedication from the Severan period, likely a milestone or honorary inscription.

EDCS-72000099.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Brittanici maximi filio divi Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Brittanico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis XIX imperatori III consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli XXXII\n
EDCS-72000099.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 19th time, emperor for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, proconsul, milemarker 32.
EDCS-72000099.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Caracalla, indicating a distance of 32 miles and detailing his extensive titulature.

EDCS-72000101.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Brittanici maximi filio divi Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti M(arco) Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Brittanico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis XIX imperatori III consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli miliarium CXLIII\n
EDCS-72000101.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 19th time, emperor for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, proconsul, milemarker 143.
EDCS-72000101.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Caracalla, indicating a distance of 143 miles and detailing his extensive titulature.

EDCS-72000103.text_edition: \n Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Brittanico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis XIX imperatori consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli numero XLVIIII\n
EDCS-72000103.translation: To the Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 19th time, emperor for the 4th time, consul, father of the country, proconsul, number 49.
EDCS-72000103.summary: This is a milestone inscription likely from the reign of Caracalla, indicating a distance of 49 miles and listing his honors.

EDCS-72400115.translation: To Marcus Aelius Aurelius Verus Caesar, son of the deified Augustus Pius, with tribunician power, consul for the 2nd time, at public expense by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-72400115.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcus Aurelius (as Caesar), sponsored by local town councillors, likely for a public work or monument.

EDCS-72500070.translation: Cornelia Fortuna, daughter of Spurius, priestess of the Augusta, pious, lived 45 years, lies here. May your bones rest well. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-72500070.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Cornelia Fortunata, a priestess, recording her name, parentage, religious role, lifespan, and offering a common epitaph.

EDCS-72600340.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Marcus Novius, son of Titus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72700002.translation: To our saviors, the Augusti and Caesar, Egnatuleius Crescens, a most distinguished man and governor of Numidia.
EDCS-72700002.summary: This inscription honors the emperors and is dedicated by Egnatuleius Crescens, governor of Numidia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72700005.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, conqueror of the Germans, the Sarmatians, and the Dacians, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the third time, acclaimed imperator for the seventh time. [Gaius] Julius Verus Maxi[minus...
EDCS-72700005.summary: This is a fragment of an imperial titulature inscription for Maximinus Thrax and his son, likely dating to his co-reign and the period of his third tribunician power.

EDCS-72700006.translation: ...holding tribunician power for the third time, acclaimed imperator for the fifth time, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, conqueror of the Germans, the Sarmatians, and the Dacians, restored the road from Carthage up to the borders of the province of Numidia, which had been corrupted and dilapidated through long neglect.
EDCS-72700006.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Carthage and Numidia, undertaken by Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximinus Caesar, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-72700007.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, grandson of the deified Gordian...
EDCS-72700007.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription mentioning Emperor Gordian III, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-72700008.translation: To our lord, the Emperor Constantius Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-72700008.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantius II, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-72700010.translation: To Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-72700010.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Probus, likely dating to his fourth consulship and reign in the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-72700046.translation: Emperor Caesar, grandson of the deified Nerva, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the seventh time, consul for the third time...
EDCS-72700046.summary: This is a fragment of an imperial titulature inscription for Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to his seventh tribunician power and third consulship, in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-72700081.translation: To Faustina Augusta, daughter of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland...
EDCS-72700081.summary: This inscription honors Empress Faustina I, wife of Antoninus Pius, likely from the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-72700083.translation: Legio III Augusta demarcated with Gaius Vibius Marso, proconsul, for the third time: 70 quinarii in the front rank, 30 decumani, 350 beyond the dividing line.
EDCS-72700083.summary: This inscription records a financial demarcation by Legio III Augusta and proconsul Gaius Vibius Marso, likely from the 2nd century CE in Roman Africa.

EDCS-72700085.translation: Pompeianus the faithful lived in peace for 8 years, laid to rest on the Ides of May in the 9th indiction.
EDCS-72700085.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age and burial date of Pompeianus.

EDCS-72700086.translation: Pompeianus the faithful lived for 1 year and 6 months in peace, rested on the 11th day before the Ides of December in the 2nd indiction.
EDCS-72700086.summary: This funerary inscription records the age and burial date of Pompeianus.

EDCS-72700087.translation: Pompeianus the faithful lived in peace for 45 years, [was buried?] the day before the Nones of May in the [?] indiction.
EDCS-72700087.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the age and burial date of Pompeianus.

EDCS-72700097.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Aurelius Restutus lived for 35 years.
EDCS-72700097.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age of Lucius Aurelius Restutus.

EDCS-72700098.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Iunius Rogatianus lived for 3 years, is buried here.
EDCS-72700098.summary: This funerary inscription records the age and burial place of Marcus Iunius Rogatianus.

EDCS-72700099.translation: [Sacred to the departed spirits] Avielius Maximus lived for 18 years, is buried here.
EDCS-72700099.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the age and burial place of Avielius Maximus.

EDCS-72700100.translation: Sextus Marius Pudens, son of Titus, lived for 21 years, is buried here.
EDCS-72700100.summary: This funerary inscription records the age and burial place of Sextus Marius Pudens.

EDCS-72700101.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Donatus, son of Siddinis, lived for 10 years, is buried here, interred.
EDCS-72700101.summary: This funerary inscription records the age and burial place of Donatus.

EDCS-72700102.translation: Hotmilcot Arinis [daughter?] of Labio lived for 71 years, is buried here.
EDCS-72700102.summary: This funerary inscription records the age and burial place of Hotmilcot Arinis.

EDCS-72700103.translation: Munda son of Aechanes lived for 75 years, is buried here.
EDCS-72700103.summary: This funerary inscription records the age and burial place of Munda.

EDCS-72700104.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sattaras, son of Alauchus, lived 61 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-72700104.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, parentage, age, and burial of Sattaras.

EDCS-72700105.translation: Amot, daughter of the dutiful Rusticus, daughter of Asmunis Macas, lived 26 years.
EDCS-72700105.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, parentage, and age of Amot.

EDCS-72700106.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Coloapis, son of Fronto, the dutiful, lived 83 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-72700106.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, parentage, age, and burial of Coloapis.

EDCS-72700107.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Tulius Rogatus lived 68 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-72700107.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, age, and burial of Tulius Rogatus.

EDCS-72700108.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Valeria Lathinia lived 51 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-72700108.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, age, and burial of Valeria Lathinia.

EDCS-72700109.translation: Publius Aufidius Saturninus, a veteran, saw to the construction of two columns at his own expense.
EDCS-72700109.summary: A dedication recording the commissioning of two columns by Publius Aufidius Saturninus, a veteran, at his own expense.

EDCS-72700111.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Nonia Aurelia lived 82 years.
EDCS-72700111.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name and age of Nonia Aurelia.

EDCS-72700112.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Modia Victoria, the dutiful, lived 31 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-72700112.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, epithet, age, and burial of Modia Victoria.

EDCS-72700113.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Baricbal Prima Serruma, the dutiful, lived 40 years.
EDCS-72700113.summary: A funerary inscription recording the names, epithet, and age of Baricbal Prima Serruma, with a lacuna at the end.

EDCS-72700116.translation: By decree of the decurions, at public expense, for Domitia Aurelia Faustina, daughter of Aurelius Caesar.
EDCS-72700116.summary: A public dedication honoring Domitia Aurelia Faustina, daughter of Aurelius Caesar, funded by public money by order of the town council.

EDCS-73400410.summary: This inscription records a personal note about a person named Firma who lived.

EDCS-73400454.translation: Sacred to Victory Augusta. Marcus Junius Crescens, son of Africanus, a municipal official holding the quinquennial office, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-73400454.summary: This is a dedication to Victory Augusta by Marcus Junius Crescens, likely a magistrate, who fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-73400455.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus. Marcus Junius Crescens, son of Africanus, a magistrate holding the quinquennial office, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-73400455.summary: This is a dedication to Mars Augustus by Marcus Junius Crescens, likely a magistrate, who fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-73900281.translation: Dedicated to the departed spirits. Felix, son of Macrinus and Menotis, lived piously for 73 years. Likewise Maior, daughter of Extricatus, lived piously for 68 years.
EDCS-73900281.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Felix and Maior, recording their pious lives and ages.

EDCS-73900282.translation: Dedicated to the departed spirits. Aulus Egnatius Mulinus lived piously for 45 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-73900282.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aulus Egnatius Mulinus, recording his pious life and age.

EDCS-73900284.translation: From the workshop of Modius Julianus.
EDCS-73900284.summary: This inscription indicates a place of origin or ownership, likely related to a workshop or business.

EDCS-73900285.translation: To Jupiter Augustus sacred. To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, son of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Nerva. [Gaetulicus] promised and contributed 2,000 sesterces and an additional 4,000 sesterces from the office of [decurion?] for the construction of the temple with a marble statue, marble columns, and all its furnishings, and the steps of the platform, and beyond that, for two [columns?], he promised and contributed, and on the day of dedication and the celebrations, he gave gifts to the decurions for the dedication, a gymnasium, and games to the entire populace.
EDCS-73900285.summary: This complex inscription records a large dedication by Gaetulicus to Jupiter Augustus and likely commemorates an imperial visit or significant public works, including a temple, gymnasium, and games, under Commodus.

EDCS-73900431.translation: To the most peaceful Emperor, our Augustus, Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Invictus Pius Felix. 94.
