EDCS-73900431.summary: This is an acclamation or dedication to Emperor Aurelian, likely dating to his reign.

EDCS-74000013.translation: For the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and [Publius] Septimius [Geta] Caesar Augustus and Julia Augusta, mother of the camp. A cistern.
EDCS-74000013.summary: This inscription records the construction of a cistern under the joint rule of Septimius Severus, Caracalla, Geta, and Julia Domna.

EDCS-74000023.summary: This inscription appears to be in a language other than Latin or is heavily abbreviated/corrupted, and its meaning is unclear.

EDCS-74100259.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus. For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Adiabenicus, [---]. [---]. The curia of Mars, they built and dedicated a shrine from the ground up and a statue with their own money, a place having been given [---].
EDCS-74100259.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a shrine and a statue to Mars Augustus and for the emperor's safety, funded by the curia Martis, likely dating to the reign of Septimius Severus.

EDCS-74200160.translation: Faunus; Oricimuicus; Leontius; Spina; [---]ecta; Pinnatus; Auspicator; Eventus.
EDCS-74200160.summary: This inscription is a list of names, possibly belonging to a group or individuals commemorated on a monument, with no further context provided.

EDCS-74700031.translation: By the authority of Quintus Junius Rusticus, prefect of the city, a man of consular rank. (Weight) ten pounds.
EDCS-74700031.summary: This inscription records a decree or authorization made by Quintus Junius Rusticus, prefect of the city, and a weight of ten pounds, likely related to official business or a levy.

EDCS-74700522.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus(?), great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan and deified Nerva, [son's son] Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus [Germanicus].
EDCS-74700522.summary: This is a lengthy imperial titulature inscribed on a monument, glorifying Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Caracalla) by listing his divine ancestors and his own titles, dating to his reign.

EDCS-74700526.translation: Philip [with tribunician power, consul designate for the third time, father of the country, proconsul] and Marcus Julius Philippus nobilissimus Caesar Augustus, prince of the youth, and Marcia Otacilia Severa Augusta, mother of the Emperor Caesar Augustus, and of the camp, and of the senate, and of the country. Seventy-eight.
EDCS-74700526.summary: This inscription lists the titles and honors of the emperors Philip I and his son Philip II, along with Empress Otacilia Severa, possibly on a public monument or milestone, dating to their co-reign.

EDCS-75200097.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name and age of the deceased, Bernus.

EDCS-75200099.summary: This is likely a tombstone inscription recording the name and age of the deceased, Flabia.

EDCS-75200103.summary: This inscription appears to be a tombstone marking the burial of Auo Anibon, possibly indicating their well-being or a dedicatory offering.

EDCS-75200105.translation: Selhan lived 61 years; Flabia lived 75 years.
EDCS-75200105.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the names and ages of two individuals, Selhan and Flabia.

EDCS-75200107.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name and age of the deceased, Ariston.

EDCS-75200108.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of an individual.

EDCS-75200110.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Balerianus.

EDCS-75200115.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Galeria.

EDCS-75200116.translation: Luna lived [X] years. Leptana lived [X] years.
EDCS-75200116.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespans of two individuals, Luna and Leptana.

EDCS-75200117.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Demetrius.

EDCS-75200119.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Domitius.

EDCS-75200120.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Nearca.

EDCS-75200122.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Rogata.

EDCS-75200123.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Iambart.

EDCS-75200124.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Iambart.

EDCS-75200128.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespans of two individuals, Arisus and another person whose name is lost.

EDCS-75200129.summary: This epitaph records the exceptionally long lifespan of an individual named Therus.

EDCS-75200132.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of a young girl named Peregrinia.

EDCS-75200133.translation: Saturna died before the third month.
EDCS-75200133.summary: This epitaph records the death of Saturna, indicating she died before the third month of a year.

EDCS-75200134.summary: This epitaph records the age of an individual identified as 'grandfather' at the time of their death.

EDCS-75200138.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of an individual named Mercuras.

EDCS-75200139.summary: This epitaph records the partial lifespan of an individual named Irus, with the exact age lost.

EDCS-75200143.summary: This epitaph commemorates Agag, describing him as an innocent boy.

EDCS-75200144.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of an individual whose name is lost.

EDCS-75200145.summary: This epitaph records the age of an individual identified as 'grandfather Amonis' at the time of their death.

EDCS-75200146.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of the deceased, Kallios, at death.

EDCS-75200148.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Avolim.

EDCS-75300322.translation: To our lords Emperors Flavius Valerius Constantinus and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most pious, fortunate, unconquered Augusti, when they were consuls for the fifth time.
EDCS-75300322.summary: A dedication to Emperors Constantinus and Maximianus, dating to a period when they both held the consulship for the fifth time.

EDCS-75300323.translation: Of Valerius Pudentis Marsilliani, a man of clarissimal rank.
EDCS-75300323.summary: A fragmentary inscription likely recording the name and status of Valerius Pudentis Marsilliani.

EDCS-75300325.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Augustus, greatest victor in Parthia, greatest victor in Britain, greatest victor in Germany, holder of tribunician power for the 18th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, restored.
EDCS-75300325.summary: An imperial inscription detailing the titles and achievements of Emperor Caracalla, indicating he restored something, likely a building or monument.

EDCS-75300331.translation: To our Emperors Caesars Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Invictus Augustus, four times, and Marcus Aurelius.
EDCS-75300331.summary: A dedication to Emperors Diocletian and Maximian (whose name is only partially preserved), referring to their multiple consulships.

EDCS-75300332.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, greatest victor in Parthia, greatest victor in Britain, greatest victor in Germany, holder of tribunician power for the 19th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, established.
EDCS-75300332.summary: An imperial inscription detailing the titles and achievements of Emperor Caracalla, indicating he established something, likely a building or monument.

EDCS-75300335.translation: To our most pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus Flavius Valerius Constantinus, born for the good of the state.
EDCS-75300335.summary: A dedication to Emperor Constantine, praising him as beneficial to the state.

EDCS-75300336.translation: To our lords Emperors Gaius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felicis Invictissimis Augusti, when they were consuls.
EDCS-75300336.summary: A dedication to Emperors Diocletian and Maximian, noting their consulship.

EDCS-75300337.translation: To our lord Licinius Licinianus Pius Felicis Augustus.
EDCS-75300337.summary: A dedication to Emperor Licinius, praising his piety and good fortune.

EDCS-75300338.translation: To Flavius Valerius Crispinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-75300338.summary: This inscription is a dedication, likely on a monument, to Flavius Valerius Crispinus, who held the title of Caesar and is described as most noble, probably dating to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-75300339.translation: To our lord Flavius Julius Constantius, most noble and most flourishing Caesar, born for the good of the state.
EDCS-75300339.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantius I, referring to him as 'Caesar' and praising his birth for the benefit of the public, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-75300341.translation: Crementius and his associates to the god Neptune, built a temple at their own expense, with Tertius as master, urging them on.
EDCS-75300341.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple to Neptune, built at private expense by Crementius and his associates, under the supervision of Tertius as magister, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300350.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, grandson of the deified Gordian, son of the deified Gordian's sister, Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus.
EDCS-75300350.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Gordian III, listing his imperial titles and ancestry, dating to his reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-75300353.translation: To Aulus Gabinius Dato, son of Aulus, of the Arnensis tribe, flamen of the deified Titus, enrolled in the five decuries by Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, patron of the village and of the city, the village and city of Thugga, placed (this monument) with their own money, with [---] supervising and dedicating.
EDCS-75300353.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the people of Thugga in honor of Aulus Gabinius Dato, who held significant civic and religious positions, including flamen and a position granted by Emperor Hadrian, dating to the Hadrianic period (mid-2nd century CE).

EDCS-75300356.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Chrysanthus, freedman of the emperor, chief steward, made this for his well-deserving friend Titus Flavius Venustus, freedman of Emperor Domitian Augustus, who lived eighty years without torture.
EDCS-75300356.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Venustus, a freedman of Emperor Domitian, who lived to 80, with the monument erected by his friend Chrysanthus, also an imperial freedman and steward, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-75300359.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus, Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the second time, father of the country, consul designate for the second time, proconsul; and Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus.
EDCS-75300359.summary: This inscription is a fragment of an imperial titulature, listing the titles of Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, dating to their joint reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-75300361.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Victor, always Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 6th time, acclaimed emperor for the 14th time, father of the country, proconsul. The council of the municipality of Zicchilium placed (this) through its liberality, with Marcus Lutatius Luzanus, son of Marcus, curator of public affairs, overseeing and dedicating.
EDCS-75300361.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the municipality of Zicchilium to Emperor Constantine I, listing his extensive titles and powers, dating to his later reign, around 324-325 CE.

EDCS-75400168.translation: To the prince of youth, Flavius Valerius Constantius, most noble Caesar. The colony Aelia Hadriana Augusta of the Bullenses Regii, devoted to his divine majesty and his greatness.
EDCS-75400168.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the colony of Aelia Hadriana Augusta to Flavius Valerius Constantius, honoring him as 'prince of youth' and 'most noble Caesar', likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-75700194.translation: Titia, daughter of Namphama, pious, lived 60 years. Here she lies. May your bones rest well, may the earth lie light upon you.
EDCS-75700194.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titia, daughter of Namphama, who lived to 60 years, including common Latin funerary formulae, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75700196.summary: This inscription likely records the names of Gaius Cornelius Fuscus and Aemilia, possibly as part of a dedication or funerary monument.

EDCS-75700197.translation: Gaius Cesennius Secundus, dutiful, lived 75 years.
EDCS-75700197.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name, piety, and age at death of Gaius Cesennius Secundus.

EDCS-75700199.translation: Gaius Rasinius Faustus, veteran, dutiful, lived 50 [plus] years. Here he lies.
EDCS-75700199.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates the veteran Gaius Rasinius Faustus, noting his piety and age at death.

EDCS-75700200.translation: [---] Manlius, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, Receptus, aedile, duovir, perpetual flamen(?), lived [---] years.
EDCS-75700200.summary: This incomplete inscription records the career of Manlius Receptus, who held various municipal magistracies and priesthoods.

EDCS-75700201.translation: Cornelia Fausta, daughter of Sextus, dutiful(?), lived 26 years.
EDCS-75700201.summary: This funerary epitaph records the name, parentage, and age at death of Cornelia Fausta.

EDCS-75700202.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription beginning with the name Titus Veturius, possibly part of a larger text.

EDCS-75700203.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription for Capito, son of Cossutius, noting his lifespan.

EDCS-75700205.translation: May your bones rest well. Iulia Rosa, daughter of Caius, dutiful, lived 100 years. Here she lies. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-75700205.summary: This epitaph is for Iulia Rosa, who lived to 100 years of age, and includes common funerary formulae.

EDCS-75700206.translation: Sextus Pompeius, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, R[---], lived 75 years. Here he lies.
EDCS-75700206.summary: This funerary inscription records the name, tribal affiliation, lifespan, and burial place of Sextus Pompeius.

EDCS-75700207.translation: Gargilia Zara, dutiful, lived 35 years. Here she lies.
EDCS-75700207.summary: This funerary inscription records the name, piety, age at death, and burial of Gargilia Zara.

EDCS-75700208.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Pontius Umbrianus, son of [---], a veteran, served for [3] years. He lived for [3] years. He is buried here.
EDCS-75700208.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Pontius Umbrianus, a veteran, indicating his military service and lifespan.

EDCS-75900077.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XVI consule III et Imperatore Caesare Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto tribunicia potestate II consule II aedem Apollinis pecunia publica facta Quinto Voconio Saxa Fido proconsule Marcus Ummidius Annianus Quadratianus legatus fecit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-75900077.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, pontifex maximus, in his sixteenth year of tribunician power, and consul for the third time, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, in his second year of tribunician power, and consul for the second time, Marcus Ummidius Annianus Quadratianus, legate, built the temple of Apollo with public funds and dedicated it, while Quintus Voconius Saxa Fidus was proconsul.
EDCS-75900077.summary: A dedication for the construction and dedication of the temple of Apollo, commissioned by Marcus Ummidius Annianus Quadratianus under the joint consulship of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, during the proconsulship of Quintus Voconius Saxa Fidus.

EDCS-76100169.translation: Aurelius Chrestinus and Caelia Dativa made this for their daughter Caelia Chrestina, on account of her innocence.
EDCS-76100169.summary: A funerary monument set up by Aurelius Chrestinus and Caelia Dativa for their daughter Caelia Chrestina.

EDCS-76100171.translation: Urbanus set this up for his most beloved wife, Varia Victoria, for remembrance, on account of their cohabitation for 45 years.
EDCS-76100171.summary: A memorial for Varia Victoria, erected by her husband Urbanus after 45 years of marriage.

EDCS-76100173.summary: A simple epitaph for Vincentia, indicating her peaceful repose.

EDCS-76100180.translation: Aurelius Iustianus lived for 2 years, 1 month, and 13 days.
EDCS-76100180.summary: An epitaph recording the short lifespan of Aurelius Iustianus.

EDCS-76300055.translation: Marcus Caecilius Honoratus set this up for his dearest brother, [---] Caecilius [---] Secundus, son of [---] Secundus, [---] honored with public office, [---] prefect of the first cohort of Thracians, Roman citizens, perpetual flamen. The place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-76300055.summary: A funerary monument for Secundus, a former prefect and flamen, erected by his brother Marcus Caecilius Honoratus and approved by the local council.

EDCS-77400004.translation: Publius Annius Fuscus Masurius, son of Lucius Annius Constans, lived for 15 years.
EDCS-77400004.summary: An epitaph recording the lifespan of Publius Annius Fuscus Masurius.

EDCS-77400005.translation: Gaius Selius Felix made this for himself and his family.
EDCS-77400005.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by Gaius Selius Felix for himself and his household.

EDCS-77400006.summary: A fragmentary inscription likely related to Publius Faustinus Rufus.

EDCS-77400008.summary: This inscription records the name of Flavia Aponia Saloninula, likely a tombstone or an epitaph.

EDCS-77400017.translation: Sabbat(i)olus, who is also Iubentinus, placed in peace.
EDCS-77400017.summary: This inscription records the burial of Sabbat(i)olus, also known as Iubentinus, with the deceased being placed in peace.

EDCS-77700145.translation: Marcus Vettius Lupus, son of Titus, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-77700145.summary: This inscription records Marcus Vettius Lupus, son of Titus, fulfilling a vow, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-77700146.translation: Quintus Vettius Datus, son of Titus, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-77700146.summary: This inscription records Quintus Vettius Datus, son of Titus, fulfilling a vow, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-77700153.translation: Publius Popillius Neo, son of Publius, freedman, a merchant, is buried here.
EDCS-77700153.summary: This inscription records the burial of Publius Popillius Neo, a freedman and merchant.

EDCS-78300373.translation: Victor, son of Nabor, willingly fulfilled his vow with his own expense.
EDCS-78300373.summary: This inscription records Victor, son of Nabor, willingly and at his own expense fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-78300376.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Dead. [Name]adius lived 25 years.
EDCS-78300376.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a person named [3]adius, who lived 25 years.

EDCS-78300379.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Dead. For his dearest wife Fabricia Donatilla, [Name] Veturius Speratus erected this statue for her merits; she lived 27 years.
EDCS-78300379.summary: This funerary inscription records Veturius Speratus erecting a statue for his wife Fabricia Donatilla, honoring her merits, and states she lived 27 years.

EDCS-78300380.translation: Sacred to Sobrius Augustus Mercury, for the well-being of Hadrian Augustus. Ianuarius, son of Merula, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-78300380.summary: This inscription records Ianuarius Merulae fulfilling a vow to Mercury Sobrius Augustus for the health of Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-78300383.translation: Sacred to Neptune and the August Nymphs. The community of Thabor, with its suffetes Secundus, son of Zdruba, and Verus, son of Marcellus, fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-78300383.summary: This inscription records the community of Thabor, through its officials Secundus Zdrubalis and Verus Marcelli, fulfilling a vow to Neptune and the Nymphs.

EDCS-78300391.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely honoring Emperor Severus, possibly marking a monument or a dedication.

EDCS-78300392.translation: To the most pious, fortunate Emperor Maxim(us) Augustus, from the devoted colony Julia Augusta Numidica Simitthus. Twice devoted.
EDCS-78300392.summary: This inscription records the devotion of the colony of Simitthus to Emperor Maxim(us) Augustus, possibly indicating a repeated pledge of loyalty.

EDCS-78300394.translation: Lucius Gavius Felix and Valeria Isara, for the honor of the perpetual flamonic office, had this built at their own expense, for the Roman district.
EDCS-78300394.summary: This inscription records a private benefaction by Lucius Gavius Felix and Valeria Isara, who funded the construction of something related to the perpetual flamonic office in the Roman district.

EDCS-78300395.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Corfidia Tertia lived 110 years. Here she lies. Felicius and Fortunatus placed this for their devoted mother.
EDCS-78300395.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Corfidia Tertia, who lived to be 110, erected by her sons Felicius and Fortunatus.

EDCS-79300035.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sissinas the pious lived 21 years. His heir placed this for his friend.
EDCS-79300035.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sissinas, who died at age 21, with the monument erected by his heir for his friend.

EDCS-79600002.translation: To Marcus Vibius Gemellus Marcianus, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe, patron of the district and the city, for his exceptional generosity by which he adorned our homeland with most excellent temples, the city of Thugga, by decree of the decurions, erected this at public expense, with the curatorship of Mucius Felix, the senior sufete, and Julius Macro, son of Felicis.
EDCS-79600002.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the city of Thugga to Marcus Vibius Gemellus Marcianus, a patron honored for his generosity in building temples, with officials named.

EDCS-79600050.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Rutilius Primitivus, a shipping agent for the navigators of Suff..., his friends made this. To Pomponia Crescentia, his most beloved wife. Publius Turpilius Victor, husband, and Turpilia Victoria, daughter, made this.
EDCS-79600050.summary: This inscription is a double funerary monument, first honoring Rutilius Primitivus, an agent for shipping interests, and then his wife Pomponia Crescentia, with both epitaphs erected by surviving family members.

EDCS-79600051.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aemilia Bonifatia, a most beloved and incomparable woman, lived 22 years, 2 months, and 15 days. Severianus, her husband, made this for the deserving woman.
EDCS-79600051.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aemilia Bonifatia, who died at a young age, erected by her husband Severianus.

EDCS-79600052.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Licinius lived 20 years. Faustus, his father, [placed this] for his son.
EDCS-79600052.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Lucius Licinius, who died at age 20, erected by his father Faustus.

EDCS-79600059.translation: To the god Fruit-Bearer. Marcus Pomponius Montanus dedicated this offering.
EDCS-79600059.summary: This is a dedication of a votive offering to the god Frugifer by Marcus Pomponius Montanus.

EDCS-79600060.translation: To Lucius Caelio Saturninus, son of Caius Caelio, Flamen of Augustus, and former duovir, by the curiales of the college of Liber Pater, as patron, for his merits, they made (this) with their own money.
EDCS-79600060.summary: This is a dedication by a college to a patron, likely commemorating his service to the community and religious office, dating possibly to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-79600064.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Appius Donatus, son of Donatus, called Nigrinianus, lived for 54 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-79600064.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name, patronymic, filiation, cognomen, and age of a deceased individual, likely from the 2nd to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-79600068.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records the name and origin of an individual, with the date being uncertain but potentially Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-79600069.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Julius Celerio lived for 27 years, 11 months, and 16 days.
EDCS-79600069.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name and lifespan of a deceased individual, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-79600071.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name and lifespan of a deceased individual, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-79600072.translation: Natesius the pious lived 96 years and... months.
EDCS-79600072.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name, epithet, and lifespan of a deceased individual, with an unusually long lifespan noted, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-80000233.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records the name of an individual, possibly a tombstone or a dedication, with the date being uncertain but potentially Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-80600018.translation: To the Health of the Augusti, Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, father of the fatherland, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, and...
EDCS-80600018.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to the health of Emperors Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, dating to their joint rule, likely early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-81100017.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records the name of an individual, possibly a tombstone or a dedication, with the date being uncertain but potentially Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81100018.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records the name of an individual, possibly indicating ownership or a dedication, with the date being uncertain but potentially Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81100041.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely the name of the person commemorated or the maker of the object, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81100042.summary: This inscription consists of a personal name, Lucius Munatius Phile, likely identifying an individual, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81200039.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Minucius Fidelis lived 70 years, 5 months. Nothing was, nothing is mine. Nothing pains me.
EDCS-81200039.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Marcus Minucius Fidelis, who lived 70 years and 5 months, with a philosophical reflection on mortality.

EDCS-81300088.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales

EDCS-81300090.translation: Tiberius Larcius Carialis, Quintus Clodius Crescens, [son]nianus, Quintus +AR[---]
EDCS-81300090.summary: This inscription appears to list multiple Roman names, possibly indicating a group of individuals or a dedication, with incomplete information.

EDCS-81300091.translation: Gaius Vibius Animosus [---] of the Marcii Rusonianii [---]
EDCS-81300091.summary: This inscription mentions Gaius Vibius Animosus and the Marcii Rusonianii family, suggesting a connection between them, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300092.translation: Of Heraclius // of Serenos // Of Barea [---]NT
EDCS-81300092.summary: This inscription includes Greek names and potentially Latin affiliation, possibly relating to ownership or dedication of a monument.

EDCS-81300101.summary: This inscription lists three personal names, possibly indicating a dedication or a group of individuals, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300102.summary: This inscription presents variations of the name Macar, possibly indicating different individuals or grammatical uses, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300104.summary: This inscription repeats the Greek name Marrion, possibly for emphasis or as an owner's mark, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300107.translation: To Nemesis Augusta. Marcus Junius Crescens, son of Afri, imperial priest, curator of the public service, augur, twice chief magistrate, bearing the cost from his own funds, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-81300107.summary: This is a dedication to Nemesis Augusta by Marcus Junius Crescens, who held several important civic and religious offices, recording his fulfillment of a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300110.translation: To Nemesis Augusta, Marcus Junius Crescens, son of Africanus, twice-chiliarch and quinquennial official, a munerarius, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-81300110.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Nemesis by Marcus Junius Crescens, who held several local magistracies, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300116.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Cenonis, son of Cerialis, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300117.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius, freedman of Augustus, Aphrodisius, to his dearest wife, Pompeia Zois.
EDCS-81300117.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Marcus Aurelius Aphrodisius to his beloved wife Pompeia Zois, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300118.translation: Publius Saturnini Guddul, son of Publius, made their vow.
EDCS-81300118.summary: This is a votive inscription recording a vow made by Publius Saturnini Guddul, son of Publius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300120.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, VII times holding tribunician power, III times consul, Father of the Fatherland; Gaius Junius Maximus, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, perpetual priest, built the temple to Hadrian Augustus for the honor of the perpetual priesthood, valued at 21,000 sesterces, with multiplied money, from his own foundations, along with his sons Gaius Junius Severus and Publius Junius Maximus, son of Gaius, and he himself dedicated it.
EDCS-81300120.summary: This is a monumental dedication by Gaius Junius Maximus, a priest, for the construction and dedication of a temple to Emperor Hadrian, detailing the cost and his sons, dating to Hadrian's reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-81300122.translation: [For X]alissa, a most distinguished man, [for his] exceptional defense and distinguished leadership, for the common good, because through his exemplary services by which he diligently and with singular devotion considered the fatherland and all citizens, publicly and privately, the distinguished order of Arados, responding with his merits and repaid services with greater than expected duties, with its own devotion, has erected a statue to his name and honor for eternal memory, felicitously.
EDCS-81300122.summary: This is an honorific inscription by the city of Arados to an esteemed individual named Xalissa for his public and private services, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300123.translation: Ten pounds. By authority of Quintus Junius Rusticus, prefect of the city, a most distinguished man. Ten pounds.
EDCS-81300123.summary: This inscription records a quantity of ten pounds, authorized by Quintus Junius Rusticus, the prefect of the city, likely a record of goods or payments, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300134.summary: This is a short inscription, likely a name or a dedication, possibly funerary, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81700355.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Julius Zabullus lived piously for 66 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-81700355.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Julius Zabullus, who lived 66 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81700356.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Secundula Fulvia lived piously for 75 years.
EDCS-81700356.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Secundula Fulvia, who lived 75 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-82000130.translation: Of Flabius Vasira, son of Artimst.
EDCS-82000130.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Flabius Vasira, likely from the Roman province of Arabia.

EDCS-82000132.translation: Of Kaysnis, son of... Beatino, son of... Ekyc, son of... Duc.
EDCS-82000132.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording a patronymic line of descent.

EDCS-82000134.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription, possibly funerary or dedicatory, with names and partial words remaining.

EDCS-82000137.translation: Ioses Theodorus, the tax collector, from the estate of Vinius.
EDCS-82000137.summary: This inscription identifies Ioses Theodorus as a tax collector possibly connected to the property of Vinius.

EDCS-82200009.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Pompeius Nabor, who lived 29 years and 7 months. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Pompeius Barea, who lived 27 years and 10 days. Pompeius [...]pus, his parent, for his children.
EDCS-82200009.summary: This is a double funerary inscription honoring Pompeius Nabor and Pompeius Barea, with a parent dedicating the monument to their children.

EDCS-82200021.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletian Pius Felix Augustus, for the fourth time. To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian Pius Felix Augustus, for the third time. consuls(?). To the unconquered lords... 54 miles.
EDCS-82200021.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring the emperors Diocletian and Maximian, possibly related to a milestone or an imperial dedication, dating to their consulships.

EDCS-82200022.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Flavius Venustus, who lived 70 years. His sons [dedicated this] to the deserving [father].
EDCS-82200022.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavius Venustus, set up by his sons.

EDCS-82200023.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Cornelia Quintula, who lived 22 years. Her husband made [this].
EDCS-82200023.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cornelia Quintula, set up by her husband.

EDCS-82200025.translation: Julius Flavus, healthy, 66 years old. This is the grave of Mia, 33 years old.
EDCS-82200025.summary: This is a simple epitaph recording the ages of two individuals, Julius Flavus and Mia, likely a family or related individuals.

EDCS-82200026.translation: Marcius Metasan, son of Fidelis, and Flavia Hanochulam, and Seic[---] and Fidel[is], grandsons, erected this tablet for the eternal rest of Flavia. And [---] VII [---] and [---]VIS [---] VI[---] and [---] BUNOM MRAYSYNAU[---]YSFELUMBUL AYBYDBANNOM.
EDCS-82200026.summary: This inscription records the erection of a tablet by Marcius Metasan and other relatives for the memory of Flavia, with the latter part of the text being largely illegible but potentially containing a dedication or personal names.

EDCS-82200031.translation: In this sign, Granius Bibas with his sons and grandsons.
EDCS-82200031.summary: This is a funerary inscription marking a grave or tomb belonging to Granius Bibas and his family.

EDCS-82200107.translation: A funerary inscription in an unknown language.
EDCS-82200107.summary: This is a badly damaged funerary inscription, possibly in a non-Latin language, with unclear meaning.

EDCS-82200111.translation: An inscription in an unknown language with some legible names and titles.
EDCS-82200111.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely funerary, with some Latinized names and titles like 'procurator', indicating an administrative role.

EDCS-82200112.translation: A funerary inscription in an unknown language.
EDCS-82200112.summary: This is a damaged funerary inscription in an unknown language, with numbers possibly indicating age or quantity.

EDCS-82200115.summary: This is a short Greek funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of a person named Faustus.

EDCS-82800061.translation: Julia Quieta, daughter of Quintus, priestess, lived 91 years. Quintus Julius Ampliatus, veteran, lived 85 years. Quintus Julius Respectus, son of Quintus, lived 17 years.
EDCS-82800061.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription recording the lifespans of at least three individuals, two of whom are from the Iulius family.

EDCS-82800063.translation: Valeria Nica placed this at her own expense while alive on these lands.
EDCS-82800063.summary: This is a dedication by Valeria Nica, who funded the erection of something on her property during her lifetime.

EDCS-82800064.translation: Sacred to the shades of the departed. Aelius Nepotianus, Roman equestrian, lived 39 years, 5 months, and 10 days. His brother Aelius Clementianus managed the burial. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-82800064.summary: This is a Roman funerary inscription for Aelius Nepotianus, specifying his age and the person responsible for his burial, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-82800065.translation: Sacred to the shades of the departed. Iulia Rogatina.
EDCS-82800065.summary: This is a simple Roman funerary inscription dedicated to Iulia Rogatina.
